game_name: 恋爱吗？我家猫会后空翻
review_text:
This game requires an internet connection (it has online verification – 3rd-party DRM) and has no cloud saves. If your internet connection is lost, your game progress will be lost. Your save file also seems to sync to the developer’s server, not Steam. If the developer’s server is no longer available or cannot be reached, the game becomes unplayable.

I keep FMV games that have no online verification and can be played offline, and I refund FMV games that have online verification. You can’t keep games with online verification because the server will not stay online forever—in other words, it feels more like renting than purchasing.

This is my first negative Steam review. A negative review on Steam is more like reporting the issues the game has, rather than simply saying whether to recommend it or not, as most FMV games usually get around a least 6/10 score. These issues are enough to not recommend to buy it, and the game with online verification is basically a rental.

Below is the complete topic about whether Online Verification is necessary and how it eventually prevents the game from running:

[url=steamcommunity.com/groups/AsianFMV/discussions/4/668348325619280646]Asian FMV Issue 1: 🔒 Online Verification[/url]

[hr][/hr]

该游戏需要互联网连接（具有在线验证 - 第三方 DRM）并且没有云保存。如果您的互联网连接丢失，您的游戏进度将丢失。您的保存文件似乎也同步到开发者的服务器，而不是Steam。如果开发者的服务器不再可用或无法访问，则游戏将无法玩。

我保留没有在线验证且可以离线玩的FMV游戏，并且退款有在线验证的FMV游戏。你不能保留有在线验证的游戏，因为服务器不会永远在线——换句话说，感觉更像是租赁而不是购买。

这是我在 Steam 上的第一个负面评论。Steam 上的负面评论更像是报告游戏存在的问题，而不是简单地说是否推荐它，因为大多数 FMV 游戏通常至少获得 6/10 的分数。这些问题足以不建议购买，而且具有在线验证的游戏基本上就是租赁。

以下是关于在线验证是否有必要，以及它如何最终导致游戏无法运行的完整讨论：

[url=steamcommunity.com/groups/AsianFMV/discussions/4/668348325619280646]亚洲 FMV 议题 1：🔒 在线验证[/url]

BTW, you can vote to the girl(s) you love best. I pick Gu Panpan, cause I like her characteristc. lol

review_text:
In my opinion, the story of this game is just average. Only a few characters are well-developed, while the others feel underdeveloped. Some characters also have limited screen time, which results in a lack of attachment for the player.

MAIN PROBLEM This game requires online verification, and you cannot play without an internet connection.

Some of the character audio in this game is poor and feels unprofessional, which ruins the immersive experience. For example, distorted audio or poor audio quality.

The English subtitles were made with MTL, so don't expect them to be good. Be grateful that you can understand English so you can correct them in your mind.

Rating? 4/10

game_name: 恋爱吗？我家猫会后空翻
review_text:
[b]Updated Review:[/b]
First, I want to thank the developer for replying to my previous review. It is rare to see developers engaging so actively with feedback, and I really appreciate it.

Regarding the updates, the [b]audio quality[/b] and [b]translation[/b] are definitely better now, though there is still some room for improvement. However, a few cons remain:
[list]
	[*][b]AI Implementation: [/b]As mentioned in my past review, the use of AI for characters is still a downside. I am not against AI technology, but in an FMV game, it would be much better to use a real human actor to portray an AI character rather than using generative AI imagery.
	[*][b]Annoying Chatbox UI:[/b] The chatbox is quite intrusive. Currently, it automatically turns back on after every choice/scene. It would be much better to have a persistent toggle in the settings to keep it permanently off.
	[*][b]Story Continuity Issues:[/b] I understand that rewriting the core story is difficult at this stage, but the narrative logic needs to be fixed. For example, when reaching [b]Suzuki Rei's ending in Chapter 5[/b], I triggered a flashback referencing events that never happened in my specific playthrough. The game shouldn't show memories from routes I didn't take.
[/list]

[b]Original Review:[/b]
I have mixed feelings regarding this game. It is playable, but keep these major issues in mind:
[list]
[*] [b]Weak Story Development:[/b] The narrative progression is poor. There is almost no cause and effect during gameplay; decisions are just binary switches to increase or decrease a relationship bar, meaning the story remains static until the ending cutscene.
[*] [b]Poor Audio Quality:[/b] The sound quality is consistently bad.
[*] [b]Poor English Localization:[/b] The translation is rough and often difficult to understand, making it hard to follow the plot accurately.
[*] [b]Inconsistent Visuals due to AI:[/b] The inclusion of AI-generated characters is a major downside. Unlike the AI music which is tolerable, the AI characters clash with the game's aesthetic and distract from the experience.
[/list]

It doesn't matter if it's free if I can't play it.

Please, indie devs, if you're going to make anything in FPP, you MUST include mouse Y axis invert! This is not optional for about 30% of all FPS gamers!
Crashes before it can begin for me.

Nothing is straight forward. Without watching a dozen YouTube tutorials you’ll never figure out how to do much of anything and die over and over. And if you manage to get any progress the game will randomly bug out and not let you interact with anything. It’s one of those games where simple tasks are unnecessarily convoluted processes and it’s not obvious how the game wants you to do them. I like the idea but the game isn’t worth $5

I tried to enjoy this game, I really did, but it doesn't look like the devs really tried that hard.
Let me start off by saying I was given this game. I was undecided on getting it myself because the game really looked janky, and my fears were indeed proven true.

The core of the game is really cool. You build yourself a car out of old pieces, and try to survive a finnish summer in the late 90's. The problem is that while the core of the game is interesting, it falls flat when it plays like a free early access game. 

The physics and polish of this game are something unlike I have ever seen in any Unity game. The bad includes, but is not limited to:
-Touching the body of Satsuma can send you flying
-Touching the body of Satsuma without using the push can exert a phantom force upon it that can either lift it slightly off the ground or flip it
-When phantom forces are applied onto Satsuma, the windshield may spontaneously shatter
-The moped fails to be upright when you get on it for a considerable percentage of times
-The job offers of the game are completely, uncontrollably random. You may get a phone for your grandma asking to get her groceries, and then a second later it starts raining, causing grandma to despawn. You may get a phone call to deliver lumber as close as ~1 minute before the deadline, leading you to go there and find no one to deliver to
-The player only ever carry exactly one (1) item at a time, whether it be a sausage or a fully built car engine, and has no pockets(Yes, there's a mod for that, I am aware)
-When dropping items, you may inadvertently drop them partially inside furniture, the ground, a wall, or the vehicle. when you do, a few things may happen. The item in question may get flung at great speed at a certain direction. The item in question may go through whatever it is touching, leading you to lose it forever* (*items that fall out of the map reappear in the landfill, but you also have to haul your ass over there to get your stuff back). When this is done with a vehicle, the vehicle in question may move, flip, fly, and/or have the windshield shattered.

Ultimately, a cool game, but unpolished to the point of feeling unfinished. As far as I know, My Winter Car is also the exact same and the dev has not learned anything.

This game is absolutely terrible. First of all, lets start with the Satsuma, the car you have to fix up. So, imagine you don't know much about engines, and you would like to fix up your old car. Surely you could find  a manual for it, right? WRONG! There is literally NO WAY to know how to build the car unless you know absolutely EVERYTHING about engines, or watch a tutorial online that is a LOT slower than if they just added a manual into the game. Then, after that, the ONLY OTHER THINGS TO DO are to either do a few missions, and race other cars. After you do the very little missions there are to do, the whole rest of the game is racing and repeatable missions. Also, the only two races that you can even do is a drag race and a rally race. That is it. No other types of races, or custom races. There are only like 5 or so repeatable missions as well, so you get VERY bored after that. On my first and only playthrough of the game, I was almost done with the Satsuma, and was about to order tires to drive it around, until i got a call from Grandma asking to take her to the church. After looking online, i figured out that you HAVE to have the Satsuma complete before Sunday, or Grandma will walk all the way to church, with a high risk of getting hit by cars, since it is the day that the races happen. So, either optimise your time as much as you can, and take grandma to church, or risk her getting hit by a car, and PERMANENTLY DYING, with no way to revive. This means that if she does get hit, all of the missions for her are gone, leaving even less things to do after you finish the main missions. Because it took longer than 2 hours to even make it to Sunday, i cant refund the game anymore, so now i am stuck with a game i will never play again.

There is a fine line between being funny and being just bad. For me - in this case it's the latter. I've wasted 8 hours of my life trying my best to like this game. The premise is extremely appealing for any car enthusiast, and I am one so how bad can it be, right? RIGHT?

80% of my playtime was spent commuting from A to B by the moped or boat (mostly between the shop and house) and it was one of the dullest things I've ever experienced.

Even if you know really well how cars work, you're gonna have a hard time having fun because:
- Nothing is explained, so you basically have to read or watch an online tutorial before playing to know what to do.
- Half of the more advanced stuff like parts ordering is completely not translated so have fun reading finnish.
- The map is huge and it's not a good thing - it takes a VERY long time to make a round trip to the town with the shop and don't even get me started on actually bringing something useful with you when you still don't have a working car.
- If you try to assemble the engine without the ratchet upgrade, you're gonna break your scroll wheel because the screws take their sweet time until they are tight.
- Also, physical screw sizes are inconsistent with their spanner sizes - or maybe I'm just going mad after being subjected to 30 minutes of looped motorboat sound - anyways, the fact this is even a mechanic which survived  for such a long time in this game makes me think this is all just a very elaborate ragebait.
- I once got woken up by a phonecall, but it was so dark, that you couldn't see anything. And okay, it was pretty funny at first, but then I've spent 10 minutes trying to find a light switch and was really close to just alt+f4 this game.
- Oh, and also there is no autosave, of which I forgot and lost 2h because there is no warning that you'll lose progress.

Also, the moment you come up with something fun - like bringing the moped on a boat with you - the physics will 99% glitch out and you will die or get ejected into oblivion.

Don't get me wrong, I know that this game doesn't treat itself seriously, that's not a bad thing. But you know what is? BAD GAME DESIGN. And I will stand by this opinion because I value my time, sanity and passion for cars.

To finnish on a lighter note - the map design has a great mood and even the archaic graphics don't bother me. Also, the detailed car model is something one of a kind, but if you have at least some self respect, do not subject yourself to this game.

asked for a refund... save your money from this glitchy nightmare... the concept would of been cool of your game wasn't this buggy. get up from kneeling after buiilding the suspension flips the car to the roof and breaks the window. restart... house burns down.. not fun at all.

Don't by this game unless you want to spend an unreasonable amount of time trying to get a car to run. Fun at first with building the car and drinking booze n pissin. After you put everything together the car can still not start. Have to take apart every thing to fix it. Is it the fuel? Is it the starter? Is it the battery? You don't know. Not to mention when you start to run out of money and they shut off your power. Awesome...can't get money because you need a car to do missions. Can't fix car because you need money. Ass game. Waste of time.

First save got lost for some reason. Second save spent an hour and a half building the engine then went across the map to get grandma food and my bike dropped through the floor. Decided to stand in traffic and wait for death instead of going through the 30-40 minute walk back home to see if my bike respawned. Too buggy imo for how old it is. No volume sliders and alot of the sounds are very loud and annoying. I can see where this game goes and it looks cool but im seeing alot of people suggest using a save editor for me to continue playing the game for these bugs. As a standard i think games should function without cheating to fix the issues.

Unplayable without mods, a fantastic work for over 10 years of development.
Do yourself a favor: just play (albeit less complex) Mon Bazou.

Literally no instructions on what to do or how to play, no markers or map to tell you where you are or where to go. I should have listened when they said this game is hard, but I didnt know it was so hard that I cant even attempt to enjoy 99% of what the game has to offer.

The core gameplay mechanic is an ultra-realistic life simulation with no real goal besides your own desire to restore a family car that is in pieces in the garage. While the game is undoubtedly unique and original, the experience is severely hampered by bugs and issues that impede you from simply living the life you chose.

The most prominent flaw is the horrendous object movement system, which adds multiple minutes to simple tasks like restocking a pantry or fridge. Because these chores become so time-consuming, you have to address survival meters (Thirst, Hunger, etc.) more often, creating a repetitive loop of constantly driving down meters instead of them being something you address less frequently. A simple solution would be modding the game to tweak or remove the survival meters, but there are deeper issues.

Installing a single car part can take as long as 30 minutes because of how buggy and janky the snap-on system is just to get a part in place. Once in place, you'll find yourself switching spanners or sockets 5–6 times due to how impossible it is to physically identify a bolt size. I have zero desire to spend two hours finishing a car only to drive for 10 seconds and experience a bug that leaves the car totaled in a ditch because the game didn't register a tire installation correctly—an error I only identified by checking my save file with MSC Editor (which is basically the only advice you find when searching for help). I understand the want for ultra-realism, but in real life, I don’t pre-install every bolt before figuring out which spanner I need, and I can certainly check if a tire is secure before driving. Many of these mechanics feel less like a simulation and more like a lack of explanation or logic to how a task is completed.

Ultimately, I cannot recommend a game that suffers from such poor survival elements and janky mechanics. Making something hard just to be hard is not interesting or fun; a challenge should be logical and rewarding to the player.

I like this game and will likely continue playing it; it truly has a pleasant vibe and can surprise you with unexpected mechanics and attention to detail. However, I cannot recommend it to anyone who values their time and sanity.

It's buggy, clunky, full of issues that nobody fixes or even intends to fix, riddled with poor design choices, and completely unplayable without guides and third-party software.

A few examples:

1. You won’t be able to properly place a part even when you know where it goes. It just won’t snap into place, forcing you to spend several minutes trying to find the exact spot.

2. There’s a bus stop that exists but doesn’t work - the bus simply won’t stop there, something you’ll only learn from the wiki.

3. If someone near you gets killed by someone else - say, a pedestrian is run over - you’ll be sentenced to 10 in-game days in prison, where there’s literally nothing to do.

4. One prison day equals 2 hours of real time. Enjoy sitting AFK for 20 hours.

5. At some point, the days might just stop counting. You’ll sit through a day, but your Jail Days won’t decrease.

6. The quirky physics, where a multi-ton truck suddenly does a flip and lands on its roof. Good luck flipping it back without third-party tools.

7. Characters speak only Finnish, with dialogues lasting several minutes, while subtitles show a short, minimal sentence. Learn Finnish! 

8. Almost everything written in Finnish, even with English language settings. Learn Finnish!

9. MSC Editor - third-party software - is practically mandatory because, without it, you can’t flip a flipped vehicle, for instance.

10. By the way, there are no vehicle diagnostics tools in the game. You won’t be able to figure out why the car isn’t running unless you’re a real-life mechanic who fixes such bolt buckets IRL. Again, bless the developer of MSC Editor, which lets you check part conditions.

11. You go to a store on a Wednesday evening turning into Thursday? Too bad - the store’s inventory won’t restock, and you’ll starve to death because there’s NO food. Game over. Start over. OR check the wiki, read guides, and find out that there’s actually a fishing trap in the game, locate it, and survive on nothing but fish until the store restocks.

12. Half the achievements are just tedious, uninteresting chores tied to RNG.

13. The bus won't open its doors after you're released from jail. You walk for 10 real-life minutes until your always-drunk cousin appears (the one who crashed into your car and died, which is why you went to jail the first time). You get into his car, and he runs over some random NPC who walks out of nowhere, so you get sent to jail for another 10 days. 

And these are just a few examples I could recall off the top of my head. The game was made by a talented, creative person who cared deeply about details but clearly lacked development skills. A real shame.

EDIT: Added a 13th point.

This game is designed to steal as much time away from your real life as possible while constantly spitting in your face. I legitimately feel scammed. For an example, the only vehicle you get at the start is a tractor which moves so slowly if you buy food from the store and try to bring it home with the tractor it will spoil before it gets there causing you to softlock. There are no tutorials, so I had to look up how to take the helmet off. If you can't figure it out you will die because it prevents you from eating or drinking. I had to look up a map because the game is designed for you to get lost in it and there are no road signs that have the name of your home location on them anywhere in the game - not that the game will actually mention where it is you live at any point. It's very important that you know what time it is so you don't end up wasting a bunch of real life hours waiting for stores to open and stuff, but you don't start with a watch and I don't know where to find it. The game is not translated, only a few elements of the UI, so buying stuff from the upgrade guy can be extremely unclear what it is you're actually purchasing, and getting around to do tasks for the money you're likely buying the wrong thing with will invariably lead you to some kind of incident where you are softlocked because there are at least 12,000 ways to softlock yourself in the game and nothing is explained. It also has a hardcore save system where you can only save your game at a toilet, meaning when the game punishes you for its own physics bugs, the punishment is permanent and continuing unless you start a new save. Only buy this game if you already consider your life a waste, because this game has nothing at all but disdain for you as the player and it will go out of its way to make sure every moment you spend in it was all for nothing. A big waste of money. Would need mods to be playable... good luck modding this game. I can just tell it will be extremely janky and will probably consume even more real life time. There is a fine line between being funny and being just bad. For me - in this case it's the latter. I've wasted 8 hours of my life trying my best to like this game. The premise is extremely appealing for any car enthusiast, and I am one so how bad can it be, right? RIGHT?

80% of my playtime was spent commuting from A to B by the moped or boat (mostly between the shop and house) and it was one of the dullest things I've ever experienced.

Even if you know really well how cars work, you're gonna have a hard time having fun because:
- Nothing is explained, so you basically have to read or watch an online tutorial before playing to know what to do.
- Half of the more advanced stuff like parts ordering is completely not translated so have fun reading finnish.
- The map is huge and it's not a good thing - it takes a VERY long time to make a round trip to the town with the shop and don't even get me started on actually bringing something useful with you when you still don't have a working car.
- If you try to assemble the engine without the ratchet upgrade, you're gonna break your scroll wheel because the screws take their sweet time until they are tight.
- Also, physical screw sizes are inconsistent with their spanner sizes - or maybe I'm just going mad after being subjected to 30 minutes of looped motorboat sound - anyways, the fact this is even a mechanic which survived  for such a long time in this game makes me think this is all just a very elaborate ragebait.
- I once got woken up by a phonecall, but it was so dark, that you couldn't see anything. And okay, it was pretty funny at first, but then I've spent 10 minutes trying to find a light switch and was really close to just alt+f4 this game.
- Oh, and also there is no autosave, of which I forgot and lost 2h because there is no warning that you'll lose progress.

Also, the moment you come up with something fun - like bringing the moped on a boat with you - the physics will 99% glitch out and you will die or get ejected into oblivion.

Don't get me wrong, I know that this game doesn't treat itself seriously, that's not a bad thing. But you know what is? BAD GAME DESIGN. And I will stand by this opinion because I value my time, sanity and passion for cars.

To finnish on a lighter note - the map design has a great mood and even the archaic graphics don't bother me. Also, the detailed car model is something one of a kind, but if you have at least some self respect, do not subject yourself to this game.

This game is absolutely terrible. First of all, lets start with the Satsuma, the car you have to fix up. So, imagine you don't know much about engines, and you would like to fix up your old car. Surely you could find  a manual for it, right? WRONG! There is literally NO WAY to know how to build the car unless you know absolutely EVERYTHING about engines, or watch a tutorial online that is a LOT slower than if they just added a manual into the game. Then, after that, the ONLY OTHER THINGS TO DO are to either do a few missions, and race other cars. After you do the very little missions there are to do, the whole rest of the game is racing and repeatable missions. Also, the only two races that you can even do is a drag race and a rally race. That is it. No other types of races, or custom races. There are only like 5 or so repeatable missions as well, so you get VERY bored after that. On my first and only playthrough of the game, I was almost done with the Satsuma, and was about to order tires to drive it around, until i got a call from Grandma asking to take her to the church. After looking online, i figured out that you HAVE to have the Satsuma complete before Sunday, or Grandma will walk all the way to church, with a high risk of getting hit by cars, since it is the day that the races happen. So, either optimise your time as much as you can, and take grandma to church, or risk her getting hit by a car, and PERMANENTLY DYING, with no way to revive. This means that if she does get hit, all of the missions for her are gone, leaving even less things to do after you finish the main missions. Because it took longer than 2 hours to even make it to Sunday, i cant refund the game anymore, so now i am stuck with a game i will never play again.

I tried to enjoy this game, I really did, but it doesn't look like the devs really tried that hard.
Let me start off by saying I was given this game. I was undecided on getting it myself because the game really looked janky, and my fears were indeed proven true.

The core of the game is really cool. You build yourself a car out of old pieces, and try to survive a finnish summer in the late 90's. The problem is that while the core of the game is interesting, it falls flat when it plays like a free early access game. 

The physics and polish of this game are something unlike I have ever seen in any Unity game. The bad includes, but is not limited to:
-Touching the body of Satsuma can send you flying
-Touching the body of Satsuma without using the push can exert a phantom force upon it that can either lift it slightly off the ground or flip it
-When phantom forces are applied onto Satsuma, the windshield may spontaneously shatter
-The moped fails to be upright when you get on it for a considerable percentage of times
-The job offers of the game are completely, uncontrollably random. You may get a phone for your grandma asking to get her groceries, and then a second later it starts raining, causing grandma to despawn. You may get a phone call to deliver lumber as close as ~1 minute before the deadline, leading you to go there and find no one to deliver to
-The player only ever carry exactly one (1) item at a time, whether it be a sausage or a fully built car engine, and has no pockets(Yes, there's a mod for that, I am aware)
-When dropping items, you may inadvertently drop them partially inside furniture, the ground, a wall, or the vehicle. when you do, a few things may happen. The item in question may get flung at great speed at a certain direction. The item in question may go through whatever it is touching, leading you to lose it forever* (*items that fall out of the map reappear in the landfill, but you also have to haul your ass over there to get your stuff back). When this is done with a vehicle, the vehicle in question may move, flip, fly, and/or have the windshield shattered.

Ultimately, a cool game, but unpolished to the point of feeling unfinished. As far as I know, My Winter Car is also the exact same and the dev has not learned anything.

The game is not translated, only a few elements of the UI, so buying stuff from the upgrade guy can be extremely unclear what it is you're actually purchasing, and getting around to do tasks for the money you're likely buying the wrong thing with will invariably lead you to some kind of incident where you are softlocked because there are at least 12,000 ways to softlock yourself in the game and nothing is explained. Only buy this game if you already consider your life a waste, because this game has nothing at all but disdain for you as the player and it will go out of its way to make sure every moment you spend in it was all for nothing. I usually don't write bad reviews at all, but this will have to be an exception. The game is a indie project that was made by one guy or a very small team, I get that, I know it will not be perfect.
My main problem with the game is that it constantly feels frustrating. There is no guide or explanation to anything. I'm not talking about the obvious "building the engine" that is literally the easy part... 
I'm talking about having to figure out where to shop, when to shop, how to arrive etc. The hunger and thirst are just there to mess with you honestly.
Flies will buzz permanently on your ear even if you shower. Food spoils in less than a day if it is not inside of the fridge. If you go to sleep with the lights off and wake up in the middle of the night there the room is pitch dark, 100% dark... and guess what... good luck finding the light swtich. 
Also there is no pause. I mean... come on... I had to install cheat engine to be able to pause the game because I wanted to follow the wiki, which btw starts with (you should watch this game from another person before playing). That was already a huge redflag tbh...
Controls are fine for the most part, some pieces of the engine really refuse to connect if you don't exactly place them pixel perfect, this doesn't happen always tho... 

Overall, I wanted a game about building and maintaning a car, but at the end of the day I spent over 6 hours doing groceries just to notice I am missing sparkplugs for the engine... I refused to go shopping again.
If you have patience and this is really your kind of itch, then go for it. Almost 97% of the players agree is good to a certain degree. However I wouldn't ever recommend it because it honestly pissed me off so many times due to some mechanic/features.
Dev threw in AI later on down the line after alot of people already brought the game, and it seems the excuses are about typical as to why they used it.

Literally no instructions on what to do or how to play, no markers or map to tell you where you are or where to go. I should have listened when they said this game is hard, but I didnt know it was so hard that I cant even attempt to enjoy 99% of what the game has to offer.

The core gameplay mechanic is an ultra-realistic life simulation with no real goal besides your own desire to restore a family car that is in pieces in the garage. While the game is undoubtedly unique and original, the experience is severely hampered by bugs and issues that impede you from simply living the life you chose.

The most prominent flaw is the horrendous object movement system, which adds multiple minutes to simple tasks like restocking a pantry or fridge. Because these chores become so time-consuming, you have to address survival meters (Thirst, Hunger, etc.) more often, creating a repetitive loop of constantly driving down meters instead of them being something you address less frequently. A simple solution would be modding the game to tweak or remove the survival meters, but there are deeper issues.

Installing a single car part can take as long as 30 minutes because of how buggy and janky the snap-on system is just to get a part in place. Once in place, you'll find yourself switching spanners or sockets 5–6 times due to how impossible it is to physically identify a bolt size. I have zero desire to spend two hours finishing a car only to drive for 10 seconds and experience a bug that leaves the car totaled in a ditch because the game didn't register a tire installation correctly—an error I only identified by checking my save file with MSC Editor (which is basically the only advice you find when searching for help). I understand the want for ultra-realism, but in real life, I don’t pre-install every bolt before figuring out which spanner I need, and I can certainly check if a tire is secure before driving. Many of these mechanics feel less like a simulation and more like a lack of explanation or logic to how a task is completed.

Ultimately, I cannot recommend a game that suffers from such poor survival elements and janky mechanics. Making something hard just to be hard is not interesting or fun; a challenge should be logical and rewarding to the player.

The balance between knowing you will have your just reward for the pain, and that the proper punishment is waiting the criminals, is most crucial to maintain your sanity in such dark circumstances, only God can provide that, because he is the only one always with you and sees everything.

( Do not think that God is unaware of what criminals do. He only delays them until a Day when ˹their˺ eyes will stare in horror. On that day the earth will be replaced by another earth, and the heavens [as well], and all creatures will come out before their God, the One, the Prevailing. On that Day you will see the culprits bound together in chains. They devised every plot, which was fully known to Allah, even if their plans had been [sufficient] to eliminate mountains. So be patient. Surely Allah’s promise is true. Whether We show you some of what We threaten them with, or cause you to die ˹before that˺, to Us they shall ˹all˺ be returned.)
Quran (Final Book from God)

No comfort features like drawing in the game to find contradictions, which becomes very annoying in later levels. Glitchy in some infinite puzzles. And the credits give me a unwell feeling supporting such AI generated asset mishmash.
It's not a bad puzzle concept (a novel variation of a minesweeper-style game), but later puzzles are more suited for a computer than a human to solve

I really like this game. Can recommend for most ages. I will change this review back into positive when issues are fixed. 

In it's current state the game has become unplayable for me. When starting a game, the game uses so much RAM that it freezes within the first round of drawing. I have 16GB, game used up to 8, while min. requirements state 4?

The database for words it CAN guess are tiny, probably as small as the list of words it gives you to guess from, and that list it gives you to guess from isn't shared with the players, so its really unfair against the players unless you use meme's that are insanely abstract.

Edit to respond to dev: The reason I say this is because we had a new person join the game who didn't know what was going on, they didn't draw the thing in the answer, and because the pool of words the AI is choosing from is so small, it guessed the right answer. There were no pictures of the answer, but it guessed the right answer. Humans don't have that short list, we have our entire lived experience.

This is a frustrating one. I love this game. This game has a lot of potential and I really enjoyed it, but the roadmap is leading me to believe they're winding down a bit too much after release. This feels like REALLY early access, and the roadmap looks like the developers think they're done. 

The gameplay is fun. I love the style of combat mixed with RPG elements and the time-stopping strategy feel to it. Gear crafting is OP and scratches the power fantasy itch. 

Pros:
	- Some cool class variety. 
	- Combat style is great.
	- Full Diablo style loot+equipment system.
	- Character building is fun. I always love some good theorycrafting.
	
Cons:
	- There are no boss monsters. Just the occasional elites. 
	- Enemy variety is paltry. The loading screen has a gelatinous cube and it doesn't even exist. 2 adventures and I guarantee you've seen all the enemies in the game. 
	- Very few maps. Roadmap only shows addition of 2 more.
	- "Quests" are just complete each difficulty dungeon on every character, numbered 1-40.
	- Stat "bonuses" are DND style with milestones, meaning later in the game you could add 9 strength and get zero benefit until that 10th strength is added, and the bonus cap ends at 150 stats. It doesn't particularly mix well with Diablo style gear/skills adding percentage to stats. There are so many ways to get overpowered from crafting that these limitations seem arbitrary and limit choice. 
	- No inventory sort, but at least there's a filter. 
	- Physical spells are limited to your class. The only skills you can learn from the thieves guild and the stronghold are your class skills. So pure physical combat characters only have up to 3 different spells, which feels bad. 
	- Spell balance between the schools is way off. Late game, even as a grandmaster, some skills just scale so horribly that they become unusable. Devs need to exclusively play each spell school to see how they feel. There's no way they've done this. 
	- Massively overpowered skills dwarf all the other skills, especially late game. The worst, Grandmaster Bard, has an insta-kill song (aura) that plays for 2 minutes, ignores immunities, and has no limit to what it can kill. This skill needs to be completely overhauled.
	- Random collectibles around the map don't scale with difficulty, and the paltry resources (usually 1-2) you do get pale in comparison to even tier 1 adventure shrine purchases where you can buy anywhere from 1-30 (and 4 purchase options per shrine), and you almost always have enough currency to buy all of them. Collectibles don't feel good.
	- There's inconsistency with how skills work. Most skills increase stats additively, some skills lose their tier 1 and 2 bonuses when you upgrade to tier 3 (spell mastery), some skills have typos in what their bonuses actually apply (resistance)
	- The fountain EXP transfer function (late game function) has an extreme resource cost that makes it borderline unusable. It costs 250 of every resource to bring a level 1 to level 10. It costs like 3 resources to increase your gears' enchantment tier. 
	- The "arena" maps having these random spawn points is stupid. Enemies just teleport in the middle of the map preventing you from taking a defensive position anywhere. 
	- Rats are so GD annoying in this game. Rats that sit back in brush casting fireballs and meteors shouldn't be a thing. Make rats behave like rats. Have humanoid monsters exclusively cast spells. Bats casting blood magic is annoying too, but at least it semi makes sense and can be likened to vampirism. Bats being as tanky as humanoids shouldn't be a thing either though.
	
Features I want:
	- Add a lock for items you don't want sold. 
	- Add boss rooms. Make bosses immune to instant kill/enslave, and progressively resistant to hard disables.
	- Revamp fire spells. Too many enemies have natural ~95% fire resistance, and too many fire spells can kill your own team. No other element has these problems. Meteor shower is a tiny radius with way too long of an activation period. Also, fix the "Jumping Fire" spell tooltip, wtf. Grandmaster "Incinerate" is absolutely horrible in every way. 
	- Ground target radius spells should show the size of the area being affected. 
	- Remove the Grandmaster Bard skill, for the love of god.
	- Turn shift into an active blocking mechanic. Blocks made in this way force the damage onto the active character and yield a brief recovery time. Maybe scale the recovery time with a new Block skill. 
	- Percentage damage reduction added to blocks instead of simply increasing physical resistance for that damage instance. Current block implementation feels bad. 
	- Physical schools of magic for Stronghold and Thieves Guild.
	- Add unarmed weapons. Not having something to enchant feels bad.
	- Add a cooldown reduction cap.
	- Add a way to reduce spellcast recovery. Add a cap for this recovery. 10-15 second recovery spells are absurd. Let energy be our bottleneck. Or maybe separate spellcast recovery from physical, that way my character isn't just effectively stunned for 10 seconds. 
	- More ways to increase radius for skills or projectile count maybe. I love stuff like that.
	- Adventures are too short. Some are as short as 2 fight maps, and there isn't a good reason not to take the shorter path, ever. 
	- Adventures need more path options. I don't see a good reason why there are only 2 paths. 
	- Too frequently a rest point on a path is followed by a chest room, or a shrine room making these rest points completely meaningless. I have literally never made use of the rest point regen buff, because it only applies to the subsequent map which is always a peaceful map. 
	- I would like to keep levelling beyond 35, and dungeoning beyond tier 40. After tier 40 is beaten, unlock the 35 cap and the new endless difficulty. Character power is already soft-capped by skillpoint allocation scaling and 150 stat limits.
	or
	- Add a character reincarnation feature where they can keep some stats and skillpoints, but make them restart at level 1. This would also make the "Apprentice" enchantment suck less. I would also just increase the maximum effectiveness of this enchantment from 90% to 100% item level requirement reduction. There's no good reason to limit this to 90%.
Needs a LOT of content. Very, very basic and unfinished. Comprehensive class/skill/race system, but there's nothing to do with it?

GOOD GAME THAT'S TOO DIFFICULT FOR ME TO ENJOY.

Really cool game — exactly what I hoped it would be... at first. 

Unfortunately, it ramps up in difficulty fast (really ****ing fast), and after just half an hour I already feel discouraged from playing further.

For some players, that steep challenge will be a huge selling point. But for others (like me — someone who loves games but isn’t particularly skilled), it means each run lasts maybe 10–15 minutes before hitting an insurmountable wall (akin to paying full price for a demo). That’s fine in short bursts, but it’s clear that most of the deeper gameplay mechanics and features are locked behind a skill ceiling that only a small group of talented players will ever reach...and, judging by the reviews, i guess that's who this game is for.  

it definitely sucks to suck because it looks like i'll be missing out on what seems to be an awesome game.

this game is executed quite well despite its ambitious idea and sole developer. furthermore, its cool enough for me to hypothetically wait and hope that either A. new difficulty levels are implemented (doubtful) or b. someone creates a mod to alter the game's settings (even less likely).  

The sad reality is that I'll probably give it another few minutes but it will likely get lost in the digital pile and never be considered again - whether my aforementioned conditions are met or not. I don't say this because it doesn't deserve my attention but because there are just too many other great games coming out (with adjustable difficulty and accessibility settings) for me to get stuck, frustrated and unhappy, on this one game. 

yet another reason why STEAM needs a "fair-to-middlin" option for reviews.
Cute game but cyborgs are "God" flee or die - there's no smart play around them and that makes the entire game just feel cheap.

The concept is cool, but there needs to be some serious QA / polishing on the game mechanics. The days are too short, the boundary box for objects doesn't necessarily match the shape of the shop and makes it hard to rearrange without either deleting a shelf or playing tetris with your furniture. It's not clear who cursed you in between rounds (maybe intentional) which I think would add a sense of 'rivalry' to the mix. Some items are extremely expensive and because the days are short, take several days to gain a meaningful supply of. The witch that appears in between rounds look like she's made with AI and the voice lines sound generated, too.

-You cannot join another person lobby. 
-If you want to play it, you need to convice your friends to buy it, and if they dont like the game, you just wasted 8 euros because, you can't play it alone.
-The game discord is dead, so no use going there to try to get someone to play. 

Its one of those games that a friend says "lets buy it" you play it once or twice, and it sits in ur library

I love the concept of this game, but it doesn't seem to function properly at the moment. I'm still on the first level, and no matter what the AI says it's going to do, it never actually does it. I asked it to turn the movie on several times, but it just walked over to the tv and said it was on (it wasn't on). I tried to cook the dish she asked me to make but the ingredients were nowhere to be found. The AI said they were right in front of me on the counter (they weren't). She said she was going to make popcorn (she walked into the hallway, no popcorn was ever made).

When I ask things like if she ever leaves the apartment, the AI bugs out and says, don't say that, it makes it feel embarrassed, and if you continue to ask it repeatedly with different wording (trying to find out what word that was glitching it), she just starts killing you for no reason whatsoever. I was hoping for something as engaging and in depth as the AI Darth Vader from Fortnite awhile back, but this isn't anywhere close.

It seems like the AI has extremely strict barriers in place here that don't even allow you to accomplish normal conversation. Another question I asked, was what she played on the keyboard, and that got flagged into that same "don't say that, you make me embarrassed" reply.

The AI also feels very disconnected from the gameplay (doesn't see the same as me), and is completely unaware of it's surroundings. It only knew that I pushed her while walking in a doorway, when I opened cabinets, and where to walk around the map. It didn't know what room I was in, or what I was currently doing aside from that.

I hope they continue to configure it, and get it to a playable state. It's just not there yet.

While I love this game and the strategies used to play it. I just can't help but be upset with the balancing of the last level of kreydell. Why did the Soul Wardens already high damaging 4 main front cannons get buffed to fire weaker but still super strong massive aoe attacks 5 times per gun, it was already super powerful before but now bases get killed in a single burst from one gun. Moving on from the Soul warden on of my biggest annoyances with the campaign or enemies/allies in general is the fact that they don't expand their forces, or they start with preset bases that they don't even bother rebuilding, you know ONE OF THE MAIN COMPENETS OF THIS GAME WHICH IS BASE BUILDING, like I understand having preset bases that's fine but can't the AI even be bothered to even try to rebuilding destroyed structures? They have build timers so it's not broken, and the AI would still have a limited economy. The last mission feels so frustrating because you are the only one actually doing something while Ajax and the other guy do fuck all with the stuff they get besides having the regular base and units they start with. It would be so much more engaging if you have to defend the one guy so he can build up his forces while you give assistance to Ajax who should continue expanding or just rebuilding his base. OR have a simple balance change where it takes TIME for the soul warden to warp in and then Ajax's timer starts so that you can get some economy before Ajax is already dead..
I love the gameplay I just wish that your allies and enemies actually did something from the ground up without starting with an entire base that they do nothing with. The cool nature of ATS is that bases are constantly changing and growing because of the spance and its abundance of resources, while more ships are being produced and are fighting and being destroyed among the chaos.
To be fair to the devs I am playing on an unbalanced test version so I will potentially change my review to recommended if the balancing of the levels becomes better.

No in mission save function, and constant issues with crashing in long games. Stability desperately needs to get fixed, as there is currently no point in even trying to beat the harder/longer campaign levels when it crashes towards the end of the level every time.

Very fun game but obviously unfinished. Only the first two of the five campaigns in the game are playable. Only two factions you can play as. And the game crashes randomly maybe once an hour on average. And you can't save in the middle of a mission so if the game crashes, you're out of luck. Big thumbs down for this game until the devs get back to actively working on this again and optimizing and finishing the game then I'll change my review. It's just a cash grab at the moment. I'm writing this in December and last update by the devs was 3 months ago.

While I love this game and the strategies used to play it. I just can't help but be upset with the balancing of the last level of kreydell. Why did the Soul Wardens already high damaging 4 main front cannons get buffed to fire weaker but still super strong massive aoe attacks 5 times per gun, it was already super powerful before but now bases get killed in a single burst from one gun. Moving on from the Soul warden on of my biggest annoyances with the campaign or enemies/allies in general is the fact that they don't expand their forces, or they start with preset bases that they don't even bother rebuilding, you know ONE OF THE MAIN COMPENETS OF THIS GAME WHICH IS BASE BUILDING, like I understand having preset bases that's fine but can't the AI even be bothered to even try to rebuilding destroyed structures? They have build timers so it's not broken, and the AI would still have a limited economy. The last mission feels so frustrating because you are the only one actually doing something while Ajax and the other guy do fuck all with the stuff they get besides having the regular base and units they start with. It would be so much more engaging if you have to defend the one guy so he can build up his forces while you give assistance to Ajax who should continue expanding or just rebuilding his base. OR have a simple balance change where it takes TIME for the soul warden to warp in and then Ajax's timer starts so that you can get some economy before Ajax is already dead..
I love the gameplay I just wish that your allies and enemies actually did something from the ground up without starting with an entire base that they do nothing with. The cool nature of ATS is that bases are constantly changing and growing because of the spance and its abundance of resources, while more ships are being produced and are fighting and being destroyed among the chaos.
To be fair to the devs I am playing on an unbalanced test version so I will potentially change my review to recommended if the balancing of the levels becomes better.

The game is fun for the first hour as the Win95 esthetic very much drags you in if you have nostalgia for that late-mid-90's technology theme. The visuals and music are incredibly thematic and build a fun atmosphere but gameplay drags it down really quick. The initial 15 minutes or so is a logic game when you need to click on pictures. Then, it's an upgrade simulator. And not even a complex, robust one with lots of strategy. Once you play it for the first 30-60 minutes you realize oh f*** that's the entire game isn't it?
 
To add insult to injury, the story is honestly painfully predictable considering the premise of the game i spelled out instantly, even advertised. It's incredibly simplistic "AI datacenters bad, corpo bad, world go boom" commentary  which is a valid message sure but you're not experiencing it in any meaningful way here. It's just generic videos and some ok e-mail messages of the environment going more and more to heck the more you play. Not even a significant number of those. And gameplay isn't really fun as I mentioned. I play a lot of games which are rather repetitive in nature but this here feels like a browser based Flash game I'd play years ago. And not a very good one I'm afraid. Maybe it's a low key attention span test in the sense if you can't play for long then you're "brainrotten". Yeah thanks I'd rather read a book or Wikipedia at this point. One more annoyance to the collection is that in order to manual save you have to pay with in-game money that you also use to upgrade everything it's a "unique" feature but just a nuisance. Auto-saves take place not more than 30 minutes though at least based on my progress so it's not a complete disaster.

I do want to again point out the esthetics of the game are incredibly thematic and if you love that mid-90's and like upgrade simulator-style games then buy this one at least 50% discount to get those vibes. Other than that, due to it's gameplay and "story", even at its price point, skippable.
trash

ive got a lot of feelings about FACEMINER. its a game that makes you think on several very prevalent issues in our modern society. but the game itself can feel like a bit of a drag to actually play.

FACEMINER is a very blatant criticism of ai usage and the effects of ai usage, like the environmental and economic factors. theres also themes of cyber security, specifically once you unlock the ability to purchase facefinder cameras to harvest your own data. the game itself is an interesting form of criticism.

the story is heavily told through the various emails you receive. most of the emails are incredibly short. some are messages from the FACEMINER administration on things like work efficiency or claiming the negative effects are just fearmongering. other emails are from other FACEMINER employees discussing the ethics and effects of their work. a lot of emails provide nothing to the story, just standard emails about mundane stuff. its both an interesting and incredibly boring method of story telling. 

its interesting in the way that it allows you to see into the lives of your fellow coworkers. some seem to be hysterical and outraged at anyone who opposes their work, others fearful of the world theyre creating others are jaded and dont seem to care. these are all very real responses to the usage of ai in modern society, so i definitely think it was important to be included. however, your inbox very quickly gets full of messages. notices about efficiency or expenses, rank up emails, babbling between employees, messages from people opposing the work of FACEMINER, etc. it all just gets jumbled up. and since the inbox is the method of storytelling, all this nonsensical data makes it hard to keep track of the story. 

the gameplay loop is also a bit boring. you can select how fast you want your game to go or if you want to play an endless game. even on the quicker speed, the game still takes ages to actually reach the ending. and endless isn’t even all that fun– it’s the same exact game, just without an ending. there’s no new infrastructure or offsets to switch things up. which, gets frustrating even in a standard run. things get more expensive exponentially. it’s really easy to dig yourself into a financial pit or to basically keep your game at a standstill. 

one major bone to pick is that you have to pay to save your game. you get a free autosave after ranking up, but in order to save, you need to pay. and like all other upgrades in the game, saving becomes exponentially more expensive the more saves you make. it’s honestly just a nuisance.

the game does use ai for image generation. specifically, all datasets are made up of ai-generated faces. it uses nvidia’s ffhq dataset to generate these faces. while the devs claim it’s for thematic purposes (as well as it would obviously be tough and expensive to legally gather enough faces to create the datasets). but how many of the people whose faces were used in nvidia’s dataset consented to their faces being used? and since the ai is trained on real people who may or may not have consented to their images being used for ai training, it undermines the criticisms the game it trying to make. at least, in my opinion. the game’s whole point is to criticise ai usage through the unethical gathering of data and the effects it has on the world. if the game’s face data was gathered unethically, then FACEMINER has become what it set out to criticise. it ‘s a slippery slope.

all in all, a summary of the story is just “ai bad”. do i think it’s got a good story? yes, if you’re willing to do some digging to read the important emails. do i think it’s fun? no, not really. once you’ve made it to mid-/late-game, you’ve done basically everything but get the story’s finale. most late-game features, such as the emissions/offsets have barely any upgrades. if you’re interested in the criticisms this game has on ai and a cynical worldview, then maybe you’d enjoy the game. but it’s definitely a type of game you dont actively play, you just check in every now and then to upgrade it and let it run on its own.

Decent incremental, but not really worth it.

It's got the cute Win95 aesthetic going and it's got some mechanical depth that's initially interesting, but the depth disappears by the mid-game. There's three paces you can choose from when you start which decide what kind of idle game you want this to be, and I chose the 'normal' one. I had unlocked everything just about an hour or so in, and the remaining hour was spent just watching YouTube and mindlessly buying upgrades while I waited for the game to give me the ending it kept teasing me with.

On that note, actually: the game's plot and ultimate message.

Going in, it seems pretty on-the-nose: a warning about the dangers of modern technology - specifically AI - and the impacts that it has on the world, which could ultimately kill us.
Except then you actually get past mid-game and the company you're working for is actually essentially just a cult that's running its business to willingly heat the Earth up because it'll apparently mutate humans into some ascended form or something. Also, none of this is actually happening and it's just a training simulation for the real thing.

It's just so weird, and that in combination with how the gameplay suddenly tapers off makes me feel like one of those things where someone has a great concept, but they're only able to build out half of it before they ran out of idea.

For what it's worth, I did mostly enjoy the few hours I spent on it. But not enough that I'd say I really got $8 out of it, and I think that's my real sticking point here, especially because I can't see myself going for another run.
If this was ~$2, I'd probably recommend picking it up.
As it stands, though, I just can't.

Aesthetic and music are great selling points, but these are the only things going for this game. Gameplay is really boring and shallow (by clicker games standards). You unlock all game mechanics 30 minutes in and then just re-buy the same upgrades to keep the 4-6 values (CPU, RAM, heat, electricity, etc.) balanced with each other. The story is so strangely stupid that I have no idea if the whole thing tries to be a satire or not.

If the game wants to be so environmentally friendly maybe it shouldn't use electron js lmao
Really cool vibe, but becomes less engaging due to the lack of interesting narrative developments.

This is a very disappointing game. The core gameplay loop isn't engaging, the new systems that get unlocked don't really change anything, and the storyline is trite. Only thing it had going for it are the aesthetics, but it isn`t enough to recommend this game.

Game was interesting at first, but after around 20 minutes there was really no visible progression. The game also doesn't have much content, everything is introduced in the first 20 minutes and nothing new shows up after.

My experience with the game wasn't great.
While I had graphical bugs that bothered me (but I was seemingly the only one it happened to), I also had troubles enjoying the game passed a few hours.

Midgame, you don't have anything left to do than circle water/elec/heat/minersoftware ameliorations because no new window or gameplay element is introduced. You just... Wait.

But you can't wait passively, because if you alt tab and do something else, the game pauses.
You also receive emails that are voiced over by an artificial voice, so, the game in itself is distracting and not a great fit for multitasking.

Also, I think having some old pc games like mineseweeper or else in there would make up for the lack of content midgame. Also at some point I lacked money and I couldn't sell my own data on the market, which I found was surprizing.

The ranks also add nothing else than more autosaves after a while and I found it disapointing, I really wanted more features.

Despite the game having an interesting story I don't recommend it because the gameplay lacks and the "idle" part is not really idle.
I am aware you can untick the emails being read automatically or disable the pause when alt tabbing... But the pause should be an optional feature you have to activate and not the other way around.

Good concept overall, but it needs to be improved.
Cheeky little idle game although it's really hard for me to see the appeal...

Glitched achievements, also poorly explained achievements (certain ones are only available in some modes without saying so), also, making the player go through the slowest mode to unlock some stuff was pretty monotonous. Definitely needs a bit of work. Hope the dev puts in some more work and keeps at it, but in it's current state, not recommended.

This game doesn't know what it wants to be. I would put this in the automation genre, but it doesn't let you design layouts for anything, so it's kind of barebones in that genre. You just click upgrades and balance stats. I would put it in the mystery thriller genre, but there's really not much narrative to this game. You get some emails to read, but that's about it. I would put this in the clicker genre, but you barely have to worry about the clicking of faces at all after a few minutes in the beginning of the game, and no other clicking mechanism is ever introduced. It is most closely related to the clicker genre, but I wouldn't recommend it for that.

Some aspects of the game are basically missing. You may see some footage of video where it's spotting faces. That's not gameplay. There's no video footage of faces other than a little video clip in the documents that serves no gameplay purpose. I also found the emergency transmission windows annoying and not at all realistic... to the point where they were the same videos over and over with no rhyme or reason to when they show up.

That said, the Win95 theming is done extremely well, and I hope it gets expanded on. I want to see  improvements to the music player. Maybe add a Winamp upgrade? Also, it'd be nice if early days GPUs were introduced to help with mining the video feeds. I could also see part of the narrative being that you have to upgrade the OS to support newer video codecs but you'd give up your anonymity in the process and have to waste more power on a newer OS. None of that is done here. Everything takes place on a semi-convincing Windows-like OS that doesn't have Paint and doesn't let you edit documents. It also doesn't have a command line interface, something I expected to see considering the focus on energy consumption.

What's unclear to me is whether or not the hardware is on your premises or networked into as an agent. I think that's the weak point I noticed before anything else. It appears to be a situation where the hardware is in a solar farm location nowhere near your real home, but when it overheats, your own computer overheats, so that breaks that illusion. Which is it?

Overall, I like the game, but it feels shallow. It could be a ton better. It doesn't feel particularly special. It is, however, a little bit relevant to today's AI-related problems. It's an interesting base for a game that could evolve into something special later, but right now, it's just not enough to do to keep things engaging. I suffered through the first playthrough just hoping it got more interesting, and it never did.

Cool aesthetic and premise, unfortunately it's not a fun clicker/incremental game. It's very easy to lose progress if you're not actively paying attention to all aspects of your in-game computer. Didn't have fun micromanaging everything and after losing half an hour of my time because I seemed to have adequate upgrades to put it on auto and go piss for one minute to return to a game over didn't help.

Plenty of great clicker games on Steam and a lot of them are free. I'd suggest Digseum, Pinata Go Boom, or Nodebuster if you're in the mood for incremental games.

The story is a jumbled mess at the end with a reveal about your player character that comes out of nowhere.

This is a very disappointing game. The core gameplay loop isn't engaging, the new systems that get unlocked don't really change anything, and the storyline is trite. Only thing it had going for it are the aesthetics, but it isn`t enough to recommend this game.

Game was interesting at first, but after around 20 minutes there was really no visible progression. The game also doesn't have much content, everything is introduced in the first 20 minutes and nothing new shows up after.

My experience with the game wasn't great.
While I had graphical bugs that bothered me (but I was seemingly the only one it happened to), I also had troubles enjoying the game passed a few hours.

Midgame, you don't have anything left to do than circle water/elec/heat/minersoftware ameliorations because no new window or gameplay element is introduced. You just... Wait.

But you can't wait passively, because if you alt tab and do something else, the game pauses.
You also receive emails that are voiced over by an artificial voice, so, the game in itself is distracting and not a great fit for multitasking.

Also, I think having some old pc games like mineseweeper or else in there would make up for the lack of content midgame. Also at some point I lacked money and I couldn't sell my own data on the market, which I found was surprizing.

The ranks also add nothing else than more autosaves after a while and I found it disapointing, I really wanted more features.

Despite the game having an interesting story I don't recommend it because the gameplay lacks and the "idle" part is not really idle.
I am aware you can untick the emails being read automatically or disable the pause when alt tabbing... But the pause should be an optional feature you have to activate and not the other way around.

Good concept overall, but it needs to be improved.
Cheeky little idle game although it's really hard for me to see the appeal...

Glitched achievements, also poorly explained achievements (certain ones are only available in some modes without saying so), also, making the player go through the slowest mode to unlock some stuff was pretty monotonous. Definitely needs a bit of work. Hope the dev puts in some more work and keeps at it, but in it's current state, not recommended.

This game doesn't know what it wants to be. I would put this in the automation genre, but it doesn't let you design layouts for anything, so it's kind of barebones in that genre. You just click upgrades and balance stats. I would put it in the mystery thriller genre, but there's really not much narrative to this game. You get some emails to read, but that's about it. I would put this in the clicker genre, but you barely have to worry about the clicking of faces at all after a few minutes in the beginning of the game, and no other clicking mechanism is ever introduced. It is most closely related to the clicker genre, but I wouldn't recommend it for that.

Some aspects of the game are basically missing. You may see some footage of video where it's spotting faces. That's not gameplay. There's no video footage of faces other than a little video clip in the documents that serves no gameplay purpose. I also found the emergency transmission windows annoying and not at all realistic... to the point where they were the same videos over and over with no rhyme or reason to when they show up.

That said, the Win95 theming is done extremely well, and I hope it gets expanded on. I want to see  improvements to the music player. Maybe add a Winamp upgrade? Also, it'd be nice if early days GPUs were introduced to help with mining the video feeds. I could also see part of the narrative being that you have to upgrade the OS to support newer video codecs but you'd give up your anonymity in the process and have to waste more power on a newer OS. None of that is done here. Everything takes place on a semi-convincing Windows-like OS that doesn't have Paint and doesn't let you edit documents. It also doesn't have a command line interface, something I expected to see considering the focus on energy consumption.

What's unclear to me is whether or not the hardware is on your premises or networked into as an agent. I think that's the weak point I noticed before anything else. It appears to be a situation where the hardware is in a solar farm location nowhere near your real home, but when it overheats, your own computer overheats, so that breaks that illusion. Which is it?

Overall, I like the game, but it feels shallow. It could be a ton better. It doesn't feel particularly special. It is, however, a little bit relevant to today's AI-related problems. It's an interesting base for a game that could evolve into something special later, but right now, it's just not enough to do to keep things engaging. I suffered through the first playthrough just hoping it got more interesting, and it never did.

Cool aesthetic and premise, unfortunately it's not a fun clicker/incremental game. It's very easy to lose progress if you're not actively paying attention to all aspects of your in-game computer. Didn't have fun micromanaging everything and after losing half an hour of my time because I seemed to have adequate upgrades to put it on auto and go piss for one minute to return to a game over didn't help.

Plenty of great clicker games on Steam and a lot of them are free. I'd suggest Digseum, Pinata Go Boom, or Nodebuster if you're in the mood for incremental games.

the management aspect makes it a little more engaging, the aesthetic and music are pretty good, it's a shame that the rest doesn't come together, like the story or the final parts of the gameplay (gets repetitive and kinda tiresome)

Incredibly unsubtle, anti-AI/exploitative tech resource manager/idle clicker that loses momentum about ~30m-1h before the ending stretch; it doesn't really justify using AI assets for the dataset from one of the biggest offenders of reckless, forced AI expansion IRL (NVIDIA) since the faces will stop being relevant to the plot about 10 minutes into a playthrough once auto mode's used — an actual artist or even stock images could've easily been used in this regard.

I'd say the only positives are the visuals, which do a better job of emulating the 90s desktop vibes than other games I've seen like Hypnospace Outlaw, and the soundtrack. Pretty much everything else has been explored in much better ways.
Fell for aestetics, expected some digital horror/mystery/triller vibes, got an emotionally charged lecture about climate activism instead. Unfortunately, this is doomslop.
I'm usually excited over automation/idle games, but this one just isn't my style.

Sounds important (but isn't), has nostalgia. Yeah that's it.

The game is fun for the first hour as the Win95 esthetic very much drags you in if you have nostalgia for that late-mid-90's technology theme. The visuals and music are incredibly thematic and build a fun atmosphere but gameplay drags it down really quick. The initial 15 minutes or so is a logic game when you need to click on pictures. Then, it's an upgrade simulator. And not even a complex, robust one with lots of strategy. Once you play it for the first 30-60 minutes you realize oh f*** that's the entire game isn't it?
 
To add insult to injury, the story is honestly painfully predictable considering the premise of the game i spelled out instantly, even advertised. It's incredibly simplistic "AI datacenters bad, corpo bad, world go boom" commentary  which is a valid message sure but you're not experiencing it in any meaningful way here. It's just generic videos and some ok e-mail messages of the environment going more and more to heck the more you play. Not even a significant number of those. And gameplay isn't really fun as I mentioned. I play a lot of games which are rather repetitive in nature but this here feels like a browser based Flash game I'd play years ago. And not a very good one I'm afraid. Maybe it's a low key attention span test in the sense if you can't play for long then you're "brainrotten". Yeah thanks I'd rather read a book or Wikipedia at this point. One more annoyance to the collection is that in order to manual save you have to pay with in-game money that you also use to upgrade everything it's a "unique" feature but just a nuisance. Auto-saves take place not more than 30 minutes though at least based on my progress so it's not a complete disaster.

I do want to again point out the esthetics of the game are incredibly thematic and if you love that mid-90's and like upgrade simulator-style games then buy this one at least 50% discount to get those vibes. Other than that, due to it's gameplay and "story", even at its price point, skippable.

Game wants you to pay a subscription for its good AI and is very heavy on censorship, there are better alternatives like AI Roguelite that are much more detailed and have a good free AI to use.

[Edit in response to the developer]
Wow that was the most kneejerk schizo response from a dev i've ever seen on a short negative review (which im happy for, it will be a nice red flag to anybody else interested in this) so i will respond to their wrong assumptions

1) they are right its not a subscription per say but instead a token system where you buy more tokens... so if you wanna use it everyday its kinda like a subscription...and i used the free one that THEY suggested in the list and it was not very good.

2) I never claimed AI Rougelite didnt have a subscription service, BUT they have a very very good free ai. I have never felt the need to upgrade to the higher teirs so its not really an issue unlike with this cheap clone of AI Roguelite.

3) not sure why it shows i only played for 6 minutes, it for sure took longer than that to just start up a game but i do agree it didnt take me very long to see how low quality this game was where if you look at my playtime on AI Roguelite it obvious i like this style of game its just this one underdelivered 

4) not a good look to try and falsely bash your competition that is vastly superior to you, but AI Roguelite DOES have censorship its just not as invasive as this games censorship the AI will actually try to work around themes and shutdown any sexuality in a clever way where this game just gives you a wall of angry red text.

5) I did not mention any sexual content or a wish for it to be in the game i simply stated that this game is very heavy in its censorship so not sure why the dev is so obsessed on that point.

If your gonna try and copy something then it better be well done or have its own identity, but in the very short time i played this i saw absolutely nothing i liked about it and it just screamed "Gimmie money or use a bottom barrel AI" so i assume this was made as a cash cow and will now be putting this developer on my ignore list especially after that very strange reply i got from them that assumed an awful lot off of a short review

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
What we’re seeing is that as our Terran‑playing friends develop more trade routes, things start getting buggy, choppy, and unstable. If more than one of us is playing Terran, the problem basically doubles. The wild part is that this does not happen in single‑player at all, which makes it feel less like a hardware issue and more like a problem with how the game handles data in multiplayer.
​

From our perspective, it looks like there’s some kind of cap or bottleneck in how much the game can process and sync between players over the network, regardless of the fact that we all have high‑speed internet and solid PCs. Once we hit late game, one or two of us will drop to 1–2 frames per second, which makes it basically impossible to play or micro anything.
​

In our most recent match, two of us were stuck at a literal 1 FPS slideshow while the third friend managed to push through and beat the AI. The desync or slowdown was so bad that the two of us at 1 FPS didn’t even get a clear indication that the game had ended or that we’d actually won. For a game this good at its core, these late‑game multiplayer performance issues are incredibly frustrating and really hurt the experience for groups who want to play big, extended matches together.
