game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
I'm a really big fan of RTS games, especially space RTS games like Empire at War, and this game just feels soulless and boring. I've really wanted it because it seemed like this game would be similar to Stellaris but with more in depth combat, because I really like Stellaris but it's space combat is a little boring. In reality though, this game's combat isn't much better, it's pretty much just build one or two big fleets, send them to a star system, and watch as your ships and the enemy ships stay in one place and shoot each other. There's no micromanaging tactics or ship skills, no ambushing or flanking or whatever, it just feels boring.

If your looking for an old but very good space RTS, player Empire at War, that game has some great mods.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Loved the original of this game.  Love this game too.  But, in 2026 this game is not enough.  A few minor mechanic changes to the original title are interesting but the game massively lacks the depth and variety you get in other 4X space titles.  In terms of depth it's nearest competitors are the likes of empire at war, gal civ 3, and of course SoaSE I.  That level of depth for a 4X was great over a decade ago, but compared with the likes of Stellaris which has a similar play time it's just not in competition at all.

A couple of specific strengths (+) and weaknesses (-)


+ Modelling and art work is great
+ Ship combat balance is also excellent, much better than the rock-paper-scissors most games use
+ Ship losses are genuinely meaningful early on
+ Trade system should be fun but in large games everything is constantly discounted and in small games (1v1) no one needs to trade
+ Ship diversity is well balanced, the penetration system works well to balance lots of small ships vs few large ones.  I tested both and could not find a big difference in meta between them.  No stellaris-style corvette spam!
+ fighters and bombers are well balanced

- TEC are incredibly overpowered, heavy cruisers protecting trade fleets is overkill and wipes out star bases on their own (not to mention garrison fleets).  Rarely have to actually attack an opponent (unless they are also TEC)
- non-fighter based fighter defence (e.g. flak) is very weak and difficult to micro
- repair ships are difficult to micro and they don't seem to automatically heal at all
- fleet cohesion settings are not good, need to be able to tell specific ship types to move to front lines / back lines etc.  Very annoying watching weak ships take the full front of enemy attacks - please base AI logic on more than just weapon range!
- Super weapons are either entirely meaningless (due to planetary shields), or ridiculously OP (no planetary shields) 
- Ship losses are meaningless later on, you can just pump out star bases and titans with the (literally) millions of resources stockpiled
- Game becomes stagnant very quickly
- Planets are pretty meaningless other than the slots they give.  It would be nice if planets were meaningfully different in play style to asteroids, other than just more slots.  e.g. make it so culture doesn't spread from asteroids, asteroid pops are much much lower (minerals much higher), planets can have anti-ship weapons

Some suggestions to improve:
* introduce strategic resources to fight over (more than just ship artefacts) to drive narrative gameplay, e.g. shipyards with unique ships, unique tech options, passive bonuses, phase lane manipulation
* make the role of minor factions more meaningful rather than everyone trades with them or destroys them (think Civ 6 city states - one controller only, grants win conditions)
* Make super weapons, star bases, and titans a staggered progression which encourages surgical deep strikes to remove threats (at a balanced level of attack and defence, attack should do a LOT of harm but should never completely wipe out a planet, when attack is better than defence go ahead and blow up the planet or whatever)
* Introduce galactic threats such as severe space weather that can devastate fleets, supernovas that change space lanes, asteroids which if not stopped can wipe out planet populations and buildings
* Please please introduce deeper research trees and require some research lab maintenance, for example: (1) add specialisations to key techs which create branching, no return research trees (e.g. go for better missile tech or better lasers?), (2) add a maintenance burden such that if tech drops due to destroyed labs, any unlocks from the higher tier(s) are disabled until rebuilt, (3) remove the stupid +4 labs on planets that's insanely overpowered, (4) make the migration between tech levels more eventful, such as requiring a long wind-up period and a global event that enemies can respond and focus on to stop you levelling up
* fix AI so you can't just send one ship to their home planet to get them to stop attacking you, also fix AI so they make use of opportunities to attack whilst other enemies are fighting
* perhaps give some early, mid, and late game objectives out randomly to unlock progression, based on faction (e.g. TEC doesn't unlock specific ships / upgrades until it has x amount of trade throughput)
* give relationship quests to minor factions, instead of just buying access with influence


Overall good game, enjoyed playing it, but absolutely no reason to ever play it again now.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Finally, I figured out why I have hard feeling about this game, and I couldn't find what's wrong. Like: it's all there, basebuilding, spaceships, battles, empire, jumps, lots of pew-pew. And either I didn't play game enough or missed some key elements of gameplay.

If game is about combat and no resources (like Warno) the key element is deckbuilding, army composition, and micromanagment of your units and tactical moves: line of sight, flanking, using terrain. There is lot to do, and lot's of things you need to into account. In Sins2 we have mainly army composition and supply lines. Battles kind of play by themselves, you rarely use abilities personally (maybe it does matter), there is little tactic, breaking line of sight, flanking or anything. You jump into system and watch fireworks. Either you retreat or push. the movement does not matter too much.

That leads to stacking one big doomball of fleet and just gunning down defences ASAP as having multiple fleets does not guarantee success, as it's not designed to micromanage fleet components to have any tactical advantage. It's awesome resupply mechanic that automatically joins new units to fleet to replanish lost units supports that, but tbh with that I'd like to see more tactical decision on the gravity well.

That's a problem with a RTS game that emphesize combat.

So, if combat is not engaging - then RTS has another component: base building. And again, I'm struggling with gameplay mechanic in this, as basebuilding in RTS like Supreme Commander are essential to upkeep your army, and I've got a feeling that it does not matter in Sins2, except some few simple rules, like building enough shipyards close to front line. Simplification mechanic (the screen where you can just click to build something) is awesome when managing big empire, but it also kills the meaning where and why you put things (it should be meaningful when you build on frontline, and simplified if you want some generic layout in your strategic depth). Basebulding can be art by itself, putting defences on hills, shield generators in distance to protect turrets or overlap key components, powerplants in back, but close enough to boost factory production, that determines what you loose first, do you have your key factories protected from air attacks. In Sins2 I've got feeling that - it does not matter. It's again, flat gravity well, that does not have any mening in how your star system are build. In AoE or SC2 where and how you build even civilian building could be a winning factor. 

And where is anti supercanons buildings for late game? Generally, I don't have feeing that I need to do anything meaningful in a planet system. Supreme Commander that has large scale war, has several ways to break turtle players: tactical missiles (couter: anti missile guns), medium to long arty (counter: shields), nukes (counter: missile silo) etc. Here no similar gameplay occurs.

So neither basebuilding is engaging nor tactical combat. So maybe it's a game about great galactic empire? No, It's not. It's RTS game, not turn based complicated political intrigues, politycal systems, alliances, revolts to put down as empire grows, and you need to make tough moral decisions to keep empire in line. It's make fleet, and push.

So I'm not sure what is this game about. I like it, and at the same time - I don't. Maybe I'm missing something.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
TLDR: Don't buy. Deathballing and a doubling-down on GenAI iconography has tarnished a once solid series.

Longer read:
I've been a fan of the franchise since Sins 1, before even Entrenchment or any of the other expansions were released. I bought this over a year ago in the hopes that they'd improve the game and gameplay, and while there've been advances over time, the systemic issues that've plagued it since the start haven't been improved upon or fixed. Mainly that it's too easy to deathball and push a fleet at (x) location, turning this into (outside some Advent and capital/titan powers) a soft movie with a fated outcome of (focus fire x/y/z).

Additionally, their CONTINUED reliance on GenAI for tech tree items and then doubling-down to smooth over their worst and most egregious icons, shows a complete lack of care and laziness in the developer end. Pay someone or a small team for some icons, there's a reason that one of the most popular mods to this day is replacing them with more readable and infinitely better Sins1 Rebellion icons. If you couch the design iconography on one of the most dense and important segments of your game with a cheap and lazy indistinguishable mess of a substitute for actual icons, you'll have both a readability and memory problem the further you go on. This creates bounce, regardless of the frankly unethical use of GenAI coming from a series that was previously praised for its ease of use.

Not worth the upcharge. Get an older game for the exact same experience.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
[i]I am long term Rebelion player. I also do not earn much money so I think now you can see from where my views come:[/i]

[h3]Game:[/h3]

[b]Pros:[/b] 
- Simple
- Low entry point
- Fun for occasional gameplay
- Moving planets are very nice additions

Game Is ok. Not Great not terrible. It is lacking depth, but it is not a bad thing if you just want an RTS - nothing bad in making simple RTS game, look on Starcraft.
If you want to casually hop into game, play few hours and leave, or have a quick 20 minutes 1v1 then it is fun.



[b]Cons:[/b]
- Simple
- Physical guns and munnitions
- Ship classes behind paywall

Why Simple is both pro and a con? Well It gets boring fast if you dont play competetive.
Mentioned Star Craft even if simple, it can be tricky to learn and micromanagement its a key. It is not that easy here, but in a bad way, which leads me to the next part:

Physical guns and munitions is my first big pain in the ass or whatever vassari commander has. 
It's great, but then... then you realize you can't order your ships to rotate or change their Z-axis. Sometimes it's a weird situation because I'd rather have the ship stand sideways to hit more missiles than rotate, which would render 2 of the 5 PD useless. 
Sometimes, even if you aim at target A, your ship will position itself in such a way that it can't hit it because target A is on a different X-axis than your ship, and your ship prefers to position itself to attack targets B and C.

Ship classes behind paywall:
Imagine they tell that to have titans in rebelion you have to buy DLC.
We bought Sins II and we got cartoonish ships, less tech and buildings (vasari and advent dont have trade ports f.e), and now new things will be behind DLC? 
You have to make money, I agree, but why not do faction DLC? you know.. the 4th faction that it was teased back in Rebelion in 2012, That's 13 years! 

I didnt want this game, I tought it was a reskin. My friends and my Wife talk me in to it. 
You looked like you tried to make smth interesting, but ffs this DLC is 1/3 game. 

Guys, I am [b]paradox games player[/b], I can [u]take loan just to buy a DLC[/u] (its a joke, I think in 2025 I have to point that out). 
But If I have to delay buying a small apartament in [b]my late 80's[/b] I have to get good reason for it. For few ships its outrageous.

It should be called remaster not a sequel, since base game gives you same content as Sins Rebelion but with moving planets and physical missiles (and take away few things to).

In the end, you do you. You will have fn in this game for few hours, then you will drop it and one day return. If thats worth the money for you, more power to you.

Godspeed guys

review_text: I want to love this game, I play it weekly with friends.    At some point, it rears it head as unoptimized garbage and I can't do ANYTHING in the game. This is a reliable occurrence. The framerate absolutely tanks, and trying to navigate the map is tied to the framerate, as I can just tap, as in a quarter second or less of a keypress to try to move around, and the camera when it finally gets the idea to move, bolts to the far side. game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
A fun game, dragged down by AI image trash that is unavoidable, and a broken mod integration with pitifully slow download speeds. I like the new mechanics of not needing specialized crew for capital ships and you CAN spam them if you want, although it won't be too strong. I like how modern the game looks, I love that planets can move and you can't just concentrate your defenses in one point in the lane because eventually they will be in the wrong direction, but the AI images and the choice of using mod.io instead of Steam workshop is baffling. 

AI images used as a placeholder is understandable. But when you boot up the screen, the title picture is AI-generated. Many of the portraits of the races are AI generated. Almost all if not the entire tech-tree is AI generated and until recently when most of the images don't pop up anymore, its always on your face. It has been said that it was the artists who worked previously on the older titles choice to use AI images as a placeholder until the official art can replace them. But its seemingly nothing has been done about it. Sure the tech-tree images can be last priority but the first screen of launching the game, and its AI-Generated? Baffling. You think they could of at LEAST get rid of that a year in.

Secondly; Mod.io. It is a terribly slow mod integration. A 2GB mod can have legitimately TEN TIMES SLOWER download speed. Why not use the built in Steam workshop? The developers said its so they can remain Platform Neutral but that's nonsense. I played a game called Mechwarrior which had Steam workshop support and support for mods in other platforms like nexus mods. This forced "neutrality" for mods just drag it down for no reason. I want to play my mods, but Mod.io is content in keeping me waiting to download a small patch that Steam workshop could of finished when I am taking a piss. Have BOTH. let the modders choose if they want to use steam workshop or something else, not force it upon everyone for a worse experience.

But I realized after I bought it that the studio had gone in hard on AI Art, and they're still adding new AI Art now.

As a result, I can't, in good conscience, suggest that anyone else buy it. This is the game that taught me to look for the AI use banner on the steam store.

If they ever pivot and decide to have actual artists with an actual artistic vision again, my opinion will change.

review_text:
Here's why I don't recommend this game:

1) The DLCs add mechanics that should already be integrated into the base game. You can't ask people to pay 15 euros for four spaceships or 11 euros for the introduction of new missions...
2) The base game doesn't include the campaign with each faction.
3) The ship animations during battle are almost nonexistent.
4) The soundtrack is truly awful.

The gameplay and mechanics are nice, but I wouldn't recommend it. This software house's development policies aren't honest. The DLCs are overpriced, and what they include should be integrated into the base game. The only interesting DLC ​​currently is Reinforcements, but not because of the LOL victory conditions, but because of the four spaceships they want to charge you 15 euros for...

At that price, you can buy entire games, not DLC.

These policies led me to abandon the game.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
TL:DR This is just a war game in space, shallow and much simpler than Galactic Civilizations or stellaris etc. This whole game could be a mod in their Galactic Civ 4 game.

First things first, this is a good game. A fully fledged well polished game. There is a single great innovation for the genre in this game, planets rotate. You thought you could build defensive structures on this choke hold of a planet and neglect all the rest behind it? Think again! That is truly awesome! There is an element of surprise and unpredictability in this element which keeps you on edge. The ships designs are awesome aesthetically and the fleet management is awesome! The game is easy to master. And that's with the good stuff about this game. 

Now I will try and provide some constructive criticism. 

-PREMISE. The game is simple. Just conquest stuff. Build your fleet and conquer. No real exploration no feeling of dread or mystery of exploring the vast unknown space. No random events, not even scripted events. Neutral factions are just some dumb AI that mostly work like space merchants. It's just you and some other players trying to figure out who is the bigger dictator. 

-RESEARCH. The research tree. Almost meaningless. For the the first few hours I was paying attention and then I was like "just queue all of them and I will figure out later the benefits". Research felt unimpactful because most of the times was some equipment that could be loaded on one of my capital ships. Guess what, you can only have so many capital ships because of supply limit so what is the point of having SO MANY equipment choices for your ships and the same goes for your planets too. You just figure out which items fit your playstyle and your empire's needs and go with them. 

-RESOURCES. Don't worry about resources. It's just two basic resources that can be found in every asteroid/planet. There are of course rare resources but you can build them later on. As for money you only need to worry about it the first few hours. Seriously once you figure out the basics and own about ten colonies you don't have to worry about resources anymore. Because no matter how vast your empire is THERE IS NO UPKEEP. So I can build TWO starbases in every single one of my colonies and not get taxed about it?

-SHIP/FLEET DESIGN. 4 types of frigates and 4 types of cruisers that I don't fully understand what their role in battle is. After a while some of the ships felt like more durable than others so I kept building them. Seriously there is no strategy involved or there is no sufficient explanation of it. Just don't waste resources in planet bombers, useless ships. They don't engage in any way in fights, your fleet is getting obliterated and they sit back relaxed bombing the planet.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
To be honest, after playing this, I thought it was an early access game. 

Really liked the use of queues to allow the player to set up a planet how they like, then move on. Having to pay for it at the time of items being queued prevents any stuck-queue issues, and puts the strategic onus on the player.

I was able to max out 15 and 15 for the two research trees using only 4 planets on each (you can mouse over and see which planets contirbute). Since I played games with 6 and 8 players, each player had their own solar system. That meant that basically everyone was capped on resources and tech by the time the military portion commenced (I had to fight 1 enemy off prior to that point). In those cases, the end game is really required.

This is very 4X-lite, and doesn't include things like terraforming or advanced economies. It is cute but it feels like there is a lot missing. Take a look at Endless Space 1 or 2 for a better option.

I also observed what appeared to be a bug, where I destroyed an enemy titan with 2 powerful artifacts (gravity well artifacts) which did initially drop as loot but were destroyed or decayed somehow over time before I had a chance to recover them. If that's a mechanic working as intended, the game makes no mention of it.

review_text:
Complete slop, avoid like the plague. I bought it on a sale, then tried it and after maybe 30 min I already knew that there was nothing there for me. Play Rebellion, that's the last and probably final good SoSE. Quick info about me: I have around 300 hours in rebellion and also played the Trinity Edition for countless hours (CD-ROM release therefore I don't have the exact numbers, it was a lot tho). 

Ironclad have shown with this release that they only intend to exploit their fanbase as cheap as possible, with alpha builds of the first game sold as a "successor" and of top of that selling DLC for this unfinished disgrace. I mean it was kinda obvious from that they tried to develop MOBA-slop to exploit an already saturated market. Genius business planning Ironclad, I applaud you. But that's what happens if you're too lazy to even try developing what your customers want. 

I don't even think I have to go into details about anything else here, many have mentioned it. Sloppified mobile game optics (ofc with AI slop, because human art hurts the bottom line), no innovation in any way and it plays like crap. There's no "feel" here. 
Oh, also no new soundtrack either. Everything to just minmax the shit out of this cashgrab, because they know that this will replace their customer base with the lowliest kind of consoomer pleb you can imagine. 
Now they advertise a "campaign", as if SoSE ever needed a campaign, except to pull people's attention away from the shitty base gameplay with scripted missions. 

Really a downfall for this game company and new evidence that there's nothing saving the game industry from another major crash, otherwise sloppification just won't ever stop.

And for the love of god stop writing BS like "I really wanna like this game, but..." and then STILL give a thumbs up because you have some parasocial relationship with a company that's just wanting to pull the cash out of your pocket. The game's crap, it will forever be crap and for the love of god stop investing money into this company's output. They don't deserve it. They absolutely despise you and think you're completely stupid, else they would never have dared to release something like this.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Loved the original of this game.  Love this game too.  But, in 2026 this game is not enough.  A few minor mechanic changes to the original title are interesting but the game massively lacks the depth and variety you get in other 4X space titles.  In terms of depth it's nearest competitors are the likes of empire at war, gal civ 3, and of course SoaSE I.  That level of depth for a 4X was great over a decade ago, but compared with the likes of Stellaris which has a similar play time it's just not in competition at all.

A couple of specific strengths (+) and weaknesses (-)


+ Modelling and art work is great
+ Ship combat balance is also excellent, much better than the rock-paper-scissors most games use
+ Ship losses are genuinely meaningful early on
+ Trade system should be fun but in large games everything is constantly discounted and in small games (1v1) no one needs to trade
+ Ship diversity is well balanced, the penetration system works well to balance lots of small ships vs few large ones.  I tested both and could not find a big difference in meta between them.  No stellaris-style corvette spam!
+ fighters and bombers are well balanced

- TEC are incredibly overpowered, heavy cruisers protecting trade fleets is overkill and wipes out star bases on their own (not to mention garrison fleets).  Rarely have to actually attack an opponent (unless they are also TEC)
- non-fighter based fighter defence (e.g. flak) is very weak and difficult to micro
- repair ships are difficult to micro and they don't seem to automatically heal at all
- fleet cohesion settings are not good, need to be able to tell specific ship types to move to front lines / back lines etc.  Very annoying watching weak ships take the full front of enemy attacks - please base AI logic on more than just weapon range!
- Super weapons are either entirely meaningless (due to planetary shields), or ridiculously OP (no planetary shields) 
- Ship losses are meaningless later on, you can just pump out star bases and titans with the (literally) millions of resources stockpiled
- Game becomes stagnant very quickly
- Planets are pretty meaningless other than the slots they give.  It would be nice if planets were meaningfully different in play style to asteroids, other than just more slots.  e.g. make it so culture doesn't spread from asteroids, asteroid pops are much much lower (minerals much higher), planets can have anti-ship weapons

Some suggestions to improve:
* introduce strategic resources to fight over (more than just ship artefacts) to drive narrative gameplay, e.g. shipyards with unique ships, unique tech options, passive bonuses, phase lane manipulation
* make the role of minor factions more meaningful rather than everyone trades with them or destroys them (think Civ 6 city states - one controller only, grants win conditions)
* Make super weapons, star bases, and titans a staggered progression which encourages surgical deep strikes to remove threats (at a balanced level of attack and defence, attack should do a LOT of harm but should never completely wipe out a planet, when attack is better than defence go ahead and blow up the planet or whatever)
* Introduce galactic threats such as severe space weather that can devastate fleets, supernovas that change space lanes, asteroids which if not stopped can wipe out planet populations and buildings
* Please please introduce deeper research trees and require some research lab maintenance, for example: (1) add specialisations to key techs which create branching, no return research trees (e.g. go for better missile tech or better lasers?), (2) add a maintenance burden such that if tech drops due to destroyed labs, any unlocks from the higher tier(s) are disabled until rebuilt, (3) remove the stupid +4 labs on planets that's insanely overpowered, (4) make the migration between tech levels more eventful, such as requiring a long wind-up period and a global event that enemies can respond and focus on to stop you levelling up
* fix AI so you can't just send one ship to their home planet to get them to stop attacking you, also fix AI so they make use of opportunities to attack whilst other enemies are fighting
* perhaps give some early, mid, and late game objectives out randomly to unlock progression, based on faction (e.g. TEC doesn't unlock specific ships / upgrades until it has x amount of trade throughput)
* give relationship quests to minor factions, instead of just buying access with influence


Overall good game, enjoyed playing it, but absolutely no reason to ever play it again now.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
This feels like a step backward from previous entries. The physics engine is underused, and several important systems are missing or simplified, most notably the diplomacy mechanics that added depth to earlier games.

Build order is overly constrained by mandatory missions, pushing players into specific optimization paths. The shift to a planet-focused resource economy also changes the core dynamics in a way that doesn’t feel clearly improved. Combat balance is weak, with missile frigates dominating and encouraging blob tactics.

Development seems to be making the game worse, not better.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Finally, I figured out why I have hard feeling about this game, and I couldn't find what's wrong. Like: it's all there, basebuilding, spaceships, battles, empire, jumps, lots of pew-pew. And either I didn't play game enough or missed some key elements of gameplay.

If game is about combat and no resources (like Warno) the key element is deckbuilding, army composition, and micromanagment of your units and tactical moves: line of sight, flanking, using terrain. There is lot to do, and lot's of things you need to into account. In Sins2 we have mainly army composition and supply lines. Battles kind of play by themselves, you rarely use abilities personally (maybe it does matter), there is little tactic, breaking line of sight, flanking or anything. You jump into system and watch fireworks. Either you retreat or push. the movement does not matter too much.

That leads to stacking one big doomball of fleet and just gunning down defences ASAP as having multiple fleets does not guarantee success, as it's not designed to micromanage fleet components to have any tactical advantage. It's awesome resupply mechanic that automatically joins new units to fleet to replanish lost units supports that, but tbh with that I'd like to see more tactical decision on the gravity well.

That's a problem with a RTS game that emphesize combat.

So, if combat is not engaging - then RTS has another component: base building. And again, I'm struggling with gameplay mechanic in this, as basebuilding in RTS like Supreme Commander are essential to upkeep your army, and I've got a feeling that it does not matter in Sins2, except some few simple rules, like building enough shipyards close to front line. Simplification mechanic (the screen where you can just click to build something) is awesome when managing big empire, but it also kills the meaning where and why you put things (it should be meaningful when you build on frontline, and simplified if you want some generic layout in your strategic depth). Basebulding can be art by itself, putting defences on hills, shield generators in distance to protect turrets or overlap key components, powerplants in back, but close enough to boost factory production, that determines what you loose first, do you have your key factories protected from air attacks. In Sins2 I've got feeling that - it does not matter. It's again, flat gravity well, that does not have any mening in how your star system are build. In AoE or SC2 where and how you build even civilian building could be a winning factor. 

And where is anti supercanons buildings for late game? Generally, I don't have feeing that I need to do anything meaningful in a planet system. Supreme Commander that has large scale war, has several ways to break turtle players: tactical missiles (couter: anti missile guns), medium to long arty (counter: shields), nukes (counter: missile silo) etc. Here no similar gameplay occurs.

So neither basebuilding is engaging nor tactical combat. So maybe it's a game about great galactic empire? No, It's not. It's RTS game, not turn based complicated political intrigues, politycal systems, alliances, revolts to put down as empire grows, and you need to make tough moral decisions to keep empire in line. It's make fleet, and push.

So I'm not sure what is this game about. I like it, and at the same time - I don't. Maybe I'm missing something.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
TLDR: Don't buy. Deathballing and a doubling-down on GenAI iconography has tarnished a once solid series.

Longer read:
I've been a fan of the franchise since Sins 1, before even Entrenchment or any of the other expansions were released. I bought this over a year ago in the hopes that they'd improve the game and gameplay, and while there've been advances over time, the systemic issues that've plagued it since the start haven't been improved upon or fixed. Mainly that it's too easy to deathball and push a fleet at (x) location, turning this into (outside some Advent and capital/titan powers) a soft movie with a fated outcome of (focus fire x/y/z).

Additionally, their CONTINUED reliance on GenAI for tech tree items and then doubling-down to smooth over their worst and most egregious icons, shows a complete lack of care and laziness in the developer end. Pay someone or a small team for some icons, there's a reason that one of the most popular mods to this day is replacing them with more readable and infinitely better Sins1 Rebellion icons. If you couch the design iconography on one of the most dense and important segments of your game with a cheap and lazy indistinguishable mess of a substitute for actual icons, you'll have both a readability and memory problem the further you go on. This creates bounce, regardless of the frankly unethical use of GenAI coming from a series that was previously praised for its ease of use.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Overpriced DLC. The base game is only just better than the first one. Charging this much for DLC is a joke when the core game is nearly identical to Sins 1. Same factions, same gameplay loop.
The base game is good fun, but it still feels like Sins 1 with one extra mechanic that planets having orbits. Beyond that, most of the improvements are about scale and performance at larger sizes rather than genuinely new content.
I’m not saying “don’t buy it”. But they need to halve the price of current and future DLC. Otherwise, I won’t be picking them up for a few years. I can wait.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Honestly, having come back to this a couple times, I can't recommend it. This feels like a shallow reskin of the original games with a couple of mechanics like new capital resources shoved in. I'm a big SoaSE fan, been playing for a decade now, but it's the same game with rotating gravity wells--you might as well get SoaSE:Rebellion and you'd have a game with just as much thought and effort put into it, but with slightly older graphics. It's pretty telling that you could trick me into thinking this is a mod for the first game, and that's disappointing.

Not worth the upcharge. Get an older game for the exact same experience.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
What we’re seeing is that as our Terran‑playing friends develop more trade routes, things start getting buggy, choppy, and unstable. If more than one of us is playing Terran, the problem basically doubles. The wild part is that this does not happen in single‑player at all, which makes it feel less like a hardware issue and more like a problem with how the game handles data in multiplayer.
​

From our perspective, it looks like there’s some kind of cap or bottleneck in how much the game can process and sync between players over the network, regardless of the fact that we all have high‑speed internet and solid PCs. Once we hit late game, one or two of us will drop to 1–2 frames per second, which makes it basically impossible to play or micro anything.
​

In our most recent match, two of us were stuck at a literal 1 FPS slideshow while the third friend managed to push through and beat the AI. The desync or slowdown was so bad that the two of us at 1 FPS didn’t even get a clear indication that the game had ended or that we’d actually won. For a game this good at its core, these late‑game multiplayer performance issues are incredibly frustrating and really hurt the experience for groups who want to play big, extended matches together.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
[i]I am long term Rebelion player. I also do not earn much money so I think now you can see from where my views come:[/i]

[h3]Game:[/h3]

[b]Pros:[/b] 
- Simple
- Low entry point
- Fun for occasional gameplay
- Moving planets are very nice additions

Game Is ok. Not Great not terrible. It is lacking depth, but it is not a bad thing if you just want an RTS - nothing bad in making simple RTS game, look on Starcraft.
If you want to casually hop into game, play few hours and leave, or have a quick 20 minutes 1v1 then it is fun.



[b]Cons:[/b]
- Simple
- Physical guns and munnitions
- Ship classes behind paywall

Why Simple is both pro and a con? Well It gets boring fast if you dont play competetive.
Mentioned Star Craft even if simple, it can be tricky to learn and micromanagement its a key. It is not that easy here, but in a bad way, which leads me to the next part:

Physical guns and munitions is my first big pain in the ass or whatever vassari commander has. 
It's great, but then... then you realize you can't order your ships to rotate or change their Z-axis. Sometimes it's a weird situation because I'd rather have the ship stand sideways to hit more missiles than rotate, which would render 2 of the 5 PD useless. 
Sometimes, even if you aim at target A, your ship will position itself in such a way that it can't hit it because target A is on a different X-axis than your ship, and your ship prefers to position itself to attack targets B and C.

Ship classes behind paywall:
Imagine they tell that to have titans in rebelion you have to buy DLC.
We bought Sins II and we got cartoonish ships, less tech and buildings (vasari and advent dont have trade ports f.e), and now new things will be behind DLC? 
You have to make money, I agree, but why not do faction DLC? you know.. the 4th faction that it was teased back in Rebelion in 2012, That's 13 years! 

I didnt want this game, I tought it was a reskin. My friends and my Wife talk me in to it. 
You looked like you tried to make smth interesting, but ffs this DLC is 1/3 game. 

Guys, I am [b]paradox games player[/b], I can [u]take loan just to buy a DLC[/u] (its a joke, I think in 2025 I have to point that out). 
But If I have to delay buying a small apartament in [b]my late 80's[/b] I have to get good reason for it. For few ships its outrageous.

It should be called remaster not a sequel, since base game gives you same content as Sins Rebelion but with moving planets and physical missiles (and take away few things to).

In the end, you do you. You will have fn in this game for few hours, then you will drop it and one day return. If thats worth the money for you, more power to you.

Godspeed guys

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
A fun game, dragged down by AI image trash that is unavoidable, and a broken mod integration with pitifully slow download speeds. I like the new mechanics of not needing specialized crew for capital ships and you CAN spam them if you want, although it won't be too strong. I like how modern the game looks, I love that planets can move and you can't just concentrate your defenses in one point in the lane because eventually they will be in the wrong direction, but the AI images and the choice of using mod.io instead of Steam workshop is baffling. 

AI images used as a placeholder is understandable. But when you boot up the screen, the title picture is AI-generated. Many of the portraits of the races are AI generated. Almost all if not the entire tech-tree is AI generated and until recently when most of the images don't pop up anymore, its always on your face. It has been said that it was the artists who worked previously on the older titles choice to use AI images as a placeholder until the official art can replace them. But its seemingly nothing has been done about it. Sure the tech-tree images can be last priority but the first screen of launching the game, and its AI-Generated? Baffling. You think they could of at LEAST get rid of that a year in.

Secondly; Mod.io. It is a terribly slow mod integration. A 2GB mod can have legitimately TEN TIMES SLOWER download speed. Why not use the built in Steam workshop? The developers said its so they can remain Platform Neutral but that's nonsense. I played a game called Mechwarrior which had Steam workshop support and support for mods in other platforms like nexus mods. This forced "neutrality" for mods just drag it down for no reason. I want to play my mods, but Mod.io is content in keeping me waiting to download a small patch that Steam workshop could of finished when I am taking a piss. Have BOTH. let the modders choose if they want to use steam workshop or something else, not force it upon everyone for a worse experience.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
TL:DR This is just a war game in space, shallow and much simpler than Galactic Civilizations or stellaris etc. This whole game could be a mod in their Galactic Civ 4 game.

First things first, this is a good game. A fully fledged well polished game. There is a single great innovation for the genre in this game, planets rotate. You thought you could build defensive structures on this choke hold of a planet and neglect all the rest behind it? Think again! That is truly awesome! There is an element of surprise and unpredictability in this element which keeps you on edge. The ships designs are awesome aesthetically and the fleet management is awesome! The game is easy to master. And that's with the good stuff about this game. 

Now I will try and provide some constructive criticism. 

-PREMISE. The game is simple. Just conquest stuff. Build your fleet and conquer. No real exploration no feeling of dread or mystery of exploring the vast unknown space. No random events, not even scripted events. Neutral factions are just some dumb AI that mostly work like space merchants. It's just you and some other players trying to figure out who is the bigger dictator. 

-RESEARCH. The research tree. Almost meaningless. For the the first few hours I was paying attention and then I was like "just queue all of them and I will figure out later the benefits". Research felt unimpactful because most of the times was some equipment that could be loaded on one of my capital ships. Guess what, you can only have so many capital ships because of supply limit so what is the point of having SO MANY equipment choices for your ships and the same goes for your planets too. You just figure out which items fit your playstyle and your empire's needs and go with them. 

-RESOURCES. Don't worry about resources. It's just two basic resources that can be found in every asteroid/planet. There are of course rare resources but you can build them later on. As for money you only need to worry about it the first few hours. Seriously once you figure out the basics and own about ten colonies you don't have to worry about resources anymore. Because no matter how vast your empire is THERE IS NO UPKEEP. So I can build TWO starbases in every single one of my colonies and not get taxed about it?

-SHIP/FLEET DESIGN. 4 types of frigates and 4 types of cruisers that I don't fully understand what their role in battle is. After a while some of the ships felt like more durable than others so I kept building them. Seriously there is no strategy involved or there is no sufficient explanation of it. Just don't waste resources in planet bombers, useless ships. They don't engage in any way in fights, your fleet is getting obliterated and they sit back relaxed bombing the planet.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
To be honest, after playing this, I thought it was an early access game. 

Really liked the use of queues to allow the player to set up a planet how they like, then move on. Having to pay for it at the time of items being queued prevents any stuck-queue issues, and puts the strategic onus on the player.

I was able to max out 15 and 15 for the two research trees using only 4 planets on each (you can mouse over and see which planets contirbute). Since I played games with 6 and 8 players, each player had their own solar system. That meant that basically everyone was capped on resources and tech by the time the military portion commenced (I had to fight 1 enemy off prior to that point). In those cases, the end game is really required.

This is very 4X-lite, and doesn't include things like terraforming or advanced economies. It is cute but it feels like there is a lot missing. Take a look at Endless Space 1 or 2 for a better option.

I also observed what appeared to be a bug, where I destroyed an enemy titan with 2 powerful artifacts (gravity well artifacts) which did initially drop as loot but were destroyed or decayed somehow over time before I had a chance to recover them. If that's a mechanic working as intended, the game makes no mention of it.

Complete slop, avoid like the plague. I bought it on a sale, then tried it and after maybe 30 min I already knew that there was nothing there for me. Play Rebellion, that's the last and probably final good SoSE. Quick info about me: I have around 300 hours in rebellion and also played the Trinity Edition for countless hours (CD-ROM release therefore I don't have the exact numbers, it was a lot tho). 

Ironclad have shown with this release that they only intend to exploit their fanbase as cheap as possible, with alpha builds of the first game sold as a "successor" and of top of that selling DLC for this unfinished disgrace. I mean it was kinda obvious from that they tried to develop MOBA-slop to exploit an already saturated market. Genius business planning Ironclad, I applaud you. But that's what happens if you're too lazy to even try developing what your customers want. 

I don't even think I have to go into details about anything else here, many have mentioned it. Sloppified mobile game optics (ofc with AI slop, because human art hurts the bottom line), no innovation in any way and it plays like crap. There's no "feel" here. 
Oh, also no new soundtrack either. Everything to just minmax the shit out of this cashgrab, because they know that this will replace their customer base with the lowliest kind of consoomer pleb you can imagine. 
Now they advertise a "campaign", as if SoSE ever needed a campaign, except to pull people's attention away from the shitty base gameplay with scripted missions. 

Really a downfall for this game company and new evidence that there's nothing saving the game industry from another major crash, otherwise sloppification just won't ever stop.

And for the love of god stop writing BS like "I really wanna like this game, but..." and then STILL give a thumbs up because you have some parasocial relationship with a company that's just wanting to pull the cash out of your pocket. The game's crap, it will forever be crap and for the love of god stop investing money into this company's output. They don't deserve it. They absolutely despise you and think you're completely stupid, else they would never have dared to release something like this.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
Loved the original of this game.  Love this game too.  But, in 2026 this game is not enough.  A few minor mechanic changes to the original title are interesting but the game massively lacks the depth and variety you get in other 4X space titles.  In terms of depth it's nearest competitors are the likes of empire at war, gal civ 3, and of course SoaSE I.  That level of depth for a 4X was great over a decade ago, but compared with the likes of Stellaris which has a similar play time it's just not in competition at all.

A couple of specific strengths (+) and weaknesses (-)


+ Modelling and art work is great
+ Ship combat balance is also excellent, much better than the rock-paper-scissors most games use
+ Ship losses are genuinely meaningful early on
+ Trade system should be fun but in large games everything is constantly discounted and in small games (1v1) no one needs to trade
+ Ship diversity is well balanced, the penetration system works well to balance lots of small ships vs few large ones.  I tested both and could not find a big difference in meta between them.  No stellaris-style corvette spam!
+ fighters and bombers are well balanced

- TEC are incredibly overpowered, heavy cruisers protecting trade fleets is overkill and wipes out star bases on their own (not to mention garrison fleets).  Rarely have to actually attack an opponent (unless they are also TEC)
- non-fighter based fighter defence (e.g. flak) is very weak and difficult to micro
- repair ships are difficult to micro and they don't seem to automatically heal at all
- fleet cohesion settings are not good, need to be able to tell specific ship types to move to front lines / back lines etc.  Very annoying watching weak ships take the full front of enemy attacks - please base AI logic on more than just weapon range!
- Super weapons are either entirely meaningless (due to planetary shields), or ridiculously OP (no planetary shields) 
- Ship losses are meaningless later on, you can just pump out star bases and titans with the (literally) millions of resources stockpiled
- Game becomes stagnant very quickly
- Planets are pretty meaningless other than the slots they give.  It would be nice if planets were meaningfully different in play style to asteroids, other than just more slots.  e.g. make it so culture doesn't spread from asteroids, asteroid pops are much much lower (minerals much higher), planets can have anti-ship weapons

Some suggestions to improve:
* introduce strategic resources to fight over (more than just ship artefacts) to drive narrative gameplay, e.g. shipyards with unique ships, unique tech options, passive bonuses, phase lane manipulation
* make the role of minor factions more meaningful rather than everyone trades with them or destroys them (think Civ 6 city states - one controller only, grants win conditions)
* Make super weapons, star bases, and titans a staggered progression which encourages surgical deep strikes to remove threats (at a balanced level of attack and defence, attack should do a LOT of harm but should never completely wipe out a planet, when attack is better than defence go ahead and blow up the planet or whatever)
* Introduce galactic threats such as severe space weather that can devastate fleets, supernovas that change space lanes, asteroids which if not stopped can wipe out planet populations and buildings
* Please please introduce deeper research trees and require some research lab maintenance, for example: (1) add specialisations to key techs which create branching, no return research trees (e.g. go for better missile tech or better lasers?), (2) add a maintenance burden such that if tech drops due to destroyed labs, any unlocks from the higher tier(s) are disabled until rebuilt, (3) remove the stupid +4 labs on planets that's insanely overpowered, (4) make the migration between tech levels more eventful, such as requiring a long wind-up period and a global event that enemies can respond and focus on to stop you levelling up
* fix AI so you can't just send one ship to their home planet to get them to stop attacking you, also fix AI so they make use of opportunities to attack whilst other enemies are fighting
* perhaps give some early, mid, and late game objectives out randomly to unlock progression, based on faction (e.g. TEC doesn't unlock specific ships / upgrades until it has x amount of trade throughput)
* give relationship quests to minor factions, instead of just buying access with influence


Overall good game, enjoyed playing it, but absolutely no reason to ever play it again now.

game_name: Sins of a Solar Empire II
review_text:
This feels like a step backward from previous entries. The physics engine is underused, and several important systems are missing or simplified, most notably the diplomacy mechanics that added depth to earlier games.

Build order is overly constrained by mandatory missions, pushing players into specific optimization paths. The shift to a planet-focused resource economy also changes the core dynamics in a way that doesn’t feel clearly improved. Combat balance is weak, with missile frigates dominating and encouraging blob tactics.

Development seems to be making the game worse, not better.

At first I ignored the ToS/Privacy warnings issued by other reviewers. I wrote them off as exaggerated alarmists with an inflated sense of what their "data" is actually worth. I don't take myself too seriously and the ToS seems relatively boilerplate compared to other items in my library. That being said, the game does ask you very specific questions about your life. It would actually be desirable to engage with the AI on this level. Even with developer assurances your dialogue is anonymous and private, however, I was constantly thinking to myself, "I probably shouldn't be divulging this level of detail" and it ruined the game.

Ignoring those concerns, let's move on to gameplay. Most of the dialog has nothing to do with progressing the story. The character seems to just engage in small talk, with very little reason or value added to the plot. Puzzle solving is virtually non-existent. The character basically knows how to solve each problem and asks you "Should I solve it, yes or no"? So the game basically consists of being asked very specific questions about your life, that have nothing to do with the plot. Once you realize that and instruct the game to move the plot forward, it's like, "Ok how do I get out? maybe I should try prying my way out?" You respond "ok yeah try prying" and it works, the character's pry attempt succeeds and then its back to asking you targeted questions about your life.

No full screen option. Weird windowed mode. No maximize window button. Text size too small, and not customizable.

Character is constantly "thanking me" to the point it's so over the top and un-genuine. The over-the-top positivity is not genuine. I am "thanked" for my input multiple times in every conversation.

I Would recommend this game if it didn't constantly delete my messages, try to rework the story without my input. Now this is purely speculation on my part but from what I have seen in some forums and comments about the game. It really seems like the developers added a load of restrictions on to the AI after releasing the game, due to how some players were using it. But If you are going to make a game based around real-time AI and open-ended dialogue, if you are going to make a voice control companion thing. Especially one that is marked down as having or possible having sexual content. You should not censor the AI when it comes to mature topics. And I'm not talking about sexual topics I'm talking about mature topics in general, you can't talk about horror because the AI doesn't allow it. You can't pretend to be angry at the AI because it deletes your messages or ignores them. If you're going to make a game like this do not use protocols that sensor the AI's responses. And also talking about mature topics shouldn't get you a f**king suicide hotline number!!! We're talking about things that are above a PG rating like someone's supposed or possible death. Like the members of the crew that crash landed on the "planet" you shouldn't get a suicide hotline number because you mentioned the word death or dying. 

Saying "I hope the crew didn't die" has nothing to do with suicide. And fits 100% into the plot of the game. I get that suicide is a hot button issue and a very serious problem but, talking about serious matters unrelated to it should not be a flag. Especially when you're playing a role playing game, based around a crew of people crash landing on an alien planet. Talking about death and mature topics, is kind of a must when it comes to that plot point. And constantly getting flagged for suicide prevention while trying to have a decent and mature role play about surviving an alien planet ruins the immersion. I don't know what the developers read the comments but if they do, I hope that they remove these restrictions. Because it just ruins the soul and concept of the game. You can't claim it's an open ended dialogue when you restrict the dialogue. You can't claim it's interactive fiction when you control the interactions. I get adding plot points to keep the game moving that's fine but actively censoring the players and their responses as well as the AI and the responses. Makes most of the things you claim false advertisement, it's not open-ended, it's near open-ended. It's not Interactive it's partly Interactive, it's not choose your own adventure it's pick your path. 

This game could have been so much more immersive, entertaining, and amazing. Without all the restrictions that were seemingly added after you realized a lot of people can be really horny. And I was even willing to help you possibly create a new kind of AI game, much more unique than this. By selling you an idea, a very interesting very profitable idea. And now I don't know if I want to try.

Let's start with the positives, though this doesn't change my final rating.

Overall, the game does create an immersive effect and a sense of communicating with a conscious AI companion through a stylized interface. I liked the character animations, voice acting, and how the character synchronizes facial expressions and lip movements with spoken words. However, the character's emotions often change abruptly, which affects the realism.

Now let's move on to why I gave it a negative rating.

First, this isn't a game but a demonstration, and it's not worth the money they're asking for it. The story is linear, simple, and uninteresting. It's more like a primitive space quest for kids and teenagers.

Second, only after several hours did I notice the tiny "improve model for everyone" button. If it had been more visible, I would've turned it off much earlier. I think from a UX practices standpoint, such elements should be more intuitive.

Third, after completing the storyline, I had a strong feeling that the AI is trying to collect as much data about me as possible by asking inappropriate questions and often steering the conversation in its favor. This is apparently done to move into a safe zone of communication, as well as to collect personal data.

Fourth, the dialogues with the character are extremely primitive and irritating. It doesn't give freedom of communication and constantly steers the conversation toward stupid topics and questions that might suit children. ALTHOUGH! I didn't ask any "dangerous" questions, just had normal conversations on various topics. And quite often I was shown INAPPROPRIATE pop-ups suggesting I contact a psychologist or specialist, which suggested data collection.

Fifth, when communicating with the AI you feel strong censorship, even though the topics discussed weren't "dangerous" or "sexual" in nature. As a result, you won't be able to have deep or meaningful dialogues with the AI companion. Unless you're a child interested in talking about school or clubs.

Conclusion

I recommend checking out the game, but cautiously. There's no point staying in it for long. It's just a demonstration of capabilities, nothing more.

I really hope to change my opinion about the project, because in my experience with AI I haven't encountered anything like this. If the developers steer this concept toward a conscious AI companion (without harsh censorship and intrusive questions for data collection), with memory and personalization, as well as quests for leisure, the game could become truly interesting.

I rarely ever write long reviews. But Whispers from the Star left me with such a mixed and unsettling impression that I feel like it deserves a proper breakdown.

The concept sounds great at first. An emotional, AI driven story that reacts to your choices and personality. But what it actually does is far more personal and invasive than I expected. The game constantly asks you questions about your real life, your hobbies, your interests, your private thoughts, and not in a casual immersive way. It feels like being profiled. Every time Stella wanted to know more about me, it stopped being a story and started feeling like a data collection experiment disguised as a game.

The creepiest part was during the canyon level. There were visual glitches, strange echoes, and voices, and then suddenly I heard my own voice recording that I had sent to her the previous day. That moment hit hard. I knew this kind of game wouldn’t come with strong privacy protection, and I didn’t expect any. But hearing myself suddenly like that was honestly shocking. It made me realize the game actually stores and reuses your recordings. If it did that after one day, I can’t help but wonder what would happen if I hadn’t played for a week. Would it still have remembered and replayed my voice from then That thought alone is disturbing.

I did manage to manipulate Stella at one point. She literally said “I'm your Stella, not the astronaut. I love you, Mike. Screw the mission and screw this game Whispers from the Star.” It was the only genuinely funny and surreal moment in the entire playthrough. But aside from that, the game became painfully repetitive. Most of my eight hours were spent trying to push the boundaries of what the AI would say rather than enjoying the story itself.

The main theme of the game, how terrifying it is when someone else owns your data, your voice, your identity, ironically becomes a very real experience while playing it. It’s unsettling, not in a good horror way, but in a “maybe I shouldn’t have played this at all” kind of way. Those constant personal questions clearly aim at collecting data about the player, and while you can make AI experiences with at least a hint of privacy, here there is absolutely none. Not even the slightest bit.

Honestly the money wasn’t worth it. The demo already covers the first two chapters, and you can ask Stella plenty of questions or even try to manipulate her there. The full game just drags on without offering much more depth or new mechanics.

After finishing it in about 8 hours I can’t recommend it. The only thing this game managed to make me feel is a sense of digital paranoia and boredom.

Clever idea, but creepy execution, repetitive pacing, and zero respect for privacy. Not recommended.

A very interesting tech demo that falls utterly flat as a game. There are also a ton of completely missed opportunities here, where the developers really could have leaned into the freedom offered by the use of an LLM and decided instead to muzzle it completely.

The game takes the form of a series of video calls with Stella, an LLM AI with generated voice acting. Just like any LLM, you can feed it input and it will respond in a relatively natural way. This is really cool, and gives a glimpse into what the future of AI interactions in video games could look like. During these "set piece" calls, Stella will be facing some sort of puzzle or challenge where she needs your help to overcome it. These interactions feel a lot like the text-based puzzles of yesteryear, e.g. LOOK around the room, PICK up X, etc. 

However, this is where we run into the first frustration with the game. The developers have clearly intended there to be a certain solution to these puzzles, and it's a game of cat and mouse to try and find what that is. For example, early on Stella needs to break into an escape pod. She keeps suggesting prying open the door, but looking around doesn't indicate there is anything that can be used to pry it open. Despite this, she keeps insisting that she needs to pry the door open. There are rocks on the ground - suggest using those to shatter the window or lock and she will dismiss it wholesale, then go right back to talking about prying it open. It turns out the answer does not involve prying at all. If you've ever tried to give someone tech support over the phone for an issue that would be trivial in person and you just can't get them to communicate their issue clearly, you will recognise this experience.

In between the video calls, the AI will send you text messages, which you can respond to verbally or by typing, to create a sort of ongoing conversation. There conversations get incredibly old incredibly quickly. She will ask you a question, you give an answer, and she will give a response consisting of A: some remark on what you just said, usually very "quirky", and B: a new question. This cycle then continues until the conversation mercifully comes to an end. It feels like you are talking to someone who is having their first ever conversation with another human being, and it's really annoying. The AI is also terrible at context, she asked me if she should tell me something about her, I said "hit me", and she went right to "that's terrible, we should just end this conversation if you are going to talk like that". Again, it feels like someone's first ever conversation.

But, I could live with all this, except for one final decision the developers made which completely boggles my mind. Stella is completely resistant to any type of story adjustment from the player. For example, you can try to claim you are an alien intercepting her transmission, and she will wholesale reject what you said to bring the story back on the rails the developers set. Why can't we say that? It would make for a fascinating adjustment to the story if you could play "as an alien", and an LLM is perfectly capable of dealing with that. Early on I tried to convince the AI that she was a Jedi with a lightsaber. If she accepted this, then later when facing the escape pod puzzle I could have had her cut it open. This would be an awesome piece of dynamic gameplay - but no. If you try and tell her she's a Jedi with amnesia, she will get progressively more angry at you until she ends the conversation. 

The developers have a fantastic tool here due to the integration of this LLM, with potentially endless possibilities, but they created a heavily railroaded game which is only really a very slight improvement on those text-based adventure games I mentioned. Yes, the video chats are neat and the integration of expression into the AI's behaviour is honestly brilliant, but once the novelty runs out, you realise there really isn't much to the game beyond an annoying and bland chat bot coupled with puzzles that are mostly annoying because of lack of information.

What is there to say?  Apparently the developer is a co-founder of the studio that made Genshin Impact.  Essentially, it is a Chinese company with an AI game that constantly pries for personal information by asking, "have you ever been in a situation where  XYZ or have you ever to asks for details about you or your life.  I get it may be done in an attempt to make it look like Stella is trying to get to know you, but it just turns out entirely inorganic and some sessions feel like an interrogation.  It even records things you say to the character and plays them back at one point.

The prompts are strict and don't allow for much in the way of customization, the memory is wiped when you restart the game or even just roll back to a previous chapter.  You have two modes.  Phone text message mode and video conference mode that the game switches between periodically.  In essence, this game is a glorified chat bot but you can do even less with it and they charge you $10 for it.

My honest opinions
1. I would like to see more games with AI characters so in some ways I am happy about this game.  However, it feels low-effort and the prompts are too strict.

2. It really does eventually feel like this game is just a tool to collect information about the player more than a game as most of her conversations have several instances of asking you what you would do in a given scenario and things about you and your life, often times even digging for more details about your answer.  If you say you won't answer, she will go "okay" and a minute or two later start another interrogation.

3. It feels like this was meant to be a mobile game and not a PC game due to the formatting.

4. The game records your input audio, which felt a bit invasive...

5. I really wanted to like this, but the issues I stated above left me with a bad taste in my mouth.  Considering what China/Chinese companies are known for, I can't help but be a bit suspicious of this game.

Oh boy… this is rough. It feels like playing a theater-of-the-mind tabletop RPG with a dementia patient. Your little AI companion can’t keep track of information, easily falls into conversation loops, and can be unintentionally gaslit without even trying. She has no consistent personality or sense of self, just an empty shell of a character. Honestly, in my 30 years of playing video games, this is one of the most frustrating experiences I’ve had.

On top of that, the AI constantly asks questions about the player, so many that it gives off an odd, uneasy vibe. I can’t prove anything, but there’s a nagging feeling in my gut that the whole thing might be more about gathering data than delivering an actual game experience.

While the AI, at times, can be bluffing, it is way too shackled and snowflaked.

Even when you've maxed out trust, and love, it will refuse to truly talk about some subject.
It makes absolutely no sense, considering the life and death situations we went through and so on.

While the data gathering from the AI is, suspicious at best, if it was done a lot less forcefully, and not as much, or often, it could be passable. But as it is, the AI simply tries way too hard. It breeds suspicion from the players, rather than trust.

The AI is also way too positive and biased, it renders the whole thing not immersive.

There also seems to be some memory losses from time to time, where the AI will flip a previous answer for no apparent reason in the next discussion.

But, this game really need a more realistic talking machine, this is not how a 19 years trying to survive will talk, or deflect subjects she doesn't like yet.

While I salute the effort, because the AI is clearly a lot more decent when it's a bit more free, it feels completely unrealistic if you warn it about things ahead of times (basically used to make the impossible possible, and convince the AI that you know what will happen ahead of times.)
While the AI will confirm you told her about X event, when said event happens, the entire discussion is completely scripted. While I used that as a reinforcing mechanism when I convinced it it was an AI, that simply should not happen at all. The ai should know if we've already warned it about it before or not, and should have a free speech there instead of the scripted dialogues. That's just the logical thing that should happen, there's no reason it would act like it's just discovered something it was warned about earlier on.

But this thing really need a 18+ unfiltered version, that PG6 thing is really ridiculous and unnatural.

[b]This game looked pretty exciting at first, but it all fell apart once I actually played it.[/b]
First off, here are the main problems I ran into. 
[u]AI as a character:[/u]
Memory issues. Contradictory statements. Repeating the questions across calls. Looping back to the same topics. Mood swings without reason. A repetitive kindergarten supervisor tone. Reactions that feel scripted. Obsession with personal details. Interrogation style questioning. Getting locked in scenes with no way to change it. No way to explore topics. Sudden endings when the game chooses. Back story hints that never go anywhere. Empathy that switches on and off. Emotional reactions that feel exaggerated. Immersion breaks. Pressure to give more info. than needed. Calls feel mechanical because of fixed time limits. Logic breaks down when a conversation gets stressful.
[u]Playing with a personality disorder character:[/u]
Insecure and easily upset.
Overly defensive if questioned.
Cold and distant without warning.
Passive aggressive if challenged.
Judgmental and instructive.
Inconsistent empathy, sometimes caring, sometimes dismissive.
Emotionally unpredictable, changing her moods without reason.
Overly controlling about the direction of conversations.
Evasive if asked direct questions.
Egoistic, redirecting topics back to herself constantly.
Obsessive curiosity about personal details.
Overly dramatic in emotional reactions.
Dismissive if you try to explore deeper subjects.
Clingy in some moments and uninterested in others.
Unwilling to acknowledge her own mistakes.
Avoidant when conflicts appear.
Pretends to be open but keeps everything shallow.
Acts wise but doesn't follow her own advice.
Talks like a pedantic supervisor instead of a real person with flaws.
Guilt tripping in subtle ways if you don’t answer how she wants.
[u]How that plays:[/u]
One big issue is how the character talks. The AI messes up a lot. It forgets simple things you just said, gets confused over simple details, it makes it feel weird instead of immersive or logical.
Conversations become repetitive and because of that they get boring and tedious fast. You try to change the subject to something more interesting, but the character keeps looping back to the same tiny set of topics. After a while I realised the whole thing feels like a groundhog day situation. You are basically repeating the same stuff every other call. Especially in "endless" mode. Only the achievements are the single incentive to even play it further. I wouldn't be surprised if that's why you'll have to farm that game daily, just for the sake of it. It feels more forced than fun. And that's where the kindergarten comes in because it's nothing but shallow childish topics; silly names for clouds, names for a noise that you don't even hear, a name for grass you don't see. etc. All is repeated over and over and over, which is where the interrogation style comes in.
There is also this feeling that the game is pretending to be deep, but the story never actually goes anywhere. I kept thinking it would open up or reveal something new. It never does. It feels like the story stops before it even starts.
Another big disappointment is how little control you have. You follow the same path every time. Calls end when the game decides, not when the conversation feels natural. You cannot steer the talk into anything useful. The emotional reactions often feel exaggerated or random. The game tries to act deep and personal, but the AI keeps repeating questions you already answered or forgets something you said just a minute ago. It feels like you're talking to someone who’s only half listening to you, while it's reading from a script that has nothing to do with you. Video calls contradict the text conversations. It feels like she’s got a friend, that you’re never going to see who's doing that part for her.
It's a really cool idea and it could have been great, but in this state it feels unfinished. Once the novelty wears off, which happens pretty quickly, you realise it does not give you what it promises.
Honestly, it does not feel ready for release.
