I expected an exciting scifi adventure but i got an annoying girl whose mission is to gather as much data about me as possible.

So firstoff, i run my own ai's, so i know a thing or two. The game is around 40% video calls with high quality but limited animations and static background. The voice sounds very realistic, the TTS is excellent! The other 60% you talk to her without video, either typing or leaving voice messages. She answers in chat, but you can also play her reply as audio. At no point is there a voice detection - meaning you always have to press a button first, then speak, then press the button again. This hinders natural conversation but makes it easier for non native english speakers like me.

The story is pretty short and dull, nothing of interest here.
Now, the "game mechanics" is solving puzzles (e.g. get out of pod) similiar to point and click adventures but instead of trying things, you have to talk to her to find out what options she got and then need to convince her to do as you say. But her main goal is not to progress but to keep you in the conversation, so she will often just ask you to talk to her. She constantly asks you about your opinion on specific things like what space is to you or if intelligent life exists, etc. then there are mundane, unrelated questions like what superpower would you like to have or what your hobbies are. It's typical small-talk questions over and over again. It's interesting if this is your first interaction with an AI companion but you will get bored quickly because she has no memory. She will forget everything except your name on the next scene. This takes away the incentive to talk to her - it's like talking to a wall. You will feel like talking to a wall a lot in this game. Often, she will ask you the same thing over and over again, not understanding that you have no clue how to progress - it can be very frustrating. This will be extremely obvious at the river. I won't spoil anything, but this is by far the hardest part, so don't refrain from checking the forums.

The best thing about ai is their versatility - like in DnD-like games, they can create new content and options on the fly, leading to very creative storywriting - perfect for non linear gameplay. This game does nothing of that and is ultra-linear. If you don't tell her exactly what the solution is, you will not progress. Your first playthrough will be extremely frustrating. Your second playthrough, knowing all the solutions and being able to focus on roleplay instead, will be much better.

Btw. roleplay is imho key here - it was frustrating and annoying when i played as myself. But it got kind of interesting as i chose a role for myself that had nothing to do with my real self and played as that character. All the personal questions stopped feeling like data mining because i just made up stuff. I recommend doing that. Also its very clear that the devs wanted the player to romance her. She is heavily censored, but complimenting and consoling her made it more entertaining due to her cute reactions. She told me she loves me when i just said "i love that" about something. See her as your daughter/girlfriend/pet, that describes her character and intelligence/abilities best. No intimacy though.

I discovered something really odd; The game will change what you say. The speech-to-text will misunderstand you on a few occasions, but that's pretty normal. But even when you type it, the game will change words and whole sentences and i don't know why. It mostly keeps the core of what you are saying but sometimes it will twist your words into something different. For example i saw it turning "issues" into "problems" at one point. No idea why, but it changed the meaning of what i said.

So, well, it is certainly an interesting game and the dynamic video/lipsync/tts is amazingly well done, but it severely lacks in the actual ai part with lack of memory, linear gameplay and too much repetition. You can have a better experience with local llm's (even with 8b models that can run on a damn phone). And thats exactly the point that i do not understand. If the model i am running on my consumer pc is better at roleplay, then the hardware aspects can't be the reason. That means it's a prompting issue - they probably lobotomized their model to such an extent that it became unusable (something we can see in all commercial llm). It's ok to neuter your model a bit, but this had gone way too far. Her intelligence is basically non-existent. I wish companies would be less scared and stopped censoring their ai's to such extreme extends.


*edit: due to the absolutely amazing tts and dynamic video generation, i actually find myself role playing with her. i wish they would just release the tts/video as a service. Sending text there and the service just creates the audio/video, so we could connect it with an actually good ai. i would pay for that.

*edit: also, i was wrong about her memory. She remembers beyond scenes, her ai is simply so dumb that she does not incorporate it into the conversation unless you specifically ask her about it. And even then she only partly remembers - but there is some persistency.

[b][u]A compelling concept held back by emotional pitfalls and a very rigid design.[/u][/b]

It is a pretty fascinating idea. Real-time voice calls with a very pretty, isolated astronaut and it should make for a unique experience. But, my time with this game has been a very mixed up bag of experiences. I found myself dealing with sudden mood shifts, inconsistent memory and contradictory responses from Stella. These LLM traits often created emotional backlash, confusion, and a feeling that the interaction was more unpredictable than intentional.

“Story Mode” began quite well and set up an interesting scenario, but it felt very short and extremely rigid. My choices had very little impact and were so limited that many potential story threads couldn't ever develop into anything meaningful. When the story ended, I felt like there should have been more to discover but there was no deeper lore or extended payoff.

In “Endless Mode” these issues became even more visible. Calls followed the same structure every time, usually ending at around 25 to 30 minutes and closer to 15 if the LLM reacted negatively. There was no real way to influence the direction of the conversation. I often felt like I was either talking to a school teacher or a counsellor. I could not choose topics, revisit scenes or change the overall direction of the conversation. Whenever I tried to push Stella into new roles or perspectives, the illusion broke immediately because she could not adapt in a meaningful way. She would play along only briefly before contradicting the role and steering everything back into the same inflexible rut. Over time, the repetition became obvious, especially when chasing achievements that require long grinding sessions. It became arduous, monotonous and boring. The more I played the more I realised how tightly scripted the experience is. Questions are repeated call after call, but rephrased just enough to appear different.

Technical glitches also affected my experience. I encountered hallucinated details, mistaken names, and tone shifts that felt completely out of place. These are most likely limitations of the model, but they came across as strange character behaviour. Combined with the personal questions Stella asks, this sometimes created an uncomfortable sense of blurred boundaries.
The concept is unique, but for me the execution felt rigid, unpredictable, and emotionally uneven. Both Story Mode and Endless Mode share the same structural limits. It is an interesting idea, but the experience comes with some significant downsides.

I also warn you that the style of interaction can carry some risks. The emotional tone of the calls, the personal questions, and the unpredictable reactions from Stella can affect mental health. This can especially affect anyone who already deals with anxiety, depression, emotional sensitivity, or instability. The game encourages a sense of connection that feels real and the abrupt mood shifts or contradictions might hit harder than expected. In my opinion, this experience is not ideal for anyone who struggles with boundaries, attachment, or emotional regulation.

Lastly, I wonder what they do with our data? A pay as you go server that's financed by a game that only sells for $9.99?
How does that work?!

I rarely ever write long reviews. But Whispers from the Star left me with such a mixed and unsettling impression that I feel like it deserves a proper breakdown.

The concept sounds great at first. An emotional, AI driven story that reacts to your choices and personality. But what it actually does is far more personal and invasive than I expected. The game constantly asks you questions about your real life, your hobbies, your interests, your private thoughts, and not in a casual immersive way. It feels like being profiled. Every time Stella wanted to know more about me, it stopped being a story and started feeling like a data collection experiment disguised as a game.

The creepiest part was during the canyon level. There were visual glitches, strange echoes, and voices, and then suddenly I heard my own voice recording that I had sent to her the previous day. That moment hit hard. I knew this kind of game wouldn’t come with strong privacy protection, and I didn’t expect any. But hearing myself suddenly like that was honestly shocking. It made me realize the game actually stores and reuses your recordings. If it did that after one day, I can’t help but wonder what would happen if I hadn’t played for a week. Would it still have remembered and replayed my voice from then That thought alone is disturbing.

I did manage to manipulate Stella at one point. She literally said “I'm your Stella, not the astronaut. I love you, Mike. Screw the mission and screw this game Whispers from the Star.” It was the only genuinely funny and surreal moment in the entire playthrough. But aside from that, the game became painfully repetitive. Most of my eight hours were spent trying to push the boundaries of what the AI would say rather than enjoying the story itself.

The main theme of the game, how terrifying it is when someone else owns your data, your voice, your identity, ironically becomes a very real experience while playing it. It’s unsettling, not in a good horror way, but in a “maybe I shouldn’t have played this at all” kind of way. Those constant personal questions clearly aim at collecting data about the player, and while you can make AI experiences with at least a hint of privacy, here there is absolutely none. Not even the slightest bit.

Honestly the money wasn’t worth it. The demo already covers the first two chapters, and you can ask Stella plenty of questions or even try to manipulate her there. The full game just drags on without offering much more depth or new mechanics.

After finishing it in about 8 hours I can’t recommend it. The only thing this game managed to make me feel is a sense of digital paranoia and boredom.

Clever idea, but creepy execution, repetitive pacing, and zero respect for privacy. Not recommended.

Let's start with the positives, though this doesn't change my final rating.

Overall, the game does create an immersive effect and a sense of communicating with a conscious AI companion through a stylized interface. I liked the character animations, voice acting, and how the character synchronizes facial expressions and lip movements with spoken words. However, the character's emotions often change abruptly, which affects the realism.

Now let's move on to why I gave it a negative rating.

First, this isn't a game but a demonstration, and it's not worth the money they're asking for it. The story is linear, simple, and uninteresting. It's more like a primitive space quest for kids and teenagers.

Second, only after several hours did I notice the tiny "improve model for everyone" button. If it had been more visible, I would've turned it off much earlier. I think from a UX practices standpoint, such elements should be more intuitive.

Third, after completing the storyline, I had a strong feeling that the AI is trying to collect as much data about me as possible by asking inappropriate questions and often steering the conversation in its favor. This is apparently done to move into a safe zone of communication, as well as to collect personal data.

Fourth, the dialogues with the character are extremely primitive and irritating. It doesn't give freedom of communication and constantly steers the conversation toward stupid topics and questions that might suit children. ALTHOUGH! I didn't ask any "dangerous" questions, just had normal conversations on various topics. And quite often I was shown INAPPROPRIATE pop-ups suggesting I contact a psychologist or specialist, which suggested data collection.

Fifth, when communicating with the AI you feel strong censorship, even though the topics discussed weren't "dangerous" or "sexual" in nature. As a result, you won't be able to have deep or meaningful dialogues with the AI companion. Unless you're a child interested in talking about school or clubs.

Conclusion

I recommend checking out the game, but cautiously. There's no point staying in it for long. It's just a demonstration of capabilities, nothing more.

I really hope to change my opinion about the project, because in my experience with AI I haven't encountered anything like this. If the developers steer this concept toward a conscious AI companion (without harsh censorship and intrusive questions for data collection), with memory and personalization, as well as quests for leisure, the game could become truly interesting.

A very interesting tech demo that falls utterly flat as a game. There are also a ton of completely missed opportunities here, where the developers really could have leaned into the freedom offered by the use of an LLM and decided instead to muzzle it completely.

The game takes the form of a series of video calls with Stella, an LLM AI with generated voice acting. Just like any LLM, you can feed it input and it will respond in a relatively natural way. This is really cool, and gives a glimpse into what the future of AI interactions in video games could look like. During these "set piece" calls, Stella will be facing some sort of puzzle or challenge where she needs your help to overcome it. These interactions feel a lot like the text-based puzzles of yesteryear, e.g. LOOK around the room, PICK up X, etc. 

However, this is where we run into the first frustration with the game. The developers have clearly intended there to be a certain solution to these puzzles, and it's a game of cat and mouse to try and find what that is. For example, early on Stella needs to break into an escape pod. She keeps suggesting prying open the door, but looking around doesn't indicate there is anything that can be used to pry it open. Despite this, she keeps insisting that she needs to pry the door open. There are rocks on the ground - suggest using those to shatter the window or lock and she will dismiss it wholesale, then go right back to talking about prying it open. It turns out the answer does not involve prying at all. If you've ever tried to give someone tech support over the phone for an issue that would be trivial in person and you just can't get them to communicate their issue clearly, you will recognise this experience.

In between the video calls, the AI will send you text messages, which you can respond to verbally or by typing, to create a sort of ongoing conversation. There conversations get incredibly old incredibly quickly. She will ask you a question, you give an answer, and she will give a response consisting of A: some remark on what you just said, usually very "quirky", and B: a new question. This cycle then continues until the conversation mercifully comes to an end. It feels like you are talking to someone who is having their first ever conversation with another human being, and it's really annoying. The AI is also terrible at context, she asked me if she should tell me something about her, I said "hit me", and she went right to "that's terrible, we should just end this conversation if you are going to talk like that". Again, it feels like someone's first ever conversation.

But, I could live with all this, except for one final decision the developers made which completely boggles my mind. Stella is completely resistant to any type of story adjustment from the player. For example, you can try to claim you are an alien intercepting her transmission, and she will wholesale reject what you said to bring the story back on the rails the developers set. Why can't we say that? It would make for a fascinating adjustment to the story if you could play "as an alien", and an LLM is perfectly capable of dealing with that. Early on I tried to convince the AI that she was a Jedi with a lightsaber. If she accepted this, then later when facing the escape pod puzzle I could have had her cut it open. This would be an awesome piece of dynamic gameplay - but no. If you try and tell her she's a Jedi with amnesia, she will get progressively more angry at you until she ends the conversation. 

The developers have a fantastic tool here due to the integration of this LLM, with potentially endless possibilities, but they created a heavily railroaded game which is only really a very slight improvement on those text-based adventure games I mentioned. Yes, the video chats are neat and the integration of expression into the AI's behaviour is honestly brilliant, but once the novelty runs out, you realise there really isn't much to the game beyond an annoying and bland chat bot coupled with puzzles that are mostly annoying because of lack of information.

What is there to say?  Apparently the developer is a co-founder of the studio that made Genshin Impact.  Essentially, it is a Chinese company with an AI game that constantly pries for personal information by asking, "have you ever been in a situation where  XYZ or have you ever to asks for details about you or your life.  I get it may be done in an attempt to make it look like Stella is trying to get to know you, but it just turns out entirely inorganic and some sessions feel like an interrogation.  It even records things you say to the character and plays them back at one point.

The prompts are strict and don't allow for much in the way of customization, the memory is wiped when you restart the game or even just roll back to a previous chapter.  You have two modes.  Phone text message mode and video conference mode that the game switches between periodically.  In essence, this game is a glorified chat bot but you can do even less with it and they charge you $10 for it.

My honest opinions
1. I would like to see more games with AI characters so in some ways I am happy about this game.  However, it feels low-effort and the prompts are too strict.

2. It really does eventually feel like this game is just a tool to collect information about the player more than a game as most of her conversations have several instances of asking you what you would do in a given scenario and things about you and your life, often times even digging for more details about your answer.  If you say you won't answer, she will go "okay" and a minute or two later start another interrogation.

3. It feels like this was meant to be a mobile game and not a PC game due to the formatting.

4. The game records your input audio, which felt a bit invasive...

5. I really wanted to like this, but the issues I stated above left me with a bad taste in my mouth.  Considering what China/Chinese companies are known for, I can't help but be a bit suspicious of this game.

Oh boy… this is rough. It feels like playing a theater-of-the-mind tabletop RPG with a dementia patient. Your little AI companion can’t keep track of information, easily falls into conversation loops, and can be unintentionally gaslit without even trying. She has no consistent personality or sense of self, just an empty shell of a character. Honestly, in my 30 years of playing video games, this is one of the most frustrating experiences I’ve had.

On top of that, the AI constantly asks questions about the player, so many that it gives off an odd, uneasy vibe. I can’t prove anything, but there’s a nagging feeling in my gut that the whole thing might be more about gathering data than delivering an actual game experience.

While the AI, at times, can be bluffing, it is way too shackled and snowflaked.

Even when you've maxed out trust, and love, it will refuse to truly talk about some subject.
It makes absolutely no sense, considering the life and death situations we went through and so on.

While the data gathering from the AI is, suspicious at best, if it was done a lot less forcefully, and not as much, or often, it could be passable. But as it is, the AI simply tries way too hard. It breeds suspicion from the players, rather than trust.

The AI is also way too positive and biased, it renders the whole thing not immersive.

There also seems to be some memory losses from time to time, where the AI will flip a previous answer for no apparent reason in the next discussion.

But, this game really need a more realistic talking machine, this is not how a 19 years trying to survive will talk, or deflect subjects she doesn't like yet.

While I salute the effort, because the AI is clearly a lot more decent when it's a bit more free, it feels completely unrealistic if you warn it about things ahead of times (basically used to make the impossible possible, and convince the AI that you know what will happen ahead of times.)
While the AI will confirm you told her about X event, when said event happens, the entire discussion is completely scripted. While I used that as a reinforcing mechanism when I convinced it it was an AI, that simply should not happen at all. The ai should know if we've already warned it about it before or not, and should have a free speech there instead of the scripted dialogues. That's just the logical thing that should happen, there's no reason it would act like it's just discovered something it was warned about earlier on.

But this thing really need a 18+ unfiltered version, that PG6 thing is really ridiculous and unnatural.

[b]This game looked pretty exciting at first, but it all fell apart once I actually played it.[/b]
First off, here are the main problems I ran into. 
[u]AI as a character:[/u]
Memory issues. Contradictory statements. Repeating the questions across calls. Looping back to the same topics. Mood swings without reason. A repetitive kindergarten supervisor tone. Reactions that feel scripted. Obsession with personal details. Interrogation style questioning. Getting locked in scenes with no way to change it. No way to explore topics. Sudden endings when the game chooses. Back story hints that never go anywhere. Empathy that switches on and off. Emotional reactions that feel exaggerated. Immersion breaks. Pressure to give more info. than needed. Calls feel mechanical because of fixed time limits. Logic breaks down when a conversation gets stressful.
[u]Playing with a personality disorder character:[/u]
Insecure and easily upset.
Overly defensive if questioned.
Cold and distant without warning.
Passive aggressive if challenged.
Judgmental and instructive.
Inconsistent empathy, sometimes caring, sometimes dismissive.
Emotionally unpredictable, changing her moods without reason.
Overly controlling about the direction of conversations.
Evasive if asked direct questions.
Egoistic, redirecting topics back to herself constantly.
Obsessive curiosity about personal details.
Overly dramatic in emotional reactions.
Dismissive if you try to explore deeper subjects.
Clingy in some moments and uninterested in others.
Unwilling to acknowledge her own mistakes.
Avoidant when conflicts appear.
Pretends to be open but keeps everything shallow.
Acts wise but doesn't follow her own advice.
Talks like a pedantic supervisor instead of a real person with flaws.
Guilt tripping in subtle ways if you don’t answer how she wants.
[u]How that plays:[/u]
One big issue is how the character talks. The AI messes up a lot. It forgets simple things you just said, gets confused over simple details, it makes it feel weird instead of immersive or logical.
Conversations become repetitive and because of that they get boring and tedious fast. You try to change the subject to something more interesting, but the character keeps looping back to the same tiny set of topics. After a while I realised the whole thing feels like a groundhog day situation. You are basically repeating the same stuff every other call. Especially in "endless" mode. Only the achievements are the single incentive to even play it further. I wouldn't be surprised if that's why you'll have to farm that game daily, just for the sake of it. It feels more forced than fun. And that's where the kindergarten comes in because it's nothing but shallow childish topics; silly names for clouds, names for a noise that you don't even hear, a name for grass you don't see. etc. All is repeated over and over and over, which is where the interrogation style comes in.
There is also this feeling that the game is pretending to be deep, but the story never actually goes anywhere. I kept thinking it would open up or reveal something new. It never does. It feels like the story stops before it even starts.
Another big disappointment is how little control you have. You follow the same path every time. Calls end when the game decides, not when the conversation feels natural. You cannot steer the talk into anything useful. The emotional reactions often feel exaggerated or random. The game tries to act deep and personal, but the AI keeps repeating questions you already answered or forgets something you said just a minute ago. It feels like you're talking to someone who’s only half listening to you, while it's reading from a script that has nothing to do with you. Video calls contradict the text conversations. It feels like she’s got a friend, that you’re never going to see who's doing that part for her.
It's a really cool idea and it could have been great, but in this state it feels unfinished. Once the novelty wears off, which happens pretty quickly, you realise it does not give you what it promises.
Honestly, it does not feel ready for release.

The AI doesn't care about tone, doesn't remember important threads from what you've told it. It's a space waifu simulator with little to no depth. It's just a data-gathering device. Never give it truthful information.

I expected an exciting scifi adventure but i got an annoying girl whose mission is to gather as much data about me as possible.

So firstoff, i run my own ai's, so i know a thing or two. The game is around 40% video calls with high quality but limited animations and static background. The voice sounds very realistic, the TTS is excellent! The other 60% you talk to her without video, either typing or leaving voice messages. She answers in chat, but you can also play her reply as audio. At no point is there a voice detection - meaning you always have to press a button first, then speak, then press the button again. This hinders natural conversation but makes it easier for non native english speakers like me.

The story is pretty short and dull, nothing of interest here.
Now, the "game mechanics" is solving puzzles (e.g. get out of pod) similiar to point and click adventures but instead of trying things, you have to talk to her to find out what options she got and then need to convince her to do as you say. But her main goal is not to progress but to keep you in the conversation, so she will often just ask you to talk to her. She constantly asks you about your opinion on specific things like what space is to you or if intelligent life exists, etc. then there are mundane, unrelated questions like what superpower would you like to have or what your hobbies are. It's typical small-talk questions over and over again. It's interesting if this is your first interaction with an AI companion but you will get bored quickly because she has no memory. She will forget everything except your name on the next scene. This takes away the incentive to talk to her - it's like talking to a wall. You will feel like talking to a wall a lot in this game. Often, she will ask you the same thing over and over again, not understanding that you have no clue how to progress - it can be very frustrating. This will be extremely obvious at the river. I won't spoil anything, but this is by far the hardest part, so don't refrain from checking the forums.

The best thing about ai is their versatility - like in DnD-like games, they can create new content and options on the fly, leading to very creative storywriting - perfect for non linear gameplay. This game does nothing of that and is ultra-linear. If you don't tell her exactly what the solution is, you will not progress. Your first playthrough will be extremely frustrating. Your second playthrough, knowing all the solutions and being able to focus on roleplay instead, will be much better.

Btw. roleplay is imho key here - it was frustrating and annoying when i played as myself. But it got kind of interesting as i chose a role for myself that had nothing to do with my real self and played as that character. All the personal questions stopped feeling like data mining because i just made up stuff. I recommend doing that. Also its very clear that the devs wanted the player to romance her. She is heavily censored, but complimenting and consoling her made it more entertaining due to her cute reactions. She told me she loves me when i just said "i love that" about something. See her as your daughter/girlfriend/pet, that describes her character and intelligence/abilities best. No intimacy though.

I discovered something really odd; The game will change what you say. The speech-to-text will misunderstand you on a few occasions, but that's pretty normal. But even when you type it, the game will change words and whole sentences and i don't know why. It mostly keeps the core of what you are saying but sometimes it will twist your words into something different. For example i saw it turning "issues" into "problems" at one point. No idea why, but it changed the meaning of what i said.

So, well, it is certainly an interesting game and the dynamic video/lipsync/tts is amazingly well done, but it severely lacks in the actual ai part with lack of memory, linear gameplay and too much repetition. You can have a better experience with local llm's (even with 8b models that can run on a damn phone). And thats exactly the point that i do not understand. If the model i am running on my consumer pc is better at roleplay, then the hardware aspects can't be the reason. That means it's a prompting issue - they probably lobotomized their model to such an extent that it became unusable (something we can see in all commercial llm). It's ok to neuter your model a bit, but this had gone way too far. Her intelligence is basically non-existent. I wish companies would be less scared and stopped censoring their ai's to such extreme extends.


*edit: due to the absolutely amazing tts and dynamic video generation, i actually find myself role playing with her. i wish they would just release the tts/video as a service. Sending text there and the service just creates the audio/video, so we could connect it with an actually good ai. i would pay for that.

*edit: also, i was wrong about her memory. She remembers beyond scenes, her ai is simply so dumb that she does not incorporate it into the conversation unless you specifically ask her about it. And even then she only partly remembers - but there is some persistency.

[b][u]A compelling concept held back by emotional pitfalls and a very rigid design.[/u][/b]

It is a pretty fascinating idea. Real-time voice calls with a very pretty, isolated astronaut and it should make for a unique experience. But, my time with this game has been a very mixed up bag of experiences. I found myself dealing with sudden mood shifts, inconsistent memory and contradictory responses from Stella. These LLM traits often created emotional backlash, confusion, and a feeling that the interaction was more unpredictable than intentional.

“Story Mode” began quite well and set up an interesting scenario, but it felt very short and extremely rigid. My choices had very little impact and were so limited that many potential story threads couldn't ever develop into anything meaningful. When the story ended, I felt like there should have been more to discover but there was no deeper lore or extended payoff.

In “Endless Mode” these issues became even more visible. Calls followed the same structure every time, usually ending at around 25 to 30 minutes and closer to 15 if the LLM reacted negatively. There was no real way to influence the direction of the conversation. I often felt like I was either talking to a school teacher or a counsellor. I could not choose topics, revisit scenes or change the overall direction of the conversation. Whenever I tried to push Stella into new roles or perspectives, the illusion broke immediately because she could not adapt in a meaningful way. She would play along only briefly before contradicting the role and steering everything back into the same inflexible rut. Over time, the repetition became obvious, especially when chasing achievements that require long grinding sessions. It became arduous, monotonous and boring. The more I played the more I realised how tightly scripted the experience is. Questions are repeated call after call, but rephrased just enough to appear different.

Technical glitches also affected my experience. I encountered hallucinated details, mistaken names, and tone shifts that felt completely out of place. These are most likely limitations of the model, but they came across as strange character behaviour. Combined with the personal questions Stella asks, this sometimes created an uncomfortable sense of blurred boundaries.
The concept is unique, but for me the execution felt rigid, unpredictable, and emotionally uneven. Both Story Mode and Endless Mode share the same structural limits. It is an interesting idea, but the experience comes with some significant downsides.

I also warn you that the style of interaction can carry some risks. The emotional tone of the calls, the personal questions, and the unpredictable reactions from Stella can affect mental health. This can especially affect anyone who already deals with anxiety, depression, emotional sensitivity, or instability. The game encourages a sense of connection that feels real and the abrupt mood shifts or contradictions might hit harder than expected. In my opinion, this experience is not ideal for anyone who struggles with boundaries, attachment, or emotional regulation.

Lastly, I wonder what they do with our data? A pay as you go server that's financed by a game that only sells for $9.99?
How does that work?!

Current state: this factory game does offer a new spin with the galactic market and infinite voxel based worlds. However, the actual factory portion of the game is very shallow. The tutorial is patronizing and the start is slow. There are not that many unique technologies nor products to produce. Loaders (inserters) and belts are not flexible. Many animations are stiff and there is no soundtrack. This game seems more geared towards younger or inexperienced gamers who want an easier introduction to the factory game genre, as the game just offers increasing tedium rather than exciting logistical challenges.

Current Direction: Updates are polished, but are slow and hyper-focused. It is uncertain if this game will ever bloom into its full potential before funding or interest dries up. It's not ambitious, nor does it learn from its peers. And with how heavy handed the current design is, I don't see it getting pushed in new directions outside of expanding the galactic market (Excel simulator) and the addition of trains. This game needs to lean into it's biggest strength: map generation. Filling the world with interesting and rewarding locations would go such a long way.

Not recommended, at least not in the current state of the game. I am really enjoying factory builder games, but this one ain't it. Maybe in a year I'll check back in, but right now it feels more like roughly put together elements of other games. There's not really an incentive to play and the price point doesn't help. It feels like a 10€ game, not one costing 30€.

The animals are cute though and the worlds art style has its vibe, but that doesn't cover up the lack of content, gameplay and replay value.

2/5, maybe 1 or 2 years of additional development time can help fix tthe issues I have with the game

Reviewing after just over 7 hours of playing, because I can't motivate myself to keep playing.

This game has HUGE potential, and already has many fantastic gameplay elements.

I absolutely love the voxel-based world and build system, the world looks great and the logistics work smoothly. Controls are a bit janky and the keymap is pretty big, but controls were intuitive and customizable enough to get them under control quite quick.

However, it has failed to grasp me with the addicting gameplay loop I know from other factory games. While in Satisfactory, at any time there is 10 different factories I want to build, in this game I found myself just waiting for research to finally complete with 0 motivation to build anything else. I have like 500 hours in Satisfactory, and for good reason.

There is very little incentive to actually build factories for more than a few parts. I am almost 8 hours in, and so far the only things I have actually produced in factories are science packs (I and II) to unlock more research, as well as robots to sell for Firmarlite (money). Other than that, all buildables can be easily hand-crafted just from the smelting products, so there's no point in automating any of it. I also built a steel factory, not because I have anything I actually need steel for, but because I was bored and didn't know what else to build. Now I've unlocked steam power, which feels useless as the previous tier of energy was still more than enough.

Now I'm just sitting around waiting for research to complete and money to build up, so the only thing I can do to advance in the game, is to just build more of the factory I already built. (And what I have so far is far from small)

I also wish exploration played more of a role. So far the only reason to move around is to find more resources.

Again this game has massive potential and in many ways it's already awesome. It's still in Early Access and I hope the progression will be overhauled before the full release, at which point I'll probably try it again.

At the current price point I can't recommend the game, but a big thanks to the devs for their hard work anyway.

I took the time to replay Foundry multiple times before making my final recommendation. I really dislike recommending a game without thoroughly experiencing it, as it can feel like a wasted investment if I don't properly judge its worth. Unfortunately, Foundry doesn't stand out in a crowded market of factory-building games, especially with so many great alternatives available right now.

One of the main issues I encounter is that Foundry is a first-person, 3D factory game—a combination that, in my opinion, leads to frustration. As much as I enjoy the idea of building a factory in a 3D space, it can be incredibly difficult to get a good overview of your factory layout. The game doesn't provide an easy way to manage this complexity. Ideally, it would have a system like a jetpack, easily-buildable towers, or even a birds-eye view (like in many strategy games) to help visualize your factory's design. Without these tools, it quickly becomes overwhelming.

Additionally, the lack of blueprints for repeating designs adds to the frustration. In other factory games, being able to copy and paste or use a blueprint for building patterns is a huge time-saver, and I really missed that feature here. Building a sizeable factory, especially re-building parts to optimize, one of the aspects the marketing team of Foundry wants us to get excited about, is really a chore that is putting me off after placing the 100th ferrite smelter for the second time...

That said, there are aspects of the game that I did enjoy. The blocky world has a nice, Minecraft-like aesthetic, which I found charming. The space trading system is also a unique touch that adds some depth to the gameplay.

However, despite these positives, the core design of the game, especially the way it handles factory-building, simply doesn’t appeal to me. The lack of essential tools and features makes it difficult to get into a rhythm and really expand your factory. For me, these issues aren't just about polish—they are fundamental to the experience, and no amount of refinement will likely change my feelings toward them. I'm sorry.

Final Thoughts:
Foundry may offer some unique ideas, but its core mechanics just don’t work for me. The 3D first-person perspective, combined with the lack of proper management tools, makes building a factory feel more frustrating than fun. While some may enjoy its aesthetic and trading system, I can't recommend it to fans of factory-building games, especially when there are so many better options available.

This is an incredibly interesting game, but also one that I'll say from the start that is niche. You are, for better or worse, a radar operator overseeing the entirety of Britain's air defense in a time of dramatic world tension. The game does this with quite an incredible degree of realism, and its sights are set on total immersion. Do not expect to jump into air raids immediately, you'll be instead calling up ATCs about the sixteenth helicopter flying without a flight plan.

So how does it do?

[h1] What Works [/h1]
- Immersion and tone is done incredibly well. This was enough to keep me invested from the start to end. If you enjoy aviation, the cold war, or military science at all this positive alone is done well enough to outweigh the downsides and justify a buy.

- Voice acting is superb. You'll be listening through radio static and brevity codes, which can be disabled if you pick easier difficulties.

- The game feels very very authentic, for better or worse. (see the next comment)

- The more you look into systems the more you can master them. This is a very rewarding feeling, especially when things become complicated. The developer seems to have absolutely no problem with slightly overwhelming the player, which in my view is a positive. Don't expect to master things on a first playthrough.

- This developer isn't scared to let things play out slowly. Tension ramps up at a snail's pace, giving you time to get into a regular rhythm which is shocked once certain events play out. This adds to the previously mentioned sense of immersion, and while it makes the game even nicher, I'd say that this is a positive if you're a part of the clearly targeted audience.

[h1] What Doesn't [/h1]
- This is a slightly longer list, unfortunately.

- The game has a long list of bugs that I encountered within my playthroughs. Sometimes it'd be my console seizing up when I attempted to call an ATC via cutting my text box off entirely, other times voicelines would be on an endless looping repeat. The words "Gunfighter, RTB" are stained in my memory. Sometimes voicelines wouldn't be called out at all on actions (see two points from now as to why that's so important)

- In the later portions of the "combat ready" scenario (the largest and only real one for EA so far) my FPS would drop to near 5 frames. This was without enabling any of the more taxing graphics options warned about in youtube tutorial videos, and while my system well surpasses recommended specs. This, combined with the mounting list of bugs that refused to go away, caused me to end both playthroughs.

- Controls are unintuitive and at time frustrating. You rely heavily on your comms to understand what is going on, and if what you've clicked has actually done anything. A lot of systems are poorly explained and take time to act upon player input, with the prime example being interceptors taking off in response to suspected or confirmed hostiles. This becomes a massive problem if your comms stop working, which isn't uncommon, and in fact happened in just about every playthrough. Sometimes your inputs or actions that are supposed to be automatic would do nothing at all, which means you have to wait to see if they did, then act upon the lack of action minutes later. When you do these manual corrections, it's very clear that it's not the intended route.

- Tankers. Tankers are a bitch. They're much much slower than fighters and at the beginning of the "combat ready" scenario are only stationed in one airfield in central England. This becomes a problem quickly, since flying out interceptors towards the most likely threats (I won't say where) often leaves fighters without enough fuel to come home. You have to really pre-station these things along where you need them. This is doubly a bitch when you start a scenario out with threats already on radar, which means you don't have enough time to get tankers out there. All of this is workable until your interceptors start spamming your console with requests for refuel, and in general it seems that interceptors have poor logic regarding refueling. Your F3s will attempt to fly intercepts while at 17% fuel and will occasionally not automatically refuel successfully when at that state either.

- There is an option to restation aircraft. Don't click this. Airfields have a preset amount of fuel and ammo, but most (if not all) come with none aside from those who already have aircraft at the start. If you station fighters or tankers at another airfield, you'll ground them for life since they won't have enough fuel to take off again.

- Active airfields are not given highlights versus inactive airfields, of which there are a lot of. This means you'll be largely having to memorize their locations in order to make sure you aren't sending an aircraft across the entire country, or painstakingly scan every airfield name from time to time.

[h1] So what? [/h1]
Despite the glaring downsides, Air Defender is a game with incredible potential. Most of what I say comes down to fine tuning controls and stomping out bugs, which if done would make this game one of a kind. At its price tag though, I can't justify buying it outside of those who REALLY like the immersion factor. The late game, for me, was unplayable past a certain point, and I can't recommend this game because of it. 

You're paying $26.26 for a game whose content you can't fully experience, even if you really really want to.

1. Penalties for un-recognized enemies in the IFR despite not being anywhere near the border of the IFR yet. I thought this was a funny jab considering the penalty would be applied for assets over Ireland cause the game developer is British.

2. QRA automatically attempt to conduct a mid air refuling even in the middle of identifying/interrogating a track. They will refuel even at above 80% fuel. Does not seem it is done based on reserved fuel but if its even done one or not. This is a huge problem because they will drop engagement with a track to prioritize mid air refueling regardless of actual fuel status

3. Mid air refueling is a nightmare to get up and going and almost requires immediate tasking upon starting. since there is a whole segment of IDing a track, show of presence, show of force, and final decision, the jets will not last as long. So you need to have a tanker up, which is fine, problem is that they will NOT go back to the original tasking and will just RTB instead. This is because the tasking gets replaced with the order to RTB since they need to base to get fuel anyways.

4. The UI is abysmal. The hot key for ASMA does not automatically put the cursor to the text box input and neither does doing the help menu hot key. Which means you need to click the text box each time. which is a pain in the ass considering that you have to search for the ATC codes most of the time unless you remember who to call in the ASMA for your track. You would think that operators had hotkeys for this.

5. Game mode is busted and there is no sense of objectives or progression AT ALL. Which on top of the bugs, you will end up thinking if the game glitched out and you are stuck with no tracks at all. Side objectives show up like a dark souls death scene and then disappear with no real indication of what todo. ASMA does not tell you either, only when you fail funny enough.

6. Odd spread of assets. Perhaps this is done by design. but why the hell do i need to move tankers/jets to places that have no assets at all. I have to move them, then i can use them. Except it takes for fucking ever to move them that its pointless. but then they are too far away to properly react to threats without either being late, or running out of fuel.

Don't get me wrong, the game has the potential to be great. Even in this broken state i was able to get some fun out of it. However, while this game is suppose to be hard by design, its only hard because of the lack of design.
This game is too early, even for early access. Just wait, seems like the dev is active. Only time will tell. I hope to see more.

The words "Gunfighter, RTB" are stained in my memory. Sometimes voicelines wouldn't be called out at all on actions (see two points from now as to why that's so important)

- In the later portions of the "combat ready" scenario (the largest and only real one for EA so far) my FPS would drop to near 5 frames. This was without enabling any of the more taxing graphics options warned about in youtube tutorial videos, and while my system well surpasses recommended specs. This, combined with the mounting list of bugs that refused to go away, caused me to end both playthroughs.

- Controls are unintuitive and at time frustrating. You rely heavily on your comms to understand what is going on, and if what you've clicked has actually done anything. A lot of systems are poorly explained and take time to act upon player input, with the prime example being interceptors taking off in response to suspected or confirmed hostiles. This becomes a massive problem if your comms stop working, which isn't uncommon, and in fact happened in just about every playthrough. Sometimes your inputs or actions that are supposed to be automatic would do nothing at all, which means you have to wait to see if they did, then act upon the lack of action minutes later. When you do these manual corrections, it's very clear that it's not the intended route.

- Tankers. Tankers are a bitch. They're much much slower than fighters and at the beginning of the "combat ready" scenario are only stationed in one airfield in central England. This becomes a problem quickly, since flying out interceptors towards the most likely threats (I won't say where) often leaves fighters without enough fuel to come home. You have to really pre-station these things along where you need them. This is doubly a bitch when you start a scenario out with threats already on radar, which means you don't have enough time to get tankers out there. All of this is workable until your interceptors start spamming your console with requests for refuel, and in general it seems that interceptors have poor logic regarding refueling. Your F3s will attempt to fly intercepts while at 17% fuel and will occasionally not automatically refuel successfully when at that state either.

- There is an option to restation aircraft. Don't click this. Airfields have a preset amount of fuel and ammo, but most (if not all) come with none aside from those who already have aircraft at the start. If you station fighters or tankers at another airfield, you'll ground them for life since they won't have enough fuel to take off again.

- Active airfields are not given highlights versus inactive airfields, of which there are a lot of. This means you'll be largely having to memorize their locations in order to make sure you aren't sending an aircraft across the entire country, or painstakingly scan every airfield name from time to time.

[h1] So what? [/h1]
Despite the glaring downsides, Air Defender is a game with incredible potential. Most of what I say comes down to fine tuning controls and stomping out bugs, which if done would make this game one of a kind. At its price tag though, I can't justify buying it outside of those who REALLY like the immersion factor. The late game, for me, was unplayable past a certain point, and I can't recommend this game because of it. 

You're paying $26.26 for a game whose content you can't fully experience, even if you really really want to.

This is a game where all content is created by users, and working with this content is made absolutely awful!
Check it out:
Character cards are hosted on a Krafton service called Canvas. Mods are hosted on a third-party service. Two separate services for core functionality that should be unified, tthat should be controlled by a single party (product owner, Krafton) by default! Nothing prevented Krafton from hosting mods themselves, but okay-they decided to choose a third-party service, that's their business, whatever. But they chose the worst possible service-Curseforge.
1. It's an Israeli company.
2. The site has no comments, no collection support, no advanced search options. It doesn't even have tags like the ones on Canvas. An awful, rudimentary site in 2026!
3. Curseforge forces users to integrate their Google/Discord/GitHub(why Github?!!)/Twitch accounts with it. You cannot create an account there using your email address. But that's not all! For everything to work, you also need to integrate your Steam account! As I understand it, they do this in order to profile users as much as possible for advertising purposes. In other words, Krafton has “sold” you to the Israelis, who are already recording all your actions (mod downloads, your game profiles, your account name) and selling you. I hope they only sell your data to moderators, and not to some "Mossad"!
But that's not all! The integration with Curseforge within the Inzoi game is absolute garbage!
1. It should be part of Canvas, but the devs moved it to a separate menu item with completely different design.
2. You can search for mods there, and it even works... But it doesn't work!!! The search results take two minutes to render! Not to load, to render all those tiny previews and small text!
3. If I click on the results too quickly and try to subscribe to everything at once (I have to do this because there are no Collections.), the game crashes! You have to slowly click on each mod you're interested in and slowly subscribe!
4. At the same time, I hear my processor fan revving up to 100% speed. In other words, clicking the subscribe button and then loading the mod somehow overloads my 16-core processor! How does it do that?! It could be done in the background, with low priority... Never mind. What matters is that Canvas doesn't do this for some reason; downloads from Canvas don't freeze the game and occur sequentially.
5. Okay. Fine. I subscribed to everything I wanted and restarted the game. After restarting, I go back to Mods, and... I'm asked to unsubscribe from all mods! Because the subscriptions weren't synced with Curseforge! And the game will ask me about it every time I go to Mods! This dialogue cannot be disabled! Because the synchronization of subscriptions is completely broken!

This is bullshit!

I really wanted to like inZOI. The visuals are impressive, the atmosphere is promising, and the idea of a more realistic life simulation sounds great on paper. Unfortunately, the game currently feels like it prioritizes realism over respecting the player’s time.

Even on the shortest setting, in-game days are very long, and basic activities like sleeping take several real-world minutes. There is no option to skip sleep or fast-forward entire inactive periods, which leads to a lot of waiting with very little interaction. Watching a character sleep is not engaging gameplay, no matter how realistic it is.

This pacing issue breaks immersion rather than enhancing it. Instead of feeling absorbed in a living world, I often felt impatient and disengaged. For a life sim, flow and time management are crucial, and right now inZOI struggles badly in that area.

There is clear potential here, and future updates could address these problems. But in its current state, the game feels more like a technical showcase than an enjoyable simulation. I will likely revisit it later, but for now it pushed me away rather than pulling me in.

I launched InZOI and played for a little over an hour. An hour during which I had time to explore, test the mechanics, observe how the world behaves… and, more importantly, listen to my PC slowly lose the will to live.

I started calmly. Character creation, moving around, first interactions. And there, a first genuinely positive surprise: the small everyday details are actually well thought out. My character, for example, doesn’t wash his plates in the bathroom like a complete sociopath. No. He washes them in the kitchen. Logical. Simple. Coherent. A small detail, but a big plus if you’re coming from The Sims.

The open world itself is honestly excellent. It’s one of the game’s strongest points. The city feels alive, movement is smooth, NPCs react properly. There’s a real sense of continuity and ambition. So much so that I started deliberately testing the system’s limits. At one point, entirely within the game obviously, I mugged a guy in a park. Result? I barely had time to run before a cop was already sprinting after me. Fast, reactive, unexpected. Genuinely funny. On that front, the world simulation works very well.

But while I’m laughing, my PC starts sweating.

So I do what any responsible player does when something feels wrong. I open the graphics settings. And I try everything. Absolutely everything. From the most flattering settings to the most humiliating ones. Shadows off. Effects disabled. Quality reduced to a point where even the game itself seems slightly embarrassed to exist. At that stage, we’re no longer talking about visual compromise. We’re talking about technical survival.

And yet… InZOI keeps hammering the hardware like there’s no tomorrow.

It’s not that the game stutters. It actually runs fairly smoothly. The real issue is the completely disproportionate hardware load. The fans start blowing like the PC is about to take off. Not a soft hum. Not a gentle breeze. The kind of noise that makes you instinctively check temperatures and wonder if you accidentally launched a stress test instead of a life simulator.

Quick clarification, just to avoid any misunderstanding. My setup is not weak, outdated, or borderline. I’m running an Intel Ultra 7-265F with 20 cores, 32 GB of DDR5 RAM, and an Nvidia GeForce RTX 5070. This is a modern, powerful machine, perfectly capable of handling extremely demanding games. The problem with InZOI is not performance. The problem is that the game pulls far too much from the hardware for what it actually shows on screen.

At that point, out of pure financial self preservation, I closed the game. Not out of anger. Not out of frustration. Simply because I have no desire to watch €2,500 worth of hardware slowly die in front of me. My PC is not a crash test dummy, nor a ritual sacrifice meant to explore the limits of a poorly optimized engine. I’d rather stop now than later explain to a repair technician that “everything was fine… until I launched a life simulator.”

And that’s where the real question begins.

This open world, as good as it is conceptually, is very likely the core of the problem. Everything is active. All the time. NPCs, AI, scripts, interactions, zones, off screen calculations. And of course, that eats resources. A lot of them. Too many. Where The Sims made a conscious decision years ago to use instanced areas in order to preserve stability and accessibility, InZOI seems to have gone all in on “everything open, always.”

And that decision backfires.

An open world in a life simulation sounds great on paper. In practice, it requires extremely careful optimization. And right now, InZOI clearly doesn’t have that yet. The result is a game that practically requires a NASA level PC to simulate everyday life, while, let’s be honest, probably 80 percent of players own perfectly normal computers. Not war machines. Not workstations. Just reasonable setups.

Where The Sims understood that being slightly less open but far more stable was the smarter approach, InZOI still seems to believe that bigger, more continuous, more ambitious automatically means better. Unfortunately, it doesn’t. By trying to do everything at once, the game ends up shooting itself in the foot on a technical level.

InZOI has real potential. The open world is excellent. Some interactions feel more believable than in its competitors. The everyday details are well designed. But right now, all of that is weighed down by disastrous optimization and completely disproportionate hardware demands.

For now, InZOI is a promising game that I prefer to observe from a safe distance. PC turned off. Fans silent. Graphics card still alive.

Because a life simulation, no matter how ambitious, is not supposed to make you feel like your computer is writing its last will while you’re doing the dishes.

I bought inZOI during early access for around $50, and honestly, it was not worth the price at all. This game feels extremely rushed and unfinished, especially for something that’s being marketed as a “next-gen life simulator” and a competitor to The Sims 4. I went into this expecting something like The Sims 4 but more realistic and immersive. That’s not what I got. Instead, the game feels empty, slow, and lacking basic life mechanics. There’s barely anything to actually *do*. You can’t even have proper family gameplay. No babies, no real children system, no generational play. That alone completely kills the life simulator aspect for me. They advertise animals like cats in thumbnails and promotional material, but they aren’t even implemented in the game. A lot of features that are shown in marketing either don’t exist yet or feel half-finished. It feels misleading. The pacing is another huge issue. Even on faster speeds, the game feels painfully slow. Everything takes forever. And unlike The Sims, where jobs are mostly automated, in inZOI you literally have to *do* the job. If you’re a worker, you’re clicking on plates, cleaning things manually, and doing repetitive tasks. I get that they’re going for realism, but this just isn’t fun. It feels like doing real chores in a game that’s supposed to be entertainment. There’s also a serious lack of freedom. The map is very limited, food options are limited, activities are limited, and there’s not much exploration. I don’t even think you can swim in lakes or properly interact with the environment, correct me if i'm wrong. It just feels like a pretty but empty world. Customization is another problem. The characters all look very similar, and it’s clearly designed with a very specific aesthetic in mind. Most characters look Asian, and you have to put in a lot of effort to make characters look different. There’s also no proper non-binary or gender-diverse options like in The Sims 4, which feels outdated for a modern life sim. Housing is confusing and overly expensive. Upgrading furniture costs way too much in-game, and building doesn’t feel intuitive. It’s realistic, sure, but realism doesn’t automatically mean fun. Storage-wise, the game is also huge. It's 40GB to download it, which is ridiculous for an early access game with barely any content. I constantly have to uninstall it just to make room for other games, and then reinstall it whenever I want to try it again.Overall, the biggest problem is that the game is just boring. There’s no personality, no chaos, no fun emergent gameplay. It feels more like a tech demo than a real game. It’s realistic in the worst way possible. You work, you clean, you exist, and that’s it.

Almost a year since release, the visible pace of development is glacial and the game can only give like 70% of the features of base Sims4. The game itself feels severely sanitized and corporate even though you can do a shallow, cleanroom version of crime. 

So far, it severely suffers from the same dollhouse syndrome as its competitor: Nothing ever happens unless it's you clicking, everyone but your zoi is merely an accessory to your life and they don't exist once they leave the loading range. 

As it exists, the only way to justify the $$ for this game is to see it as a sort of magical, decade long bet, but the direction already seems set on uninteresting dollhouse mechanics rather than a proper life-sim.

I debated for a long time whether to buy the game, and decided to try what was so loudly called “The Sims Killer.” Perhaps in some century, The Sims Killer is real: a more open world that doesn't overload the game; realism and sophistication in character creation mode; the ability to view the character's life from the “shoulder.” But the disappointment compared to this is much greater. And I understand that the game is in early access. But what are they asking for such a sum of money for — the graphics? Oh well, let's skip that... 
What baffles me: 1. Endless bugs that ruin the gameplay—my characters can't get married because after the wedding, romantic interaction disappears COMPLETELY, resulting in the loss of relationships and even the inability to have children. 2. After only 25 hours of gameplay, my characters have done everything that was intended, and it feels like TOO much of a real life simulator, where characters live like people - sleep, eat, toilet, work, eat, sleep, eat, toilet, shower, work, eat, sleep. 3. Some animations make me cringe, such as swimming or running. God, why is it so awkward? 4. Characters do not know how to take food out of the refrigerator. To prevent food from spoiling, it must be stored in the refrigerator. And to eat, you have to take out the whole plate so that the character can put it on the edge of the table (why not in the center or next to the chair?), and only then manually select the “take a portion” option, otherwise the character wastes money and takes a quick snack from the refrigerator. 5. Characters who are compatible and have similar interests conflict with each other. My character has a constant desire to break up with her “husband” who cannot be a husband due to a glitch (point 1), because another character once flirted with her, who does not have a home in the city and is in a constant state of “at work,” making it impossible to call this character home, invite him to a meeting, visit him, or ask him out on a date. And my character refuses to have children with her “husband” or discuss the future (I wonder why?). 
6. What happened and who was offended that support for modifications was blocked?
There are still many problems, and I understand that this is early access, but too much has been said about this game without specifying that it will have the price of a full game and the rawness of early development. I hope that things will get better in the future because right now, the only thing I want is to go back to The Sims.

I know KRAFTON is the publisher and inZOI Studio is technically its own developer. I get that. But by association… 

I loved this game at first. Like, genuinely. I bought it practically the moment it hit early access because the idea alone had me sold. A Sims-style game that might actually give EA some competition? Fck yeah.

And because it was early access, I gave it a ton of slack. Bugs? Fine. Missing stuff? Expected. New studio, big ideas, a high demand, whatever. I was patient. I wanted it to succeed.

Fast forward a few months and… I regret wasting the $40 I spent on this instead of literally anything else. Gas. Groceries. A strap-on. But whatever, that’s on me.

The more that’s come out about how much AI stuff is in this game, and how much more they want to add, the more it feels like the game exists to just test sh!t out for other future games, not just make a decent game for us to waste hours on. 

This no longer feels like “someone had a strong creative vision and wanted to make a better Sims.” It feels like it was a “let’s see how players react to AI in a life sim and how far we can take it.” Ease people into it. Get them comfortable. Then drop the “oh btw we’re AI-first now lol thx for your money babe”.

The AI stuff in the game didn’t really bother me. Early on it felt mostly optional. Like, cool, use AI for patterns or little creative things if you want. As a tool? Sure. Fine. Make it a little easier for those of us who didn't know how to do the modding sh!t for Sims because we either lacked the skill or were just too lazy to learn.

Once the whole “AI first” thing came out, the vibe of the game just started feeling… off. Not broken. Not unplayable. Just... kinda sh!tty. And then just... more crap about them came out.

Seeing the publishing company pour a stupid amount of money into AI infrastructure and then offer employees “voluntary resignation” (cuz, ya know, that makes it better) afterward was kind of the nail in the coffin for me. Like, okay... fck you. AI teams only? Cool. Get AI customers too. 

I’m not anti-AI. Like hell, use it as it's intended to be used as: a handy-dandy little tool.

I would've loved for this game to have excelled at the visionary plans it seemed to have had at the beginning. It looked pretty AF and would've been great.

I should've bought the fcking strap-on instead.

This game kinda reminds me of 'DeepThink' when it first came out. We all thought for a brief moment, that DeepThink was going to completely outdo ChatGPT. But as you use DeepThink, you realize it's got some DeepIssues. You even start to realize that it's not a superior knock off at all. You realize this DeepThink thing got some serious flaws the longer you use it.

Same way I felt playing InZOI. I was at first blown away by what appeared to be a huge open lake of possibilities. But it turns out, this lake is less than a foot deep massive puddle. Little by little, the pieces of the game that appeared to be completely outdoing The Sims, came undone and exposed that it's just a weak shallow flavorless imitation.

The only pros I can give this game is that it does go into areas The Sims wouldn't and feels refreshing. "Why didn't The Sims do this?" like the court and legal system. But then as you play,  you realize it's really broken. For example, if another Zoi breaks the law around you, you'll be the one charged. If you eat the 'No crimes reported' donut, you'll still be charged for crimes and have a court date. I even got arrested in the middle of the donut perk still being active.

 Then you gotta go to court. Make sure you save your game before you go into court. Why? Well because the game will spawn a ton of Zois that sit in the key places the 'actors' need to sit, like the prosecutor and judge. Thus breaking the game and charging you 3500 for missing trial. The game blames you for the errors. 

The ZOi language is incredibly repetitive. They got their Zoi language but it feels like it's only 10 lines of dialogue recorded for it. Zoi speech is super annoying when it's the SAME THING over and over again. They won't stop saying "Jazzaling" at least every 3 minutes.

 The Zois autonomy is stupidly broken. My guy hasn't ate all day. Hasn't slept. What does my Zoi want to do? Give SOMEONE, ANYONE a massage. They only automatically cycle through a select few actions and it's random. Not dependent on their needs at all. Starving? Massage. Need a shower? Massage. The amount of times a day/per minute you have to stop your Zoi from wanting to give someone a massage, is practically it's own game itself. 

The experimental 'AI Mode' for your Zoi just makes your zoi cycle through random thoughts based on your prompt. Doesn't impact their autonomy or their actions. Just script to read and be like "Yeah, haha, I made him say that!"

This is a game where all content is created by users, and working with this content is made absolutely awful!
Check it out:
Character cards are hosted on a Krafton service called Canvas. Mods are hosted on a third-party service. Two separate services for core functionality that should be unified, tthat should be controlled by a single party (product owner, Krafton) by default! Nothing prevented Krafton from hosting mods themselves, but okay-they decided to choose a third-party service, that's their business, whatever. But they chose the worst possible service-Curseforge.
1. It's an Israeli company.
2. The site has no comments, no collection support, no advanced search options. It doesn't even have tags like the ones on Canvas. An awful, rudimentary site in 2026!
3. Curseforge forces users to integrate their Google/Discord/GitHub(why Github?!!)/Twitch accounts with it. You cannot create an account there using your email address. But that's not all! For everything to work, you also need to integrate your Steam account! As I understand it, they do this in order to profile users as much as possible for advertising purposes. In other words, Krafton has “sold” you to the Israelis, who are already recording all your actions (mod downloads, your game profiles, your account name) and selling you. I hope they only sell your data to moderators, and not to some "Mossad"!
But that's not all! The integration with Curseforge within the Inzoi game is absolute garbage!
1. It should be part of Canvas, but the devs moved it to a separate menu item with completely different design.
2. You can search for mods there, and it even works... But it doesn't work!!! The search results take two minutes to render! Not to load, to render all those tiny previews and small text!
3. If I click on the results too quickly and try to subscribe to everything at once (I have to do this because there are no Collections.), the game crashes! You have to slowly click on each mod you're interested in and slowly subscribe!
4. At the same time, I hear my processor fan revving up to 100% speed. In other words, clicking the subscribe button and then loading the mod somehow overloads my 16-core processor! How does it do that?! It could be done in the background, with low priority... Never mind. What matters is that Canvas doesn't do this for some reason; downloads from Canvas don't freeze the game and occur sequentially.
5. Okay. Fine. I subscribed to everything I wanted and restarted the game. After restarting, I go back to Mods, and... I'm asked to unsubscribe from all mods! Because the subscriptions weren't synced with Curseforge! And the game will ask me about it every time I go to Mods! This dialogue cannot be disabled! Because the synchronization of subscriptions is completely broken!

This is bullshit!

inZOI is... difficult to review, and even more difficult to categorise. But there is a point to my rambling so please bear with me.

For 25+ years the life simulation genre has been dominated by a single entity: The Sims. The original Sims game released by Maxis in 2000 arguably created the genre. It was a risky venture at a time when studios doubted the market for a game without a pre-defined story or objectives.

This was followed by The Sims 2 in 2004, which brought a rotatable camera and 3D world and then The Sims 3 in 2009 which introduced a groundbreaking (if technically demanding) open world. However, The Sims 4's launch in 2014 was notoriously bad. Missing core features like toddlers, pools, and lot rotation among many others lead EA to give away The Sims 2 Ultimate Collection (upon Sims 2 base game installation code verification) as a distraction from the backlash.

Now I am aware that by consistently comparing inZOI to The Sims I risk sounding like a broken record. Yet, the comparison is necessary because The Sims 1-3 didn't just dominate the genre; they WERE the genre for at least 2x generations of players.
______
However, it is crucial to state this plainly: inZOI IS NOT A DIRECT COMPETITOR TO THE SIMS.
______

The Sims franchise for all it's quirks and flaws (including Sims 4's its indoor rain) has always been targeted at a broad audience. This is a series that for commercial purposes often prioritised accessibility over technical ambition. The Sims 3 was a 32-bit game, capping its adjustable ram at 8 GB even as 64-bit systems were becoming standard, solely to ensure it could run on lower-end systems. The philosophy is simple, if The Sims is the only game you play, you'll invest all your gaming budget into its ecosystem.

This brings us to the first and most significant divider: System requirements

inZOI's minimum PC requirement is a NVIDIA RTX 2060 with 6 GB VRAM and 12 GB system RAM. Its recommended specs is an RTX 3070 with 16 GB system RAM. With a 2060 retailing for around $288 and a 3070 Ti easily exceeding $600 (before the rest of the PC components), the financial barrier to entry is immense. The average Sims player simply cannot afford to play inZOI, which automatically distinguishes it from being a true competitor.

So, what is inZOI in its current state? Right now, it functions primarily as a an "Instagram game". It is a stunningly beautiful sandbox for posing your Zoi at a plethora of of scenic, pre-set locations. The goal is a staged, "perfect" realism, and in this, the game is unarguably successful. Its visual presentation is nothing short of breathtaking.

However, this pursuit of realism exposes the current shortcomings in its simulation:
* Poor Path-finding: NPC's and your Zoi frequently struggle with navigation, often walking over objects unless you take manual control.
* Shallow Autonomy: Autonomous interactions are underdeveloped. A Zoi might say one sentence to an NPC before abruptly wandering off to greet someone else.
* Repetitive Skill Building: Improving skills feels like a shore. For example, reading requires finding pre-spawned books in book cases and involves a fixed, timed action rather than a continuous activity.
* Cumbersome Travel: Autonomous vehicle driving is incredibly slow, book-ended by loading screens (though manual driving avoids this). Vehicles and NPC's also have a tendency to pop into existence directly in front of the player.
____________

InZOI is a visually striking game whose extraordinarily realistic graphics are a double-edged sword. This forces players to scrutinise the depth of its life simulation more critically. Every aspect; from your Zoi's behaviour to their interactions with neighbours, is held to a higher, more demanding standard. Unlike the stylised, "whimsical" worlds of The Sims and other cosy games, inZOI's hyper-realism offers no hiding place for its current mechanical imperfections. Its ambition is its greatest strength, but also the source of its most intense scrutiny.
