Felt inclined to write a review because the game feels like a cash grab and nothing else. 
What I have seen, nothing much, gameplay wise, has been done in the game since it was "released" and second of all it has used gen AI despite them telling us their tool only accesses their own data base.

I really wanted to like inZOI. The visuals are impressive, the atmosphere is promising, and the idea of a more realistic life simulation sounds great on paper. Unfortunately, the game currently feels like it prioritizes realism over respecting the player’s time.

Even on the shortest setting, in-game days are very long, and basic activities like sleeping take several real-world minutes. There is no option to skip sleep or fast-forward entire inactive periods, which leads to a lot of waiting with very little interaction. Watching a character sleep is not engaging gameplay, no matter how realistic it is.

This pacing issue breaks immersion rather than enhancing it. Instead of feeling absorbed in a living world, I often felt impatient and disengaged. For a life sim, flow and time management are crucial, and right now inZOI struggles badly in that area.

There is clear potential here, and future updates could address these problems. But in its current state, the game feels more like a technical showcase than an enjoyable simulation. I will likely revisit it later, but for now it pushed me away rather than pulling me in.

[h2]General Impressions[/h2]
A [b]life simulation[/b] game where, in my observation, the story unfolds very stiffly - and only for your own character.
You can give your Sim all possible traits, but they barely affect anything. In the end, you create a nice-looking shell.
[b]Will this be improved after EA? Unlikely.[/b]

[h2]Customization[/h2]
Pleasant but [b]limited[/b]. Body sliders barely change anything, and it's not always clear what each one does - pull the wrong way and you end up with a Frankenstein.
Skills seem diverse, but in practice offer little to do. It feels like they created the "meat" of the system but forgot to add any "flavor".

[h2]Daily Life and Character Behavior[/h2]
Cooking exists, but you can't prepare multiple servings (or I just didn't find how). After cooking, the character [b]automatically starts eating[/b], and you can't disable it.
Conversations between characters are mostly fake variety. There are many dialogue options, but they all mean the same thing: "Talk, talk, talk."
Some compare this game to The Sims, but the only real similarity is the [b]pushy behavior of other characters[/b], who constantly interrupt you and break your task queue.

[h2]Canvas and User Content[/h2]
The Canvas platform works, and yes, there's content there.
But [b]there are almost no filters[/b], and finding something specific is frustrating. Everything just drowns in chaos.

[h2]Bugs and Optimization[/h2]
Plenty of small (and not-so-small) bugs.
The interaction system works poorly when objects overlap. Click on a plate, and the game decides you meant the table. Menus disappear if you move the cursor a little too far.

And of course, [b]everyone's always hungry[/b]. Your Sim eats, and an hour later they're starving again. Half the city walks around thinking about food.

[h2]Conclusion[/h2]
inZOI [b]could have been a great life simulator[/b], but right now it's an empty shell filled with meaningless mechanics.
There's more and more content, but [b]no real depth[/b].
Optimization is poor, and honestly, it's hard to believe it will ever be fixed.

I launched InZOI and played for a little over an hour. An hour during which I had time to explore, test the mechanics, observe how the world behaves… and, more importantly, listen to my PC slowly lose the will to live.

I started calmly. Character creation, moving around, first interactions. And there, a first genuinely positive surprise: the small everyday details are actually well thought out. My character, for example, doesn’t wash his plates in the bathroom like a complete sociopath. No. He washes them in the kitchen. Logical. Simple. Coherent. A small detail, but a big plus if you’re coming from The Sims.

The open world itself is honestly excellent. It’s one of the game’s strongest points. The city feels alive, movement is smooth, NPCs react properly. There’s a real sense of continuity and ambition. So much so that I started deliberately testing the system’s limits. At one point, entirely within the game obviously, I mugged a guy in a park. Result? I barely had time to run before a cop was already sprinting after me. Fast, reactive, unexpected. Genuinely funny. On that front, the world simulation works very well.

But while I’m laughing, my PC starts sweating.

So I do what any responsible player does when something feels wrong. I open the graphics settings. And I try everything. Absolutely everything. From the most flattering settings to the most humiliating ones. Shadows off. Effects disabled. Quality reduced to a point where even the game itself seems slightly embarrassed to exist. At that stage, we’re no longer talking about visual compromise. We’re talking about technical survival.

And yet… InZOI keeps hammering the hardware like there’s no tomorrow.

It’s not that the game stutters. It actually runs fairly smoothly. The real issue is the completely disproportionate hardware load. The fans start blowing like the PC is about to take off. Not a soft hum. Not a gentle breeze. The kind of noise that makes you instinctively check temperatures and wonder if you accidentally launched a stress test instead of a life simulator.

Quick clarification, just to avoid any misunderstanding. My setup is not weak, outdated, or borderline. I’m running an Intel Ultra 7-265F with 20 cores, 32 GB of DDR5 RAM, and an Nvidia GeForce RTX 5070. This is a modern, powerful machine, perfectly capable of handling extremely demanding games. The problem with InZOI is not performance. The problem is that the game pulls far too much from the hardware for what it actually shows on screen.

At that point, out of pure financial self preservation, I closed the game. Not out of anger. Not out of frustration. Simply because I have no desire to watch €2,500 worth of hardware slowly die in front of me. My PC is not a crash test dummy, nor a ritual sacrifice meant to explore the limits of a poorly optimized engine. I’d rather stop now than later explain to a repair technician that “everything was fine… until I launched a life simulator.”

And that’s where the real question begins.

This open world, as good as it is conceptually, is very likely the core of the problem. Everything is active. All the time. NPCs, AI, scripts, interactions, zones, off screen calculations. And of course, that eats resources. A lot of them. Too many. Where The Sims made a conscious decision years ago to use instanced areas in order to preserve stability and accessibility, InZOI seems to have gone all in on “everything open, always.”

And that decision backfires.

An open world in a life simulation sounds great on paper. In practice, it requires extremely careful optimization. And right now, InZOI clearly doesn’t have that yet. The result is a game that practically requires a NASA level PC to simulate everyday life, while, let’s be honest, probably 80 percent of players own perfectly normal computers. Not war machines. Not workstations. Just reasonable setups.

Where The Sims understood that being slightly less open but far more stable was the smarter approach, InZOI still seems to believe that bigger, more continuous, more ambitious automatically means better. Unfortunately, it doesn’t. By trying to do everything at once, the game ends up shooting itself in the foot on a technical level.

InZOI has real potential. The open world is excellent. Some interactions feel more believable than in its competitors. The everyday details are well designed. But right now, all of that is weighed down by disastrous optimization and completely disproportionate hardware demands.

For now, InZOI is a promising game that I prefer to observe from a safe distance. PC turned off. Fans silent. Graphics card still alive.

Because a life simulation, no matter how ambitious, is not supposed to make you feel like your computer is writing its last will while you’re doing the dishes.

This game is very pretty, but to be perfectly honest, I feel like people glaze it purely because of its looks. The characters are gorgeous and yet you're given barely any hairstyles/outfits to work with. The worlds are also incredibly gorgeous, but at the same time feel as though they were made in mind for AI to fill in the gaps. That being said, I have not done very much research about this game, but it does kind of have the vibe that AI is implemented in every aspect of the game. I really don't know any other way to explain it. I used to play Sims for hours at a time, but I typically find that after creating my Zoi and their home, I can only play the game for about an hour or two before I start really getting bored. 

Additionally, the autonomy in this game is so incredibly frustrating. I typically like to leave this on and see where the characters take themselves, but to an extent. I still like to be in control enough that the characters do what I tell them to when I click around and fill up their queue. InZoi makes this nearly impossible when autonomy is active and I found that most of the time, the Zoi's wouldn't even listen to me and I was forced to turn the autonomy off. 

I'm also really not a fan of the fact that your Zoi's can't partake in basic cooking when hungry? I found myself ordering out and loading up the fridge with these meals (which, btw, takes so so long - unless, again, there's something I'm missing. Having to individually place groceries/meals in the fridge is so incredibly annoying). I understand that once you start building their cooking skill, they CAN start cooking proper meals, but I feel like this takes so long and again, buying ingredients for these meals takes even longer, which just made me not want to partake in this particular function of the game.

Lastly, this is just kind of something minor, LMFAO! But it's seriously so annoying that you can't assign proper beds/bedrooms, because my mom&dad kept doing the devil's tango in their teenager's bed and it was just so.............. 🫥 Haha!! 

For anyone that can find joy in this game, I am very happy for you! Unfortunately, I could not seem to find or understand the love for this and I really wish I could sell this game to another user or claim a refund for it. Sadly, it just does not itch that itch for me. I'm hopeful that in time, as the game grows, I may come back to it with a different opinion. But this is like the 5th time I've tried picking up this game (twice after their recent island update) and thus far, it just is not any fun for me.

I bought inZOI during early access for around $50, and honestly, it was not worth the price at all. This game feels extremely rushed and unfinished, especially for something that’s being marketed as a “next-gen life simulator” and a competitor to The Sims 4. I went into this expecting something like The Sims 4 but more realistic and immersive. That’s not what I got. Instead, the game feels empty, slow, and lacking basic life mechanics. There’s barely anything to actually *do*. You can’t even have proper family gameplay. No babies, no real children system, no generational play. That alone completely kills the life simulator aspect for me. They advertise animals like cats in thumbnails and promotional material, but they aren’t even implemented in the game. A lot of features that are shown in marketing either don’t exist yet or feel half-finished. It feels misleading. The pacing is another huge issue. Even on faster speeds, the game feels painfully slow. Everything takes forever. And unlike The Sims, where jobs are mostly automated, in inZOI you literally have to *do* the job. If you’re a worker, you’re clicking on plates, cleaning things manually, and doing repetitive tasks. I get that they’re going for realism, but this just isn’t fun. It feels like doing real chores in a game that’s supposed to be entertainment. There’s also a serious lack of freedom. The map is very limited, food options are limited, activities are limited, and there’s not much exploration. I don’t even think you can swim in lakes or properly interact with the environment, correct me if i'm wrong. It just feels like a pretty but empty world. Customization is another problem. The characters all look very similar, and it’s clearly designed with a very specific aesthetic in mind. Most characters look Asian, and you have to put in a lot of effort to make characters look different. There’s also no proper non-binary or gender-diverse options like in The Sims 4, which feels outdated for a modern life sim. Housing is confusing and overly expensive. Upgrading furniture costs way too much in-game, and building doesn’t feel intuitive. It’s realistic, sure, but realism doesn’t automatically mean fun. Storage-wise, the game is also huge. It's 40GB to download it, which is ridiculous for an early access game with barely any content. I constantly have to uninstall it just to make room for other games, and then reinstall it whenever I want to try it again.Overall, the biggest problem is that the game is just boring. There’s no personality, no chaos, no fun emergent gameplay. It feels more like a tech demo than a real game. It’s realistic in the worst way possible. You work, you clean, you exist, and that’s it.

When I first heard of InZoi, I got so excited. As a long-time Sims player, I love seeing devs come up with their own versions of life-sims and I'm always interested in seeing their approach on these types of games. 

With the Sims, you get to build an entire virtual life for yourself, a character you have an attachment to, etc. I thought InZoi would take that and turn it into something more realistic, and it did to some extent, but sadly it was not what I expected or hoped for.

InZoi looks wonderful on the surface and the character creation is honestly the best I've seen in these types of games. It's so detailed and so beautiful in every sense! However, unless you're someone who thoroughly enjoys the visual appeal over actual gameplay and the game being immersive to the point where you can get a bit lost in it, the game loses it's substance very, very quickly. To me, the game lacks emotion and the zois feel a bit robotic... Time moves a lot slower, giving the game that more realistic feel but it doesn't feel like a life sim to me. It feels more like a massive sandbox test for people to create models and watch them move around for experimental purposes. The world is gorgeous, the architecture is incredible, the environment and every other visual aspect in this game is stunning beyond belief, but I can't bring myself to spend more than the hours I already have on this game. 

If you're someone who loves to create, build, and experiment with mods, then this game is probably right up your alley. If you're someone like me who prefers to feel like you can immerse yourself fully and get lost in your imagination through life sims, then I'd say try a different game OR if you're caught by the graphics and visuals, maybe wait until it goes on sale to give it a try. Who knows, maybe you'll have a better experience than me.

This isn't a jab at Krafton or anything btw. I love their creative approach and the way they are constantly communicating with their players to improve the game, but it's just not for me. At least not right now. Even with the recent AI announcement, I still hope for great things in the future.

On the surface it looks like the killer app for Sims 4, a departure to the tried and true method. Underneath its a tech demo more than anything. 

With Krafton moving to being AI-first I wasn't really surprised given the game's MINOR implementation of it. I was willing to look past it for the (at the time) only promising competitor to Sims. However now not only can I not recommend this title for supporting their slop machine, but I also have my doubts that from anyone complaints will be addressed. The game will likely continue to be developed with the sterile appearance it has today.

AWFUL. Spend more time waiting for the game to load ANYTHING. I've tried to love this game so many times now after being a lifelong fan of the Sims. Inzoi is pure embarrassing. The application is constantly freezing and glitching, making it painful to even try to play. A** game.

Prior to the last patch, my game ran fine with super quick to load in and everything. Since the last patch it crashes upon launching the game. I have a gaming laptop, please fix this. It doesn't even go to the menu or anything just a black screen with no sound, no mouse nothing. I have to restart my laptop. All of my other games installed run fine. This is not OK.

Long way to go for Inzoi to be able to compete with a properly modded Sims 4 (Yes you need many mods to fix the mess that Sims 4 is and deepen the gameplay because what EA releases is half-assed junk). Maybe in a few years it will be good and offer the same gameplay variety, but right now it's a complete snooze after you get over the excitement and intrigue of playing a new release.

Some cool features in the game like Canvas, 3d printer and instantly being able to upload a photo to a frame or wall. I see potential with Inzoi, but how many times has that been said of Early Access releases? The question is if they can actually deliver a game you can sink hundreds if not thousands of hours into without getting bored, because I already am after 20 hours. The game lacks a soul maybe because of the heavy use of AI and right now doesn't seem to have a clear direction, but is just a random set of features.

The problem for Inzoi is this: If I want to mine rocks and ore i'll play Conan Exiles. If I want to be a criminal/outlaw i'll play GTA Online, RDR2 or Cyberpunk. If I want to have a laugh and play a life sim with gameplay variety i'll play a modded Sims 4. They gotta figure out what kind of game they want to be. 

I'm also pissed the game randomly decided to cull my main Zoi instead of the premades and completely reset the business I was running with that character like it never existed. They seriously need to look into that and fix that shit, because it shouldn't be possible. They need a "My Households" feature like in Sims 4. 

Maybe wait a few years and see where the game is at before you spend any money. Good thing they don't seem to as greedy as EA at the moment so they have that working for them.

I debated for a long time whether to buy the game, and decided to try what was so loudly called “The Sims Killer.” Perhaps in some century, The Sims Killer is real: a more open world that doesn't overload the game; realism and sophistication in character creation mode; the ability to view the character's life from the “shoulder.” But the disappointment compared to this is much greater. And I understand that the game is in early access. But what are they asking for such a sum of money for — the graphics? Oh well, let's skip that... 
What baffles me: 1. Endless bugs that ruin the gameplay—my characters can't get married because after the wedding, romantic interaction disappears COMPLETELY, resulting in the loss of relationships and even the inability to have children. 2. After only 25 hours of gameplay, my characters have done everything that was intended, and it feels like TOO much of a real life simulator, where characters live like people - sleep, eat, toilet, work, eat, sleep, eat, toilet, shower, work, eat, sleep. 3. Some animations make me cringe, such as swimming or running. God, why is it so awkward? 4. Characters do not know how to take food out of the refrigerator. To prevent food from spoiling, it must be stored in the refrigerator. And to eat, you have to take out the whole plate so that the character can put it on the edge of the table (why not in the center or next to the chair?), and only then manually select the “take a portion” option, otherwise the character wastes money and takes a quick snack from the refrigerator. 5. Characters who are compatible and have similar interests conflict with each other. My character has a constant desire to break up with her “husband” who cannot be a husband due to a glitch (point 1), because another character once flirted with her, who does not have a home in the city and is in a constant state of “at work,” making it impossible to call this character home, invite him to a meeting, visit him, or ask him out on a date. And my character refuses to have children with her “husband” or discuss the future (I wonder why?). 
6. What happened and who was offended that support for modifications was blocked?
There are still many problems, and I understand that this is early access, but too much has been said about this game without specifying that it will have the price of a full game and the rawness of early development. I hope that things will get better in the future because right now, the only thing I want is to go back to The Sims.

Originally bought the game for these features as listed in the description:

Elo-Boosting Challenges: Earn experience points and level up your game as you defeat AI opponents. Tackle Easy (+1), Medium (+3), Hard (+5), and Expert (+7) levels to become the ultimate chess champion.

Timed Games: Craving a speedy match? Set the clock and test your quick-thinking skills with timer-based games.

The Elo-Boosting Challenges don't exist. There's no option in the menu, and nothing shown during games either.

Timed games feature is also oversold. Clock / timer setting is not available - it just a countdown clock showing up during games.
Board editor doesn't work. No increments available for time control.

The idea is great. It's the same Phasmaphobia that many people love, but with a ghost player. It sounds cool, but what I see in the game is terrible. Starting with the menu, where the interface often freezes with no way to "fix" it without restarting the entire game. In the game itself, the characters' appearance raises many questions, and the physics of their movements are simply terrible. Interacting with objects and using items to figure out clues is simply terrible. The only good thing I can say is that the items work well; I haven't noticed any issues with them when they're already active.
I have very big questions about the "Camera." Why do I see different levels of illumination in the room when standing in the same place? I'm literally standing in the center of the room with the flashlight on, and one corner is bright and clear, while the other is so dark that it's completely unclear how I'm supposed to see anything.
At the moment, the game is very rough and unfinished. I don't like giving bad reviews to promising projects, but there's little that's good about this one. We're waiting for the developers to polish the game to a "playable" state.

Played a few rounds with my friend and I believe this game has such potential HOWEVER I feel a few mechanics need changing for it to get there. For starters the game is advertised as 1-5 players. I am currently in a lobby by myself but where the start button would be, it's telling me "need 2+ players". So it's not for solo players. Secondly (spoilers ahead) forcing a player to be the ghost is a really cool mechanic, I love that idea, but the player ghost is not the ghost investigator players are looking for.....which makes me think "what's the point then?". I think it would be really cool for the ghost player be the one investigator players are trying to find and determine. The game already tells the ghost player what the ghost room is and what evidence investigator players are looking for, but there's nothing currently a ghost player can do with that information. I think it'd be insanely cool to allow ghost players to be able to interact with the equipment needed for the evidence types but maybe lock it so that they can only interact with it within the ghost room, using the provided energy and cool down mechanics already implemented. Can't say I recommend this game as it is, but if the above mechanics were implemented, this would probably be my new favorite game.

The idea is great. It's the same Phasmaphobia that many people love, but with a ghost player. It sounds cool, but what I see in the game is terrible. Starting with the menu, where the interface often freezes with no way to "fix" it without restarting the entire game. In the game itself, the characters' appearance raises many questions, and the physics of their movements are simply terrible. Interacting with objects and using items to figure out clues is simply terrible. The only good thing I can say is that the items work well; I haven't noticed any issues with them when they're already active.
I have very big questions about the "Camera." Why do I see different levels of illumination in the room when standing in the same place? I'm literally standing in the center of the room with the flashlight on, and one corner is bright and clear, while the other is so dark that it's completely unclear how I'm supposed to see anything.
At the moment, the game is very rough and unfinished. I don't like giving bad reviews to promising projects, but there's little that's good about this one. We're waiting for the developers to polish the game to a "playable" state.

Played a few rounds with my friend and I believe this game has such potential HOWEVER I feel a few mechanics need changing for it to get there. For starters the game is advertised as 1-5 players. I am currently in a lobby by myself but where the start button would be, it's telling me "need 2+ players". So it's not for solo players. Secondly (spoilers ahead) forcing a player to be the ghost is a really cool mechanic, I love that idea, but the player ghost is not the ghost investigator players are looking for.....which makes me think "what's the point then?". I think it would be really cool for the ghost player be the one investigator players are trying to find and determine. The game already tells the ghost player what the ghost room is and what evidence investigator players are looking for, but there's nothing currently a ghost player can do with that information. I think it'd be insanely cool to allow ghost players to be able to interact with the equipment needed for the evidence types but maybe lock it so that they can only interact with it within the ghost room, using the provided energy and cool down mechanics already implemented. Can't say I recommend this game as it is, but if the above mechanics were implemented, this would probably be my new favorite game.

[quote][url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AI_slop][b]Warning: AI Slop[/b][/url]
This game is partially or completely made with talentless, soulless AI generated content.[/quote]

"Alchemy Unbound" is a godawful mostly-text ripoff of another game called InfiniteCraft, and that in turn is a ripoff of an old iPhone game called "Element" (also ripped off by Doodle God and other crapware like it), except this one has a difference... it's all AI Slop.  Disgusting.

What do you do in the game?  Drag different things together and then watch a badly trained LLM turn it into something else for you.  Yeah, it's AI Slop, and AI Slop has no place in gaming.

As mentioned, this features really godawful, "wrong" looking AI slop instead of genuinely and sincerely created game assets.  It's hard to say if this was because the developer didn't have the skill to do the job of creating real game assets or couldn't afford to hire someone who does, but it also doesn't matter.  What matters is that this looks bad because of that decision.  AI slop sticks out like a sore thumb... and it comes across as insulting.

There's also the old adage that those who lack the budget and talent to make their own game assets usually lack the budget and talent to do the rest of the job of game development properly, too, and that's glaringly obvious here.

This is only free in a rudimentary sense... the developer has their hand out for a $10 USD "donation" for this as you're expected to buy some extra DLC from Steam... digital panhandling.  Like the homeless guys who splash dirty water on your windscreen at the traffic lights.  Yeah, the developer here wants YOU to pay HIM for someone else's AI Slop.  No thanks.  

Even though this is "free", it failed to appeal to the 120 million+ gamers on Steam.  SteamDB showed the game peaked at just a dozen or so concurrent players, and now there's basically nobody playing it.  Even though it's free.  This is a dismal public reception, but it's easy to see why gamers rejected it.

Bearing in mind that free games don't add +1 to your game collector count, should you bother adding this to your library and downloading it?

Nope.  This isn't worth downloading.  While this didn't cost me any money up front, I still feel ripped off.

I've played this for 71 hours? Heck no.
I don't know why this ran in the background for days without my knowledge, but I sure do find it suspicious. A fun little 30 minute game, but I don't trust this not to be spyware.
Do not download.

There are two ways to play [i]Alchemy Unbound[/i]. One is with the knowledge that [i]InfiniteCraft[/i] exists, and the other is without the knowledge that [i]InfiniteCraft[/i] exists.

If you are aware of [i]InfiniteCraft[/i], you will be found wanting: [i]Alchemy Unbound[/i] is rigid in its crafting, and lacks a lot of the "aha!" moments that [i]InfiniteCraft[/i] has. This is an Early Access version, which they cite as the reason for the lack of content.

If you are not aware that [i]InfiniteCraft[/i] exists, you are still met with a weird grind of a game that requires very specific answers to generate what you want to make.

The implementation of AI to generate results is stupid: it will literally just mash words together into nonsensical gibberish. I've only used it in order to get the AI-related achievements, beyond that it has served zero enjoyment.

Some of the achievements are also pointlessly grindy, especially with how rigid the game is in its Early Access state.

I am a little interested to see what the final product is, an [i]InfiniteCraft[/i] knock-off but on Steam feels appealing, but as the game is right now I cannot recommend it. 

3/10. The AI is pointless, the achievements are a grind, and overall lacks a lot of the discovery fun that the game it is mimicking offers.

Makes my computer howl when I sort by all results. Not gonna touch this until optimizations are implemented. 

Was fun while it lasted.

Too boring without any restrictions as there is no any real thought behind those elements and their recipes so not particular reason to think much about how to make a new one. Anything you throw together will produce anything. Can't recommend in the state it is now

This is essentially a knock off version of InfiniteCraft. The major difference is that this game will force itself to create new objects and words when you put things together, regardless of how much sense it makes. The game does work but it gets to a point where the things it is creating don't make any sense and it would be hard to know how to combine them with other nonsensical things to create objects and words. I don't normally like to compare games so directly, but at least in InfiniteCraft you can make things that you're familiar with. When it gets whacky it still makes sense to a degree. This thing just combines syllables until Germans blush with how long and ridiculous these "words" get.

The game might be functional but I don't think it's worth the disc space to even get it a look.

While some people may enjoy the fact that you can combine just about anything and the AI will come up with a name for this 'new' combination, others will not enjoy it or not enjoy it ALL of the time. There are merits to either style of play - infinite creations or limited combinations but you should be able to make your own choice as to which style you WANT to play.

When I TRIED to turn off the AI in the settings, it didn't stick. Any time I tried a combination that was outside the pre-programmed combinations the AI naming would automatically switch back on.

I do see the potential in this game but will not be playing again unless there is some form of user control on whether or not to use the AI to accept and name out of script combinations.

[i]Warchief plays like a poorly designed Custom Map, failing at being both a MOBA and an RTS. The game struggles at getting the fundamentals of both genres correct and ultimately feels bad to play.[/i]

[h1]The Good[/h1]
[list]
[*] Unique concept
[*] Customisable decks
[*] Large variety of units[/list]

[h1]The Bad[/h1]
[list]
[*] A single, uninspiring map
[*] No (apparent) learning curve
[*] AI is is unenjoyable and unintelligent
[*] Season pass feels like an afterthought
[*] Large armies suffer from poor unit pathing
[*] Tutorials fail at explaining all the mechanics
[*] No campaign – Only vs AI or PvP (non-existent playerbase)
[*] No custom content – Dev tools or a map editor are key for survival
[*] Too expensive – Lack of content and competitors do it better, for less
[*] Lack of overall polish e.g. key bind errors by default, poor UI elements

Incredibly disgusting and exploitative AI slop software. It’s a disgrace that something like this is even allowed to be around here. I still have vomit in my throat from the few minutes I had to force myself through in order to write this review. Anyone who truly loves art or DJ culture gets a rusty, blunt dagger driven straight into the heart by this revolting product. Most AI slop games with a connection to music usually come from an EDM background, which is hardly surprising since that scene has the highest share of fake artists. Here we have yet another wannabe who calls himself a DJ and has probably never carried a record crate in his life, nor performed a live mashup or demanding beatmatching without the sync button, let alone understands anything about scratching or beat juggling. The text lines in the early access section alone already show what kind of person this most likely is. The three most frequently used words are: I, AI and DLC.

"In the name of beauty we embrace the AI revolution!"
This is actually written on the shop page and all I can say in response is: in the name of common sense, some people really should take fewer drugs and seriously consider therapy. But hey, if someone is into this kind of nonsense and has no problem exploiting art and real artists, they should go ahead and download this thing right away and also buy all the DLCs worth around 210 bucks. Anyone who fails to recognize the blatant rip-off and exploitation the way I do deserves nothing else and should just be happy with their AI-slop garbage.

If I need visuals for DJ shows, I do it the same way it has been done for decades and I do not care if someone mistakenly calls that outdated or technophobic. What matters to me is that it is genuinely artistically appealing and fair. That is why I either create things myself or commission real artists whose craft I respect and actively support. Who would even want visuals where CDJs do not look like real CDJs and the DJ has six fingers? But instead, this software simply claims: “For DJs it’s the best VJ software to quickly get going!” And anyone who prefers lousy AI-generated GIF loops from some random nobody will of course fall for a stupid slogan like that. Let's make one thing clear, Fake it till you make it actually means studying and already using software like Resolume, Modul8, MadMapper or QLab, while not openly advertising the fact that you still have gaps in your knowledge. Anyone using what is offered here is nothing less than a full-blooded faker and fraud, and deserves not the slightest bit of respect.

Once again at least half of the positive reviews come from the so-called dev's circle of friends, which he even admitted himself. And if I were to go into the technical problems and the sync issues as well, I could easily spend hours more sitting here writing this review instead of playing a good game or producing a song without any AI junkware involved. Stay away from this unethical sludge. Support real artists.

[h1]RIP Playstation Home.[/h1]
This app for phones/tablets used to be a really cool semi decent replacement to another better game called "PlayStation Home" back in the day.. after that game on the ps3 was shut down. Avakin was made by a company that created a lot of cool things in PSH which were very popular and it was pretty nice as a social sim game for its time. Although never nearly as good as the greater whole it spawned from that had a much higher budget and bigger set of teams collaborating. So for that it deserves some forgiveness  as well as some praise as it was the best clone for its time. However in modern times it sadly does not currently hold up very well on its own anymore. Even if its cool to FINALLY see it come to pc. Which is something that should have happened years ago. If it had been prioritized much sooner I think the game would be in a much better place currently than it is right now.
[h2]The Problems...[/h2]
[list]
 [*]The monetization is absolutely horrid. Items cost too many coins compared to the daily cap earn-able.
 [*]The graphics, textures, monetization methods, Pc Ui, animations, and controls all need major overhauls. 
 [*]Optimization isn't very good on mobile devices anymore without overheating issues.(not a Pc issue that i noticed.)

[h1]RIP Playstation Home.[/h1]
This app for phones/tablets used to be a really cool semi decent replacement to another better game called "PlayStation Home" back in the day.. after that game on the ps3 was shut down. Avakin was made by a company that created a lot of cool things in PSH which were very popular and it was pretty nice as a social sim game for its time. Although never nearly as good as the greater whole it spawned from that had a much higher budget and bigger set of teams collaborating. So for that it deserves some forgiveness  as well as some praise as it was the best clone for its time. However in modern times it sadly does not currently hold up very well on its own anymore. Even if its cool to FINALLY see it come to pc. Which is something that should have happened years ago. If it had been prioritized much sooner I think the game would be in a much better place currently than it is right now.
[h2]The Problems...[/h2]
[list]
 [*]The monetization is absolutely horrid. Items cost too many coins compared to the daily cap earn-able.
 [*]The graphics, textures, monetization methods, Pc Ui, animations, and controls all need major overhauls. 
 [*]Optimization isn't very good on mobile devices anymore without overheating issues.(not a Pc issue that i noticed.)
 [*]Lack of a sound setting bar that lower volumes (all we have is a on/off button not a slider.)
 [*]The chat censorship is too heavy in both private and public lobbies. (This started a few years ago but only got worse.) 
 [*]The Pc version is sadly a very lazy port where everything is over sized on the screen and there is no walk-able controls such as aswd apart from 3 worlds but even those feel badly clunky to move and also you move extremely slow when doing that. Compared to almost any other modern social sim game.
[/list]
I still again think this game was good as a alternative after losing Psh for when it was first made. Although it would have been better if it had a Pc/Console release at the time. However in modern times compared to other social sims it doesn't hold a candle to the alternatives maybe in time it can become better again but laziness to change for the better, outdated systems, and greed has plagued this game into a really rough spot that it will have a hard time getting out of. I hope the dev's the best as its clear they always meant well and needed to make ends meet to keep the servers running through nonstop cosmetic items but this game genuinely makes me wish Psh was carried to newer gens rather than shut down. because we don't have many good current alternatives besides Vr Chat, Rec Room, or Avakin Life. I will give this game some time to fix itself abit as its early access then come back to see if it ever got better on here but currently its just not in a very good place and has not been for a few years now. Hopefully that changes.. :)

This game feels genuinely unplayable. 

The bots take MINUTES to think up a response and when they're done it takes even longer for them to just type it out in the text box for you to read. The bots feel flat and after waiting 4 minutes for it to generate something, it's answers don't even make sense in the story or in general. This especially becomes a problem when a conversation begins with a bot talking to you. Just to start a scene you have to wait for the bot to generate something just for it to either not generate anything or be unreadable. Not to mention they forget literally ANYTHING you say even though you've just said it. It's like a baby learning to talk, you don't understand what they're saying and they probably don't either.

The game has astronomical lag as well, it crashed like 5 times just trying to boot up. I can't even play a video in the background while it's running because the game lags my computer so hard that nothing will load. I don't know what computers everyone else in the reviews have but running this game is torture. I've played more intense games on my computer all at the same time with little to no lag, so a simple story game like this should not be giving me problems - ai or not. 

I think the idea is really interesting, but the game needs to be less laggy and the bots need faster and more coherent responses alongside more personality. I haven't played the full game thus far - and I don't plan to - so take this with a grain a salt. However, in it's current state it'd be better off as a scripted visual novel, simply because the bots ruin the flow of the story/game, and I cannot in good faith recommend this game to anyone.

Yikes. I saw the new “updated AI system” and figured I’d give Millennium Whisper another try. The first time I played it, it was fine, just not quite what it promised. But somehow the “updated” AI is genuinely worse. Most of the character dialogue is nonsense sentences that don’t make sense, responses that contradict themselves, or the AI repeatedly acting like it’s the first time you’ve ever met. And when it’s not confused, it talks like a generic virtual assistant. I could get more personality out of Alexa. 

I’m honestly curious what model it’s running, because this is awful. The devs claim they trained the system themselves on their own creative work, which… might be the root of the problem. There’s just no way they’ve produced enough material to train something supposed to be capable of playing various characters. I hate that this is getting worse, because the idea has so much potential. It's just not working.

At this point the game feels gimmicky and not immersive.

Night Club Simulator has a promising concept, but its execution falls short. The game lacks distinctive features, and, despite being in early access, it needs significant improvements to justify its $15 price tag. When taking orders, you run the risk of losing track of who ordered what, as all the customers look identical; the character models are the same. Without any tools to help you remember each person's order, you are likely to serve the wrong drink or mix to the wrong customer. For cleaning, it would have been helpful if the trash and urine were consistently highlighted when you selected the broom or trash can. Currently, you have to search for every single stain, whether it’s dirt, blood on the floor, or plastic cups discarded on the ground. Additionally, I find it both amusing and indicative of a significant oversight in attention to detail that when you choose to play as a female character in fight mode, your character's arms appear as the male model's arms, complete with hair and other features. Terrible, terrible, terrible. A huge no for me, and I absolutely do not recommend getting this game, at least not right now. Its current state is completely outrageous.

--no longer valid see below ...  I have played this a fair bit and I like where it Could go.  I've been playing the bannerlords adventure, and I have been enjoying how things are unfolding.  the AI seems to keep a REASONABLE grip on the story thread, though I have noticed a few instances where it makes changes that are either a retcon or a complete skip of details.

I'll likely keep playing occassionally with the 50/day free stuff, but I can't justify a $15 subscription. -- see below..


*EDIT* on further playing, I have decide I can no longer recommend this game.  The AI is entirely to unstable on story elements.  I have started multiple new games, and within an hour it is already making ridiculous errors, like having NPC conversate from across the map, moving NPC randomly to new locations, Giving NPCs control of areas they have no business being (Greta owns all the Taverns), Killing NPCs without any direction to do so, and soldiers dissappearing from the roster for now reason.  
It almost seems like it has a problem updating it's data file.

it's an idea that has to be perfected by someone at some point, but this isn't it. The free/cheap AI models are not good enough yet it seems.

The game doesn't create extra value as an LLM wrapper. The ai behind this can't keep up a conversation over 5 requests or a combat with more that one enemy.
The rolls system just don't let you do anything, all difficulties are 15-18 range. So you have about 10-25% to complete any action. It's adding anti-user RNG where it doesnt belong

So the game locks up after you use it for awhile (I think 40 prompts allowed?) then you can buy "credits" or pay 30 bucks a month for unlimited access. I already was using Microsofts copilot to run a walking dead dnd campaign for just me and thats 100% free, can do all the same features (and in alot of cases better). I think this was a very cool concept but the insane cost for use when theres better and free ai already out there makes me have to say nah to this.

Unfortunately, this game was extremely frustrating and disappointing.

The AI behavior is completely inconsistent and often breaks the game. Characters say random or incorrect things, some NPCs don’t respond at all, and others literally confess to crimes they did not commit. Looks like a cloned idea of Yi-Xian : The Cultivation Card Game.

Almost everything (Characters and side-jobs) is locked behind premium currency. Well.. you basically CAN get the other characters, but the time investment for a mere 2-day trial is ridiculous.
As for Yi-Xian : the game initially was a paid game, now a free-to-play - but the only thing you can buy with premium currency are cosmetics. (And you can still unlock 1 or 2 costumes of your choice through playing) Characters and side-jobs are unlocked through ingame-currency at a not-so unrewarding rate and that game also offers animations. And also.. this game is not even nearly as enjoyable as Yi-Xian.

I am kind of on the fence and would give it a 'not bad, not great' if i could but as it is I am a little bit bemused; most games like this keep the gameplay mostly free and you can spend real money on cosmetics and non-ptw stuff if you want to show support or really like a certain cosmetic (or spend money on 'tickets' or 'gold' and outright buy stuff straight away without grinding)..but this game does the complete opposite as far as i can see and you can't actually buy any new characters without spending real money...f2p players are stuck with the basic characters and are locked out of gameplay (since the characters have different decks and abilities)...but the cosmetics CAN be purchased with f2p money...lol whut? Someone seems to have gotten confused while working out how to monetise this game i think, you need the f2p players to PLAY so the whales have someone to smash and feel good about themselves for getting all that stuff that the f2p players are still grinding for...but if there is no way of actually getting it why would f2p players play? I’ve been looking forward to this game since the first trailer. WIII and SCII are some of my all-time favorite games, so I was happy to support the developers on their Kickstarter (my first time ever). I spent some time in the Beta and Early Access, and I liked what I saw, though it was clear the game still needed work.

I waited for the full campaign to unlock so I could dive in as a singleplayer gamer. About a year later, I returned… only to be disappointed that the campaign isn’t fully available for me, and I’d basically have to buy the game again. I don’t really understand this – I already backed the game for 60€, and beyond early Beta access and the first three missions, I’ve got almost nothing.

I played the first three missions for about an hour and a half. The graphics are solid and appealing. RTS is my favorite genre, and Stormgate truly feels like a new contender in this space. Aside from a few minor glitches, the first missions were enjoyable.

That said, I’m not willing to pay for additional missions. Objectively, as a demo, I’d give it 7/10 – too short to rate higher. I could recommend it to someone willing to pay just once. Subjectively, 4/10, because I probably won’t return to the game again and I feel disappointed.

Long story short, 
This game is boring. 

Apparently the devs tried to make an E-sport game instead of one that is entertaining. 

I am done waiting for this game to ever be good. 
This game made me want to play StarCraft2 again. Because this game is so bad, it shows you how good SC2 in comparison is.

I think if they continue to update this game that within the next 10-15 years it will be the best rts on the market. However I give them about 8 months before they scrap it and are never heard from again.

I think a more comprehensive review is required for why no one likes this game:

-
Slow input reaction: There isn't a snap to command inputs needed for this to be a E-sports game as if the UE5 engine is just a bit too thinky about what it will make anything do.
-
Scaling Issues: much like the worst element of SC 1 and 2, tech scaling feels real shitty. It's both a zerg rush vs adrenaline glands first issue. A lot of units don't get teeth without upgrades, or if they get them, some are still why would you make these when x or y exist? others just make the winners win more. 
-
Unit and Rock vs Shotgun meta: back in ye olden times, it was understood why you had x type of unit. basic infantry to prevent engineer rushing, Rocket infantry against tanks and larger more expensive units/air, flamethrowers/building clearing for infantry anti infantry, dogs to kill spies and sneaky shit. You had basic vehicles for speed/scouting and infantry control, tanks for big damage, bigger tanks for killing tanks, and air to scalpel objectives and force people to build more defense than sit on top of a hill and laugh as you charge into obelisk range. This game just copies loosely SC2 without understanding the actual purpose of each unit and makes them functionally useless outside a very narrow band of them.
-
The Fat Elephant in the room: I don't think I need to explain that no one is going to pay 60$ for a game made with UE5 jankiness, poorly made aesthetics, preachiness, and Further DLC to unlock on top of that if the game is just a poorer, less polished attempt at SC2. Worse, it shits on the very basis of what SC was functionally doing to the RTS meta at the time.
-
Aesthetics: This has become a leading indicator of failure. Female protag that isn't hot. SD 1.5 looking splash art for all the dialogue with characters. Brown lady escaping world turned shit hole. Daddy issues. Tired Heaven and Hell conflict.  Cinematics that were considered good 10 years ago but no heart to make it appeal to a timeless story.  This is a phoned in legacy cash grab from people who are used to just collecting a paycheck for showing up. 

Final note: If you don't understand microeconomics and basic cost value analysis you don't belong in unit design much less a strategy game design.

I was very excited to play Stormgate's campaign, Ashes of Earth, but I was extremely disappointed. I wanted to love this game but I just could not. Since leaving early access and releasing Ashes of Earth (which I presume is one third of the overall campaign), one would expect a higher level of polish. Unfortunately, Stormgate's first campaign is janky and just not done very well at all. 

The art style and character designs are lacking, some missions play like they have not been thought out very well, and while I like the units for the Humans/Warhawks, they feel a little uninspired and haphazardly done, not to mention they feel like reskinned versions of Starcraft 2 Terran units. Kind of a letdown too to learn that the unit portraits in pre-missions were animated using AI. 

I truly wish the best for Frost Giant but I cannot in good conscience, recommend Ashes of Earth. I hope they revise and extend on the single player campaign.

As a lifelong RTS fan I was really excited for Stormgate. Unfortunately this led to me purchasing the early access of the game for a full $60. I played the couple of missions available to me had an okay time, but quickly forgot about it as I could tell the game needed a lot more work. 

Imagine my surprise when I decide to boot up the game months later only to find out that despite paying a full $60 dollars for the game during the early access period, I still don't have access to the campaign and I have to buy the missions micro transaction style? To add more insult to injury I find out that they shifted their monetization and now the game is free to play, so I paid 60 dollars to play an hour or two of very mediocre content, when I could have just waited a bit longer and had everything I do now but for free. 

This is the worst I've ever felt treated as a customer and honestly I'm surprised it's even legal. I encourage everyone to stay away from this company.

A Casual StarCraft 2

TLDR: 
If you're bad at SC2 or don't like how competitive it is but like the core gameplay loop, Stormgate might be for you. 

Stormgate can be a lot of fun, responsive and when it isn't fighting its own bugs or optimisation issues it feels really good to play. 
Unit control is snappy like StarCraft 2 but not as snappy, The visuals are clean and modern, The core gameplay loop has a lot of potential. But as someone who comes from StarCraft's higly competitive gameplay, Stormgate feels VERY VERY casual in almost every corner of its design. 

Where starcraft challenges you to juggle macro,micro,scouting, production and multitasking all at once, Stormgate actively removes many of those high-skill demands. The built-in AI/Bot can handle tasks like building depots,setting up production,and even remaxing yuour army for you. In a competitive RTS these are the skill expressive fundamentals your ability to mange them under pressure is what separate Bronze from GM. Oflloading these mechanics to automation turns stormgate into something closer to an assisted RTS rather then a competitive one, And promotes lazy play, Removing the ability to learn from mistakes. 

This casual design is clearer at the end game screen, Where APM/EPM is deliberately hidden, Stormgate downplays exacution speed, mechanical performance and player improvement curves. Rather then encourage you to push your limits. refine mechanics or climb(as SC2 does), Stormgate promotes slower,more casual style of play. 

Overall, Stormgate is enjoyable, smooth and very accessible but leans so heavily into accessibility that it sacrifices a competitive Identity. 
Fans of StarCraft 2 may find stormgate charming, But ultimately too casual.
This is a game that is desperately trying to be Star Craft, and failing miserably.

I think a more comprehensive review is required for why no one likes this game:

-
Slow input reaction: There isn't a snap to command inputs needed for this to be a E-sports game as if the UE5 engine is just a bit too thinky about what it will make anything do.
-
Scaling Issues: much like the worst element of SC 1 and 2, tech scaling feels real shitty. It's both a zerg rush vs adrenaline glands first issue. A lot of units don't get teeth without upgrades, or if they get them, some are still why would you make these when x or y exist? others just make the winners win more. 
-
Unit and Rock vs Shotgun meta: back in ye olden times, it was understood why you had x type of unit. basic infantry to prevent engineer rushing, Rocket infantry against tanks and larger more expensive units/air, flamethrowers/building clearing for infantry anti infantry, dogs to kill spies and sneaky shit. You had basic vehicles for speed/scouting and infantry control, tanks for big damage, bigger tanks for killing tanks, and air to scalpel objectives and force people to build more defense than sit on top of a hill and laugh as you charge into obelisk range. This game just copies loosely SC2 without understanding the actual purpose of each unit and makes them functionally useless outside a very narrow band of them.
-
The Fat Elephant in the room: I don't think I need to explain that no one is going to pay 60$ for a game made with UE5 jankiness, poorly made aesthetics, preachiness, and Further DLC to unlock on top of that if the game is just a poorer, less polished attempt at SC2. Worse, it shits on the very basis of what SC was functionally doing to the RTS meta at the time.
-
Aesthetics: This has become a leading indicator of failure. Female protag that isn't hot. SD 1.5 looking splash art for all the dialogue with characters. Brown lady escaping world turned shit hole. Daddy issues. Tired Heaven and Hell conflict.  Cinematics that were considered good 10 years ago but no heart to make it appeal to a timeless story.  This is a phoned in legacy cash grab from people who are used to just collecting a paycheck for showing up. 

Final note: If you don't understand microeconomics and basic cost value analysis you don't belong in unit design much less a strategy game design.

I’ve been looking forward to this game since the first trailer. A Casual StarCraft 2

TLDR: 
If you're bad at SC2 or don't like how competitive it is but like the core gameplay loop, Stormgate might be for you. 

Stormgate can be a lot of fun, responsive and when it isn't fighting its own bugs or optimisation issues it feels really good to play. 
Unit control is snappy like StarCraft 2 but not as snappy, The visuals are clean and modern, The core gameplay loop has a lot of potential. But as someone who comes from StarCraft's higly competitive gameplay, Stormgate feels VERY VERY casual in almost every corner of its design. 

Where starcraft challenges you to juggle macro,micro,scouting, production and multitasking all at once, Stormgate actively removes many of those high-skill demands. The built-in AI/Bot can handle tasks like building depots,setting up production,and even remaxing yuour army for you. In a competitive RTS these are the skill expressive fundamentals your ability to mange them under pressure is what separate Bronze from GM. Oflloading these mechanics to automation turns stormgate into something closer to an assisted RTS rather then a competitive one, And promotes lazy play, Removing the ability to learn from mistakes. 

This casual design is clearer at the end game screen, Where APM/EPM is deliberately hidden, Stormgate downplays exacution speed, mechanical performance and player improvement curves. Rather then encourage you to push your limits. refine mechanics or climb(as SC2 does), Stormgate promotes slower,more casual style of play. 

Overall, Stormgate is enjoyable, smooth and very accessible but leans so heavily into accessibility that it sacrifices a competitive Identity. 
Fans of StarCraft 2 may find stormgate charming, But ultimately too casual.
This is a game that is desperately trying to be Star Craft, and failing miserably.

I think a more comprehensive review is required for why no one likes this game:

-
Slow input reaction: There isn't a snap to command inputs needed for this to be a E-sports game as if the UE5 engine is just a bit too thinky about what it will make anything do.
-
Scaling Issues: much like the worst element of SC 1 and 2, tech scaling feels real shitty. It's both a zerg rush vs adrenaline glands first issue. A lot of units don't get teeth without upgrades, or if they get them, some are still why would you make these when x or y exist? others just make the winners win more. 
-
Unit and Rock vs Shotgun meta: back in ye olden times, it was understood why you had x type of unit. basic infantry to prevent engineer rushing, Rocket infantry against tanks and larger more expensive units/air, flamethrowers/building clearing for infantry anti infantry, dogs to kill spies and sneaky shit. You had basic vehicles for speed/scouting and infantry control, tanks for big damage, bigger tanks for killing tanks, and air to scalpel objectives and force people to build more defense than sit on top of a hill and laugh as you charge into obelisk range. This game just copies loosely SC2 without understanding the actual purpose of each unit and makes them functionally useless outside a very narrow band of them.
-
The Fat Elephant in the room: I don't think I need to explain that no one is going to pay 60$ for a game made with UE5 jankiness, poorly made aesthetics, preachiness, and Further DLC to unlock on top of that if the game is just a poorer, less polished attempt at SC2. Worse, it shits on the very basis of what SC was functionally doing to the RTS meta at the time.
-
Aesthetics: This has become a leading indicator of failure. Female protag that isn't hot. SD 1.5 looking splash art for all the dialogue with characters. Brown lady escaping world turned shit hole. Daddy issues. Tired Heaven and Hell conflict.  Cinematics that were considered good 10 years ago but no heart to make it appeal to a timeless story.  This is a phoned in legacy cash grab from people who are used to just collecting a paycheck for showing up. 

Final note: If you don't understand microeconomics and basic cost value analysis you don't belong in unit design much less a strategy game design.

Let me start off this review by saying I absolutely love the IP of Synduality. The show if good, and the world and concept is just awesome. Drifters going around fighting off Ender, collecting AO crystals to help power this post-apocalyptic world, forming teams of Drifters to compete against one another. Just all around a really cool idea and series. If you are coming to this game because of the series, you are gonna be disappointed, if you are coming to this game because you love mechs and extraction shooters, then you are going to have an okay time. This game could be way better.

Now let's talk about the game.. Early access was great a fun, we all thought oh these normal features other extraction shooters were gonna get added later, because they are staples to the genre, well we were all in for a surprise when the game finally launched...

This game could be amazing, it could be a contender for other popular extraction shooters, but sadly that is no the state this game has been released in. There is no team based match making, you can only select from a limited number of maps at a time, progression is very long and steep before you feel like you can be really impactful on each of your sorties, and the devs make the game feel like you need to invest a lot just to get to rare or better equipment.

Here is a basic understanding of the game, you are a Drifter that either works for the Association or the Black Market. Both have pretty much similar quest and progression, expect ones gets live ammo weapons and the other energy based weapons. Each drifter gets their own hanger which has various station to upgrade and customize like other extraction shooters, but for the most part only a handful actually do anything. Which is a bummer because some of the flavor text makes it sound like it improves your Magus companion, and other features of the game, but only the Pit and Crafting area really do anything for you. This game is good for giving you time and credit sinks. Once you get over the fact that you need to get large quantities of materials to even upgrade a single station, you then realize you need even more than that to actually craft your Cradles (Mechs) and weapons. Now I have been grinding MMOs and other grindy games for years, and even I find it an immense amount of effort to build something that I could easily lose the second I hope into a match, but that is also why we love extraction shooters, high risk high reward, however for this game it is not balanced at all. Combat is fun for the most part, they definitely could have taken some liberties from the series, but sadly it is just straight up gun play with some Magus having sorta useful abilities, but not as diverse as the series. The meat and potatoes of this game, is going on sorties to collect resources, not really focusing too much on AO crystals, because the spawn rate of crystals that actually make you money are slim, so your main goal is collect resources to upgrade your hanger and build your weapons and cradles. Kinda falls off from the point of the series this game is based on.


Honestly, I would love to leave this game a positive review, but morally I can't, even though I enjoy it, I know it isn't good at the end of the day. Instead I will list the features and quality of life changes I would love to see for me to ever change this.

1. Team based match making, let us go out on sorties in teams of two or three, simple and easy to do since they already have a team up function while on sorties.

2. Let us customize our Cradles, I would love if the cradles could be customized to change the colors and skins, instead of having 50 different versions of the Bowierabbit cradle, we could swap the skins (decaled versions of the cradles), the texture of the cradle (Metallics, Matte, and Worn), and the color schemes. Currently each rarity has a different color scheme, but in the series, Drifters customize their cradles. Hell even Arc Raiders does a better job at this, and there isn't as complex loadouts as this game. They already sell us a bunch of color swaps and reskins of clothing items for our Magus, they could easily do this with patterns and decals for our cradles, miss opportunity for keeping this game funded and alive.

3. More selectable maps and accessibility without heavy progression. They have added new maps, that are heavily locked behind progression, which progression is fine, but you shouldn't need to spend more than 50+ hours unlocking them. One of the maps has four different versions of it depending on how far you have progressed in the game. Most players are only going to see the North and South versions of the map. Meanwhile if you have invested a ton of time in the game, you will get to see the Desert and Forest areas. But only for limited times, once you unlock a new map, it should now have a limited time window to go onto. They need to follow what Tarkov and Arc Raiders does with their maps. Then depending on when you have time to play the game, you will still only ever get to play on the East Amasia map, and that is it.

4. Player Character to Customize. Simple give us our drifter, I don't want to be a faceless ghost that you never see. Let us represent ourselves, the characters in the series were so unique, but we don't exist in the game. Another miss opportunity ignored from the source material this game is based on. I would love to see my Magus and Drifter wandering around my base.

5. More in-depth combat. Give us the unique abilities the magi had from the series, give us more melee options like the series has for the cradles. Combat just needs an overhaul in general to be more fun and engaging.

6. Better PVE and PVP balance, would love to see Arc Raiders system for matchmaking in this game. Make AO crystals feel actually important like the series does, not just something I occasionally go collect for quests to progress to open up new maps.

7. Make the Hangers more meaningful. Actually implement what the flavor text of the different stations of the living area actually do what they say. There are quite a few that say they boost your magus's performance, or increase hanger space, but don't actually do that.

I am sure there is more stuff I would love to see in this game, but these are the big ones for me. Overall, this game is fun for me, but I know it is not a great game. I would love to see this game be an actual competitor for other extraction shooters, but I feel like the live service of this game will only be around for a short time, which is a shame. Hopefully one day I can change this review to recommended, but I feel like that will never happen.

I stayed because I believed in its world, its atmosphere, and its potential.

But with Season 2, Linux and Steam Deck players were effectively locked out by kernel-level Easy Anti-Cheat.
A platform that previously worked is now completely excluded.

What hurts is not just losing access —
it’s realizing that players who stayed when the game was struggling are the easiest ones to abandon.

I don’t feel angry.
I feel dismissed.

This isn’t about technical limitations.
It’s about choosing convenience over loyalty.

I genuinely hope the developers remember that small, dedicated communities are not expendable.

This game could have been good... But the devs decided to force PvP through quest/task/mission/whatever progression. The Story missions were a great idea poorly executed. The prime reason to play extraction shooters (looting and scooting with your homies) is totally missing, as the team opted for a solo drop in system where you are able to group up with other random players mid sortie, which might be ok if there wasn't 1:1 friendly fire damage and no voip communication.

If they want to save this game they'll make the PvP map(s) optional, open up to pre-made fireteams of up to 3 with a "fill group or find group" style option and focus on making good PvE content like the game reasonably showcases they are capable of doing.
This game is way to restrictive to be enjoyable. Every fun thing to do there is something else that ruins it.

SYNDUALITY for me is a mixed experience as I didn't know this was inspired of an anime... however I have been enjoying it so far~

At first I thought I was going to experience a story with a mix of looting and shooting with mechs, however the game focuses more on what I think are the basics of how to thrive/play the game:

- Mine for AO Crystals which gives you $ depending on the purity of the crystal...
- Explore different areas of the map for materials to upgrade your base...
- Hunt down Bandit leaders that spawn on the map for a bonus $...
- Team up in Co-Op with another player and tackle a random objective. [that is IF, you bump into someone in the field]

But despite that I think there is probably somethings that the game needs fleshing out more, like how the game starts opening up and become less restrictive when you complete requests and rank up your drifter license (as the map you start in at first is not the full scale of the zone so that rookie drifters could get familiar with the area and materials of the zone before removing the training wheels and letting you roam after recognizing familiar areas.) or how the rain mechanic is not only something to avoid but to take advantage since it increases AO Crystal purity and material drops from monsters...

Another thing that most drifters will get to experience will be the "Death Stranding Rain" and battery life as they're probably the most important thing that you'll be keeping an eye out while on the field aside from inventory space... PvP is a choice as there is an option to go rouge and hunt down anyone who is unfortunate to caught your line of sight, but starting out there isn't any PvP as you are basically under prepared and are getting your base and crafting materials to make runs and crafting more manageable for your runs...

What drifters will also get to experience too is their Magus talking a lot... aside from them reminding me of my party of Pawns in Dragons Dogma (if you know, you know... lol), you will be working together for the majority of the game as each Magus as their abilities to carry on tasks, combat or close calls, I thankfully started with a Weather Magus which granted me a Forecast on my map and a Shield to protect myself from the rain. Which is one of the best Magus to start out (imo) if you're trying to mine AO crystals as their detection range is better than the others. But is good that the game gives you a choice of customizing your Magus to whatever you deem acceptable whether it be an aggressive, defensive or supportive play style...

NOW... "Is the game any good?" I think it's okay, nothing massive like perhaps Arc Raider's or Tarkov however I think it's an option if you want to experience a mech extract shooter with PvE mechanics and a hint of PvP, but it's one of those games that I would recommend getting on sale if you are still interested. 

TLDR; a flawed extract shooter with a vibe similar to Death Stranding with a hint of Tarkov where you and your AI companion helps you repair your bunker, explore and complete jobs requests together to thrive on the Drifter business that can get repetitive or at times boring for people wanting more action and fast pace gameplay like Mecha Break or Armored Core rather than a casual and at times challenging experience...
Honestly the game is dead and it's sad i was hyped about the game but now it just on life support

Free to play ultragacha game for the price of a AAA title designed to waste your time and mess you up. Even the menus - scrolling is intentionally slow in some parts and as soon as you get close to the top or bottom, woosh, it jumps to the end. WTH?! Oh and you get aim sabotage for free. Try aiming at an enemy 50 meters away next to an object. Your crosshair starts shifting. 
And you can buy things for up to HOLY POOP is that expensive. A dollar per level/item in your pass and it goes up to 99. Sum it up. Why is nobody talking about that? 

PS: Did you know that your most expensive mechas break more easily and have really low repair limits? 
PPS: Servers suck in EU - they lag when you pick things up. 
PPPS: You buy a one time use key (per sortie) and the doors open a certain amount of times only. Funny that they place 4 enemies directly behind the door. I sincerely begin to admire the mind behind this. Never thought it possible to be....

So basically if you didn't grow up with mommy's high heels in your butt, this game is not for you.
The only other alternative: Play this game as Buddha training. Let's see wether I shall end up with a golden belly or go full otaku mental.

PPPPS: I hope some other studios from the far east will pick up on that idea and make it a co-op game in which you can simply choose which areas (pvp - pve - pvpve you enter). Could be what Borderlands promised to be. 
They left so much potential untouched and chose to make it the most greedy and "rear exit" game I've ever encountered. 
Feels really weird to see what two childhood hero companies have turned into. Loved Bandai for DBZ and Namco for the Ridge Racer series.

PPPPPS: If my name was Ronny Bepp, I would call my ex-wife an angel after playing this game. I wouldn't even be surprised if they used my pc for cryptomining while I'm in the menus - considering how hard the fans run even there.

Conclusion: Don't touch this. It is designed as a digital drug and it gets worse. They doubled prices for parts with the objective of slowing your progression down - as if the game wasn't unfair/poopy enough already. Found out that max XP gained per day is limited, missions are time gated - what the hell is this? A collection of everything that can be wrong with human beings?! 

Thought this game was cute / interesting - I like mechs and even though it sucked more and more, I kinda still hoped there would come a point where you get enough ressources, the game becomes kinda fair and you can just enjoy collecting stuff and building your base. Forget it... 

Check the forums if you need a red flag the size of our sun.
