Let me start off this review by saying I absolutely love the IP of Synduality. The show if good, and the world and concept is just awesome. Drifters going around fighting off Ender, collecting AO crystals to help power this post-apocalyptic world, forming teams of Drifters to compete against one another. Just all around a really cool idea and series. If you are coming to this game because of the series, you are gonna be disappointed, if you are coming to this game because you love mechs and extraction shooters, then you are going to have an okay time. This game could be way better.

Now let's talk about the game.. Early access was great a fun, we all thought oh these normal features other extraction shooters were gonna get added later, because they are staples to the genre, well we were all in for a surprise when the game finally launched...

This game could be amazing, it could be a contender for other popular extraction shooters, but sadly that is no the state this game has been released in. There is no team based match making, you can only select from a limited number of maps at a time, progression is very long and steep before you feel like you can be really impactful on each of your sorties, and the devs make the game feel like you need to invest a lot just to get to rare or better equipment.

Here is a basic understanding of the game, you are a Drifter that either works for the Association or the Black Market. Both have pretty much similar quest and progression, expect ones gets live ammo weapons and the other energy based weapons. Each drifter gets their own hanger which has various station to upgrade and customize like other extraction shooters, but for the most part only a handful actually do anything. Which is a bummer because some of the flavor text makes it sound like it improves your Magus companion, and other features of the game, but only the Pit and Crafting area really do anything for you. This game is good for giving you time and credit sinks. Once you get over the fact that you need to get large quantities of materials to even upgrade a single station, you then realize you need even more than that to actually craft your Cradles (Mechs) and weapons. Now I have been grinding MMOs and other grindy games for years, and even I find it an immense amount of effort to build something that I could easily lose the second I hope into a match, but that is also why we love extraction shooters, high risk high reward, however for this game it is not balanced at all. Combat is fun for the most part, they definitely could have taken some liberties from the series, but sadly it is just straight up gun play with some Magus having sorta useful abilities, but not as diverse as the series. The meat and potatoes of this game, is going on sorties to collect resources, not really focusing too much on AO crystals, because the spawn rate of crystals that actually make you money are slim, so your main goal is collect resources to upgrade your hanger and build your weapons and cradles. Kinda falls off from the point of the series this game is based on.


Honestly, I would love to leave this game a positive review, but morally I can't, even though I enjoy it, I know it isn't good at the end of the day. Instead I will list the features and quality of life changes I would love to see for me to ever change this.

1. Team based match making, let us go out on sorties in teams of two or three, simple and easy to do since they already have a team up function while on sorties.

2. Let us customize our Cradles, I would love if the cradles could be customized to change the colors and skins, instead of having 50 different versions of the Bowierabbit cradle, we could swap the skins (decaled versions of the cradles), the texture of the cradle (Metallics, Matte, and Worn), and the color schemes. Currently each rarity has a different color scheme, but in the series, Drifters customize their cradles. Hell even Arc Raiders does a better job at this, and there isn't as complex loadouts as this game. They already sell us a bunch of color swaps and reskins of clothing items for our Magus, they could easily do this with patterns and decals for our cradles, miss opportunity for keeping this game funded and alive.

3. More selectable maps and accessibility without heavy progression. They have added new maps, that are heavily locked behind progression, which progression is fine, but you shouldn't need to spend more than 50+ hours unlocking them. One of the maps has four different versions of it depending on how far you have progressed in the game. Most players are only going to see the North and South versions of the map. Meanwhile if you have invested a ton of time in the game, you will get to see the Desert and Forest areas. But only for limited times, once you unlock a new map, it should now have a limited time window to go onto. They need to follow what Tarkov and Arc Raiders does with their maps. Then depending on when you have time to play the game, you will still only ever get to play on the East Amasia map, and that is it.

4. Player Character to Customize. Simple give us our drifter, I don't want to be a faceless ghost that you never see. Let us represent ourselves, the characters in the series were so unique, but we don't exist in the game. Another miss opportunity ignored from the source material this game is based on. I would love to see my Magus and Drifter wandering around my base.

5. More in-depth combat. Give us the unique abilities the magi had from the series, give us more melee options like the series has for the cradles. Combat just needs an overhaul in general to be more fun and engaging.

6. Better PVE and PVP balance, would love to see Arc Raiders system for matchmaking in this game. Make AO crystals feel actually important like the series does, not just something I occasionally go collect for quests to progress to open up new maps.

7. Make the Hangers more meaningful. Actually implement what the flavor text of the different stations of the living area actually do what they say. There are quite a few that say they boost your magus's performance, or increase hanger space, but don't actually do that.

I am sure there is more stuff I would love to see in this game, but these are the big ones for me. Overall, this game is fun for me, but I know it is not a great game. I would love to see this game be an actual competitor for other extraction shooters, but I feel like the live service of this game will only be around for a short time, which is a shame. Hopefully one day I can change this review to recommended, but I feel like that will never happen.

Found out that max XP gained per day is limited, missions are time gated - what the hell is this? A collection of everything that can be wrong with human beings?!

The game is good, but not great as the overwhelmingly positive reviews suggest. In terms of gameplay and technical quality, it is lacking some important QoL improvements. As with every other politically themed game, the positive reviews mostly seem to come from approving the political message and praising the devs as a martyr for having their game banned in Germany and on Nintendo Switch (which for the record I do not approve of). It seems like that, because most of those reviews have under 2 hours of playtime, at which point you should barely have unlocked just a third of the meta-progression upgrades and have seen most of the game only if you have played it at a lowered difficulty.

Having played through to the end, I kinda get the problematic nature of the game, because it seems deliberately made to stir some drama by confronting you with its theme and having you interact with it in some way, rather than just making the theme stay on the background and keeping the interactions just optional. Even the visual style of the game seem to have been aimed towards an added shock value, all from the enemy character sprites to the aggressive neon colors used for the backgrounds.

As an example of what I mean, whenever there are NPC characters in the main game hub that can provide some in-game bonuses for the currency of negative social score, you first have to click through several boxes of political commentary/satire before they present you with a choice of buying their bonus or not. If you just quickly click through those, you may easily pick the option you do not want. In other such games like Hades, there is a separate button for the dialog with lore and the in-game benefit, so you can just skip it entirely, but here you cannot.

In another instance, you get presented with some highly charged statements attributed to the "woke" culture (such as whether there were black people in certain historical settings or not, or just plainly that being white is bad) and in order to progress within the game, you must state whether you agree with them or not, while the latter case labels your response as AI fact-checked disinformation and allows you to progress with the fight. It is as if you find some clickbait post on Twitter and instead of just reading it and continuing to the rest of the internet, you first have to be an active participant in it, must respond in a specific pre-determined way, and only then you can go on. I hope we can agree that it is not really cool to present political themes this way.

I guess the bottom line is that while many of the people would play this game and excitedly call it BASED, I call BAIT and consider the controversy around it as nothing but a clever marketing tactic, which with the amount of positive reviews it got worked for the devs quite well. So I suggest you think twice about getting offended by how the game ended banned and maybe for a while consider that you might be being used.

"Roguelite" and "2D platformer" doesn't mesh well I realize. The concept is good but just that alone just doesn't make it worth it. I get the initial main point is the political nature of the game but like level design is kinda important  if it's a 2D platformer and there is just nothing meaningful at all since it's randomly generated and doesn't feel satisfying to move through. That's it, that's why I say not recommended.

[h1]Beware of unacceptable 3rd party EULA[/h1]

This is yet another GPT service based game.

Here you have 5 crime scenes with suspects that are robots represented by GPT.
Goal is to find clues and talk to robots in order to solve a mystery.
Every robot has predefined information and answer style, each time you talk to it game will send your prompt to GPT server to get an answer. 
That is it - a low effort modern game. Soon there will be tons of similar games because it is extremely cheap to produce.

Problem with this particular one (apart from being overpriced for such a low effort) is that they require you to agree with them doing whatever they want with every single piece of your personal data they'll manage to find. Including being able to sell it to anyone.

upd: after completing game I confirm all above and want to add some detaild:
- scenes are done good, details and interactiveness are good
- chat has only main vector of speech and you may accidentally force it to say you literally anything. Starting with false game-related data and ending with how to made an explosive device at home.
- chat may and will randomly spoil you parts of game. It also will randomly go on full denial of everything. That is more than just annoying.

The idea is great, but the execution is flawed to a point that made me quit after the second mission. The reason is multiple issues with the AI.

The robots completely loose context once you exit the conversation with them. When speaking to them again, you need to start from zero, getting them to confess to things they had already confessed and having to re-establish rapport you had already established. 

The AI often bugs out, asking you to repeat a prompt because of a "system error" or similar. This is fine once or twice, but it happens very frequently, to the point where it would sometimes happen several times in a row. I got tired of having to re-type my prompts over and over again.

Furthermore, the AI feels, well, very much like AI. If you've ever tried any of the common models out there, you will know what I mean. Quirky remarks that are not really appropriate to the situation, and even the use of emojis, which does not make sense. 

Another bug is that sometimes, the robots will simply repeat back to you exactly what you told them, literally copy-pasting your prompt. Sometimes, this even shows as a [System Overload].

The AI also seems prone to hallucinations or over-corrections when you speak with confidence. This is a huge problem, because if you try to use common police interrogation tactics, like presenting a theory as a fact (bluffing), the AI will sometimes agree and confess or confirm, despite this not actually being the case, leading to confusing and contradicting situations down the road.

You can also get the robots to agree to things that aren't supported with the game mechanics. For example, I managed to talk a guilty robot into sharing his financial gains from the crime with me in exchange for me protecting him and supporting his account of events - but of course, the game doesn't let me actually do it. I also got a robot to agree to perform software changes to another robot, and they even confirmed the changes had been done, but again, the game doesn't support such things.

There is more, but it can be summarized as "typical AI chatbot issues". I'm surprised so few other players seem to have taken issue with it. For me, it quickly made the game rather unenjoyable and I can't recommend it in its current state.

I really enjoyed the demo and the first case of this game, but unfortunately, the game is broken come case 3. One of the AI robots is completely unable to respond to any questions, and the other is mostly functional except when it comes to granting access to a key location needed to progress the case further. Unfortunately, I don't think this case is solveable based on what little I am allowed to access in this case.

I will try the remaining cases in the game to see if they are similarly broken. However, until there is a fix to restore functionality, I would not recommend purchasing this game.

ETA: Case 4 is fundamentally broken - just as the AI being unable to register the proper trigger(s) to unlock a location in case 3, the same occurs in case 4. I watched a playthrough from back when the AI was working to figure out how to sequence break to what I could find in case 3, but this was not feasible in case 4, so that one I truly could not complete at all and needed to watch a playthrough to understand.

Case 5, on the other hand, appears to still work.

Overall, you can get through cases 1, 2, and 5 pretty easily, but cases 3-4 are significantly impacted by the fact that their AI are no longer working properly. I would not advise purchasing the game in this state, and certainly not for full price.

[h1]The most absurd EULA I have ever read[/h1]
This game requires you to agree to an EULA and then to a "RULES OF CONDUCT" wall of text.

Basically, they require from the player ideological alignment and behavior for a SINGLE-PLAYER GAME.

This makes no sense. YES, I know that we "license" a game, it does not belong to us. But that is a whole different level. They put a leech on our necks and give themselves the right to look down on the player and revoke their license for whatever fake reason they come up with.

It is a SINGLE-PLAYER game. I should be able to interact with it in whatever way I want. We all know that in the power relationship between the developer and the player, the latter is much weaker, but this is beyond ridiculous. This is so disrespectful I don't even have words for it.

So after being outraged by these initial conditions, I learned that ReLU Games is famous for "sell for ad games". I should have researched better. What a huge disappointment.

I'm not putting up with that. Refunding ASAP.

The premise seemed promising, but the experience is just not fun. The AI's responses make no sense at all. At times it feels like a first time dungeon master trying to railroad you to a plot and then others it feels like a coworker who isn't listening to you but nodding and saying 'yeah' I would avoid and wish I could refund it.
Good attempt, but still not it

Cleanerbots gets their position reset when starting the game so their cleaning zones are not where you originally put them, they also get stuck on stairs and keeps spinning in one spot. 

If you have more than one billing station and more than one cashier they will spawn on the same billing station when loading into the game and wont move unless you tell them to.

Would not recommend until some of these bugs are fixed

It's a reasonably fun shop management game with a cool theme, but I can't go any further. Got to the point of hiring Restocker staff and they pretty much ruined the experience. Other games solved this already -- only stock items on shelves with labels. Instead, these lobotomites just put whatever on empty shelves BEFORE shelves with labels, leaving the store in complete random disarray, impossible to keep anything organized. You also, from what I can tell, cannot remove labels once a shelf has a label on it -- only replace them with a different item to change the label. It makes managing the store an impossibly tedious hassle. I'll try playing again if they fix this, but these are huge QoL issues for a game like this to still have after being this deep into development.

There's a good card game in here, but it's held back by a few issues for me.

Something about the "physicality" of the cards doesn't feel right. Most cards require two clicks to play. Once to select and once to target. You can occasionally just drag a card from hand to target, but not always. It never seems intuitive to me when I am allowed or not to do this. This sounds trivial, but it is a constant annoyance. When I play a well made card game, I almost forget about the interface, and am thinking and acting on my ideas almost directly. But here, I am constantly distracted by having to fight just a little with the UI.

There are on-hover visual effects on a lot of cards, but it often lags just a little bit. Not much, but just enough to make everything look sluggish and unresponsive. This is especially noticeable when an animation effect is in progress elsewhere on the screen.

Probably related to the above: the game is extremely resource intensive. While some of the 3D scenes used are nice, it doesn't seem to warrant just how much this game gets the fans on my CPU spinning like no other game. I seriously cannot understand how this game chokes up so much resource. At the end of the day, it's just a card game, it shouldn't be resource intensive at all. I should add, this was even though I was running the game on "low" settings, and even though my PC has great specs.

It's a card game with a lot of unlockables, but there is no where I can look up all cards. There are lots of keyword mechanics in this game, which are explained in popover tooltips when viewing a card, but no where I can just look up all status effects. I would expect any card game like this to have something like a "compendium" where I can see a list of keyword terms/mechanics, and a collection of all cards currently unlocked (and maybe a list of requirements to unlock additional cards).

There are random event nodes with some little bit of event text and a decision. I can't quite put my finger on it, but they always feel like a totally random coin flip for something maybe positive or something maybe negative. But there's no sense of stake in the world. It's almost never clear to me why the decision I made was correct or incorrect.

It's hard to explain, but the wording on card text always refers to your characters as "allies" and enemies as "enemies" _regardless of who's point of view it's coming from_. This is constantly confusing to me, because it is the opposite of how any other card game does it. To give an example, you might see an effect on an enemy. When you hover over it, it says "allies get +1 attack when this unit attacks". I will always read that as "the allies of the card with the effect", as opposed to what it actually means which is to always refer to the protagonists. This is a minor thing, but it constantly catches me out. Just another little thing that distracts me from enjoy what is probably a good card game.

Decent deckbuilder roguelike that suffers from poor balance.

The renegade can go infinite without any real effort, and only gets more broken as you unlock talents on her.

Can see some cool concepts with the modifiers for challenge runs are interesting, with pretty much all of them being double-edged swords that you can play around, but tbh you can just slap every modifier on and the game plays almost identically as it did when you first started. My first challenge run was one with every modifier [CR50] - and was over in the space of about 15 minutes.

The game is polished in most other aspects, and while I can't recommend this to others at the moment, I am keen to check back in in future and see what Robot Cat have done with the game.

This is really close to a recommend from me, but there are some UI/UX issues holding me back.

In combat, you have to manually hover over enemies to see where they are going to attack, or leave your cursor on a button to the side that shows all the attack lines. Here's the problem: why? Just give me an option to toggle on enemy intents permanently. It's a very clunky system that causes each turn to take several times as long as they should since I have to keep checking and re-checking enemy targets while I'm thinking about my turn, then review as I start playing cards to, for example, make sure defense goes on the right character.

Similarly, the lines between enemy and player target are the same color, with no differentiation, which makes complicated board states a pain to understand. I'm sure that this is part of why the enemy intents are not always shown, but why the show button isn't a toggle or setting is beyond me. Give the lines a border, or different colors, or make the lines between targets straight instead of this arc nonsense. Anything to make combat clearer.

Oh, also show how much damage characters would take from each enemy if the turn ended right then. Having to add up all the damage each time the board state changes. With the above-mentioned hidden targeting, armor values that decrease all incoming damage, and abilities that change targets, this becomes very annoying very quickly and honestly feels like a straight up mistake not to include.

The above are the worst offenders, but there's lots of little things where the developers sacrificed gameplay or clarity for aesthetic, like the card layouts and templating being harder to grok at a glance than they should.

Also worth noting that so far the game leans heavily on "all cards are class-generic" which I personally find to be a red flag for weak design, but I cannot say one way or the other at this point since the UI has really turned me off of the game. But if that's not something that bothers you in your card battlers, then no worries. They make up for it by giving characters (again, generic) equippable items to make the decks you build for each character each run distinct, which may just be enough to keep replayability high.

All in all, though, my complaints are not with the meat of the game, just the wrapper it's in. Honestly, at time of writing, it's $13 USD and on sale for under $12, which is pretty much perfect for the game's current state. If they clean up the interface, it's an easy recommend, but for now I'd see if the developer supports the game post-launch and updates some of the issues before giving it a firm recommend.

After sitting on it for a few days I've decided to bitterly change this review to negative. 
Here's the thing; the games balance is so out of wack I don't want to keep playing. Yes, I got my money's worth. But hearing the devs talk in the forums about making any change besides balancing the overpowered characters demonstrates a gross misunderstanding of what makes deckbuilders fun for people like me. 

TLDR one of the characters starting decks can solo the hardest difficulty without help from their allies. This is not acceptable design and should be hotfixed before any other changes. The fun of deckbuilders is building the deck. Being handed a solution from the getgo begs the question; why play? Answer, I stopped playing after unlocking that character. 

I will check back when updates come and will certainly update this review if I put more hours into the game. 


Bellow is my original positive review --
It was fun. If you like deck builders you’ll get your moneys worth.


The game doesn’t have the same replay ability as the best of the genre. It’s a bit too easy to go infinite and the hero balance is quite off. 

I wrote this review shortly after finishing the last “ascension” and I left it on since buying it. 


I do love the aesthetic and quite enjoy the few bits of lore (I’m also a sucker for cyberpunk). 

The first few times you pop off with Renegade or Rook is quite fun. Honestly, the general party chemistry of Renegade and Paladin felt unique in the growing sub genre of party based deckbuilders. 

So yeah, cool game.

About 24 hours of content. That’s probably worth 10$ isn’t it? About the price of a movie ticket. Plus, the devs are responsive and say they plan to address the balance so maaaaaaayhe it is the next great deckbuilder by the time you’re reading this.
The card combat is decent, but the UI/UX makes me want to drip hot candle wax into my weeping eyeballs.

Despite several tries couldn't get past the 3rd map bosses on the 1st level (meatsack) destroyed the first bosses, only to have another round called "benevolence" after surviving that there was a third round called "Shards" which killed that last member of the party. At no stage are you allowed to rebuild or rest, so the three party members get weaker and weaker until they finally get overwhelmed. No combination of perks gets you through this, and if this is intro level one which is unplayable, how are the higher levels supposed to work?

If this was early access, I'd say I'm excited to see the full release, it looks promising so far. But it's a full release, I've had to double check, I didn't believe it. The game has everything but instead of the ideas being pulled into a tightly knit gameplay, it boils like a goulash made out of rotten meat and you're sweating like a pig just inhaling that acursed boil. It is the most obvious case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde. Lemme explain:

Graphics
Love the cyberpunk idea. It is your standard CP - you have runners, mutants, wild AI, synthetics, corporations, megabuildings, it is classic CP, solid if formulaic, although very refreshing to see in a digital card game.
That's for aesthetics, however the graphic execution is all over the place. You have working animated bosses, yet each arena is a hodge-podge of basic shapes (alright buldings, but they look like Lego blocks drenched in Primalex painting). Operators have a clean and well-executed visual appereance, yet cards featuring them have a distincting anime aesthetic and you can clearly see that some cards were generated/created through different seed. As far as I know, there was AI generation involved, which is ok, but you can really see the similarities between card art here and myriads of generative manga on DeviantArt. That is not how you want to integrate AI into your work. Mythgard might be dead, but the game still has a kickass art that should be an inspiration for many more, especially because Mythgard melds multiple genres well (thanks to cohesive art).

Progression
That one is just off up to a point of "Start over with QA". Navigating districts feels dull and boss battles having more and more stages the further I go do not feel rewarding, it's just a slog and oftentimes I decided to put my team out of misery by resigning before they die waiting for the next stage. The idea of phases is really good, but do not overdo it. I'd also probably make each district map more intricate and longer with shorter boss battles. I understand player can extract early, but it feels unintuitive and... let me ask: Player basically has the option to skip a district. Do you really want to encourage them to skip whole parts of core gameplay?
Difficulty scaling is totally off. I have read in the reviews that for someone felt the game to be too easy. Well either developers patched the difficulty track or the player came upon some truly deranged combo, but I do not have patience for that. ADD hamster wants to go brrrrrm and dakka dakka right now. That being said some fights feel to easy or too hard and they usually go one after another. One difficulty over Meatsack is ten times harder, and you have like twelve more to go! Who tested this and said: Yep, mofos are gonna enjoy this bad boy. If you have so many difficulties you should ramp it up gradually.
Unlocks are hidden behind some challenges, but most of them require you to beat higher difficulties. That is not good design decision. Players should be able to grind out the stuff to beat the difficulty they are stuck on, while nifty player get their stuff earlier thanks to beating the higher difficulties without needing to unlock more stuff (that can eventually help) and getting more XP - that way you encourage both groups to continue playing.

Cards
Where is your g*dd*mn library of cards and augmentations? Somebody forgot to implement it or was it a deliberate decision? I want to be able to check all the stuff I have unlocked, all the pretty stuff to look at, I want to sit at the table and theorycraft all the decks I could muster. But I can't, because there's no comprehensive library.
Some keywords seem useless and some cards are totally unbalanced - either too good or d*mn expensive, further narrowing possible choices.
Starter decks feel good at first, because they are fully capable of carrying you into many many fights, but that is the problem - your starter deck in Slay the Spire ain't basically flavorless because reasons, but because it is a base for you to build your own deck at the top of during the run. I was using Renegade on punch and puncher harder OTKs the whole time when I have noticed you can deck her out to do AMMO damage, but why would you? Her basic deck and abilities railroad you to do WHAM OTKs with fists, that's it.

Gameplay
There are a few bugs, but honestly - nothing gamebreaking, that's good. I only have problem with how GUI works for the player, that is - the little f*cker secretly works with your enemies to undermine you. You cannot properly zoom back to game board when fabricating cards (Hearthstone understands that you want your "strategical overview" when deciding what card to discover/fabricate to best suit your needs). Pick cards out of overcrowded hand is clunky at best (it is quite easy to pick a wrong card since the cursor detects one over another). Loading times are bit too long, or I should say - animated sequences? They just feel like overstaying a bit, in a card game you want to kapow fast.
Failsafe (a carryover sideboard if you will) mechanic is neat, however it has one fatal flaw - you either collect cards for you future dream run or you just use the same cards all over again because they just compliment your working strategy. I understand the idea, but this was better done in Across the Obelisk - you can basically precraft your starting deck and play into your build by choosing the right path. In Zero Division there is nothing done beside failsafe, it is a weird mix of random and customized gameplay.

It sounds like I ditched the game, but I really like it, I would play it more, but not with how it plays now. There are just too many hiccups along the road. I really wish it was still early access, because that would make me hopeful for a good release.

The Zone 3 boss has five phases, the last of which requires you to defeat it in three turns or it "crashes" the game, which results in the game exiting immediately. No extraction. No imprints to help you on your next run. No post-run rewards. Its as if the failed run never happened.

For only the second of the game's many difficulty tiers, this is just too much.

Games are supposed to reward players' time, not waste it.

Clicking &/or dragging cards doesn't work ~60% of the time. Which is bad because that's the point of these games.

Cards look cool
Nice setting
Music is great

But gameplay is just broken

So im giving a Thumbs down until Rotcrawler removed and Detected is explained.

ok i thought this was an ea game with how many bugs and how much broken code is in it. The game starts to get unplayable really fast, the punctuation is just not there. They need to look at the code for the robots, the storage units, and just about every thing else. in its current state its a 1/10 after about 1 hr of play it breaks .

The game starts off okay, but by the 2 hour mark it completely devolves into an afk simulator, there are finite resource nodes on the map, making the amount of throughput finite and the demands to progress each milestone get progressively more absurd, the point it takes ten hours sitting afk to finish them.
The last ore in the game is particularly absurd as there are only five nodes on the entire map, and each one takes 200 seconds to produce one ore piece, and  a bar requires three. And you need 40 bars.

And then it just...ends, with a grand total of  four resources, six different buildings and one drill. This can barely be called automation. Money is irrelevant, decoration system is pointless, the companion robots are less than useless and the levelling and exp system is so utterly vapid that by the end of the game I had 76 unspent skill points.


There is absolutely not enough game here to justify its price.

I would not recommend this game in its current state. Its a mining automation game mixed with an idler. Everything is hurry up and wait. Every time you set down new equipment not just a new type you have to wait to upgrade it. Get to the last type of ore and its the cheapest so it makes no sense to progress if the loot value goes down. I feel this game was rushed. I have 13 hours mainly to finish it out since I started.

Imagine if the tutorial for Satisfactory was twenty times longer, and then when it ended the game was over. You can't do complex logistics, you can't automate things without "leveling them up" first with a lot of mandatory and boring work, there's very little plot or exploration to speak of. When I got to the last item needed to finish the game I was still just brute-forcing it by "hand" because there was no point trying to set up the needed supply lines and level up the machines to do it automatically.

Is it a terrible game? Not really, it's playable if you're fine doing a lot of boring stuff most of the time. But it's like a $5 game, not a $20 one.

I think you can find a better use of your time for now.  This game is quirky and has a bit of a story.  It's a relatively linear dive down into your star mines to gather star ores and sell them to star customers.  It's pretty chill and kinda fun.  I'm really looking forward to playing it once it works reliably.  It just came out this week (at time of this review) so there's probably just some early bugs to work out, but I'd still wait because as it is, the game can get really frustrating.

Examples:  
1. It took me ages to figure out how to use the transport network (it seems to work off pull mechanics rather than push, so if you don't have a downstream consumer, nothing moves).  Would have been nice had it been explained somehow.
2. At some point, for a reason I still don't understand, my transport network just stopped sending *most* red ores.  The blue ones are still working fine, only 1 of 5 drills works for red.  No idea why.  No warnings on the machine or any indication as to whether it's something I've done wrong.
***EDIT 1*** - Restarting the game fixed my red ore problem.  That allowed me to start making enough money to resume factory expansion.  Then eventually the yellow ore broke in the same way the red had before.  2 restarts still didn't resolve.  At this point, I guess I just have to wait for an update.

Automation games work well when they communicate with the player.  Narrow corridors (and time) are the primary obstacles in this game.  It does OK at communicating time.  It doesn't do OK at helping you plan for capacity and efficiency to make the most of the limited space you have.

Last thing... not to be a miser, but there's just way too meany REALLY GOOD automation games on Steam to jump into this buggy mess for $18.  I think it'll be fun... eventually.  Come and check back in a month and see if there has been enough refinement justify spending time with this.

Unfortunately I have to give this a negative review mostly due to pacing problems. The beginning is very slow and I say that as someone who tends to have a slow start to automation.  It takes far, far too long to earn enough to get the automation rolling.  But I got through it and enjoyed most of the middle of the game Then I got to what the game has telegraphed as the ending, behind a big 'earn this much money' wall. So I earned that much money, that was fine, went past the wall and was told I needed a little more money. OK, irritating, but reasonable. Earn it, spend it, find now I have to spend at least as much money as the big paywall to get what I need to mine/refine one new resource to get past the 'feed me this much stuff to progress the game' wall. I'm currently stuck there because so much grind is required and it's all so boring now.
It's especially galling because until the paywall you can only automate mining 3 ores. There's one hanging around you can't do anything with making me fear even more after the stuff wall, But maybe that's supposed to be for postgame? But that'd be even MORE money/grind so big no from me. I want to complete it but it's sad that the best way to do that would be to leave the game running while I sleep so I can have a pile of things to sell when I actually play.
Some minor gripes - all the machines feel samey. The miners/refiners all upgrade exactly the same no matter what material they're using. Machines/decos cost more the more you build which just feels like a mechanic to slow down progression (aka a pacing problem). Also, the transport system desperately needs a filter option to allow only certain mats through. The game is designed around tight corridors, limiting the machines/transporters you can have in each area, incentivizing you to put as much as possible on all your transports, especially since you need to have all your end product dropped near the end of the starting ramp if you want your not-so-bright little haulers to be able to keep up with throughput. But you literally cannot filter materials on the automation network and there's a strict limit on how much a storage unit can output for the little bots to pick up, so even with only 9 products to manage before endgame things feel squeezed. 

tl;dr - game needs more polish, could be a fun little indie if they iron out the pacing and, please, add some filters.

If this had been an early access title, it would have been a positive review. It is a good game, but since this is a full release, and priced thereafter, I expect a finished and polished product. The story is complete, but almost everything else needs polish and balancing.

A few things I would expect in a fully released game:
- I completed the game, for the last 30 or so levels out of my 68 levels, skill points didn't matter anymore
- The transport system needs a filter/sorter/item blocker feature. The logistics gets semi-complex but with no tools to handle it, it just becomes an annoying mess
- Money becomes irrelevant about half way through the game. There are a few items in the end that are expensive, but just take a bio break and you can afford them
- When I unlocked the last part of the mine at the lower levels, the bots stopped working and would not head down there
- The whole decoration system is not useful as it boosts money and xp and we have plenty of both
- The map is not meant for a structured setup but for a spaghetti setup. This is fun in some parts of the game, but not all the way through.
Too much grind. Too much. Literally sales prices x2 will make a relly nice gameplay.

It's a charming game, but as of 2025-Nov-12, it's got a few bugs that drove me nuts.

I just broke 20 hours of game time before end of game.  After that, there's no real reason to continue the game as there would be no physical room to really continue.  You're space limited, and jamming things so close together that you have to literally find a route to get to the other side of your mater piece puzzle because you can't jump or move around the buildables.

The robots, after a certain point, just stop pathing right.  They get stuck, go around in circles, and end up not helping with picking up items.  I had to move the robots to an area to get them to destroy the rocks I broke when getting through the game.

In late game, it's not so big of a deal, but everything you do costs you in game money.  Want to wire two relays together?  Costs ya 48 units.  Need to move the relay? Need to disconnect, do the move, then reconnect.  And yes, you have to pay that connect fee again for both in and out.

Also, just because I CAN make something, doesn't mean I want to immediately sell it.  If it's not in the store, the expectation should be that I'm not selling it, so the ships hang around for a minute (With a roughly 30 second delay between buying ships) and just delay you for the next purchase.

One feature I'd love to have is be able to more easily take things I've made from the store and apply them to a product I'm making that's more advance.  Basically, whatever ends up in the store is meant to be sold.  You can click one by one the items to pull OUT of the store but you don't have an easy way to haul those items back out to the field to be repurposed.

It's cute and interesting to start, but since the map is static and unchanging, there's limited replay value.
Everything is a grind in this game. Everything costs money, which you have to grind for. After that, grind some more. It is slow and not very interesting. Keep grinding.

4) Repeat.

I don't mind mod content getting added into the base game but it should at least excede quality of the mod that the patch makes non functioning. At the current state of things players are paying for something that was both free and better.

A good DLC overall, unfortunately the culmination of the new end-game crisis is anti-climactic and frankly makes no sense. When Cetana appears there is good interaction and tangable ways of slowing her progress...

The culmination of the crisis is that all prompts and interactions fade away, and if you forget to check a cluncky (and not displayed in the HUD) timer for 30 minutes you (and everyone else in the galaxy) will loose the game without any other warning.

Furthermore, no other empire seems to give a toss that their doom is impending... if you are in a tough spot when this crisis appears, it might be worth giving up then - you'll have to fight it out essentially alone (unless you are custodian or have a substantial federation - so definitely not a crisis you want to encounter playing isolationist).

step by step Stellaris DLC making:
1) Find a mod in steam workshop.
2) Wait five years until mod becomes outdated due to countless unnescesarry reworks and minor adjustments.
3) Release third of the original mod with 20 euro price tag.
4) Repeat.

I don't mind mod content getting added into the base game but it should at least excede quality of the mod that the patch makes non functioning. At the current state of things players are paying for something that was both free and better.

The Cybernetic Queen crisis is a miss, but it has some good ideas.

Their introduction is memorable, but all allies being bugged to the point of just accepting their fate and watching you fight by yourself, whilst in the same system, is immeasurably cringe. Not to mention that the game just decides to anticlimactically end after a certain point, whilst every other empire in the galaxy is doing their own thing. At least have some tangible build-up to the galaxy being destroyed, like in every other crisis, other than a simple timer.

I wish I could play this because it seems like a very appealing genre-mix...

* A control scheme, you could only find in hell.

This might be a good game but is certainly overrated. The character building system clearly takes many inspiration from Caves of Qud, and the hacking and alert system reminds me of Cogmind. If you like those two games you would maybe also like this. 
But be aware that this game did not achieve the same level of polish and that is the reason I do not recommend it. The in-game terminal mimics the real world one but is so much harder to use: no syntax highlighting, need much more rich-text formatting, does not show auto-completion candidates. And you *cannot make alias*, which pisses me off so much.

First of all, this game is really good, great even, considering it's a true indie game. However I rate it down because it's so close to being an actual masterpiece, alas, it doesn't reach this title.

    First, gameplay, "Deadeye" part of the title. I know how it sounds, but it's probably the most weak major part of the game. Generated levels are not working, they are boring, they are easy and most importantly they are useless and not connected to the story. Handcrafted levels on the other hand are cool but too few. But they are hard af, at least without skills... And now to the main problem - skills. They are unbalanced. On one hand you have seducer pack, which has the most busted perk from all I've seen, on the other hand you have kinda useless burning thingy. But more importantly, for me skills presented in the game break the image of supercool cybernetic agent. The base gameplay however gives this image perfectly. It's fun and cool, and considering the length of the game, you probably wouldn't get tired of it.

    The strongest part of the game lies in its "Deepfake" part, which is aesthetics. The music is fantastic, design is great, for real now - the main menu of the game is the best part of the game I'm constantly returning to. The absolute goated music masterpiece even among other themes from the OST, exceptional graphic design and rain sound effects, it's just fantastic. However the story is also not bad. Has some interesting thoughts, but I have a little problem with it. Basically storytelling. I know it's supposed to be immersive sim or smth, idk xd, but there are too many options in dialogues, so they become quite non-linear, meaning you can miss a lot of information if you choose wrong dialog option. And with that on to the next...

    The "Simulacrum" part, basically meaning that procedural generation of ... something, Well in game there those boring generated levels I mentioned in first section. Also your guns, cybernetic skeletons and some of the other weapons, are proceduraly generated. But what for? The game has zero replay value, except for different endings, missed side quests, or even dialog options. But these are more irritating than fun, yeah I kind of hate non-linear games, at least when they are not focusing on it. And this game doesn't do that.

    So to sum up. Things that I think would make this game better. Remove or completely rework skills. Make game a little longer and thinner(more linear). Or try to add some replay value (I can't really imagine how). Make The Concierge less hateable. Seriously I so fucking hate her I want to kill her, and that doesn't do much good to the game.

I took back my previous review since some Russian guy said "skill issue," (he's glazing) and decided to give the game another chance. I really wish there was a neutral or "ok" option to this. It's not a bad game, it can be very frustrating but also very engaging. (Slight spoilers) 

 The shooting in the game needs change, my biggest complaint is that the shotgun does not act like a shotgun, it shows bullet spread like a shotgun, but you have to be pinpoint accurate when aiming at enemies. Then the story is good and the atmosphere is there. The stealth segments are fine but barebones.

 The puzzle solving is not as good as I would say other reviews would claim. I wouldn't call the puzzles themselves hard to solve or anything but the interactables. A lot of the interactable objects are very easy to miss since they have a faint white outline that can blend with the environments. A lot of the time I would have to go back and forth with the environment to try and find a tiny key or lighter, and it's annoying.  It feels like combing through rooms with a fine tooth comb. 

The vehicles drive alright, the stick shifting is a fun concept. I don't really have anything else to add to that. 

The items menu in the inventory screen is pretty useless, and the layout makes it look like you can reorganize item slots, but you actually can’t as far as I'm aware. Which is annoying because everytime I want to bring up my shotgun, I bring the drone out by accident. 

It's very clear a lot of love and intention went into this game, but the game can be too frustrating. Other reviews bring this up and I agree. The game overall has promise but the quality of life designs choices set it back.

EDIT: Finished the game. I would rate it 5/10. Story is fine, but everything else drags the game down.

Atmosphere/immersion: 9/10 So close to perfect. See writing.

Gameplay: 3/10 Dull combat and walking felt same-y after so long. Driving is almost perfect- crash detection and vehicle damage was debatable in specific spots. Lots of hide-and-go-F-yourself that left searching for secrets and upgrades undesirable. The gunplay felt inconsistent. 

Writing: 4/10 Hard to get invested in until late into the game where it gets good. Lots of substance is locked behind resource-requiring search-and-find terminals or tablets. In a dark and gritty setting, levity and jokes are needed for contrast. The contrast was meta comments and on-the-nose references that felt out of place and broke the immersion.

Audio: 8/10 Music is great, sound design is great, it's the audio mixing and volume balancing that needed work. Dread music for hiding from certain enemies made it hard to hear the enemy itself. The in-game tinnitus was so bad I had to remove my headset until it passed. Editing the volume levels to compensate made other aspects of the game hard to hear. Could just be a personal problem.

4/10. Some bland Soviet post-apocalyptic retro-futurism wrapped in a below-average game.

+ Visuals are good.

- The art style was promising but turned out to be a bit dull, the game would have benefited from a bolder, more peculiar style.
- This game has the worst inventory system I have ever seen in a video game.
- Everything gameplay- and game design-related is below average.

- Blindly copying little things from Half-Life and SOMA makes this game look very cheap and unoriginal.

This is more of an informational review than anything else as I feel like this game wasn't really meant for me, but I think even with it not really being my type of game, there are a lot of issues with it. It plays like a walking and driving simulator where you're shuffled from one area to the next with sometimes cutscenes inbetween and sometimes dream/flashback sequences with more walking simulator. [strike]Also to note - it has a tag of shooter but not once in my 2 hours was I shooting anything nor was there any sort of combat.[/strike] Apparently there is combat right after when I stopped playing.
Pros:
+ The atmosphere is fantastic.
+ Most assets are well designed
+ Sound is mostly well done
Negatives:
- The main character's model looks...weird. Like not the design itself but the texturing and facial animations - they look like they game out of the mid-2000s
- Dialog is very basic and no real feeling behind it. Also why is the AI voiced but the main character not?
- Very limited exploration or sense of finding something out for yourself. Driving down the road you see some broken down cars but the 3-4 I stopped at were just set models. Nothing inside, no interactions. In various areas attempting to explore non-progression objects or areas yields nothing. I came up on a service station and the robotic voice suggested we look around for something useful, something I was going to do anyway. Nothing really groundbreaking happened or was found while in there. The 2 hours I played essentially felt very guided.
- A lot of interactions (breaking open doors for example) have the weird "move your mouse around" to perform the action. It feels like the dev was trying to make it feel more interactive with actions when a simple keypress should've been enough.
- Some interactions are very forced and feel very janky - the "tow chain to open the gate" objective was really bad. I walked up to the car to attach the chain but was unable to do. I had to go the fence first to attach it, and when I did the chain automatically stretched way out and under/through the car and I had to back it up to attach it then. And even when I did back it up and pick up the other end to attach it, the chain was pulling me back towards the fence. Just very weird, very janky.
- There is "crafting" but it's all forcefully guided.
- Excessive headbob and vehicle movement. I've never really gotten motion sickness in a video game, but in this game the shaky/jerky movements were getting to me.
- Some things are not explained at all or very poorly explained. For instance I found a tape and was told I could play it in the car, but never could figure out how.

I've played other walking simulators I've enjoyed (like Firewatch), but this feels like a pretty decent downgrade from them. I understand it's a single dev, but it feels like it needed more time to be fleshed out into something great.

The game tries to provide a lot of different things : walking simulator, adventure/puzzle, action/fighting, stealth, platforming. Anything that's not a puzzle is not enjoyable, because the game's controls are too clunky.

I will say my down vote is related to specific user issues for those who cannot physically use the WSDA & E keys easily due to some physical issues - the game seems mostly simple fun but the mechanics need some work. 
The game is simply not user friendly for those with limited hand speed movement as you need to use WSDA and E keys to solve problems. Unlike many ganes allowing only one keystroke to use to open codes or use a mouse, you need to select at speed code shapes by using keys WSAD and E and in this game mechanic it just does not allow or permit slower times to account for accessibility issues. Its not that the code shapes are hard to recognise its just you need to select each shape so fast its not even possible for someone with restricted hand movement (work injury causing permanent slow finger movement in my case) The answer would be to take away all code requirements at easy level or slow down the timer limitations to half that which exists now but sadly its unplayable now for anyone with physical restrictions as you need to solve puzzles and codes this way to move forward. If there is no hope of a solution - like slowing the timer or even getting rid of it, better to get a refund and play something else. Sad it could be a fun simple game.

This is the definition of a game with a great gameplay loop that is absolutely marred by technical issues and bugs that just spoil an otherwise great experience.

The combat loop of ripping out cores, and either throwing them like a bomb or absorbing them for a boost/super punch is a fun mechanic. But on brutal/brutal+, the gameplay issues become super punishing. Sometimes when you go for a super punch, it'll "connect" and consume the punch but the enemy won't stagger, or take any armor/health damage and leave you open to get absolutely wrecked by the same enemy. This seems to be a bug, as far as I can tell, and seems to be more common when targeting enemies on ramps or inclines. Sometimes throwing a core doesn't explode, it seems to just clip through the ground or disappear, doing absolutely nothing. When the player doesn't do anything wrong technically and the game just decides to glitch out, it just becomes a test of patience. I had to restart many encounters until I sucessfully managed to get through without the game doing something jank. At least the auto-saves in levels are numerous and generous.

The game has other minor issues, like performance problems and game settings that can seem to break on restarting the game. It's also possible to be able to get stuck or clip through terrain if you're attempting to look off the beaten path for hidden DUST resources (the game's currency),

I suffered though these issues and completed the game on brutal and began playing on NG+, and that's when I noticed all the broken achievements. I got the achievement for beating the game on Brutal, but the achievement for killing the final boss did not unlock. And all the tracking achievements just stopped progressing, like they're stuck at certain percentage. If you don't care about achievements this is a non-issue, but for anyone looking to be a completionist on this game, I would highly suggest avoiding this. And looking at other reviews and the Steam forums, I don't seem to be the only one having this problem.

My other complaint is how half-baked the NG+ mode is. It doesn't let you change difficulty, and it resets your progress so you can't replay levels. It should at least make a seperate save file instead of overwriting your finished game. It would have been nice for BRUTAL+ to be an optional challenge on NG+. It would also be nice if the game gave the option to carry over progress when doing mission select, as opposed to you just having the progress you had when you initially started that mission.

Anyway, it doesn't look like the developer has updated this game in months, and I doubt these issues will be addressed at this point. If this game ever gets the polish it deserves, I would definitely recommend this game. But in its current state, the issues just spoil what would otherwise be a great experience.

When people say this game is short, they really mean it. I finished Metal Eden on Normal in about five hours, and my problem isn’t just that it’s short — it’s that even at 50% off, I still feel like I got ripped off. The 50% discount honestly feels like it should be the base price, and then another 50% off is where the game’s five-hour runtime would actually feel worth it.

There are other problems. There are only about six or seven distinct enemy types across the entire five hours of gameplay. Almost all the environments reuse the same stale backdrop, featuring what I can only assume is some sort of erosion or debris floating into the sky, but the game never explains what it is or why it’s happening.

The story doesn't help either. You get a handful of cutscenes, and then about four hours of two background characters endlessly droning on to Aska about their problems. She responds with half a sentence, and then they immediately resume their exposition. There are no hidden collectibles that expand the world, no notes or terminals, no environmental storytelling, and no real Easter eggs aside from a few hidden lives or Dust pickups.

If all that wasn’t enough, the story still ends up feeling nonsensical, with two ending cutscenes that make little sense — even if you did know who Aska was the whole time.

Too short for full playthrough. Around 4-5 hours on hardest difficulty. No replayability. Not worth full price tag. Bosses are too easy. Gameplay is fun at first, but becomes reptitive. Break shield, grab core, punch, shoot, repeat. If game is <$5 its worth it.

The game suffers from quite sever level traversal stutters caused by level boundaries being too small, which can at times be located in combat areas. This is an issue which has plaguing UE game since for ever. Its a known issue with known solutions. 

I understand that with limited hardware, one needs to stream data from storage, but at the same time, high end machines like mine with 32GB of RAM, a 7900XTX with 24GB of VRAM paired with a 7800X3D shouldnt be having stutter issues. Especially since I have this game installed on a Gen4 m.2 NVME SSD while running a tweaked linux nixos install. 

Its annoying to see the game stutter, while only using at max 8GB of VRAM and 12GB of RAM. Please expand level boundaries to trigger less asset streaming stutters and make sure these transitions arent present in heavy combat areas.

Another issue plaguing the game is input latency, it simply takes too long for the game to register user input, to the point where you have to actively play with this in mind.

Gameplay is great, not as good as Doom Eternal, but still solid none the less. The bosses have been fun until now and the music is also not bad. I've been enjoying the resource loop a lot and the enemy design is top notch.

The game has a lot of potential, but before it can be realised, the performance needs to be fixed. I cant in good faith recommend picking this game up at full price when it runs this badly.

As much as I've complained about the shortness of the game, it's a very good "boomer shooter" to play. You can see the developers took a lot of inspiration from Doom and Ghost Runner, enemies will drop armour, ammo or health depends on how you finish them off. You have weapons upgrades and skill upgrades, but you never really get the time to experiment with them, it's very go, go, go! It's such a shame as the art, weapon and visual designs are excellent, it would've been nice to have more "open areas" to explore and take in the world.

But a $30 price tag for a game that the community can complete in only 6 hours isn't great! And you'll feel left wanting more, but there isn't more! Again please get this game when it's on sale! If the devs do make a sequel to this I will buy it, as long as it's not another 6 hour long game. I will edit this review with the time it's taken me to do a full complete run on the hardest difficulty!

Despite some graphical bugs, this game felt like it had a lot of potential.  Unfortunately, the dev has admitted to using AI generated assets, so I cannot support or recommend a game on ethical grounds.  If I could I would refund the game, and strongly recommend people find other games that don't use AI.  

For those confused as to why this is an issue, the short of it is this: generative AI, like all LLM based AIs, requires huge amounts of whats called training data to "learn" how to do stuff.  Due to the massive amounts needed, this data is stolen by scraping the internet, and/or through other shady data collection methods, often against the expressed wishes of artists or other creators.  There are many other environmental, legal, and social issues with AI tech as well, but that's beyond the scope of a steam review :p

Bottom line is that LLM AIs as they exist now are a fundamentally unethical technology, and so is its use.  

Its sad that even indie game devs have so little integrity or respect for artists these days, but I guess this is a learning lesson for me to start doing much more research into games before buying.

hardcore tactics, but forcing you to go through the same levels "like we have a roguelike here" without the slightest changes in the tactical game is strange, and leaving you without ammo and without the ability to somehow get it in the run (while the enemy chews them with his ass, take them as much as you want) is very strange. There is no particular motivation to go through the same levels 10 times, with the same bosses that you have already knocked down earlier with the prospect of being left without bloody ammo (yes, pots and chairs help, but it's still too much), it is not clear what bonuses for ammunition will open and what needs to be done for this. It reminded me a little of old-school Budokan (a fighting game from the 80s), where they also limited the number of fighting styles/weapons used in the "run", like "train to knock everyone down with your bare hands if you want to go further." Here too, apparently you need to jack off each level to the point of vomiting, and then maybe there will be enough ammo for +2 levels to the current achievement.

it's hard to actually say that i don't recommend Shell Runners because it has a solid foundation, it is just missing the flavor a little bit of story can offer. the base game is pretty fun, but progression gets slow without adding any new challenges after the first zone. you keep repeating the same mission types while grinding to upgrade your preferred items and shells. the game is not too difficult once you get the hang of it, and some of the bosses are easy to cheese. there isn't really any story to speak of. I've encountered a few bugs where missions were completed but didn't register and a few other minor issues like being able to teleport outside the map. if you enjoy the grind, you might find more replay value here than i did.

Looks beautiful at first sight but the moment the action starts, it's just bad. 
The start of the plot is dumb (you get robbed within 6 dialogue messages?)
Enemies are spongy, spongier than The Division - probably an attempt to artificially prolong the game.
Even on easy they are spongy af - games usually scale the enemies up with time or something.
Overall not even worth your time. 
We were hoping for something similar to the Ascent but nope. Moving onto CyberCorp, that looks more promising.

Just no. Stealth is the only way early on. Take away the weapon because it is worthless. I did two mission and the gun was useless on both. If you are seen, restart. The restart from checkpoint and the restart mission are easily mistaken or misclicked so instead of restarting at a checkpoint you start all over. Early weapons are useless. Enemies are waaaay too strong. Cut scenes  are so bad. What minimal voice acting there is, is terrible. Graphics are decent. Story didn't really pan out for me. Seemed dumb.

I gave it about an hour before I lost interest and moved on. I think an hour is enough to grab a player if the game is solid. This one has pretty decent reviews but I'm just not into the slow game play or the forced stealth. I was hoping for something different. I usually love this type of top down shooter but this is not that.
a complete waste of money and time, not worth trying at all , it is just bad.

Unfortunately I cannot recommend this title in any way. Everything in it looks like it was taken straight out of the game jam project and left that way, except the pumped up graphics which looks presentable on the steam page. 

The story in story mode is close to non-existent, level design in inconsistent, none of the gameplay modes, either stealth or gun combat, really works, the enemy AI is straight up garbage. The cases do not even have simple checkpoints, which gets even more frustrating towards the end, when you encounter the unskippable cutscene sequences, which you have to go through over and over again with every repeat of the mission. 

I gave this game the benefit of the doubt. When it came out it looked undercooked, but I waited half a year to see where the things go from there. Unfortunately the devs have gone silent and there was no update since April. Probably the project was abandoned and left in the state it was released - flawed, frustrating and disappointing in the end. At the moment it is not worth your money and, more importantly, your time.

Decent eye-candy but not much beyond that. It doesn't really work as an educational app because it only gives two sentence summaries of objects and fails to distinguish between what an object literally looks like versus an artistic representation

I'd be willing to look past that though. The worst part is it doesn't really do a good job of making differences in scale understandable. I figured I'd at least be able to compare the size of things like a red blood cell and a white blood cell. Or see how big the largest known star is compared to our sun. Instead, at any given scale you're only ever going to see a few objects which are generally too far in the distance or too poorly modeled to really get a feeling for the size of them. For example, at one point you zoom in on a bald guy's head, and instead of seeing something interesting like how big a skin cell is compared to an eyelash, you jump straight to an illustration of how neurons are connected in his brain. Given that this hardly takes advantage of depth perception and has barely any interactivity, why even make this a VR experience instead of a 360 degree video?

I realize it's literally 10 times the cost of this game, but seriously save up and buy Universe Sandbox instead

Game Strengths
•	Features engaging PvEvP gameplay with beautiful graphics and enjoyable gunplay.
•	Offers decent progression and a satisfying crafting loop.
•	Arc enemies provide a challenging and lethal combat experience.
•	Finding blueprints, weapons, and loot, then successfully escaping a raid, is highly rewarding.

Major Issues
•	The game does not respect players’ time investment: 
o	High-end gear and epic weapons offer no real advantage over basic equipment.
o	Players can be easily killed by anyone, regardless of gear quality.
o	Extremely fast time-to-kill (TTK) leaves no time to react, even with strong shields.
•	Lack of social and gameplay structure: 
o	No factions or bounty system to identify enemies, leading to a “kill on sight” mentality.
o	Defensive actions are penalized the same as aggressive PvP due to aggression-based matchmaking.
o	Even looting dead raiders can result in being sent to aggressive servers.
•	Limited defensive and escape options: 
o	Few ways to disengage from combat; smoke screens are ineffective.
o	No viable defensive tank builds to enable escape.
o	Extraction points are heavily camped, especially on night servers with the best loot.
o	Players often escape with only minimal loot.
•	Tedious recovery and crafting: 
o	No ability to save loadouts or craft full replacements quickly.
o	Dying means spending significant time crafting basic supplies before re-entering a raid.

Overall Impression
•	The PvP element adds excitement but currently feels frustrating.
•	High-end gear’s poor performance makes grinding feel pointless.
•	The game needs better defensive options and ways to survive extraction/loot area camping.
•	Without improvements, the game risks becoming ruined by aggressive PvP and losing its main player base.

I've followed the game for some time, ever since I heard about the AI/reinforcement learning implemented for the ARC machines. I normally would not play an extraction shooter/looter, but reconsidered purchasing after I heard about ABMM (Agression Based Match-Making). The idea behind it is, that if you don't shoot at others you will be put in the lobbies with more friendly players.

This is where problems start; it created a division within the community. Some people really like it, some despise it - and there is a group that even gambles this system. The so called by the community "rat players" would attack unsuspecting players just before extracting, either posing as friendly players or hiding in a corner. This results in you being shot from behind, with no counter play - dead before you can respond. While this seems fun for the first few hours, it gets old quickly.

Responding to human threats, ah! Here's another problem - if you shoot back, your ABMM raises and you get put into mixed lobby of peaceful players and aggresive players. Continue to shoot back, and you will be put in kill-on-sight lobby. In the end I just don't shoot back and write off every 6-10th game as a random loss. Not fun.

The PVP crowd would say, that this is a part of the game and you should "get better" in PVP as it is a part of the game. But to get better you have to start shooting back, which will put you in strict PVP lobbies you might hate.

The PVE crowd would say, there is a need to split PVE and PVP lobby to prevent this behaviour. But this would result in almost no challenge after you learn how the ARC machines operate and pointless resource grind.

I don't know what is the right option, I don't want to guess. I can just say that whatever it is currently it is not it. Which is a shame, because the game is really good otherwise - good graphics, really well optimized. The machines are really cool. 

Should you choose to buy it, at least now you know what it struggles with.

A Masterpiece of Optimization, but a Crisis of Progression and Purpose

The game’s foundation is rock-solid: smooth optimization, beautiful visuals, and satisfying gunplay. However, once you peel back the layers, the core loop suffers from a severe lack of rewarding content and a disconnect between developer intent and player reality.

The "Exploration" Paradox: Snap and Salvage The developers clearly want us to "explore and absorb the atmosphere" of each map. However, quests like "Snap and Salvage" achieve the exact opposite. By forcing players to find a single Epic item with an abysmal spawn rate in the tiny, cramped map of Stella Montis, they aren't encouraging exploration—they are forcing monotonous farming. Instead of interacting with the map, players are locked into a 2-minute "speed-run" route, clearing the dense loot rooms before anyone else can, then extracting to repeat the cycle for 10+ hours. This isn't exploration; it's a chore.

Weapon Meta & The "Coin Hoarding" Simulator The equipment balance is currently upside down. When Common and Rare weapons consistently outperform Legendaries, the motivation to grind disappears.

    Tier Disparity: The Bettina is essentially scrap, while the Aphelion (Legendary) is outclassed by a Ferro.

    Economy: Because high-tier gear isn't worth the investment, the game turns into a "Coin Hoarding Simulator." You reach the 5-million mark for the expedition with nothing meaningful to spend it on, as your mid-tier gear is already "BiS" (Best in Slot) for all practical purposes.

Broken Challenge: The Wolfpack Problem The game offers a decent challenge until you unlock the Wolfpack recipe. From that point on, the survival horror and tactical elements vanish. By carrying 6 Wolfpacks into a raid, you effectively turn a high-stakes extraction shooter into a "Walking Simulator." Bosses and drone swarms that should be terrifying become minor inconveniences that you simply "delete" with a button press.

Community Events & Social Integrity The Queen and Matriarch events are meant to unite the community, but the lack of a contribution-based reward system turns them into a toxic sprint. It’s a race to see who can loot the carcass first, regardless of who did the heavy lifting. Furthermore, the daily influx of RMT (Real Money Trading) friend-request spam suggests that the social and economic integrity of the game needs urgent developer intervention.
game was fun but embark has promoted rat culture which has steadily ruined the entire game.

I’ll get straight to the point and then explain why.

PvP in this game is pushed mainly by selfish people who gain financially from it, while they portray PvE as something that would “ruin the atmosphere” the game is supposed to have and threaten that separating the game into two modes (PvP and PvE) would cause a loss of players.
And that part is actually true: a portion of players would be lost—but it would be the toxic ones, and those who use the game to fuel underground markets selling blueprints for real money, along with fake comments aimed solely at maintaining this status quo and selling cheats.
These are players who got rich by abusing exploits: hiding outside the map behind walls, using turbo-click macros to automate weapons that aren’t meant to be automatic, passing through hundreds of locked doors without keys, opening thousands of lockers only when they had good loot. All of this just to sell blueprints for real money. And of course, this pushes many to use cheats like wallhacks to overpower others and steal their items.
As you can imagine, none of this has anything to do with fun or with the atmosphere the game should create. And honestly, losing this category of “players” would be a good thing.
Solution: make blueprints tradable only among party members in the match where they dropped, and then bind them to the player who extracted them.

The other group is made up of selfish players, as I said—people who think only about their own needs and fail to consider that most players have a private life, a job, a family, and just want to enjoy a relaxed match in the evening. Not everyone has 24/7 to spend online or streaming.

So, to sum it up: this game needs at least two matchmaking modes. One PvP mode, where those who love that content can fight each other freely, and one PvE mode, where players who want to enjoy the game’s amazing atmosphere and gameplay can do so in peace, away from those with economic ulterior motives.


PS: Please avoid empty, unsubstantiated comments made just for the sake of talking.

This game has a serious problem with false bans caused by a poorly implemented anti-cheat system. Legitimate players are being banned without any clear evidence, explanation, or warning. Even worse, once banned, there is essentially no support, appeals are ignored or met with automated responses that don’t address the issue at all.

There is zero transparency in the banning process. Players are not told what they supposedly did wrong, and there is no meaningful way to defend themselves. It feels like the system assumes guilt by default, and the company makes no effort to investigate or communicate.
