Only writing this review because I genuinely enjoy this game and think without Embark making serious changes it will go the way of The Cycle. Similar game, couldn't/wouldn't do anything about cheaters and has now been sunset.

[h3]Cheating[/h3]
Currently there is a duping exploit that people are using for everything, attachments, utility, 15k ducks. This is especially problematic due to not forcing wipes across the community and is going to cause permanent harm. This is exacerbated by the fact that Embark has decided to take a ridiculous stance on punishing cheaters by using a system of verbal warning -> slap on the wrist -> finally taking action (30d/60d/perm) bans which can be easily mitigated by the way Steam handles sharing accounts with family.

[h3]Cheating (again)[/h3]
If you consistently do any amount of PVP and semi-regularly win your fights you will end up in Kill On Sight lobbies; this is fine, it's the main reason why I play this game. The problem is more and more often you will run into cheaters the longer this game goes on. It will range from the now somewhat mitigated macroing semi-auto weapons to being shot from people inside walls, which they CANNOT seem to fix no matter how many patches, to blatant rage hacking from level 0 steam accounts. Sure, I'll give it to embark they're pretty damn good with getting your loot back to you after the fact despite you still losing your attachments, some of which you cannot just recraft.

[h3]Lack of Communication[/h3]
There has been multiple changes every single patch since release that is not included anywhere in the patch notes. The majority of the time the community will notice and say something in their discord and then Embark will finally put out information to say this was intended/unintended (the most recent example is the semi-auto weapons change and the looting speed). The audacity to put "We always aim to include all changes in our patch notes" in their most recent hotfix is ignorant at best.

[h3]Trials[/h3]
The only sort of endgame activity this game has is fundamentally flawed; gatekeeping you to specific times of days to do on specific maps if you want to stay competitive for Hotshot to have a chance at getting into Cantina Legend. Removing the 2x multiplier for maps would greatly alleviate this problem. As it sits now you have to plan out your run in advance, tank your elo because god help you if you end up in a hyper aggressive lobby with the possibility of cheaters while trying for a high score, and hope and pray you don't get spawned into a 17 minute remaining raid with a 150k+ loadout to reset and try again.

The visuals and sound design are well done, and the gunplay feels responsive. The first hours are enjoyable and give the impression of a game with real potential. Over time, however, the experience becomes repetitive. The core gameplay loop does not evolve enough, and most raids start to feel very similar. Progression is slow and often unrewarding, with loot and upgrades that rarely feel impactful or meaningfully change how you play.

PvP feels disconnected from the core design of the game. It’s unclear why it was added in the first place, as the systems around it do not support or reward it in any meaningful way. PvP engagements rarely provide value, don’t contribute to progression, and often feel like an unnecessary risk rather than an intended gameplay pillar. And I'm not even considering the issues with cheaters and false bans of regular players who have played hundreds of hours, which makes the situation even worse.
That's why I completely understand "don't shoot" meta. And to be honest, I would like this to remain relevant to this day.

Another frustrating issue is matchmaking. Being placed into raids near the very end, when most areas are already looted, significantly increases the risk of losing everything while gaining little or nothing. This is especially frustrating because you have no control over when you are dropped into a match, making these situations feel unfair rather than challenging.

Arc Raiders has a decent foundation, but it lacks the depth, variety, and long-term motivation needed to stay engaging. It may improve over time, but right now it didn’t fully deliver the experience I was hoping for.

I honestly hesitate to leave a review on this game because i am so divided. I am not a PVP type type of player. In games where its the primary objective, sure fine. Thus heres my list of suggestions for the devs:

ARC Raiders PvP is a Complete Shitshow, Here's Why It Sucks Massive Crap

You know what grinds my soul into dust? Games that pretend to offer immersive, cinematic co-op experiences and then jam PvP right down your throat like a broken glass sandwich, and ARC Raiders is guilty as hell nothing gives you stress of hard work grinding for something.

This game had potential. The trailers? Slick. The aesthetic? Gorgeous. The promise of PvE co-op alien or AI invasion badassery? Hell yes, sign me up. But then they flipped the script, shoved PvPvE into it, and suddenly it’s not about strategy or immersion, it’s about which twitchy asshole with no life camps the extraction zone with a sniper rifle and ruins your entire playthrough.
You wanna tell a story? THEN TELL A **CKING STORY. Don't bait people with narrative vibes and then dump them into a chaos fueled fragfest where any chance of atmosphere gets obliterated by 12-year-olds yelling slurs over VOIP while teabagging your corpse.
Let’s be real here: PvP in ARC Raiders is an insult to everyone who actually wanted to experience the world. The devs had something special and they sold it out for sweaty e-sports wannabes. No boundaries. No respect. Just straight up griefing baked into the core design like it's some revolutionary idea. It’s not. It’s trash.

There’s no room for exploration when every moment is a fight for survival against people who’d rather ruin your fun than play the stupied objective. There’s no "story mode", no offline escape, no chill. Just endless loops of get shot, respawn, repeat, and for what? So some streamer can rack up kills for counts and be the best youtube streamer?
And GCL? Game-Crushing Lowlifes. That’s what it stands for now. They ruined the experience for anyone who just wanted a journey, not a goddamn PvP meat grinder.

The PvP rubbish in this game is so frustrating and toxic to deal with. Twice now, in the last two days alone, I've tried to take part in destroying a Matriarch, only to be killed by free kit losers with stitchers (think basically a pipe machine gun that requires very little to make or maintain). When I'm rocking a heavy shield, I don't they shouldn't be able to drop me with a single magazine.

This game is supposedly meant to be PvE focused—and in fact that is why I purchased it. Yet there is no incentive to not PvP; it is instead actively reward. For example, quests to earn "Cred", a type of in game currency, ask you to deal damage to other players with certain weapons. Furthermore, since players are given the chance to go in with free kits to kill players with good stuff—at no risk to themselves—I'm forced to believe this was an intentional design decision. Unfortunately, this creates a toxic ecosystem where you're only penalized for not engaging in the PvP culture. There is no reason not to do the same as these hostile players.

To be clear, I'm not against PvP in my extraction shooters. I have over 1400 hours in Escape from Trakov. Yet, in that game, my good gear and my character's stats would often allow me to handle hostile scav players (the equivalent to this game's free kits). There is no incentive to run high-quality gear as it rarely turns the tables in a gunfight and often seems to do worse in PvP situations.

At this point I don't know how much I want to play this game, let alone recommend it to friends and family, when they're going to have to deal with toxic/shitty players that shoot you on sight and who face ZERO consequences should they die. 

To have more than just a negative option, let me offer some ideas. Why not set it up where free kits can only be in lobbies with other free kits? Perhaps have there be like a an hour cooldown, which increases or decreases depending on how you interact with other players during that time? Alternatively, a system could be created where if you cause too much PvP damage to players, the main traders will no longer be willing to work with you (this would be more meaningful if the traders sold better stuff).

More than anything, I just want to see something done that is aimed at creating a system that makes PvP have some sort of a bloody consequence, rather than it being a ticket to what is often a free meal with fries and a shake.

Garbage ass game now. Launch  looked promising, but now no better than Tarkov.

This game was marketed as both a PVP and PVE experience, yet the reality is that the PVE side has been almost completely erased by the way the community and loadout system function. The overwhelming majority of players have adopted the kill-on-sight or worse, the befriend and betray mentality, which wouldn't necessarily be a problem if the risk and reward were balanced.

Right now, there is little to no incentive to bring high-tier gear into a raid. Any player can load into a game with a free loadout and still reliably kill someone who is risking valuable gear. This creates a situation where hoarding gear isn't just encouraged, but required, because bringing anything good almost guarantees you'll lose it to someone with nothing to lose.

As a result, the PVE content suffers immensely. ARCS are actively avoided because engaging them requires gear that is too risky to bring. This problem is even worse for new or casual players, because they cannot acquire and retain the gear needed to take down bigger ARCS. This means a lot of quests cannot be fulfilled unless you are blessed with a kind lobby, or you simply join the rats and steal from others. The PVE aspect essentially becomes useless then, and you are left in a frustrating loop where ignoring half of the advertised experience is the only way to progress.

The longer this continues, the worse this problem will become. As the weeks go by and the player base becomes more established, new and casual players will have an increasingly difficult time acquiring the materials needed to upgrade their workstations. Without upgraded workstations, they cannot craft better gear, meaning they cannot engage with PVE content, which is required for the majority of quests. This leaves them effectively stuck at the start of the game.

The aggression-based matchmaking does little to alleviate the loadout problem, because in most matches, there are still multiple highly aggressive players who camp the spawn and extraction zones and kill Raiders, again risking little on their part. Some of these spawn camp deaths also occur while the players load in, as mid-game spawns are enabled and poorly protected from trolls.

If this game were marketed as a PVP extraction shooter or a PVE co-op game, there would be no problem with either playstyle. However, the current blend of both creates an unfun, toxic and exhausting loop full of childish people who cannot work cooperatively and who believe that shooting people in the back while risking none of their own gear is the only way to enjoy the game. And any attempt to raise these concerns is routinely dismissed as a skill issue on the new player's part, despite the fact that the game actively rewards low-risk griefing over meaningful cooperation or PVE battles.

TLDR - ARC Raiders is supposed to be a blend of PVP and PVE, but its current systems reward zero-risk PVP with free loadouts while punishing anyone engaging with PVE. This makes progression nearly impossible for new and casual players, and any criticism is brushed off by the community as a 'skill issue', despite the issue being rooted in the poor balance and design.

Edit - If you're still not convinced that the community has a problem, just take a look at my comments. And shout out to all you people proving my point, your lack of basic reading comprehension makes my day.

I wish Steam offered a grading system for their reviews rather than the current Yes/No option.ARC Raiders is truly a mixed bag with a lot of potential and some issues and yes/no does not reflect this well at all. Anyway, after 126 hours in the game it feels like a good moment to share the impressions. 

Pros: 
*  the game is beautiful too look at, atmospheric effects are amazing, audio is great,
*  connection feels very stable, teaming up with friends is really easy,
*  sessions are no longer than 30 minutes, so even if you have little time, you can complete a session 
    and bring something back from the surface. This feels like a plus as there is no expectation to 
    commit to a raid that goes  on for 1.5 hours etc
* the idea that you go out to the surface of Earth and face killer robots is great, 
* looting in the game is quite pleasant 
* there is a lot going on in terms of updates and future plans for this game, so this review may need to 
   be reviewed. 
* if hunting down other players is your thing, this game offers abundant opportunities for this, 
* winning in PVP against someone who ambushed you can be very satisfying.

Cons: 
* managing loot is time consuming  - more advanced items require a greater variety of ingredients to 
  make ( which is fine ) but this leads to a lot of going back and forth, recycling materials to get other 
  materials and so on. If your character does not survive the round, all equipment is lost and it takes a 
  while to rebuild the loadout
* while the idea of the game is that the remnants of humanity are fighting against machines, human 
   players are far more of a threat then the ARCs. ARCs are quite predictable and can be avoided 
   without much of a trouble, 
* the reality of how PVPVE works in this game. In practice, 90% of PVP encounters begin with being 
   shot in the back. The balancing of the game is such that a player with the most basic  equipment ( or 
   a free loadout)  can take down a  player with a heavy shield and epic weapon easily if he attacks first.  
   This, in itself,  is not that much of a problem, but the time taken to put together another good 
   loadout can be really long if you do not have the right resources.  If you are really low on resources, 
   you'll need to make a couple of trips to the surface to gather them again. Putting on a free loadout 
   takes a few seconds and costs nothing. 
* Any player who walks out onto the surface of the map with PVE gaming in mind ( 
   exploration/looting/engaging ARCs/ minding own business) is automatically at disadvantage when 
   compared against PVP-minded players.  If you like PVE mostly, be prepared for a lot of frustration, 
* each time i turn the game on, there is between 4 to 10 spam/scam friend requests. Blocking and 
   reporting this does not make any difference.
* trying to find mushrooms is so annoying.

First of all, the game is genuinely good, looks great, runs really well and there is something for everyone, especially if you enjoy PvE gameplay, because it has PvE lobbies, you just have to know the rules how to be chosen into them, and honestly in the beginning PvE is quite entertaining and challenging, even late game it can be tough (unless you run around with wolfpacks iykwim). 

So if you don't have much time and want an extraction looter experience - this game is for you.

The reasons why I dont recommend this game:

1. It's an unfinished game, needs more polishing in certain areas, and if you have more time on your hands to rush through the game - at some point there is nothing much to do, the quests get boring pretty fast, and even though the whole gameplay process in enjoyable, it is lacking lategame, especially in PvE lobbies, where the game can be figured out and pretty much completed in like 50 hours or less (excluding achievements, but truthfully, with PvP it has even more gameplay time due o many factors, but PvP has it's own issues). Can't say it's that bad, because clearly I have to choose full experience (meaning PvP) to get everything out of it, but it is how it is.

2. Once you know all the loot spots you will find out that everyone knows them too and at that point it's all about who reaches them first. To be fair, you can loot anything and find good loot everywhere, especially during special conditions like Night or Cold, but when you want to use an ingame key you will need a few raids to find that specific room locked and unlooted. Not sure how I would fix this though.

3. A lot of items have no purpose, often it's not even clear if you will need them or not, but beyond a certain point in game the loot - in general - has no purpose. The hideout can be upgraded pretty fast and once you gather everything for the expedition, other than preparing for a fight (PvE or PvP), there is nothing much to it.

4. Money has almost no purpose. A lot of items needed cannot be bought, those that can be bought are limited in numbers and/or locked behind barter items, which is fine and suits the game lore, but at some point you will find yourself earning millions with no option to spend it all. (Im guessing it will either be utilized with later updates or it's just a distraction for people that need big numbers to feel good).

5. Annoying Bugs. For example, it shows that I have played over 125 hours and it is factually wrong because I played like 70-80. The problem is that it doesn't shut down entirely for me (and other people, but not everyone) after exiting the game, so it sort of runs in the background and Steam just racks up the "hours played" number. 

Furthermore, if you try to run the game again on the same day, it just doesn't run (or rather it stays running in the background) and you have to "end task" or manually close it on Steam. 

Other bugs like when you increase number of items to pick, make, give or split, that number sometimes resets to zero, or the dismantle window stays open if you move before chosing an option to cancel or dismantle. And other things.

6. PvP is both fun and not. It's NOT fun because of rats, cheaters, dishonest, hypocrite and other morally empty scum that have no feeling of honor. And since there are no teams here (like in Battlefield, CoD, CS, Valorant etc.), a potential for a 3rd party opportunists is really high.

In any case, most of these issues are subjective so I will change this review to positive once something changes (especailly "that" annoying bug, I REEEALLY don't like when apps do not do what I'm telling them to do).

The extraction concept is nothing new .The Division already did it years ago with its Dark Zone, and with much more depth in builds, talents, and tactical choices. In Arc Raiders, these systems feel shallower and more simplified, lacking the same sense of weight and strategic complexity.
Instead of evolving the formula, it copies it, strips away depth, and dresses it up with robots. The result is a game that fails to establish a strong identity of its own and comes across as a shadow of a much more refined experience that already exists in The Division 2

Embark uses different anti-cheat software, not just one. One of them is Anybrain. Do yourself a favor and do some deep research in what Anybrain exactly is doing, how it operates, how it evaluates. 

One example, of many:
I live in Dubai, have to connect to the Europe server region, the closest one for me. That results in a ping of 160 to 200. 
At 180 ms, the server often sees this pattern:
1. Your aim updates arrive late → then “snap” to corrected positions
2. Enemy movement is predicted client-side → corrected server-side in bursts
3. Shots appear to land with delayed confirmation
4. Micro-adjustments bunch together instead of flowing smoothly

To a behavior model (like Anybrain), that can look like:
1. artificial micro-corrections
2. abnormal reaction timing
3. predictive tracking
4. inconsistent input → outcome relationships

Not because you’re cheating - but because your client is constantly catching up.

The next point is, the support for ban appeals. That is even a bigger issue than the bans itself! It doesnt matter what, you will get only scripted bot answers. Zero human interaction. In the end i can't know if my bad ping is responsible for the suspension cause, simply cause the scripted bot answers wont tell you anything, literally anything!
Visit their discord, you will find tons on post regarding this issue - even if you have another technical issue for which you contact support, you still will get scripted bot answers without a positive outcome for whatever problem you have - plenty of screenshots of those answers are available from various players in the bug section reports.

In around 1 week my suspension will be lifted and i am afraid of starting to play again cause they dont tell you what the issue was, even not indications. And then, while not being able to adjust my system accordingly (if it isnt my connection), i will be slapped with another 60 day suspension....

Be careful where you invest your money, just do the research and you will see what i say brings a lot of thruth to the topic...

This game is fantastic at its core, but I cannot recommend it in its current state.

This game was marketed as both a PVP and PVE experience, yet the reality is that the PVE side has been almost completely erased by the way the community and loadout system function. The overwhelming majority of players have adopted the kill-on-sight or worse, the befriend and betray mentality, which wouldn't necessarily be a problem if the risk and reward were balanced.

Right now, there is little to no incentive to bring high-tier gear into a raid. Any player can load into a game with a free loadout and still reliably kill someone who is risking valuable gear. This creates a situation where hoarding gear isn't just encouraged, but required, because bringing anything good almost guarantees you'll lose it to someone with nothing to lose.

As a result, the PVE content suffers immensely. ARCS are actively avoided because engaging them requires gear that is too risky to bring. This problem is even worse for new or casual players, because they cannot acquire and retain the gear needed to take down bigger ARCS. This means a lot of quests cannot be fulfilled unless you are blessed with a kind lobby, or you simply join the rats and steal from others. The PVE aspect essentially becomes useless then, and you are left in a frustrating loop where ignoring half of the advertised experience is the only way to progress.

The longer this continues, the worse this problem will become. As the weeks go by and the player base becomes more established, new and casual players will have an increasingly difficult time acquiring the materials needed to upgrade their workstations. Without upgraded workstations, they cannot craft better gear, meaning they cannot engage with PVE content, which is required for the majority of quests. This leaves them effectively stuck at the start of the game.

The aggression-based matchmaking does little to alleviate the loadout problem, because in most matches, there are still multiple highly aggressive players who camp the spawn and extraction zones and kill Raiders, again risking little on their part. Some of these spawn camp deaths also occur while the players load in, as mid-game spawns are enabled and poorly protected from trolls.

If this game were marketed as a PVP extraction shooter or a PVE co-op game, there would be no problem with either playstyle. However, the current blend of both creates an unfun, toxic and exhausting loop full of childish people who cannot work cooperatively and who believe that shooting people in the back while risking none of their own gear is the only way to enjoy the game. And any attempt to raise these concerns is routinely dismissed as a skill issue on the new player's part, despite the fact that the game actively rewards low-risk griefing over meaningful cooperation or PVE battles.

TLDR - ARC Raiders is supposed to be a blend of PVP and PVE, but its current systems reward zero-risk PVP with free loadouts while punishing anyone engaging with PVE. This makes progression nearly impossible for new and casual players, and any criticism is brushed off by the community as a 'skill issue', despite the issue being rooted in the poor balance and design.

Edit - If you're still not convinced that the community has a problem, just take a look at my comments. And shout out to all you people proving my point, your lack of basic reading comprehension makes my day.

This game is incredible, and it deserves all of it's popularity. However, I strongly suggest you do not buy this game if your sole or primary interest is in the PvE or story content, if you intend to play exclusively solo, or if you have trust issues. 

I originally purchased this game because I love Embark's other game THE FINALS. Before launch, there was a bit of a rumour mill regarding the stories being interlinked. As of writing this review, this does not appear to be the case. From what I can tell, the stories were briefly interlinked before the game was rebooted halfway through development, when it developed it's own identity. The story is actively growing with each patch/event. However, as of now, I personally feel that the quests in the game do not do much to expand the story as a whole. They seem to mainly exist to teach the player how to play the game. They are also extremely repetitive, with most of them being (go to X and touch Y) or (go to X find Y and extract). There is a codex but presently it's pretty incomplete, even after finishing all the quests. I expect it to expand as the live service continues. All of this being said, I still find that I am excited to learn more about the ARC and where they are coming from.

Speaking of the ARC, they are my favorite part of this setting. Being a Helldivers fan, the PvE engagements are something I was really looking forward to going in. The way Embark utilizes machine learning animation really adds a liveliness of each different type of ARC and how they react to having parts blown off of them. Flying ARC will react accordingly to each of their thrusters being removed and walking ARC like the Leaper will flail around upon having their legs removed. The Matriarch and Queen are, as of writing this, the endgame bosses in ARC Raiders. These are crazy multi-stage fights that often require you to mount the robot Monster Hunter style and shoot into it's core while dodging waves of smaller ARC and missiles. You do all of this while trying to prevent it from stepping on you. In the case of the Queen, there's an entire puzzle for you to solve while it's shooting at you. HOWEVER, keep in mind that this is a PvPvE game, and you will often be murdered on sight for no reason while attempting the bosses, even on the friendly servers. In my opinion, Embark made a massive misstep in confirming the existence of aggression based matchmaking. This has resulted in the complete and total gamification of the matchmaking system by the playerbase. If you want peaceful lobbies to kill ARC, playing passively and avoiding PvP will get you there. The downside of this playstyle is that it removes the motivating factor in this type of game, risk. Additionally, even in the peaceful lobbies, you'll often find rats and gankers looking for easy targets and intentionally dropping themselves into the friendly lobbies to camp players killing the big ARC for their gear. This leads me into my next point.

A lot of people find the extraction shooter genre particularly abrasive, myself included. In my experience, Arc Raiders is not the exception to this rule. Any gear you bring in will be lost upon death. Veterans of this genre know that you will be shot dead the second you DARE to bring in something you care about or have invested a lot of time/cash in. Whatever malevolent spirit they have manifested into the servers for this game WILL know. The moment it senses you coveting something, it will spawn a rocketeer 50 meters away to vaporize you. This, combined with the rampant extraction exploits and players clipping themselves inside walls means you'll often find yourself losing your hard earned loot to invisible players. Embark has been fairly proactive in rolling back exploit related deaths. However, you cannot count on this happening every time.

Speaking of loot, the relative weapon rarity to lethality as of the time of writing is one of the weirdest I've ever seen in a title like this. The majority of legendary/epic weapons will frequently lose out to upgraded common/rare weapons that can be mass produced with basic materials. This is interesting considering several of the beforementioned legendary/epic weapons require special materials from endgame bosses like the Matriarch or the Queen. Excluding weapons designed for specific purposes like the Hullcracker, in most situations you'll be running grey/green weapons. Rarer crafted weapons tend to be reserved for flexing in front of friends or high roller runs simply for the fun of doing so.

[b] TLDR: If you like PvP buy this game. If you want to fight big bosses or enjoy the quests/story go play something like Monster Hunter. 80% of the text/dialogue in this game is AI generated anyway. The high tier loot sucks, just run Ferro/Stitcher and you win pretty much every fight [/b]

The fact that you don't know, and can't choose what kind of experience you want is just oversight by the developers. There are alot of good things about this game, but something has to change. But trying to complete simple missions in Stellar Montis (for example) with your friends feels more like trying to take down a brick wall with my skull. It's more frustrating than it is fun now. I’m leaving this review to make people aware of a growing issue with ARC Raiders’ banning system.

I received a 30-day ban with no explanation. I don’t cheat, I’m respectful in-game, and I’m a casual player. Like many others, I was told to contact support — which appears to be largely AI-driven. The response I received simply stated that the ban was “correctly issued,” with no details, no evidence, and no option to appeal.

The biggest concern is that no reason is ever given. This means that after the 30 days are up, there’s nothing stopping the same thing from happening again — especially if the anti-cheat system is automated and prone to false flags. Even if you’ve done nothing wrong.

After spending around £40 on the game, being locked out for a month with zero transparency is incredibly frustrating. Judging by community discussions, this isn’t an isolated case — the number of affected players seems to be growing.

People can take this review however they like, but I feel it’s important to be honest: this can happen to you, and if it does, you’re essentially powerless to challenge it.

"Not worth it" should be the slogan for this game.  This game has diminishing returns for gaining and using good gear, participating in special events, and even using most of the game's features beyond PvP with a free loadout.  In its current state, the weapons that you get with a free load out are some of the best PvP weapons in the game, and the free loadout also gives you two random utility items that can be fairly valuable.  The meta for the game is just to get a free kit and find people to shoot in the back while they're looting or camp a spawn because the free kit weapons are better at PvP than some rare and epic weapons, and you're risking nothing.  This is exactly what half of the players in the game do almost 100% of the time.

I did the character reset for a few added perks, and that is also 100% not worth it unless you like mindlessly grinding the same mats, through the same game stages, on the same 5 maps over and over again for perks that are marginal, at best.  At least the gopher delivery quests on some of these RPG games add some small amount of variation.  Although there are a few very useful skills, there is very little difference between level 0 and level 5 in many, if not most of the skills.  I've done side-by-side tests of the leveled skills with friends, and there is barely any noticeable difference.  So reseting your character (and your blueprints) for 5 extra skill points and a few other small perks is absolutely not worth it unless you like grinding a lot, in which case you would probably enjoy an MMORPG or playing Candy Crush more.

Most of doing well in this game involves looking up the specific rooms and spots with high loot value (hint: they're not always in the areas marked as high loot value on the map).  So the game mainly involves looking on Reddit to find the best loot rooms and repetitively grabbing a free loadout or going in with nothing and trying to beat the other 15 players on the map who are all scrambling for the same loot rooms.  Often, the locked rooms that require a key don't even give loot that is as good as these loot rooms, so it's often not even worth it to spend the hour repeatedly trying to get to a locked key room before someone else does.  And the keys each individually take up a spot in your laughably small stash.

Graphically, the game is beautiful.  The audio is great, although the sounds are over the top (the sound of footsteps, breaching, or someone looting a drawer from 2 floors above/below you will drawn out the sound of someone talking right next to you), the supersonic bullet cracks happen any time a bullet comes within 300 yards of you which is unrealistic and makes it useless for determining if you're actually being shot at, and they need a way to turn off the stupid rooster sound when you're in the home screen. 

Unfortunately, there are a lot of basic game mechanics that don't seem to have been thought out at all.  The main bottleneck for getting better items, and being able to make the items so you can actually bring them into matches without worrying about losing them to a free loadout camper, is blueprints.  But the blueprints are based on RNG luck and grinding away at rushes to the handful of good loot rooms during night raids and special events. Until you have the right blueprints to use the crafting materials, your stash rapidly fills up with crafting materials and random loot bloat. Then you're stuck wasting 5-10 minutes playing Tetris with your stash between every match. You're usually out in a match for 10-15 minutes before extracting, so that means you're spending a large portion of your total time in the game just screwing around with your stash.  Even considering that different weapons are intended to serve different purposes, there are also weapon imbalances that are so obvious, they should've been caught before play testing even happened.  The rare tier light machine gun is objectively worse than the basic tier sub machine guns (that you can get for free) in almost every regard in PvP, roughly equal against Arc, and exponentially more expensive. The LMG is like driving a Mitsubishi Eclipse - it looks cool and sounds cool, but it is overpriced, underpowered, and doesn't do anything well.

It's almost like being at a casino.  The shiny epic and rare items, major game events, expeditions, and other major game features - just like the shiny and colorful sections of the craps table - are the least worth it.  And the game, in general, gets extremely repetitive after you learn the basic game mechanics and map layouts.  That's not to mention the PvP experience, which is fairly highly populated with people cheating with macros and aim bots.  Most of the duo and trio PvP matches just end up like a mosh pit of every team on the map converging on the first firefight that they hear.  PvP-focused Deathmatch games and games like Helldivers have a much more enjoyable experience for PvP and/or playing with friends.

A masterclass in wasted potential.

Arc Raiders is, on a purely visual and technical level, an impressive game. The art direction is striking, the world design is evocative, and the core mechanics suggest something that could have been genuinely special. Unfortunately, all of that promise is completely undermined by one of the most toxic player communities I’ve encountered in recent years.

Player cooperation is virtually nonexistent. Despite the game being clearly designed with shared spaces and emergent interactions in mind, the reality is the exact opposite. There is no sense of camaraderie, no organic teamwork, no “we’re in this together” dynamic. Instead, the dominant culture is one of predation. Veteran players actively hunt and prey on new players, exploiting every imbalance and knowledge gap with ruthless efficiency.

Never have I seen a game where negative reinforcement is this pervasive. The player is not rewarded with the satisfaction of a good run or a well-played match. Instead, the emotional baseline is survival without misery. A “good game” isn’t one where you had fun—it’s one where you didn’t have an absolutely awful experience. Enjoyment is replaced by relief, which is a catastrophic failure of game design and community management.

New players are not taught, guided, or integrated. They are punished. Mistakes aren’t part of a learning curve; they’re an invitation to be farmed. Any attempt at experimentation, cooperation, or casual play is immediately crushed by players who seem more invested in gatekeeping than in sustaining the game’s future.

What makes this especially frustrating is that the game didn’t fail because it’s broken or unfinished. It failed because it allowed a culture where cruelty replaces challenge and dominance replaces skill expression. The tension doesn’t come from the world or the enemies—it comes from other players who treat newcomers as disposable content.

This is not “hardcore gameplay.” This is a hostile ecosystem that actively drives players away. Unless you are already deeply embedded in the meta and willing to participate in that toxicity, the experience is exhausting, joyless, and demoralizing.

Zero stars. A great-looking game ruined by a community that thrives on negativity.

I’m writing on behalf of myself and my wife.

Since ARC Raiders was first announced in 2021, we’ve been genuinely excited to play it. We fell in love with the theme, the atmosphere, and especially the premise presented in the trailers: a strong PvE experience, rich world-building, and intense action against the machines that took over Earth.

We started playing at the end of December, and while there is so much we love about the game, we’ve reached a point where continuing has become too frustrating for us.

We are not hardcore players. We like to play casually, usually 1–2 hours a day. We don’t enjoy competitive PvP, and we are not fans of FPS PvP combat. What we do love is everything related to PvE in ARC Raiders:

- exploring unknown maps
- scavenging for items
- building loadouts
- crafting grenades and upgrading weapons
- unlocking new gear
- and learning how to defeat the ARC machines

That loop is incredibly fun and rewarding for us.

Today perfectly summarizes both how great, and how discouraging, the experience can be.

We managed to destroy our first Rocketeer, just the two of us. It was an amazing moment, pure joy. This happened on Dam Battlegrounds, a map classified as difficulty level 2. We felt proud, excited, and rewarded for working together.

But as soon as we went to collect the loot, we were attacked by two other players who had clearly been waiting for us to finish the fight. All that effort, gone in seconds.

We took a deep breath and decided to try again. This time, we attempted to complete one of the traders’ quests. While inside a building, we spotted other Raiders. I used proximity chat to calmly say that we were there and that we were friendly. Instead of any form of cooperation or avoidance, we were ambushed.

That was the breaking point for us.

Unfortunately, the PvPvE structure has made the experience extremely discouraging. Even on North America servers, often described as the most “friendly”, we constantly encounter players camping exits, elevators, or mission areas. Instead of focusing on exploration and missions, we spend most of our time hiding or being ambushed by other players.

We understand that matchmaking is intended to pair players with similar skill levels, but that has not been our experience at all. We know we will never reach a competitive PvP level, and that’s okay, we simply don’t want to.

On top of that, we’ve encountered very unpleasant behavior through proximity chat. Some interactions have been outright toxic, dishonest, or disturbing. For us, this does not feel like a safe or welcoming environment.

Because of all this, after almost 100 hours invested, we made the difficult decision to stop playing, for now.

We are writing because we truly care about ARC Raiders. We love the world you created, the gameplay systems, and the progression. We respectfully ask you to consider adding a PvE-only mode, where players can opt out of PvP entirely and focus on missions, exploration, and cooperation without fear of player ambushes or harassment.

We know many players love the PvP aspect, and we respect that. We are simply asking for a space for players like us, who cherish the PvE experience and want to enjoy the game at our own pace.

Thank you for your time, your hard work, and for creating a game we genuinely want to come back to.

Arc Raiders – 200+ Ping Isn’t a “Global Release”

Arc Raiders has potential, but it’s crippled by terrible netcode and the complete lack of regional servers. Players in unsupported regions are stuck at 200+ ping, which at this point is low-key unbearable. Desync, delayed hit registration, and inconsistent fights are the norm, not the exception.

What makes this worse is the silence. Nine months later, there’s still no acknowledgment, no roadmap, and not a single developer response on the official Reddit addressing server availability. It genuinely feels like being ignored and hoping players just give up.

And the frustrating part? We’re not even asking for perfect conditions. 110 ping would be a massive improvement. A server placed in the Middle East, for example, would go a long way toward making the game actually playable for entire regions. Other studios manage this — even Call of Duty, with just one server, showed that basic inclusion matters. its even commonly said our servers 30 tick.

Instead, Arc Raiders charges the same price globally while delivering a drastically worse experience depending on location. Same cost, different product, and zero communication about it.

This isn’t about “fancy servers.” It’s about transparency, respect, and acknowledging that players outside the US and EU exist.

Arc Raiders’ inventory and crafting systems often feel more punitive than strategic. Stash space fills up quickly with low-value materials, forcing frequent cleanup that interrupts momentum between raids. Limited sorting, filtering, and search tools make managing loot unnecessarily tedious, especially as progression increases.

Crafting, while functional, is tightly coupled to inventory pressure rather than meaningful choice. Instead of feeling like an expressive progression system, it often serves as a way to compress clutter into fewer items. Material importance isn’t always clear, leading players to hoard or rely on external guides, and early crafting mistakes can feel costly due to space and resource constraints.

This game has a genuinely strong foundation, but in its current form, I cannot recommend it.   It was advertised as a blend of PVP and PVE, yet the way the community behaves, combined with how the loadout system works, has effectively erased the PVE experience. Most players default to kill‑on‑sight, or worse, pretend to cooperate only to betray you moments later. That wouldn’t be a deal‑breaker if the risk–reward balance supported that style of play. 

Right now, there’s almost no reason to bring high‑tier gear into a raid. A player using the free starter loadout can still reliably take down someone risking valuable equipment. The result is a meta where hoarding becomes mandatory, because bringing anything worthwhile practically guarantees you’ll lose it to someone who has nothing at stake.

It is a chores simulator with the gameplay of a sample project from a game engine (you would understand if you ever tried a game engine).

I already have a job and I don't want a second one without pay.

I’ve put 360+ hours into this game, so this isn’t coming from someone who hasn’t given it a fair chance. When the game works, it can be genuinely great. Looting is satisfying, fighting the ARC is fun, and the combat itself feels excellent. The player-versus-player side can also be brilliant — some of the most enjoyable moments I’ve had come from fair fights.

Unfortunately, those moments are becoming rare.

Cheating is a serious problem. I’ve personally encountered aimbot, wall hacks (players clearly knowing where you are with no line of sight), and wall/roof breach exploits. It’s not subtle, and it’s not occasional — it actively ruins matches. On top of that, desync makes already unfair fights feel completely out of your control.

Extraction camping is another major issue. It’s extremely common and honestly feels encouraged by the game’s design. Too many runs end not because of bad decisions, but because someone is sitting waiting at extraction with nothing you can realistically do about it.

Matchmaking only makes things worse. You get put against obvious cheaters, huge skill imbalances, and sometimes loaded into matches late due to poor timers — putting you at a disadvantage before you even start. No matter how well you play or how good your PvP skills are, the game often feels unfair regardless of effort or performance.

The most disappointing part is the developer response. Fixes feel slow, communication is lacking, and hearing that cheaters may only receive 30-day suspensions doesn’t inspire confidence. It gives the impression that cheating isn’t being taken seriously enough for a game that relies so heavily on fairness and tension.

This game has a lot of potential, and I wouldn’t have spent hundreds of hours if it didn’t. But in its current state, between cheating, exploits, desync, extraction camping, and poor matchmaking, I can’t recommend it. It needs stronger enforcement, faster fixes, and a real focus on making the experience fair again.

This game has an identity crisis and suffers from it. Appealing to the overly casual PVE audience and the overly aggressive PVP audience makes an uncomfortable blend for both players. There is no real risk, as there is little to no incentive to bring in high-tier loot, as there is nothing preventing people from spamming free-loadouts resulting in constant shoot on sight gameplay. Rarely have I ever encountered any players who are friendly, and whenever they are at first, it always results in a betrayal. This isn't an issue on paper, as I am used to this kind of gameplay with Sea Of Thieves, but what Sea Of Thieves does much better is that it offers a way for PVP players to do PVP and PVE players to do PVE. This prevents the two groups from clashing, and increases the enjoyment for both greatly. I would be more inclined to play this game if there was a way for both niches to play their game their own way without having to worry about being shot in the back at all times while also being blown up by rocketeers. 
Edit: SOT offers the tense feeling of high risk high reward through a blend of PVE and PVP similar to Arc Raiders, which can be fun to engage with! But for a more casual experience and fulfilling experience, it offers the best of all worlds by including everything from casual to the utmost competitive. (this makes the game good) PS. If you see Emu Boy out there in Speranza, guide as many rocketeers to him as possible... After betraying him ofc.

This game is EXTREMELY buged, people cheating DO NOT get perma-banned and they do not fix bugs like people shooting through walls, because of a glitch....PEACE OF SHIT OF A GAME!! DO NOT BUY

Any player can load into a game with a free loadout and still reliably kill someone who is risking valuable gear. This creates a situation where hoarding gear isn't just encouraged, but required, because bringing anything good almost guarantees you'll lose it to someone with nothing to lose.

Therefore, the PVE content suffers immensely. ARCS are actively avoided because engaging them requires gear that is too risky to bring. This problem is even worse for new or casual players, because they cannot acquire and retain the gear needed to take down bigger ARCS. This means a lot of quests cannot be fulfilled unless you are blessed with a kind lobby, or you simply join the rats and steal from others. The PVE aspect essentially becomes useless then, and you are left in a frustrating loop where ignoring half of the advertised experience is the only way to progress.

The longer this continues, the worse this problem will become. As the weeks go by and the player base becomes more established, new and casual players will have an increasingly difficult time acquiring the materials needed to upgrade their workstations. Without upgraded workstations, they cannot craft better gear, meaning they cannot engage with PVE content, which is required for the majority of quests. This leaves them effectively stuck at the start of the game.

So to state again...DO NOT BUY THIS SHIT OF A GAME, you will lose your money in a BS of a game

This game has so much potential that just isn't there yet. Don't get me wrong, the graphics are FANTASTIC! But there really....nothing to do...except walk and walk and walk until you reach the weather tower. You can't really craft or build or hunt which would make it the #1 game out there if you could. I'd say 95% of the objects you see in the game are interactable...but you can't use them for anything so it's kind of pointless. 
To Sum Up The Game, It's:
-Walking, Walking, Walking
-Making a fire
-Finding Cabins
-Fixing their windows
-Walking Walking Walking
-Weather Tower.

I really hope the Devs have a road map they can share for the future of this game because again, SO MUCH POTENTIAL. My fingers are crossed that down the line, this game makes me put my foot in my mouth.

I played the game for 30 hr and I do love the genre. I have hundreds of hours logged in hardcore survival mode in the long dark... Initial challenge is nice and required quite a steep learning curve, but after 10 hr I mastered the navigation and managed 30+ days in survival mode (no cabins) Then it quickly showed that the world is actually quite empty. Not a lot of variety in biomes (endless forests) Only very limited relevant gear and actually nothing further to aim for (except exploring the huge map that is the same everywhere you go). Love the gerne, and this game definitively scratched an itch for a while, but quite shallow at the moment.
The beta test worled better than this does right now. I cant even read the letters on the compass because they are so blurry. I love the concept of the game, but this isnt worth paying for.

Was very excited to play this when it release in EA, and was extremely disappointed by what I've seen.
As many have stated, it just feels like there is no game here, just a tech demo of weather changes and survival mechanics (many of which are lackluster or plain missing at the moment).

It does show promise, and can become an amazing  survival experience in the future — but I've been burned enough times by early access titles to not recommend this (for now).

If this works out, it will be amazing, but for now... meh

It's a non-glorified walking simulator that feels like a poorly optimized tech-demo. It's way too resource intensive on the PC and there's very little to do besides walking around, hoping you find a hut with useful items and even then the items feel like they make 0 impact. I drank 5 bottles of soda and ate two tins of spam and somehow am still not even above halfway on the hunger and thirst meters. Whatever black hole they found at the centre of our galaxy is nothing compared to the black hole that is apparently the stomach of our character.

It would be fine as a free demo if they're looking to just gather some feedback, but it's worth precisely $0 as it stands. It's a cool concept, and I'll likely revisit it down the road, but it's so devoid of anything engaging that calling it "early access" seems like a gross exaggeration. Based on their roadmap, I think they've jumped the gun by several milestones.

The game is poorly optimised, the randomization means you may just start in a spot where there's not enough food and water to even get going, and the map is rather non-explanatory. 

With som balancing, more features, and added functionality this can be a lovely single player survival game. It isn't there yet. It's like playing a pre-beta release, not just "Early Access".

I know it's early access, but charging money for this slop is awful.

The game is so unrefined, the graphics, even on extreme are awful, and the general functionality is both unrealistic and boring. It's overall just incredibly boring to play as well, which is disappointing considering how good the ads for the game looked.

The feel of the game is also very rushed, with very very little backstory, introduction or tutorial to the game. Even for early access, this game is incredibly raw and generally unplayable.

Don't buy it at this price, in fact I'd just say to save your time and money and not get it at all.

I played the game for 30 hr and I do love the genre. I have hundreds of hours logged in hardcore survival mode in the long dark... Initial challenge is nice and required quite a steep learning curve, but after 10 hr I mastered the navigation and managed 30+ days in survival mode (no cabins) Then it quickly showed that the world is actually quite empty. Not a lot of variety in biomes (endless forests) Only very limited relevant gear and actually nothing further to aim for (except exploring the huge map that is the same everywhere you go). Although I actually like this game, I have to downvote because I do not recommend it to anyone. Accept a loser mindset because you will die 99.9% of your runs, especially if you don't know how to read a topographical map. This game is brutally hard and unrewarding. it's confusing because it wants to take itself seriously and wants to be immersive but immersion is constantly broken with insane weather patterns, time being on super fast forward and most of your time somehow in the pitch black dark. Peaceful daylight hours are rare and fleeting, along with your water and food intake being absurd. All of these systems are so broken that you have to use unrealistic gamer instincts to over come most challenges. The maps are highly unrealistic with random cabins thrown about in unrealistic gamer patterns once you understand how it works. This is not a chill game. its a sprint to the finish across an extremely hard to get across terrain. My only real complaint is that the primary survival mechanics are far too unrealistic when it's trying to be. The only way to actually survive is to play like a gamer and not think realistically. 
Also, the only update so far has been in the wrong direction for immersion. They seem to be focused on electrical power systems to cabins that realistically you shouldn't spend more than 5mins at. Not the one forgiving system, which was finding working stoves is going to be far harder to come by. The stoves are actually such an absolute key to basic survival that it's comically unrealistic even further. 
This game has a lot of development to go through for just being a passion project. 
As of right now it's just an arcade running survival simulator racing game. It's different than likely anything you've ever played, do not come into it with expectations.
The beta test worled better than this does right now. I cant even read the letters on the compass because they are so blurry. I love the concept of the game, but this isnt worth paying for.

Frustrating UI and inventory system. Unintuitive items and combinations. Trying to figure out why a hut's power is out and accidentally removing the circuit breakers, and being unable to put them back is frustrating. Navigation in the dark is punishing. Anything the height of a chihuahua will stop you in your tracks. Has promise but needs a fair amount of work at the time of this review.

I love the idea of the game but it has the same flaw as all survival games I know; functional fixedness. In this game, only bottles can collect water. Don't try leaving a large saucepan outside, it won't collect rain. You eat tin cans whole apparently. There is no way to do anything with the empty tin can. You can't make tinder out of a dry stick.
When I go camping, empty tin cans will become small stoves, improvised shovels or coffee mugs if they need to.
A bush craft game should allow you to get creative and make do with the few resources you have on hand by overcoming functional fixedness and coming up with new ways to use a comb that MacGyver would be proud of.
I hoped that "emergent survival" meant that simple game mechanics allowed for creative solutions but not yet. Hopefully, as the game evolves, it will allow for more creative uses of the various items and game mechanics.

Given it's in early access it has all the possibilities to become an amazing game with more updates. The concept of the game is really cool - randomizable winter survival game. But currently the game is neither good or bad.

During 1.5 hours of gameplay I haven't faced any major bugs, problems or glitches. However, the game itself is kinda empty and therefore boring. 

Optimization is not great either. As the games poorly with a 4070ti super. Lots of freezes while in the forest.
While being priced at the Long Dark level this game gives you much less content, and therefore this review is negative.

I bought this after seeing an article on my Google home page which made it sound phenomenal. But I quickly found the routine of: run straight in any direction and you eventually hit a cabin. Light a fire, close the windows, eat all the food, swap any gear; your body temp is back over 100 degrees by now; so it's time to take off running straight again and eventually hit another cabin. Repeat repeat repeat. The backpack/gear management is "interesting".  Climbing around rocks is terrible. You'll fall the hell off all the cliffs and ladders. I think I made it to the weather station, but fell the hell off of whatever tower I was climbing and that was a wrap for me. With unlimited running/no running out of stamina there's nothing to force me to actually plan and survive between cabins in the wilderness. I can see there is potential there. Especially if you're a TLD fan and prefer the looting vs base building/crafting gameplay style, this might be more up your alley. But there is absolutely not yet 20$ worth of gameplay there for me. I played past Steams refund point so they would not grant a refund. Bummer. Oh well I'll check back in on it in a year. This game clearly has a lot of potential, but unfortunately it feels extremely unpolished in its current state. Several of the audio cues and message boxes are still in German, which makes basic actions like saving or closing pop-ups confusing and frustrating.

Even on Easy mode the game feels poorly balanced. The constant police takeovers of your production facilities become overwhelming to the point of absurdity — at one point I had so many factories shut down that it became impossible to keep up, which completely killed the fun.

I really wanted to enjoy this and give it the benefit of the doubt, but unless I’m missing something (especially since I don’t speak or read German), the game is borderline unplayable in its current form. Very disappointing.  :(

A wholly unconvincing attempt at capturing 'old-school' nostalgia. 

Despite boasting an enticing open world with a plethora of regions to explore, they are, unsurprisingly, bland and uninviting. 350+ quests, yet not a single quest falls outside of the cookie cutter, fetch/return or kill X amount of Y. With 0 memorable characters, questlines or engaging character dialogue, everyone and every area becomes forgettable and a slog to play through. Similarly, traversing the world seems entirely without thought, due to the lack of teleportation and restriction of mounts until you're 2/3 of the way through available content. With this being the case, if you dare to explore your own way, by the time you get to certain regions, you will be completely over-levelled, leading to every single quest in a region being pointless as XP rewards scale with level. Dungeons/Elite mobs are, in all respects, inaccessible to solo players unless you are drastically over-levelled, at which point the quest tied to them will grant no real effective reward so are pointless. While one may argue that Dungeons are made to be completed in a group, they provide no interesting gameplay to engage the players outside of mobs having more health and damage.

The community is nice despite being made up of max level players simply waiting for more content, and I can appreciate how much work the solo dev has put into this game. Nevertheless, the game is awful and is an insult to time investment for what it has to offer.

[quote][url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AI_slop][b]Warning: AI Slop[/b][/url]
This game is partially or completely made with talentless, soulless AI generated content.[/quote]

"DSS war party" is a badly done attempt to make an RTS game using AI slop and "vibe coding" (when you don't know how to make a game but want to do it anyway, ChatGPT is there to make gaming worse for everyone).  It was a complete disaster.  The ability of the developer to vibe code the game couldn't come close to the ambition here... the game barely works, crashes frequently, and that's if you can get it to run at all.

If it worked, sure, you'd get a weak ripoff of far better games like Age of Empires or a Total War game from AliExpress, but looking more like a reject from 2002. 

As mentioned, this features really godawful, "wrong" looking AI slop instead of genuinely and sincerely created game assets.  It's hard to say if this was because the developer didn't have the skill to do the job of creating real game assets or couldn't afford to hire someone who does, but it also doesn't matter.  What matters is that this looks bad because of that decision.  AI slop sticks out like a sore thumb... and it comes across as insulting.

There's also the old adage that those who lack the budget and talent to make their own game assets usually lack the budget and talent to do the rest of the job of game development properly, too, and that's glaringly obvious here.

Now on to pricing and value for gamers.

This is "free" enough (not every "free" game in the "free" section is truly free, there's usually some hidden price tag), at least you won't be expected to spend any more money after you download this, but just because something is free doesn't mean it's good, and the cost of a game is more than just financial, your time is valuable too.

In terms of public reception, this failed to appeal to the 130 million+ gamers on Steam.  SteamDB showed the game peaked at just a dozen or so concurrent players, and now there's basically nobody playing it.  Even though it's free.  This is a dismal public reception, but it's easy to see why gamers rejected it.

Given that this free title won't increase your game collector count, is it worth your time to add it to your library?

Definitely not.  This isn't worth it.  I'm paraphrasing another reviewer here, but this should be a paid product... they'd have to pay people to download it.

I'm hoping to give a better review than the other ones that I've seen here that seem to be positive without giving any useful information.

This game is trying to be an idle and casual / simplified version of Eve Online. I've tried all 3 initially available playstyles: mining / combat / salvaging, and honestly: they all seem to play the same. You go to a hub (space station), dock, go to the mission board, grab a quest, open your map, fast travel to that place, then either mine / salvage / kill any amount until you get the quest done, then go back to the station, turn in the quest, gain a minute amount of XP / money / faction reputation, then....do it again until you're the next level to go to the next sector to do it again, but the level is +1 now.

Clearly, the game is not shy about being an idler because when the gameplay loop is that repetitive, you're gonna [b]want[/b] it to play by itself. It's not that [i]that[/i] in itself is the problem, but rather that you're constantly asking [b]what am I working towards?[/b] A bigger ship? A main storyline / quest? Just to get to the highest level and see what lies beyond? Maybe the first few hours are just way too slow because I'm not sure where all of this is going, and when you have 10 hours into a game and you're still wondering [b]what am I doing here?[/b], then maybe the game needs to be re-examined a bit.

There [b]IS[/b] huge potential for this to become a great, huge, tremendous game and it [b]CAN[/b] become fantastic, but currently it oozes "Early Access" where even the skill tree has 3 tiers and "REST TO BE ADDED", so all I can do for now is also wait for the game to [i]maybe[/i] get to that level, but it's not here now.

Someone in another review called this game "disrespectful of your time" and that hits the nail on the head. Never have I seen a game with so much idle time (even when you are actively playing).

The game, for example, often expects you to travel several minutes between main missions back and forth. I am currently stuck on a main mission, because to jump between main systems, you need a jump gate pass (another of the artificial barriers the game puts up). And I once made the mistake to bulk buy and consume them, in order to "to solve the problem once and for all". But apparently the jump gate pass is not registered when not consumed directly next to the jump gate. So I have now to wait 20 real time minutes for the vendor menu to respwan, so that I can rebuy my jump gate pass. And while we ware at buying things: Earning money is, you guessed it, also snail speed slow in this game. Effectively forcing you to use the even less effective auto-pilot/idle portion of the game, to grind out the money for new ships.

The game also gate-keeps you out of 2/3 of its content, because missions require one of the three class arch type ship configurations. You can technically use all arch types (even the ones that do not match your initial pick), put for that you have to fly to a space station and swap your weapons there, so that you can do that mission type (oh, who would have guessed it - another time sink). 

The combat itself is fun (although not very complex) and here is somewhere a fun game below when you are willing to pay the disrespectful time-sink costs of the game.
Interesting concept, but in my oppinion not worth it's money (yet)

I would like to recommend this game as it is actually kind of fun.  Plus I really like the ability to run it in the background.  However the full screen version of the game uses an obscene amount of GPU and therefore Power/Electricity for a 2d sprite game.  It uses the same amount of GPU as a full 3d game like World of Tanks. So fun game but if you have a really old system with limited resources it may not be for you.
UPDATE:  I ran a program to check power usage.  Without VG the GPU power usage is 25w with VG running Full screen in the background the power usage more than doubles to 60w.  So an increase of 45w.  Basically 1kw per day.  Where I am at a KW costs 28 cents so you are talking about around $8.50 a month to run this game 24/7 in the background for a month and $102 for a full year.  Transparent and Performance modes are a little better at 50w but are unusable for me as they cannot be minimized to run in the background and take up a full 1/3rd of my screen when running. So not happening and it's still a 25w increase then. My GPU is an Nvidia GTX 1660 Super.
So my recommendation is a HARD NO at this point.  To bad, I have 360 hours into this in the background at this point. Maybe the devs will do something to fix this.

Definitively has potential, but atm it ist basically beta testing and has absolutely no entertainment factor. Dont get me wrong. i like games where you have to grind for progress. But if the "fun" is artificially limited, and you are wasting time on meaningless things, it is not grind, but time wasting. Or maybe i just dont get it why "idle" games exist from first principle. E.g. why do i have to watch 10 seconds of docking animation every time? why do i have to wait 10 minutes until 20 pieces of artifacts are extracted? why are prices exhorbitantly high for better ships / weapons where i have to do boring riskfree missions multiple dozens of times just to buy a minimal upgrade. 
Too many tedious tasks which feel like player should not be here for fun, but for chores. 
If you have a full time job, maybe family, and you want to relax for 30 minutes or even want to have some fun at the end of the day, then this game is not for you, at least now.
If you have nothing to do, you do like chores and repeating tasks just to keep you mind off something, then yes you can definitively burry tens of hours here doing basically nothing.

Now for possible improvement, b/c i would like to see Indie games succeed and i think here are definitively some potentials:
1. remove the named chores / unnecessary time wasters 
2. make encounters more engaging, give player possibilities to choose risk + rewards with bigger spectrum to choose from. nothing more boring than 0 risk low reward missions.
4. rework ship control. it is a complete unintuitive (long click to move), make standard left click move right click attack or any other established key / mouse or controller combos.
5. rework the complete UI. its really buggy, messy and not intuitive.

TL;DR at bottom.

An idle game that loves to waste your time.

Combat is atrocious. You are constantly outnumbered and the enemy are in ships that aren't even built for combat but are better and stronger than your own combat ship meant for combat. It makes no sense. They only use the best weapons, which are the same 3 weapons and it's annoying seeing the enemy spam this. Combat ships for some stupid reason get lower health than salvage/mining ships. They have so much HP and when fighting 2 or more, they wear you down so fast and you die. When traveling into an area, your ship will just sit there with enemies nearby, not shooting or moving. They'll only fight back once they've been hit which is so stupid. Your ship will also do just random crap, flying randomly and not being smart about the range of your weapons. I have to constantly order the AI of my ship to get into range to use my weapons depending on what my ship has. I can't be smart other times like stay back and use missiles only because your ships horrible AI wants to keep kamikazeing itself into the enemy ships and get itself surrounded and killed. The ship selection for combat is very poor. There are way too many mining and salvaging ships and barely any combat ships. Combat is not fun, poorly designed, has terrible performing AI, and is overall very poorly balanced. 

Mission rewards are a joke. They barely give you any XP, Money, or decent rewards. When putting on idle mode, all the rewards are now reduced to like 10% of the original reward XP and money you would earn, severely punishing you for, you know, playing this game and participating in the idle mechanic this game is being sold to us as which again, is very bad game design to punish players so heavily for going idle, the thing many of us bought this game for. There are these very small upgrades you can do to reduce this huge slap in the face penalty cost, but each upgrade is only worth a lousy two percent. A complete joke. Overall, upgrades are a joke, only increasing by a lousy 2 or 5 percent. Leveling up in this game doesn't feel fun in the slightest and is a huge slog, taking WAY too long. Putting points into combat, industry, etc all feels so worthless.

Missions are the only way you can do anything in this game. There's no exploring so you can't just go out in a mining or salvaging ship and explore and mine and salvage stuff which is dumb. You can't get into your combat ship and just find enemy ships either which sucks. Another terrible game design flaw is when you leave a mission area, it disappears for no reason. Say you just finished a battle and the enemy ships have salvage. You are forced to go back to a station and switch to either a salvaging ship with salvaging turrets already on them, or just put those turrets on your ship that you're currently using, but when you look at the map to go back, it's gone. Really stupid. A really dumb item called a location beacon or whatever needs to be thrown down just to go back. Should be removed from the game and I should be able to revisit locations I was JUST at.

Bounty hunting is the worst thing in this game. You travel to a location and have to kill all enemies that appear without dying. Fine, but the problem is again, the combat is laughably bad and broken that chances are you will not be able to complete the mission on your first go. In other standard combat missions, you can at least leave, repair at a station, and come back. With these bounty hunting missions, if you leave, the game says you're a stupid idiot and the enemy "flees", meaning you fail and wasted your time. I saw this, laughed, and immediately quit and uninstalled this game. Not gonna deal with that.

Some stations have a bar where some people offer services. One type of person will offer you a weapon or module for your ship and etc. But when you look at the price, they will charge 900% MORE than the VALUE of the item! Why on earth is this a thing? I will look at the item and it says it has a value of 15k credits. WHY on EARTH are they trying to sell it to me for 135k when on the item it CLEARY SAYS the item is worth only 15k!? This is so insanely stupid and genuinely doesn't make any sense.

Oh yeah, also uses AI art. Disgusting.

TL;DR: Horrible and very questionable game mechanics and game design decisions that feel very poorly thought out. Garbage, unfair, broken combat with horribly performing AI that you have to wrestle constantly. Tedious back and forth traveling to waste your time because the devs don't respect your time with pointless game mechanics. XP and credit rewards are a joke when played by yourself, and even more so when turning on idle mode and all rewards go down to like 10% of the original reward.
