I would not recommend the game, as it feels more like a raw trading interface than a relaxing game.

However, step by step:

1. The core idea of the game is interesting. Essentially, you trade goods from locations with a surplus to ports with a deficit.
2. You can build businesses in ports and towns.
3. You can earn reputation, participate in auctions, and so on.
4. You can, of course, upgrade your ship(s).
5. You can automate trading and routing.

That said, the game feels unfinished, as it lacks many elements essential to an enjoyable gaming experience. A few examples:

1. When you build a trade route, you receive virtually no information about town details. You only see a list of towns without any supporting data: no visual map, no information on goods produced or consumed, no buy/sell prices—nothing. As a result, you must constantly close the window to remind yourself of the actual state of the map.
2. The auto-trading features are also very limited. For example, you cannot automatically offload goods from a ship to a warehouse with a condition such as “do not store more than X units in the warehouse.” As a result, you will either continuously accumulate goods or constantly face shortages.
3. The city view is poor. You do not see a list of industries in the main view; instead, you must click a separate button and then apply filters. You also cannot easily see whether any of your businesses have shortages without checking the global quest view. Additionally, you do not see active auctions on the same page, nor information about winning or losing bids.
4. The global view is particularly problematic. You cannot see which ships are sailing, under maintenance, or waiting for orders. There is no auction statistics overview either. Periodically, you receive a “new quest” to pay taxes, which is confusing—taxes should be payable once per turn rather than presented as a quest.
5. The game mechanics are also flawed. After taking control of a new business, you are almost immediately required to deliver goods, without any cooldown period. In a more realistic setup, ownership would begin after some delay following contract signing.

Finally, the game lacks several features that are expected in an economic strategy game:

1. No business upgrades.
2. Extremely simplistic competitor logic: competitors do not use dumping strategies, do not build vertically integrated companies, and do not even upgrade their ships.
3. No options to hedge against risks, such as supply shortages in towns or pirate attacks.

This game follows in the spirit of Patrician 3 and 4. I wanted to like the game but unfortunately there are a lot of polish problems. The tutorial has no walk through guidance just mini goals. No explainers of the UI and steps and expects you to figure out how the basics work. Quests have buggy text and typos. Auctions are bugged. Turn based progress for the game is dated. Ship management to give orders is tedious with out map level controls. I just think this game needs more UX and testing. Its not worth it at the price range currently.

"A Dream Concept with an Unbalanced Reality."

As a massive Civilization fan, the pitch for Rogue Hex felt like it was made specifically for me. The idea of blending deep 4X strategy with deckbuilding and roguelike loops is a dream come true on paper, and initially, I was very optimistic about the loop. There is a clear spark of genius in the mechanics, but unfortunately, that excitement quickly faded as the game’s flaws began to overshadow its potential.

The first major hurdle is the presentation. Visually, the game is quite difficult to look at. The art direction is a total mess, lacking any sense of cohesion or polish. Between the cluttered UI and the character designs—which I can only describe as hideous—the game feels like a collection of mismatched assets rather than a professional product. The "vibe" is just off, and while the music is passable at best, it does nothing to rescue the drab and uninviting atmosphere.

The gameplay is where the most potential lies, but it is currently buried under a mountain of frustration. For a veteran strategy player, the barbarian system is particularly infuriating. They don’t just offer a challenge; they seem to exist outside the rules of the game, scaling at a rate that is consistently ahead of your own technology. This creates an experience that feels less like a fair roguelike "climb" and more like an unbalanced grind where you are punished regardless of your strategy. It is incredibly demoralizing to hit a brick wall every run because the enemies are fundamentally overtuned.

I truly believe Rogue Hex shows promise. If the developer can commit to a major balancing overhaul and clean up the visual identity, this could be a standout title in the genre. However, in its current state, the frustration of the balancing and the lack of polish make it a hard pass. It’s a great idea that just isn’t ready for prime time yet.

AI generated content.

So what if they're a solo dev? I had high hopes for this game, but then they decided to use the plagiarism engine instead of making placeholder art and staying in EA a little longer.

If it was all done instead in the same style of the unit models, nobody would have batted an eye, but now I can't trust whether or not other lazy shortcuts have been taken.

If you can't be bothered to make it, I can't be bothered to play it.

[h2] С.A.S.T.  [/h2]

You’re looking at an indie 4 player cooperative extraction horror shooter with progression and crafting — a combination that immediately caught my attention.
I won’t go into the art style or the title; that’s a matter of taste. But I will say this: despite having five maps, fifteen difficulty levels, various crafting options and skills, as well as different missions and objectives, the game’s meta is quite shallow. You jump in, shoot things, complete a mission, and leave.

It can entertain you for one or two evenings, but there’s really nothing to do beyond grinding. The missions aren’t interesting, the level design isn’t either, and apart from shooting at AI dummies with different weapons, the game has very little to offer the player.

Nah man, nah. I'm definitely leaning towards "A$$" with this game. I don't even understand why it got positive reviews. And NO, I did NOT approach it with the intent to be a hater - rather I was genuinely interested and thought it was a cheap little indie game with ambition. What I got instead was "what if there was an Android bootleg of Helldivers 2 with 'retro' graphics, Lethal Company type artstyle and generic 'zombie survival' gameplay". I know that the game is low budget but everything about it feels mobile/browser game level cheap and it doesn't really feel like something with passion and soul, rather like someone grabbing "trendy" things and mixing them together hoping to create the next viral product.
Not only that, but the game is pretty much UNPLAYABLE in singleplayer (and this is going to be your main experiencce because the game has no players and the devs abandoned it lol) because zero effort was put into balancing the game for solo play - your first mission will start you out with a shitty SMG that needs to be reloaded after like 2 seconds of firing and a shitty little pistol against hordes of enemies that spawn endlessly and always track your position. You have to run through an entire mission like this with a konga line of like 50 enemies on your tail and it genuinely FEELS bad, with a clear message that you were not expected to do this shit solo. Oh, and if you die you basically get jack sh*t. Sounds super fun, right? This is all on the lowest difficulty in the game btw.
Oh, and the best part is that there's only like 5 maps to play on with the same main objectives and you're just expected to grind those over and over again. Yeah, thanks but no thanks. 
Bonus: the game uses AI for the 'voice acting' and a unspecified amount of textures. No, I am NOT someone who will suffer a complete meltdown over AI. I gave this game a chance after all. But sadly the AIslop TTS really gets grating quickly, as you basically have to listen to a shitty TTS program go on long tangents with the tone of a elementary school teacher who is trying to talk slowly and nicely for the children before he goes home to blow his brains out. It's an eye roll for sure.
There's genuinely so many better pick over this - Deep Rock Galactic, Strange Brigade or Zombie Army 4 are way more balanced for singleplayer and have way more content, and playing actual Helldivers 2 would be way more fun than this.
Final verdict:
https://youtu.be/c-_8jBmYHKU?si=5c3AphsfzM85R5tp

Yeah OK.

Take Helldivers 2, take away the map, take away the beautiful graphics, take away the more creative enemy designs, and take away the general polish.

You've got C.A.S.T. It's too bad, cuz in the first few minutes, I was like, "Alright! This has charm. This might be the four-player retro Remnant / Helldivers I'm looking for." But then I played for another 25 minutes. Oh boy. I love an indie, but this does not give 2025 indie game. Instead, this gives 2005 PS2 demo disc games. You know the ones you got in the cereal box? Yeah, it's like one of those that you play, and go, "Huh, that's all there is to this game?" and then never buy the full version.

The only thing I will say as a positive - this game cost me about as much as a box of cereal on sale, so I can't be mad. I just wish it came with Cheerios.

I like the heart and soul put into this game, and I don't like anything else about it. 4/10 in 2025, but a solid 6-7/10 in 2005.

What I have to say about IDUN is pretty much the same as the rest of what you'll see in the reviews, both the Negative AND the Positive.

At it's core IDUN is a game I WANT to play. Holding the line against relentless hordes of vermin using a bunch of different guns almost never feels boring and the times is does, it's shaken up by the (admittedly small) pool of different mission types beyond the "hold out till the timer runs down" kind of missions. It's got a tight gameplay loop of going into missions, picking upgrade both during and between, rinse and repeat.

But then there's the times where i REALLY don't want to be playing IDUN and it's pretty much anytime a character in this game starts talking. It's pretty apparent from the jump that all the VO is done by AI in the form of text to speech, and it physically hurts to listen to it. Having actual voiced lines in a game CAN elevate it, look at the difference between the Rouge Trader game and Baldur's Gate 3, and paying for Voice Actors to come in and record can be pricey for an Indie studio. But going for full AI voice over should not be the answer if you want VO in your game and can't afford to pay actors. It got to the point in this game where i wound up enjoying it more AFTER I had turned off dialogue. Honestly, I think I would have preferred it more if it was just (real) grunts or little chirps at the beginning of each line. 

In the interest of fairness, the devs do disclose that AI Generated content is in the game, but it's all the way at the bottom of the game description, that disclosure isn't in the actual game, and there's any info on what way Gen AI images are used.

The TL;DR of this whole thing is that IDUN has a lot of potential but it winds up getting bogged down by horrendous Gen AI and all the baggage that comes with it. I don't want to bust out the torch and pick forks, but I'm just disappointed that the fun I was having was marred by just the hot, hot garbage that is Gen AI. I really don't know whether or not to recommend, but at least you know what you're getting into now.

Kinda flaccid, game works fine but overall everything just ends up underwhelming, especially at the end where the final mission is just a normal mission which is extremely anti-climatic.

AI voices are fairly gormless, game really doesn't need voices at all honestly but if needed I'm sure a few dollars could be spent on real people to make an infinitely better job, can't be worse than "Okay, now that was epic" do note that reading it as text has as much impact as the delivery from the AI again, flaccid.

If there's any notes I'd give it'd be: Hire real human VAs, remove AI [i]'enhanced images'[/i] and add more oomph to the overall experience; make em bugs crunchy, make em guns punchy, make em explosions big bangy and most of all: make the last mission more unique.
Generic tower defense game but with AI "voice actors" that deliver a very monotone dialogue that makes the already boring story even more dull.
Controls are so random. Sometimes you drag to select, sometimes you drag to move. Sometimes you right click to move.
AI voice-acting is insufferably bad

Definitely a whole lot of interesting ideas here. Valheim like is correct. Progression is slow. The environment is more deadly. I wouldn't recommend it right now as it just has way too much EA jank and lack of QoL. Missing tool tips/lack of information, poor balance, awful combat.

Some examples - Reading a book. If you move your progress is reset. Typically when you read half a book and put it down you start back where you left off! This goes for all crafting, and searching enemies, and skinning animals. The UI also covers a lot of the screen. In a game where situational awareness is supposed to be a focus, this is a pretty poor choice. There's even a mechanic where looking at the sky is supposed to help you predict the weather. But a storm can come on in seconds. I've had seemingly clear skies turn to deadly blizzards before I finished the 14 harvesting animation for some offal. (I mean you get faster with experience, but yes 14 seconds is no joke).

Combat is brain dead. Walk into enemey to trigger their attack animation, walk out of range, swing. Repeat. Some people don't mind it. I die a little bit inside. And predictably, trying to fight on a slope is a hot mess. Hit boxes are off a touch. It's just yuck.

But I have noticed an interesting attention to detail by the dev(s?). Small things like turning your character towards the screen - your character will face the camera and follow it. There was another thing that I'm failing to recall at the moment but it was those things, the small attention to detail that made me keep the game instead of refunding. Also my daughter getting into the cats water bowl pushing me over 2 hours while I cleaned up her mess. But even though I wouldn't suggest it at the moment, I intent to keep playing and actually have some hope for this one.

Boring game loop so far in my first few hours. Literally have to eat 3-5 goats a day to survive.  Hunger meter def isnt balanced at all.  Why sleep, just sit next to the fire all night???   Poor QOL, Sloppy combat and just not very well balanced overall. 

I always wondered where people found boneless chickens for boneless chicken wings.. However I have found boneless goats.. kill 4 goats in a row.. all boneless?about this..  lol wtf is this game
Kind of feels like alpha slop tbh. Hopefully they dont abandon this and can make something out of it

You spend the first in game week just trying to manage your survival meters. Hunger, Rest and Temperature are far too hard to manage in the early game and god forbid you venture out a bit to try to find some animals to kill to collect some food and a storm rolls in. You get stuck with a pack of snow leopards that do not stop chasing you until you get back to your base and essentially can't get away. Don't forget that death carries permanent debuffs, because that's a fun mechanic. One of the worst experiences I've ever had with a survival game.

Okay, as a concept, it seems like it'd be fun, but this game is just a worse version of every multiplayer survivor I've played to date. It's early access, but it doesn't really do anything *special*. 

I wouldn't recommend this game in honesty just because the blandness to it. I look forward to seeing if it improves and develops it's identity as a game, but as of right now, this game just isn't it. No disrespect, Devs. I know you put a lot of work into this game, just keep tryin and consider changing your price for the game. Not worth 20 USD full price.

While technically a Lethal Company and REPO like game, Murky divers is suffering from a lot of very notable issues that the other two games lack. Firstly, there is no scanner function. That, in combination with the (literal) murky lighting of the game forces a loss of direction that is incredibly unsatisfying and makes it very frustrating to gather the amount of items you need. In combination with the incredibly deadly monsters that show up from essentially the very start of the game makes it difficult to really grasp what is going on. It does not help that the game is missing any form of encyclopedia to figure out, how to deal with the different creatures encountered. While REPO lacks the same, REPO usually gives you enough time to figure out what each individual monster does, before you end up getting slammed into the ground.

The game has a claustrophobic, dark feel to it and invites to explore nooks and crannies while slowly dying of oxygen loss. A great, rather interesting feeling, that I would have loved to explore. But the frustrations I felt from constantly getting killed by entities that I held no counter to, all while losing any hope of finding enough scraps to survive the quote, sadly outweighs any enjoyment the game has brought.

The travelling with the ship was rather fun, though.

As other negative reviews state, the incremental gameplay mechanic is intuitive and not difficult to pick up on, and the presence of little minigames to solve for simple resources is cute, but the gameplay loop becomes agonizingly tiresome the longer you go.

Even after looking at guides from diligent players about how to optimize frame layouts, things still grind to a halt the higher you progress on the tech ladder, because there is no method to convey inefficiencies in your supply chain via a dashboard or overlay to point out where production metrics are falling short so that you can identify where to allocate resources and what to build.

The only thing you really have to convey that sort of info is the inventory popup, but it doesn't help you identify supply chain and inventory chokepoints, and even when you try to build more of a certain type of frame to get production up in an area that is lacking, you still don't know how many to actually build to meet production demand and optimize the workflow of each respective tier, because it only reports supply, production, consumption, and a theoretical maximum. But not actual demand.

Are you wasting time overbuilding power? Don't know. Are you putting too much effort towards building Random Widget Name 287 instead of Random Widget Name 341? Don't know. Is any of that wasted effort going to lead to a problematic production bottleneck elsewhere? Don't know.

Things are not well-explained as you play either. Not asking to be held by the hand, but it would be nice to have even a modicum of context for what you're doing.

The mechanic of digging for cave artifacts of increasing rarity and protecting against underground troglodytes sounds interesting, but is in reality underbaked and inconsequential, as it occurs in virtual silence in the background with minimal impact on gameplay.

Yeah, nah, I get the incremental gameplay, but in reality it's just an exercise in waiting and watching number go up. Slowly. And the waiting got tiresome by the time I reached the final tier.

If you have some kind of completionist OCD then perhaps this is your jam, but if you actually want to strategize layouts, optimize throughput, and get feedback from the game about how your strategy is actually working out, it's going to bore you to tears.

TLDR: Beautiful assets, but there is little to no gameplay.

The store page made me eager to play. The frame-to-frame and frame-to-environment interactions is something that I really love seeing in a factory game. The progression is not fun at all. You queue every upgrade all at once, which makes the clicker function to operate some factories a chore, then when automation is active, you queue up the next tier for 1,000 products and play another game until this one progresses. Got to tier 3 of 10+ before i realized that I was not having fun.

The latest update adds half-baked, features to the game that feel unneeded and out of place not to mention incomplete.  What is the need for the hostile mobs?  Why is there no way to update the new fortresses to be able to defend an area bigger than the 8 tiles around them?  Why are troglodytes randomly popping up in my buildings when I have EVERY cave surrounded by fortresses?  Why are fortresses so GD expensive to build (researching is like 700 simple Widgets and 700 Iron ingots?!?!?  Then the build cost per fortress goes thru the roof)?  This takes away considerably from the "idle" nature of the game.

It just feels soulless and dead.. The entire gameloop is:
1) Build a few things
2) Wait until the needed ressources are produced
3) Go back to 1

There is nothing else. No strategy, no math, no planning, nothing. Not even the prestige system does anything for it. It's just more of the same with different wait times. There are sooo much better games in that category you could play. You will die one day. Your time is precious. Don't waste it on Widget Inc. I mean.. i'd say don't waste it on videogames in general. But since you're going to do it anway.. at least pick a better game.

If I could give the game a neutral rating I would.

The game is fine for what it is. It's certainly lacking polish, content and is in need of a rework of scaling and balancing but all in all I had a mostly enjoyable time playing the game. If you're looking for something turn based and have played all of Larian Studios games and Solasta then this is game will give you a few enjoyable hours to spend with friends.
If you haven't played Divinity Original Sin 2, Baldurs Gate 3 or Solasta then go play those instead.

Really what bothered me most is just the way that equipment scaling works. Everything, and I mean everything, scales with the damage of your weapon. If you want to kill things then you always want to pick the weapon with the highest damage, which most of the time ends up being two handed melee weapons. Want to play a mage? Ignore the cool staves with +30 int or might on them and just use a melee weapon with high base damage. Want to play a summoner? Well your sommons don't get any of the buffs that the player gets from skills but their damage sure does scale with your weapon damage so go get yourself a melee weapon. Wonder which cool enchantment prefix or suffix you want to put on your weapon? Obviously the ones with the biggest boost to your weapon's base damage.

For the campaign mode the scaling is my main gripe, the rest isn't nearly as much of an issue.
The roguelite mode on the other hand is pretty poor as far as roguelites are concerned and very much feels tacked on.
There is no unlocking, no real progression, no interesting build variety and no way for the player to mitigate or manage the RNG in any way.

In this mode you can choose from a random selection of campaign skills after each level up. This is mostly how you shape your character, since gear is random and the gear that changes how you play is all mythical and thus costs way too much to be upgraded throughout the run if you happen to get it early on.

The abilities are no longer locked behind specific investment requirements for each of the skill trees as opposed to the campaign so you can get some neat combos that are otherwise harder to get. The problem is that very few of the skills meaningfully impact the way your character plays. Because there are no real classes and it's just a jumbled up mess of random skills, you never get anything resembling something coherent, something that feels like someone thought about how it would play. You don't get any of the cool variations that roguelites can offer like starting as a fire mage and then modifying your playstyle to specialize in big fireballs or playing a summoner and specializing in either many small minions or one big minion. Actually getting something that feels like a class happens very rarely as there is basically no way to change the RNG in your favour like banning skills, rerolls, or higher odds to get something from a skill tree you already have.

The roguelite mode boils down to: Campaign mode without the map, with entirely random skills and random equipment. Tacked on to that is "difficulty" progression from say Diablo 3 and meta "progression" from Vampire Survivors.

There's nothing bad about this game, but there's nothing really standout about it either. The strategies are your typical fantasy stuff. No hard decisions, just protect your squishies and take out things that have low health first. Even on higher difficulties. There's hardly any story to speak of. The graphics, while pleasing, are again, generic. Don't get me wrong, if you like this game, more power to you and if it's on sale feel free to check it out, but my experience was that I got bored after giving it a fair go of around 6 hours.

They failed to mention that all in-game backgrounds are AI generated, not just the loading screens and narration. I can't get behind any AI crap, though I must admit that the gameplay itself is not awful, so long as narration is muted and you don't look at the backgrounds.

This game does not feel like a 1.0 release. It feels like early access with the amount of game breaking bugs that are currently present.

Sometimes the game counts a counterattack after your turn starts this causes you to lose an AP point. When used with one punch this means you cannot take an action that turn. Feels terrible. High priority fix this bug.

When there are multiple enemies with a lot of modifiers on screen crashes the game because it cannot calculate every enemy's turn in an efficient manner. This is evident when it takes the enemy 5-15 seconds to start moving, they all move at once and then attack you at once.

I've played till endless 142 and honestly the game is fun but there are way too many game breaking bugs. I've reloaded into areas and started a fight and the fight just says "defeated" after I place my character.,

I've experienced the boss not taking an action (mountain king) after using certain dialog before he attacks. The only fix that I know of is to kill him before he does his 2nd chat bubble and then if he does it while he is dead then it doesn't delay the game longer than 30 more seconds.

I had high hopes for this game. I want it to succeed. This is very frustrating and I won't be playing anymore until these issues are fixed.

Original review for those who don't care about genAI's impact:

Absolutely phenomenal "real-time, but turn-based" fantasy RPG game that now sports two modes of playing.

First, there's the original story-based RPG progression where you pick a class and gear up along the way, unlocking a satisfying skill tree. You play in a party of up to six players, and that can be you controlling all six, or any kind of split with online friends (e.g. two of you play two or three characters each, or three of you play one each, or maybe two each, or even one  player playing two character, with others just playing one). The game's difficulty scales to the number of  players, so adding more characters to the party is really just a preference for variety, or maybe synegy across skill sets.

Then more recently, they've added an absolutely chaotic rogue-lite progression mode where every battle sees you awarded with a random skill from four potentials from ALL the classes, a piece of equipment and a bunch of stats. By the end of this mad run, you'll be super-human, with a weird mix of various skills and fortunes to boost your chances against the final boss.

The real-time element is awesome too. The only other game I've seen with this "real-time but turn-based multiplayer" mechanic is Inkbound. Here, you have a set of movement points and typically one action, along with a number of "free" actions (which are defined by your class skills), any of which can be used at all time, along with the other players, simultaneously. It's as mad, and fun, as it sounds.

In typical RPG style, you're managing your HP to stay alive and your mana to fuel skills, and skills will typically have cool down counter to balance their power.

Works perfectly on Cachy (Arch) Linux, either single, or multi player, both as host and joining, across multiple platforms (mostly Windows 10 and 11 friends in my case).

Sadly the UI and chat box were clunky and buggy. It might be better as a single player game but the co-op was rendered difficult by a fixed chat box where you couldn't chat with your team mate during fights or a text window that pops up for decisions. As for the fights and the d&d feel, they do seem like a bit of fun. And the loot is kinda cool.

There was also a bit of a disconnect in the UI design between decision sheets presented as cool scroll-like gamebook style versus the polyworld style. This just seemed an odd aesthetic and I felt that the two styles clashed. My main issue was the buggy/clunky interface and the fact the graphics would flicker a lot at the start of firing up the game.

Note: there seemed no option for gender selection. I would delve into the other issues but honestly requesting a refund.

The camera absolutely sucks in this game. Half of the time I cannot tell what I am doing. The actual game is fun but holy crap the camera makes me wish I refunded the game. Also, the actual interface of the game has screwed me over numerous times. I have not played a ton of the game, but shit it does not want you to play through the whole thing. This game will laugh at your failures, although your failures will all be due to the absolutely horrible interface. If you find this game on sale for over 50% off, it might be worth it, but the amount of time getting fucked over will make you wish you never thought about buying it.

If you enjoy watching your helplessly character getting beat to a bloody pulp, this game is for you.

You definitely need friends for this game seeing as you spend 2/3 turns frozen or stunned and the rest of your turns chugging potions and biffing attacks. "Balanced for how many players you have in your party" my ass.

No thanks.

[h1] Keep It Away [/h1]

[b] What I Liked [/b]

I enjoyed the base building for awhile.

[b] What I Did Not Like [/b]

I get what the game was going for, but I just did not like it.  While there are multiple races, only about half are actually about building a base.  A lot is not explained and most times I had no clue why I was dying or why I couldn’t do damage to something.  It’s also a roguelike since I died often, but really, there is nothing new to learn or unlock or play better.  Sure, maybe a couple races to unlock.  But that’s really it.

[b] Why I Stopped Playing [/b]

I played for a weekend, but had no real desire to go back.  For a more ersistent base build, other games like Rimworld of Necesse would be better.

[b] Disclaimer [/b]

I have been a gamer for over 30 years.  I tend to play mostly PC games.  I also bounce around between games A LOT.  I may purchase a $60 game and play it for 2 hours or spend $10 on a game and spend 50 hours on it.  Even if I played a game a short time, it does not mean it is bad.  It just means another game caught my attention.  I also complain about flaws in games a lot, even about the littlest of things.  Keep that in mind when reading my reviews.  If I do not have a complaint, then the game must be special!

I was compelled to play this game since it was developed by a small indie team from my country (just two dudes), I was drawn to the general aesthetic and the idea that Chilean topography and lore inspired the team. HOWEVER, the game ended up being extremely generic, linear and uninspiring.

There's plenty to draw from in Chilean folklore and culture, I would have loved this game delved into that, instead we got a generic "ranger must defeat demon lord and his orcish horde" (even though I laughed a bit when the earthquakes hit, that must have been the only nod to our culture). 

In terms of gameplay, it's fine I guess? There are fun skills and passives to play around, tried a melee barbarian type of build on my first run only to absolutely break the game with an attack-speed/crit bow wielding build. I ended up oneshotting everything and "beating" the game in under 4 hours, sadly the final boss bugged to hell and KO'ed me at the same exact time I got the achievement for defeating him. I ended up feeling alienated and didn't want to go through all the levels again just to see the ending. 

Most levels were okay but uninteresting to explore, except from the volcano which heavily punishes mobility with it's mazelike vertical structure, dungeons are same-y and itemization is your standard diablo-inspiring fare, with the exception that there are no rigid classes in this game, you can equip whatever you want whenever you want so you can mix and match equipment as you wish.

I know this is a passion project developed by only two people, but it sadly fails to deliver in most aspects. Yet, there are some semblances of greatness here and there, hopefully their next game will be better.

Oh, also it's cheaper than a "completo" so maybe it's worth a try for a couple runs.
4/10 for me.

Very repetitive and uninteresting gameplay. I feel increasingly disconnected from the majority of steam reviewers, as I have been consecutively mislead and let down by "Very Positive" reviewed games on Steam.

Combat was so bland, items/equipment was uninteresting. The game is marked "Verified" for Steam Deck, but the controls were certainly not optimized for controller. UI bugs abound. (Most notably a popup window for a weapon with zero stats that took up half the screen and would not go away until I restarted the game.)

The gameplay loop is indeed cool, and I do really like the idea, it just doesn't seem well executed at all.

A promising game at first, this game shows within the first few hours that it's a buggy, unpolished and super shallow mess. 

Sometimes you'll do a weapon skill and be stuck in the air for 10 seconds and can't do anything, enemies will randomly get stuck on lower parts of the map and you have to go look for them before you can progress, bosses will just stand there after doing a certain attack and look as if they're glitched out while letting you hit them for like 5 seconds straight. My first kill of the last boss, he glitched out and just stood there for like 30 seconds straight. It's also comically easy to break the game once you do a run or two and understand how it works (and here's how - do each abyssal rift that you see completely, and it will make you so strong it's impossible to lose).

All of that being said, the game really shows you how shit it is when you play the "Hideout" mode. This is essentially a 12 minute enemy-rush that ends in a boss battle against one of the games bosses. There are three areas total, and the latter two areas are unlocked by beating each subsequent area. The main issue here is that the enemies simply do not stop spawning at any point. In the first area this isn't that big of a deal since everything is rather weak, but in area 2 for instance the enemies get significantly stronger, but your character loses all of the boons & upgrades you get from area 1 (which is a really stupid choice, in my opinion) and you're forced to bring in only the gear & weapon you got from area 1. The fun part: if you die in this area, you lose all of that - and then you have to do an earlier area again to get a weapon that can even stand up to the enemies in the later areas. There are too many enemies to deal with it, and you don't have enough stamina to dodge/parry all of them - so your character essentially just burns out and you die. The only way to beat this is to keep grinding and leveling up the shop and getting better gear to where you're one shotting pretty much all of the enemies. There's basically no skill involved in this whatsoever, it's exclusively a grind.

This game would've been pretty alright with a bit more care and polish, but it ends up feeling half-assed and like slop.

There is no weight at all to any action you do, nor is there any weight to anything an enemy does. I've gotten through the first three zones and fought the first boss and nothing has changed at all. This is coming from a perspective of someone who hasn't made a game so I could be completely wrong, but it really does feel like they just took a bunch of premade assets, animations, and locations and just cobbled it all together. After 100% achievements on this game, my review is summed up as Potential but no where near worthy of being fun past one full campaign clear which takes 80mins on average. It's backfilled being just grindy and rather dull as mentioned more succinctly by other reviewers.
A jank, mid aughts graphically inspired Arena fighter with pick 3 choices after each room to next stage.

The Bad which are many: The crystal buffs per room clear are pretty negligible compared to your weapon stats and meta progression. So there was really not much to look forward to after each room power-wise, you are always looking for the best weapon dps drop and talent skills.
Due to how the magic system works on a flat cooldown timer down to 15seconds, it was always just best to use them as a utility and not a main focus. Weapons and the easy achievable parry windows are your goal. 

Classes feel unbalanced and the frost class tree is simply the way to go in terms of reducing enemy attack speed, crowd control and player survival using frost shields.

I found many many bugs in my short time playing, to long to list but didn't really cause too much detriment besides animation locking on some weapon abilities. 


The Campaign will be the source of your meta progression for general levels, once that is done after 2 or 3 full clears there is no need to go back to it.
 
The Arena mode after you unlock it, is the meat of the game at this point.
Its grindy, and extremely difficult to break into the second stage. Due to lack of power stats on gear for the first stage and gold generation is key. So extract fast and often. Each run is 12mins long and you'll be running in circles for the latter part to survive to each boss mostly.

Overall: The game needs a bit more feeling on weapon feedback, or spell viability if you wish to play more mage like. 
Not Recommended for how shallow the game can be for most people who don't want to grind the Arena. 
I will try to update this review if I can and will put on my watch list.

I’m level 38, and I have to say this game just isn’t fun. I understand that roguelikes are supposed to be a bit grindy, but it feels like this game is actively working against the player. Enemies are extremely tanky, and unless you get the +5 weapon from Abyssal Rifts, you won’t get far because enemy melee range rivals that of snipers.

And the boons… the boons. By definition, a boon is supposed to be helpful or beneficial, yet nearly all of them feel like debuffs with very weak upsides. You can often progress farther without them than with them.

The voice acting ranges from AI-sounding to nearly impossible to hear. Honestly, the only thing that feels good in this game is the combat—the attacks and the parry. Everything else is just really, really rough.

Darkblade Ascent feels more like a tech demo or asset flip than the game's cover art and trailer suggest. Melee combat in FPS games needs to feel powerful and visceral, but none of the combat in this game feels very exciting. Combat is very simplistic, with only a single attack combo per weapon. There's parries, and a few skills on cooldown, but it's disappointing to have a lack of charged attack and to have to wait so long for the weapon skills to activate.

The game also suffers from questionable UI decisions, including item drop tooltips covering up half the screen in the middle of combat and feat choices requiring you to pick from a screen filled with buttons, only a few of which light up at a time.

The game also suffers from a lot of jank. I killed the first boss next to a wall and half of my rewards fell out of line of sight. A number of enemies, including the ogres and the first boss will get stuck into neutral pose for seconds after their attacks. The gigantic item drop tooltips sometimes fail to swap after picking up an item, making it look like you haven't replaced your weapon when you have.

I just can't get into this one. As for dark fantasy roguelites, Ziggurat (and it's sequel) does it better and came out over a decade ago. For a specifically melee based FPS roguelite, Mortal Sin might be a lot uglier than this game, but it feels much better to play and is much more effective at doing the roguelite thing.

Controls are very floaty, movement is floaty, enemies spawn in 360 degrees around you so it is impossible to keep track of them to avoid being hit.

The environment is completely static, nothing to interact with at all.
Just a min-max melee simulator.

Runs are too long and do not provide enough variety. Not everything needs to be a roguelike, it would be a nice, although short level based game, but with roguelike mechanics, when I have to complete exactly the same level with no variety of enemies and the same set of weapons I used last run just not worth the time investment.

It's kinda complete ass and miserable to play.

It's a cluttered mess. An utter mess.
 It was hard to play very much at all.
The visuals are a disaster. 
Your character does not stand out well from the background. You lack animations for actions beyond running/jumping. Your attacks happen in clutter around you. Shots come from behind you to make the situation worse.
Enemies had symbols all over them. Ladders don't look like ladders and appear and disappear. Blobs that look like enemies are trampolines (and stand out better than anything important).
There are three aiming modes and none of them are right. The game has a crosshair as though it was a mouse aim game, which is a pretty bad idea for a dual sick shooter. I want only the arrow on the character for aiming, or even better an arm with a wand on the character to aim like a gun.
There are symbols everywhere. I stand on a place I can climb down and there's a character to talk to and a text prompt covering him, and then I'm covering that character, and then my own prompt is covering me, and then the crosshair is covering my prompt so I can't read it.

Text is very "default font", and is not on a fully opaque background, if any background at all.  
Combat has a terrible feel where I fire a lot and don't do much damage, but also I fire slow, and also the enemies do very little to me. It makes the firing feel limp and slow. Jumping doesn't feel tight. Walking doesn't feel good. Dash doesn't feel good.
 A door tells me I need to kill all the enemies to unlock it, but then I can't get the treasure behind it. So why have it like this?
There's a terrible flow in general.

The sound in in the cut scenes was very odd; as though there was a song playing that shouldn't be there.
There are at least 3 different art styles going on in this game and 2 of them are bad. 

I struggled to invest the time I did to give the game a chance.

It is hard to look at. It feels bad to play. This game needs a complete graphical and control makeover.

It needs a lot more time in the oven.
The game play feels very floaty, there is no weight to any of the attacks, the monsters just charge at you, there is no real mechanics to them.
The crafting system feels okay.
It feels like there is a dozen different ideas smashed together with no synergy to tie them together.

This game feels like they know what they want to make but they have no idea how to make it.

Like input lag but with animations (maple story had the same thing back in the day)... not that I would really call this animated, it's sprite based. The maps feel very over functional, just blocks linked together to make a platformer but it doesn't play like a platformer, there isn't really any momentum or smoothness to the motion, it's all so... binary.

I am a huge noita fan so when this game said combing spells I was sold but unfortunately the bar is set pretty high and this does not deliver at all. Spell interactions feel either non existent or complimentary but uninspiring. The descriptions do not help you understand how the spells work and there is no real way to test spells to see if they can combine well so I can't intuit the mechanics and learn what works.

To sum it up this would be a really impressive Newgrounds game but it is awful for a professional steam release.

Early-game? Extremely punishing.
Mid-game? Exhausting?
Late-game? Keep hitting 'Abandon Run' until decent cards help you start *Early-game, if at all.

Overall? Serial lack of balance where mid-tier enemies with maybe just ONE ability, along with one minor minion tagging along with them splits your initial deck-load between offsetting **MASSIVE** enemy damage, getting your resources **STOLEN** as a rinky-dink enemy ability so you can't afford anything in town or random inexpensive events (you read that right, even cheap stuff will drain you, again choices between survival and no money), or as aforementioned constantly hitting 'Abandon Run'.

"Balance as we go" patching means dying 10,000 times before the developer thinks static enemy passives that wipe runs (and not just one type of enemy, each enemy has a particular angle i.e. less draws per turn, clogging your hand-draw with random cards, disabling hand-loads from status conditions, enemies gaining flat x-damage per turn before multiplicatively and arbitrarily gaining INSANE amounts of damage) is worth "patching".

This game thinks it's the Dark Souls of Rogue-likes/lites, but it's not. It's a passion project where the developing party thought, "Here, let me throw the textbook at you along with the mountain of crap and call it 'challenging.'"

You will die, again and again, and the developer will "patch out" powerful, or even plainly basic and helpful, player tactics instead of taking a serious look at the fact that enemy design is seriously a hindrance to player entry, and player retention.

Worth 19.99? If you want to scream and shout and burn it all down.

Buy it when it's deeply discounted. More so than it was before now.

I really wanted to like this game.  It has an attractive UI and very nice automation features.  Unfortunately it falls flat with the theme.  This game is called Idle Awakening MAGES PATH, but it should be called Idle Awakening INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION.  There is very little magic to be had.  You spend almost all your time doing pushups, mining coal, and running a printing press.

This would be a decent idle game, plenty of different things to do and the mechanics are fairly in-depth. However, constant crashes and freezes (at least ten of each in the total playtime) have ruined any interest I have in continuing the grind. Would be a recommend once stability improves.
There is no Magic Path.

Overly grindy and aimless for a large chunk of the game certain mechanics go unexplained. 
The story elements are snarky but don't actually build to anything really.
Poor balance and a lack of offline progression is frustrating. 
instead of offline progression to stack an offline multiplier which does progress time faster but means you have to leave it open and running inflating playtime. 
Its almost good just vs other things you could play. (The Magic research games apply these concepts better) its just doesn't match up.

It has potential to be good in the future, and V0.3 should fix a lot of my gripes with it, but the main one is that the grind is atrocious and you will be idling the game for 8+ hours straight to reach a small increase.

Great for those that love tortorously slow grinds, but not so for others. I did reach end of content, and it took me about 340 hours (~20 was the Next Fest demo). 

What I really liked was that there was tons of different ways to progress later on and the story bits were quite interesting.

There's nothing really to this game. You have few choices to make. You never get synergies that make you feel strong. It was not very fun.

The layout of menus is bad. I spent a huge amount of time clicking into the wrong menu and wrong submenu.

At first it seems like you need to balance all your competing priorities. But in practice, you swap your points allocation between a generalized "do everything" build and a hyperfocused "do this one thing" build. A lot of upgrades are pointless. They don't meaningfully affect your bottom line.

The main thing driving you onwards is the story, which portrays your character as a bumbling fool with a heart of gold and limitless willpower. You don't feel like a magician. The only magical thing you do is click on some spells to autocast. It doesn't feel like magic. It feels like you're a crazy man building an estate by himself out of untamed wilderness, and one of your options happens to be themed as spells.

I also really don't like the offline mode. You bank time up to 24 hours, and cash it in at 4x speed. That's not very much. The game prefers you leave it on in the background forever.

This would be a decent idle game, plenty of different things to do and the mechanics are fairly in-depth. However, constant crashes and freezes (at least ten of each in the total playtime) have ruined any interest I have in continuing the grind. Would be a recommend once stability improves.
Game has some fun ideas but ultimately, it's kinda boring tbh.

For those who won't read the whole thing (I don't blame you):

THIS GAME USES AI "ART"
DO NOT PURCHASE UNTIL THIS HAS BEEN FIXED
IT WOULD PROBABLY BE A REALLY GOOD GAME IF THIS WASN'T THE CASE

I played the demo and really enjoyed it. It felt like a great addition to the "Life-sim" type of idle game, and had a similar feel to Orb of Creation, one of the best incremental games out there, that being a feeling of steady progress and building depth where you can either choose to let growth happen in the background or play actively for faster completion. I really did enjoy it! Played for 3 or so hours. Then I bought the game...

And noticed the opening splash. The wizard holding the book has ridiculously long fingers. Then I looked at the items you buy from the store. Then I looked at the background. It's AI "art" This was rather distressing. If you are not aware of why this might be problematic, let me give my understanding of the problems with these AI processes. 

For one, the actual cost of these AI services is astronomical. They require huge server arrays that demand massive amounts of power, water for cooling, space, and rare minerals for the graphics cards. These facilities are often built by massive companies in remote areas due to the high environmental cost, need for water access, and the noise produced. The nearby towns are then either literally torn down or rendered nearly uninhabitable due to the waste and extreme energy price spikes.

Then, before we've even gotten to the crappy picture, the data used to create the image is all stolen. I'm sure whoever is reading this has heard this before, but it's probably worse than you may know. The AI companies are, nowadays, the biggest purchasers of private data. They send out bots across the entire internet, and when I say the entire internet, I mean potentially thousands of computers dedicated to sending out these scrapers. These bots slow down the websites for us human users, steal data from every single inch of the website including but not limited to comments, images, videos, games, and screenshots. It doesn't matter who you are, they will take it. This of course leads to artists of every caliber, popular or unknown, great or terrible, images or text, stolen. The data is scraped, sent to the massive and expensive facilities (that I must reemphasize are driving up energy costs for everybody, especially those near one), and used to train their flavor of AI.

What, then, is the result of all of this theft and destruction? You get an uncanny valley picture of a really colorful person with weird fingers making odd expressions. You get oddities like lettuce with weird bulges and inexplicable asymmetry. You get regurgitated computer data built on the stolen work of real people at great environmental and human cost, as well as cheapening the game itself. And there really is no excuse. You can spend $40, or the equivalent in whatever currency, to buy an asset pack from any number of online stores and get a greater level of quality and consistency. It might be cheaper, or even free, if you get a bit lucky. Look at Orb of Creation for an example of an incredibly well used asset pack.

As it is, I commend the Dev on the excellent game they have made (at least as far as the demo goes), but refuse to play this game so long as the art's integrity is so severely compromised for so little reason. I cannot give you money in good conscience.
Downvote, because I despise the "your wallet isn't big enough to earn the money to buy a bigger wallet" concept.

I bought this to support the developers because I liked their older games (Marble Age, Egypt games). I wanted to give this one a try, but it feels very unbalanced. A common complaint in their games is that you are effectively railroaded into a certain level of progress you need to make constantly, or else you will fall behind forever. This game definitely acts the same way. 

There is no room in the gameplay to do anything that isn't an efficient means of progressing, that means:

   - Not enough time to explore everything as you progress
   - Not enough time to go back to a previous zone to complete things you skipped then
   - Limited resources means you can't experiment with crafting/building things without risking falling behind
   - Because of the time crunch, you are forced to push into lands with creatures around or above your skill level

What these point culminate in is a game where you are constantly rushing to accomplish your goals as fast as you can, with almost no time to test game features or pursue your own goals (e.g. explore a dungeon that's out of the way). While I get the idea that a game might want to force you to keep moving, the 'keep moving' part of this game isn't really satisfying:

   - Combat is simple, you just prepare for combat and let it run itself, but that just involves rest and getting gear
   - Gathering resources and crafting is stressful because of the time crunch
   - By the time you've crafted something it is almost immediately made obsolete by your progress
   - Ultimately, you are rushing around just trying not to fall behind, but the progress doesn't give you a real reward
   - There is no 'end game' or 'post game', you just rush to the ending and then its over: no exploration, no going back and clearing an area, no crafting an item to test it out on an enemy.

Those were the gameplay criticisms I have, but it bears mentioning, the game leans too heavily on AI. I understand that AI is a terrific tool for building placeholder art / placeholder music in order to outline what the game should be, but if you're releasing an allegedly complete game - the AI slop artstyle just makes it look unserious. I'm sure some manual touchups were done to refine the art, but everything just looks like its been "Ghibli-fied" with soft edges and saturation up to 100. It just makes it look fake rather than having a style. These guys' older game also had a cartoonish art style, but everything looked intentional and helped build a theme to the games. The AI just makes everything "same-ish" and like they left in placeholders.

All in all, the game needs to allow the player more time to explore the world and mechanics, it needs to allow these side jobs to provide meaningful rewards that make them not a waste of time, and it could benefit from a redesigned artstyle (not gonna happen though this far into release).

To be clear, I enjoy playing this game. But...

The game has all of the symptoms of a terrible development strategy. The game's tutorial only explains maybe 5 of the 20 systems. There's a lot of assumption that you know what each system does whether it's explained or not. There are also too many systems to really engage with them. Like, terrain changes your stats, but you aren't reasonably going to change anything to accommodate the terrain stat issues. You can change the terrain with certain systems However, there's not realistically enough time to do that. You're in a rush to get to the end before the end gets too hard to beat. 

And it sucks because I really like playing the game. I really like the point and click style of game play I like the rogue like elements. But I've been playing this for weeks on easy mode and can't seem to beat it even once. My other hobby is getting the platinum trophy on Elden Ring so I'm not exactly scared of difficulty. This is just not balanced well enough. 

Game rarely updates but developer is constantly in the discussion forum saying people are wrong for disliking things. And to be clear, I really do like this game. But I don't recommend throwing yourself against a brick wall, no matter how much I enjoy the feeling of flying through the air right up until I smash into a brick.

The design of this game is... confusing.

I wouldn't recommend it on the basis of how rigid and random the game is.
But I'll probably play some more to make sure I'm not missing anything.

Let me try to explain. So, you start on a random map, in a random place, with random encounters around you. You will be attacked every now and then by increasingly harder enemies so it is important to push forward to gain better gear because the power creep of higher level enemies is pretty strong.

But sometimes the RNG will just make you unable to progress. It happens pretty often too. Last two runs I lost because of pure rng.
- in first one there was literally no place to build a Stone Mine when I was playing as a Lord. I invested in lords skills so mining stone by myself was takiing so much time I was crushed by lvl 4 hell enemy. I couldn't do anything, since I couldn't build stat buildings fast enough and without them the lord is shit at fighting
- next run with a Knight. I was surrounded by lvl 4 enemies at some point but there were 0 weapons higher than a lvl 2 dagger. No weapons in shops, no recipes to craft, no weapons on the tiles I had around me. Fighting lvl 4 enemies with a shitty dagger was taking so long I was once again crushed

And there is no changing your build once you spend your first lvlups. You get 2 skill points per level and there are 10 possible skills I think? Each class also gets a 100% bonus for two skills. So realistically you will always put skill points in the skills you get a class bonus for because you simply don't have enough of them. By the time stronger armies start showing up (lvl 4-5), you will have maybe 10-12 skill points. So if you are going with a Knight and you can't find a better weapon from drops, you are dead, nothing more to do about it. You can't switch to using magic because without skills you can't even cast anything more than 1 dmg missile.

Also Gathering and Crafting seem pointless because they take so much time. It feels like this is a backup system but even then - if you have to rely on crafting to get an upgrade, you already lost, since you will waste so much time on getting a single piece of equipment. Focusing on making a Gatherer character is the fastes way to lose.

Overall I think the concept of the game is interesting but there is not much fun to be had with how the systems work. You are forced into one way and you need to pray to find items/encounters you need for it or you die.

The "souls-like" aspect is also meh. Enemies give clear indicators when to counter their attacks. But the window to do so is very small and the stun it causes is also very small. Most of the time you're better off using the stamina to just dodge the attack and attack back. Maybe as you play more useful options unlock. The indicator also takes part of the fun out of reacting to it. I'm not reacting to the combat but this little glow thing that pops up above them.

There is just a lot of polish that needs done.

If I could refund this game I would. Even on a steep discount, I can't see recommending this game.

This is a boring, uninspired, repetitive, poor-mileage roguelite.

This is not a soulslike as it does not have a "lose your souls" mechanic, nor any form of soulslike world.

This is not a rougeliKe as it has significant meta-progression, which is required to beat runs.

Runs get boring fast, there is no procedural generation, and some dungeons reuse the same exact room more than once.  Upgrade selections have minimal effect on gameplay and are utterly boring.  Difficultly spike between normal and hard modes is stupid, and not well tested.  And hard mode is just a boring chore, with enemies taking forever to kill.  Bosses are highly inconsistent - some do insane damage, and others are easy as cake to damage and chip away at.  World variety is significantly lacking as well.

If you have roguelite fatigue, this game will make you lose even more of your will to continue playing the genre.  At 10 hours, most experienced roguelite players will run out of content.  Games like Hades 2 have 10x more mileage.

At this point in time I would avoid this game. There are some very serious issues with performance and inputs. The game often won't accept a movement command for some reason and you fix this with moving a different direction. I can hold w to move forward and my character stands still until I tap d then he will move. The game also runs worse than Monster Hunter Wilds which is saying alot. I would crash on mhw every 3 hours but this game crashes every hour. It is a upscaling issue that this game is abusing when it shouldn't be needing to upscale these very basic textures. Without it the game looks 2bit. I love this kind of game and I missed my refund window unfortunately so I will keep playing till eventually a crash makes me rage quit for ever.

This game wants to be a Dark Souls version hades but it has Elden Ring bosses with Dark Souls 2 healing and movement. Doesn't work out well, you need to perfect dodge every single attack and use a stamina build to stand a chance against the later bosses. And if you think running away from a boss to heal is smart they normally have some combo ranged attack that can kill you while you drink doing more damage than your whole potion and health bar.

Edit: I tested out how hot this game runs my system and it runs 10 degrees hotter than mhw (pre stablility patch) and battlefield 6. I'm aiming for a refund as it crashes before I can get to or beat the final boss.

I initially gave this game a good review because it has good combat and decent boss fights despite middling to bad music, graphics and sound. 

After trying out alternate playstyles and characters I realized that I got lucky with my starting picks and roughly half of the playstyle options are kind of bad. This wouldn't be enough to make me give it a negative review normally but then I remembered the price is a factor and I don't think this is worth $20. It's worth about half that IMO.

Honestly if you'd say you're pretty experienced with top down action rogue-lites, skip it.  I was breezing through this "souls-like" with no issues whatsoever.  Sure there's higher difficulties, but I was just so bored.  I began to wonder if the class I picked was busted but it was just the mechanics making it easy.  Perfect dodges have way too long of a window, your extra skills are mostly unnecessary, enemies telegraph their moves super obviously and have really bad AI.  Just bait them into attacks and run away til they give you some free shots.  There was one boss laying down summons and I still don't even know what they did.  No damage to me, didn't heal him, he didn't hit any harder or change his patterns.  They were just there.  There's not really voice acting and the little bit of it there was, was pretty bad.  Some of the descriptions for upgrades don't really make sense.  It kinda uses different terms interchangeably to mean the same thing.  I found 3 different status descriptions which all meant "soul-state".  I didn't clear it because I Alt+tabbed to check and see if any of this was already talked about, and the game crashed so it made the refund easy for me to decide on.  Some hard work went into making it, I can't say anything bad about that.  It looks pretty good and in combat the clunks and thuds of your hits are heavy (however sometimes you might struggle to know when you yourself is taking damage due to lack of cues).  The upgrades can be pretty creative but there's no serious synergy that needs to go into building your character.  Just pick someone whose basic attack is strong and you'll blow by everyone.  If I didn't have so many better games to compare it to I'd sink more time into it but it doesn't do anything to set itself apart.  It claims to be a precise type fighter (who knows what that means) but you can't exactly say that when even on mouse+keyboard there's some form of auto-aim (didn't check to see if you could turn it off, crashed shortly after I realized).  It ultimately plays just like all of the other ones, just with a darker palette and more shallow mechanics.

I was really full of HOPE to finally see a decent visual novel created by my native devs but alas:( Miracle didn't happen. But I believe it's still can be recommended in original Russian language, so it's gonna be a neutral review.
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3444499187 
[u]Complaints[/u]:
--I seriously don't understand whyyy it's impossible for Russian devs to release a product with readable English, it's literally the easiest language in the world, why does it become like an unachievable goal to translate Russian into it?! Why can't you find some beta-testers to check if everything is all right and, for example, you didn't leave any Russian sentences because you did. It's a tad weird again to see a real person mentioned as a translator when again it's machine quality:( with the same stupid mistakes like mixture of pronouns. The majority of grammar mistakes is so primitive, kids in elementary school don't make them, like “a men, an flat, decorating her (meaning tree)”... Our Russian idioms translated literally:( Instead of the gallery it's 'arts'... The abundance of mistakes was too distracting T_T 

--too short, I was thinking about three routes while playing but then it occurred to me it feels wrong to call them that way, as the ending is one and the same, you may just come to it through a kiss of three different girls. That one different kissing scene is too short, their relationship is too rushed and unrealistic, welp, yeah kinda New Year miracle but still nope, I don't believe you, with any girl.

--the plot is ridiculous. In every “route” I felt like face-palming asking myself (in vain) wtf was even going on, like gluing broken decorations O_o or successfully pretending to be unconscious because of the fallen packet O_O I always had to question my sanity while reading it. The 'story' is insane...

--I dislike the technical part of the game :( It was annoying how every time I had to choose all the settings AGAIN ⋋_⋌  all that shaking and moving, whyyy(－^ლ) like that final scene when they were sitting at the table, why was the camera moving forth and back all the time?! I feel dizzy trying to read in such way :( I get it when the char is riding or smth and such moving effects are applied, what was the point in this novel is just beyond me... 
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3453500509 
[u]Praises[/u]:
+I liked that unique cultural vibe(^^)b
+achievements, profile features limited though.
+at least it's cheap
+lloved anime posters and visual part in general
+voiced, though hearing the way it was supposed to be and actually seeing the final result in English felt especially disappointing :(
+I'm grateful that even if you lose miserably in those mini-games, it won't affect anything. Thanks a lot!
+I really want to see more of our novels, it seriously hurts practically every time to choose a thumb down, I don't want to discourage the devs. It's their first attempt, I sincerely wish them good luck (and a more serious approach to the English localization, please).

So, this novel could be recommended only in original language for now, perhaps they will edit it. But, frankly speaking, even then the very plot is rather questionable. But being short and kinda lighthearted it could be a fun, festive read anyway.

game_name: The NOexistenceN of you AND me
review_text:
Spoilers!

TL:DR This game doesn't even believe in it's own philosophy. Fails on multiple aspects of design and character development. Overall, just go play YOU and ME and HER. Trust me, it's better in every way. 

The game revolves a tulpa called Lilith whom you speak to, adventure with, and "romance" with. Except none of these things feel connected, well thought out or executed. Lilith goes on these seemingly never-ending rants about philosophy and all of this surface level analysis like:

"Everything is your subjective opinion because you are your own person!11" and "Common sense is just human based beliefs!!1" 

News flash, this isn't a groundbreaking concept and also has nothing wrong with it. These morals, sense, and overall perspectives were rightfully built up overtime as intelligence, theorization, and philosophy itself has grown up along side humanity. They were taught to us not to blindside us, but because they had strong philosophical foundations that supported freethinking, and human based theories instead of just blindly following the herd and 
just doing biologically instinctual things. 

Another point to this is that, even if this wasn't the case, the way it's handled here is poorly done. Instead of treating it as real world situation such as in many other Solipsism or philosophical novels and giving it time for conclusions and thoughts to form. It treats like a one off gag about how "nothing can be TRULY real unless YOU see, feel, touch, or hear it directly" and tries to make you think about how the rest of the world looks. Then, proceeds show you the rest of what it JUST tried to make you think otherwise about. The entire game does this, by the way. The ending LITERALLY just tells you everything in one of the worst "tell, don't show" moments I've seen to date. I'm not a perfect writer but how can anyone think that was a fantastic way to wrap up your game? 

Moving on from the philosophical aspect because it's so surface level I really can't say much else, their is a very shoehorned tulpa as I mentioned earlier. The character is exceedingly boring, going in random directions with their conversations to the point where it congeals together in this big mess of dialogue. And before anyone says it, you could see this as intentional to create of series of endless vapid thoughts considering she isn't even real after all, and your just thinking about her and projecting it outwards, but even then Lilith doesn't even stay accurate with the character that's established beforehand. We never learned why we forget her every day, nor do we really care since it's brought up so infrequently. The conversations themselves feel fake and that's not even mentioning the extremely random "RPG" segment that is thrown in. 

I can tell that it's trying to portray the whole "one big dream" segment from the MC'S perspective featured in a lot of these games. Sadly, it doesn't give them enough time and instead just sort of wings it there hoping that it makes "sense" in the end. Like I said earlier, giving these things not ENOUGH time and having TOO MANY sections even in a short game like this, does not work very well. 

And that's about it for the entire story of this game, yeah there's really not much to say. Well, except for the 4th Wall break which is not good at all. It's abrupt, it's sudden and is in the FIRST sentence of the game on steam. It's so predictable and does nothing to try and subvert this at all, it comes abrupt especially in the second half. I've seen other VN'S do the same concept but have a much harder hitting emotional impact, which is what this game tried. 

Overall, I did not enjoy this game much at all. Please go play YOU and ME and HER instead, as that one actually got me thinking and had emotional depth to it.

2/10 for 2 dollars

game_name: The NOexistenceN of you AND me
review_text:
SPOILERS AHEAD but I'd also want to show my perspective to this game. 

Alright, first and foremost I'd like you guys don't treat this as a negative opinion. I'm very mixed when it comes to rating this game and the factors I'm going to point out are probably a basis of why I wouldn't recommend this game to averege video game player, who does not search for deeper meaning or anylize such structured the game from an objective point of view.

Speaking of this game's structure, this is where we will begin, because lot of misinterpretations start here.

Let's check the game's description. It leads to as many questionmarks as it can. It encourages you to a goal of exploring the truth of "you/girl/world"

Notice that: 1. The description does not tell you that you will recieve an objective truth. 2. It does not say "and", but puts a enigmatic "/", as if telling you to choose what exactly to search for, treating everything with speculation to how you potray those aspects (real, not real, manipulative or not, instructive or not, confusing or deep with sense, entertaining or frightening and so on).

With that I can conclude that the game is like a unfinished projection frame, and the gameplay confirms it, because the is literally no evidence for any of the theories that people can create. What games basicly does is throw questions at you and tell you to answer them yourself, at the same time confusing the player into connecting certain factors that actually have not much in common.

For example, the game personalizes your experience, making it seem like elements of the game are speaking directly to you. That is also done in the game description (explore the truth of "you [...]") as well as when creating a protagonist with no backstory apart from being at the verge of death meaning you can personalize him. But that does not mean you are him!

To group this up:

You can speculate plenty: Protagonist = Player, and Lilith = Game are probably the most wrongly interpreted aspects.

Protagonist is not the player, because the game has no tools to know the players' intentions. You'll quickly get on when you realize you wanted Lilith to live or you treated her in your mind as an existing figure (even as a concept, not a real human!), but the protagonist decided otherwise. If intentions don't match the characters doesn't either, and the game cannot judge your intentions, but only decisions.

That's the simplest thing ever, but it's just that we control the protagonist.

Lilith is not on pair with the game either. You can't 100% be sure that Lilith manipulates the player. Lilith never breaks down her role as a symbol of purest loving, but fictional being. That;s a first. Secondly, Lilth doesn't speak to the player. As contradictory as it can seem, it is that way. We can't even be sure that Lilith knows that the protagonist is being controled by a player. 

Opening sequence, Lilith: "So you are the *protagonist* of this game [...]" -> She's speaking to the protagonist, not the player!

The game's construction is what messes with the player, and it happens that Lilith is a part of game's construction, just like the protagonist, cool soft music, artstyle, decision-making etc. 

The game is made in a way that urges the player to make assuptions without proof, taking an emotional theme to stir emotions, but not answering any questions, so it can lead you absolutly everywhere. That's why the game gains its' value only because of us, reviewers or game recievers. Because the game has nothing exceptional, but urges the player to fill any gaps by themselfes. 

To conclude, for some it might be fascinating experience, and once you get all that you might actually gain a lot, but because the game can mislead (just look at discussion threads for example), and because we cannot judge it was intentional, it also doesn't take any responsibility for it.

It's a great product in the right hands, 
but in the wrong...it can be pretty troubling and misleading.
Thank you all if you read it, I know I went pretty far there, hope you had a great experience regardless.

game_name: The NOexistenceN of you AND me
review_text:
TL;DR: Plot and characters were all uninteresting, even with the twist at the end. Philosophical messaging isn't anything to write home about either. You are spoonfed philosophical ideas, no thinking required.

The big focus of this game is the question: what does it mean for things to exist? Unfortunately, it explores this question quite... boringly.

While its thesis is interesting, it's not at all groundbreaking and you can certainly find these ideas in books or other media. It essentially boils down to: [spoiler]what does or does not exist is determined by the self[/spoiler]. Thoughtful, but certainly nothing new if you dabble in philosophy. On top of that, the thesis is presented really plainly: she just talks at you. Lilith goes point-by-point and she tells you how to interpret the question of existence, she essentially hands you the answer. No critical thinking or media literacy needed on your part. It's a surface-level message paired with an uninteresting presentation.

This is bad enough, but it gets worse when the entire game is just a chore to get through. The plot feels random, disconnected, and irrelevant. Largely because each chapter is random, disconnected, and irrelevant. I have confidence that if you skipped to the ending, you would understand the same amount as someone who played through the whole thing. It's just such a grind to get to the good stuff, of which there is little.

Not only that, but none of the characters are interesting in the slightest. Lilith, who you would hope to be at least somewhat compelling, has zero personality at all other than "I am self-aware". The twist is that [spoiler]she is actually a tulpa[/spoiler], but this just feels like a cheap attempt to make the first four chapters more interesting than they actually were.

I don't know, a lot of people liked this game but I personally could not get into it. Maybe there's something I'm missing. 

Is this game worth your money? Maybe. Is it worth your time? I strongly doubt it. If you aren't too interested after the first one or two chapters, just know that it doesn't change a whole lot from there. Just look up a video and skip to the ending if you're really curious because honestly the context of the previous chapters is irrelevant.
