Pact Between Us is a perfect example of a heartwarmingly fun, cozy story with a seemingly unique gameplay concept that is unfortunately heavily crippled and held back by its repetitive gameplay alongside possessing bugs & issues up the wazoo. If the game is more polished then I'd say that the game is "palatable" in the way that the gameplay section would still be feeling repetitive, but at least it wouldn't be as jarring as it is now. The scenes, while nothing too amazing, can come off as being rather wholesome good in the way that they do cater to a niche of people who enjoy reading childcare stories, in which I'd say that the scenes are good, but not good enough to make it worth sitting through everything else. Just in case some people think that I’m overexaggerating, then below is a non-exhaustive list of bugs & issues that I’ve experienced:
[list]
[*]Audio seemingly disappear and appear whenever they feel like it. It can be rather immersion-breaking, but worst of all it can come out during important scenes, so at times you’d have happy music & sad music playing at the same time during crucial heartbreaking moments in the game.
[*]Characters spazzing out as if they have been possessed by Anabelle.
[*]Inability to pause fmv during cutscenes which not only does not allow you to easily take screenshots but also means that you must commit to watching the cutscene since, again, you can’t pause them.
[*]Memory (also known as replay) on the main menu allows you to rewatch scenes in the game, but not only does it not feature subtitles, but the main menu music is still there creating for a rather jarring watching experience.
[*]Some repeat scenes (primarily vacations) aren’t skippable, resulting in a huge time waster on the player’s behalf due to having to constantly watch the same scenes over and over again.
[*]Some scenes are improperly cut making transitions in-between shots to feel rather unnatural which breaks immersion.
[*]Terrible English subtitles possibly due to the work of a very shoddy machine-translation work. I & You being used interchangeably and worst of all the little sister’s name (Weilai) is being translated to “Future”. 
[*]Texts on papers aren’t translated, meaning that without first reading through the game description or synopsis, then you would be bit confused as to the main drama of the story being that [spoiler]the brother is adopted[/spoiler], this information is crucial to the story, yet is hard to grasp until the very end of the story.
[*]There are 3 kinds of audio in the game, voice, sound, and music. You can change increase and reduce the volumes, but by default not only is the music overly-loud making the vocals pretty much inaudible in most scenes, but sound seems to also be directly connected to voice in a way that if you have it lowered all the way down then some scenes that should have voice-over would be ended up being dead silence.
[/list]

Before going through with the conclusion, I’d like to briefly touch upon the endings first. Some of them feel like they ended rather abruptly, but this is mainly due to the work of the wacky machine translation. I say that the scenes and build-up of it all, while not really that rewarding, does give you bit of a punch to the heart(?) I believe that the rapid fast transition to the credit screen which changes the tune from sad song -> happy music really kills any sort of feeling that you might have prior to the experience, and without it + better translation then they their impact would have been felt a lot more. While I don’t fully agree with the whole message of "do whatever you want", I can still somewhat understand the goal of what they’re trying to say where instead of wasting time worrying about the future, you should learn to appreciate the present instead to which I can see it being relatable to a good amount of people out there.

Ultimately, if there's a purely fmv-only version then I'd recommend it to those who are tired of mostly romance and/or action fmv and thus may be seeking more of a heartwarming story with (surprisingly) lack of romance, but since the gameplay & bugs are huge part of the experience (that can even ruin moments in the game mind you), then I don't think that I can whole-heartedly recommend it, at least not until a proper re-structure and/or polishing have been done to the game (which doesn't seem all that likely). Above all else, I’m now proud that my child that I’ve raised for 9+ years has now become a dancer and I couldn’t have it any other way.

tl;dr: A heartwarmingly wholesome fun fmv that's heavily butchered by its drought out gameplay and lack of polish resulting with an experience that feels like it is overstaying its welcome.

6.5/10

The game doesn’t work in either English or Chinese... thankfully Japanese is available.
Too bad I don’t speak Japanese.

Either way, I played the game for the achievements, so it doesn’t bother me that much.
But the devs should maybe care a bit about their game actually working, since that’s kind of the bare minimum.

That said, the visuals are nice, but the game is genuinely endless even with an auto-clicker, and on top of that it was unbelievably boring since I couldn’t follow the story at all... because, reminder: I DON’T SPEAK JAPANESE.

Oh, and multiple times in the game there are red flashes that wreck your eyes and could probably trigger a seizure in someone with epilepsy.

The "art" and character design is uninspired (in case of the children nightmare-inducing in an uncanny valley sort of way), the story is drawn out in a way that made me lose any interest in the things happening on screen and killed all the feelings a topic like this should evoke. The bare-bone controls that are there handle abysmally. The English translation read okay to me (a non-native speaker) with some mistakes/weird word choices.
This could have been a short, emotionally hard-hitting visual novel but it definitely needed more time in the oven, so to speak. And at least someone competent enough in game/media design to raise the question if the character assets should switch that often if the viewpoint of those assets are slightly different in too many of them and be able to adjust the filter on the background photos to be a bit more uniform.

I cannot lie and say I do not like this game, but I have major issues with the game which may just be me, but it really did impact my experience with the game.
The biggest problem with this game is how tedious things can be, for example: emptying oil takes forever and it is insanely annoying to do every SINGLE day in the game. I want to recommend this game, I really do. I play it solo often because it scratches a neurodivergent itch in my head. But I can't.

First, I cannot in good conscience choose to recommend any game that uses generative AI for it's advertisement. By using AI promo images, consumer trust is broken already, even with the disclaimer. I cannot say I have complete faith that other elements of this game, such as textures, code, or sounds, are not also AI generated. 

Second, the gameplay as it is now does not seem to reward extended or solo play at all. I will of course respect that it is in early access, but there truly isn't much to do. Once you unlock all food items and the drive thru window, the game stagnates and nothing else changes about it as you continue to level up. The most you can do from that point on is customize the dining area, and for whatever reason, your in game PC's background color. This is a game that only feels rewarding or entertaining with friends. There is not a lot of variation in anything - the only thing that changes day to day is what items people order. There are no unique challenges or dynamic events - at least not yet.

In short - I don't recommend it because there's not much to it yet and because of the use of AI. If this developer starts creating their own promotional work or paying an artist to do so instead of generating it with AI, I will gladly change my stance. I want to love it and I hope I will.

The Dev team took quite a long break after first releasing their major updates, took a month off after Christmas, and then came back and released several small QoL updates, and then promised more updates focusing on content in June. Since then, it's essentially been radio silence. While they have a listed timeline for what's expected in updates, they've "given an update about what's been going on and what's to come" in September and said they'd have sneak peeks and screenshots "soon" but again, it's almost Christmas and this was September. They did a small bug fix in October for a cashier issue, and other than that, silence. Their fanbase has posted time and time again asking for just an update on what's going on, or a confirmation they're still working on it and the project hasn't been abandoned (all of this in their official discord where the dev team is supposedly active, mind you) and it's been radio silence. While again, this game has so much potential with future content if the dev team ever releases anything, I would wait for "signs of life" before I'd spend your hard earned money to purchase it.

This is no game, this is a tech-dem, a vibe-coded shovelware sim. Wastes your time to be over the 2 hour mark for a refund...

A Half-Baked Cash Grab Masquerading as a Game

Fast Food Simulator promises a sizzling experience behind the counter — flipping burgers, managing orders, and building your culinary empire. Unfortunately, what it actually delivers is a cold, greasy mess that feels less like a genuine simulation and more like an uninspired cash grab rushed out of the kitchen.

Optimization Disaster

From the moment you boot up the game, the technical issues start piling up faster than drive-thru orders at rush hour. The frame rate stutters even on high-end PCs, textures pop in and out, and character animations jitter like malfunctioning animatronics. Load times are painfully long, and random crashes make it nearly unplayable. It’s clear that Fast Food Simulator was thrown together without proper optimization or testing.

Shallow Gameplay

For a simulator, there’s shockingly little to simulate. The gameplay loop consists of repetitive, uninspired tasks — clicking on ingredients, assembling orders, and serving customers whose dialogue repeats after five minutes. There’s no meaningful progression system, management mechanics are non-existent, and the AI is laughably bad. It feels like a mobile mini-game stretched out and sold at a premium price.

Poor Visuals and Audio

Visually, the game looks like it’s from 2010 — flat lighting, low-resolution textures, and clunky character models. The soundtrack loops endlessly, and the sound effects are so repetitive that you’ll want to mute the game within minutes. There’s no atmosphere or charm to speak of, just a lifeless digital kitchen.

Final Verdict

Fast Food Simulator is a sloppy, undercooked product that feels more like a cynical attempt to cash in on the simulator trend than a real effort to entertain players. Between its poor optimization, lack of depth, and blatant monetization schemes, there’s little reason to recommend it to anyone — even die-hard fans of quirky simulation games. I am reviewing this game in context with games similar in genre: POOLS, Anemoiapolis, Complex:Expedition, etc.

If you haven't played any of those, you will likely enjoy this. Limscape has:
* Good quality sound
* Generally a good , smooth, well performing graphics engine - a few bugs but pretty rare.
* A decent amount of content to explore.

What's the problem?

The real challenge with "liminal space exploration games" is that it's an invitation for a minimal creative effort from developers. "You want to explore a spooky hallway, ok here's a completely and utterly featureless environment with a bunch of random rooms with a few props in them." That's it. It's a bunch of random rooms and halls with a few props in them. The game does not have really interesting architecture or level design so as you play , the illusion of playing in a minimally created videogame tech demo is not dispelled. Compared to Anemoiapolis, which situates you as a lost technician, or Pools, which has many beautiful rooms, or Seclusion which is a psychological journey of a man's regrets, Limscape is just a bunch of featureless concrete rooms with a few props in them. It doesn't have story, or good architecture, or puzzles, it's just featureless concrete rooms.

So, as a veteran fan of the genre, I do not recommend this one, check out the others in this list first.

The real challenge with "liminal space exploration games" is that it's an invitation for a minimal creative effort from developers. "You want to explore a spooky hallway, ok here's a completely and utterly featureless environment with a bunch of random rooms with a few props in them." That's it. It's a bunch of random rooms and halls with a few props in them. The game does not have really interesting architecture or level design so as you play , the illusion of playing in a minimally created videogame tech demo is not dispelled. Compared to Anemoiapolis, which situates you as a lost technician, or Pools, which has many beautiful rooms, or Seclusion which is a psychological journey of a man's regrets, Limscape is just a bunch of featureless concrete rooms with a few props in them. It doesn't have story, or good architecture, or puzzles, it's just featureless concrete rooms.

So, as a veteran fan of the genre, I do not recommend this one, check out the others in this list first.

As a player of Highrise since long before their launch on Steam—2020, to be exact—I can safely say this company has fallen into a cycle of endless greed and false promises to those who have invested money into their company.

This is not just a simple case of long-term players refusing to evolve and adapt to the next big thing. This is a case of Highrise actively neglecting their players by breaking their promises and delivering half finished updates that the community rarely asked for. They are attempting to expand their player base without tending to the players they currently have and to me, that is incredibly lousy. I cannot in good conscious recommend this game until they've ironed out lots of issues that have players continuously walking out the door.

For example:
— The Highrise Creature Club project that has been neglected for years despite continuous promises that there will be updates and changes. Mind you, this is after straying from the roadmap countless times.
— The fact that people who preordered items from other players have been waiting months to receive their items, and the creators of these unreleased items have been waiting just as long to receive a update.
— Instead of producing new items to excite the community, they release dupes almost every single event. Sometimes, they release these dupes without getting the original artists consent or without giving proper credit to people who came up with them.
— The constant release of account bound items when 99.9% of the items in this game have always been tradable to other players. This does not benefit the player base in any way.

This company makes absolute bank from their constant grabs (gacha systems), pay-to-play events, item packs and sales from their player base. They have the means to expand their internal team... they simply just don't care to because people continue to give them money with or without these changes anyway.

When I first downloaded the game, I asked players for "tips and tricks" about how to begin my Highrise journey. Someone had approached my character and told me, "The best thing you can do is close the app and uninstall it now."

Back then, I didn't listen but I will pose the same advice to you now. Unless you're ready to shell out endless money to a company who is never going to deliver on their promises, I recommend scrolling onto the next game. I encourage you to give your hard-earned money to a company that deserves it.

A note for the devs: Start taking care of the player base you currently have and I'd be happy to write a review about the positives this game brings to the table.

**Summary:**
Highrise is a visually creative social game with strong tools for self-expression and the potential to build genuine, long-term friendships. However, it suffers from a toxic and cliquish community culture, heavy monetization, slow support, and serious safety concerns for younger players. Altogether, I don’t recommend this game to people with gambling addictions or poor spending habits. But if you’re looking for a place to form real friendships and are able to manage your spending and boundaries, then I do recommend it.

---

**Review of Highrise**

**Pros:**
Highrise offers a wide range of events and activities, and over time the developers have continued to add more content. This helps keep the game from feeling as repetitive as it once did and gives players more to do on a regular basis.

The social side of Highrise can be very rewarding. Despite issues within the broader community, I’ve formed some genuinely meaningful and long-lasting friendships through the game. These connections are very important to me, even though many of us live far apart.

Highrise also provides robust creative tools that allow players to design their own “worlds” and spaces. For those willing to learn how the systems work, there is a lot of freedom to customize environments and express creativity.

Additionally, many areas of the community are relatively inclusive. A lot of players are welcoming and respectful of LGBTQIA+ identities and people from diverse racial and cultural backgrounds. While negative attitudes still appear occasionally, they do not seem to represent the majority, and many players feel comfortable being themselves without constant fear of judgment.

**Cons:**
I have been playing Highrise since 2018 and have spent a considerable amount of money on it. Although the game was enjoyable for a time, the overall community and environment have declined.

The social dynamics can feel very cliquish, particularly among long-term or higher-status players, which can make it difficult to form genuine friendships. There is also a significant amount of drama, and players are often judged based on how much money they do or do not spend. Free-to-play users may struggle more to connect, while heavy spenders risk being used primarily for their financial contributions.

I would not recommend Highrise for children. The presence of predatory behavior is extremely concerning, and the current 13+ rating in most app stores feels inappropriate; this game should be rated at least **18+**. It is also worrying that some parents reportedly complete age verification for their underage children, which creates unsafe situations for both minors and adult players.

From a gameplay and economy perspective, it has become much more difficult to earn gold or obtain desirable items without spending substantial amounts of money. Many features are locked behind paywalls, and a large portion of items are account-bound, reducing trading options and player flexibility.

Customer support is another weak point. Staff responses and support tickets often take a very long time to be addressed, which can be frustrating when players are dealing with urgent issues or bugs. The “Ideas” system—where players can upload their own item designs and others can pre-purchase these creations—is also poorly managed in terms of timing; it can take months for approved items to be released. Overall, the game appears to be understaffed, and faster, more consistent support and content processing would significantly improve the player experience.

Heartopia is a really fun concept and idea, but there are some matters just that make it impossible to actually recommend this game. Free or not, these issues impact gameplay as well as ethics and morals. 

Until now, Heartopia refused to comment on the use of AI in game, but finally they have released a disclosure announcement and added it here on Steam, acknowledging the use of gen-AI in the puzzles and for the in-game chat. While I think the use in the chat is fine (this is not a role that can be done by a person and is the kind of thing that I think an AI assistive tool is actually meant for), the usage on the puzzles feels, frankly, lazy and sloppy. "Reimagine the art of the game into a puzzle" when they could just have one of their artists render them in a different style? The more glaring thing about it, however, is that there is no declaration that they'll stop using it or only use it for the puzzles; they go on to note no gen-AI has been used for monetized items but if they ARE to use any AI assistive tools in the future for planning, modeling, etc., they'll disclose it. Given the refusal to declare at all in the first place and the lack of concern for the complaints about gen-AI being used, it suggests that we WILL see more AI in the future and they don't care much for whether or not people like it. I understand that not everyone cares about gen-AI usage, but the environmental impact of them cannot be ignored. We know nothing of the AI tools they use and whether they were ethically created using singularly their own source material or not, or have relied on their tool learning from other peoples' art. But it's the unwillingness to disclose, and launching on steam without having it immediately disclosed, which makes it difficult to trust them moving forward. 

Additionally, the PC version of Heartopia is simply a mobile port with very few amendments made, and it shows. The screen is divided in half and you must click and hold your left-mouse button to turn the camera around, but you must click on the right screen to do so. It's easy to click the jump button by accident, and the jump button (hot-keyed to space bar) is so close to the tool button (which is hot-keyed to F) also makes it easy to click the wrong buttons. Those who play on two monitors report that clicking and going outside of the screen can cause issues, too. Moving is done with WASD. Because it's a mobile-port, there's no controller support nor is there any kind of key-binding, and it remains a "mobile" game even on PC. In order to interact with anything but your tools, you have to click on bubbles (above peoples' heads for instance to talk to them). And some tools have limited use by the hot-keys. For instance, in gardening, you must press-and-hold F with your mouse to water 3 tiles when you unlock it, as opposed to being able to click and hold the F key. It does not make for the most conducive gameplay. 

Build mode is really impacted by this. There is an advanced mode for building, but again, because it's a mobile-port, it gets some getting used to. One half of the screen is for moving around the screen, the other half is for changing your camera angle. Zooming is, mercifully, done via mouse wheel scroll. Pieces are all placed down one by one, which extends even to painting walls. I understand that mobile games tend to simply things more, but for a game that focuses so much on us being able to build up our dream home and gives us quite a lot of capabilities - like the usage of half-walls, platforms, lofting, etc. - it feels so limiting to have to place everything one-by-one that way, and can become frustrating to many. It took me quite a while to get used to building and even once I have, I still fumble from time to time. 

I think it's a fun game - I like the clothing and cosmetics, I enjoy the social aspect and being able to play with my friends, or get together and do things like cooking parties with special buffs, do hobby-related events (fishing trips, bug hunting, bird watching) together. I find the animals in game really cute and I'm a sucker for collectibles and completing hobbies and their own collectible collections. But the lack of controller support, the poor PC controls, and especially the usage of AI make it impossible to recommend. I will be willing to change my review to positive if AI is removed from the game, but I fear the devs do not care about the impact of their AI usage.

I played for about a week, at the time of writing this steam says I should have about 17.6 hours, though that doesn't account for what I had on Taptap and mobile. It was a lot of fun, my whole group went all in on playing and I enjoyed the clothing and customization they had to offer, going so far as to find guides to fill out my collections. To me, it most resembled games like Animal Crossing and Palia, due to the similar gameplay, both of which are titles I really love to play. I was fully intending on whaling what little I had on the MLP collaboration once it was released because I was so enthralled with the game, and love MLP. Pinkie Pie's pants were adorable, how could I resist?

PROS:
+ Simple, fun, straightforward gameplay.
+ Fishing and cooking were probably my favorite, both interactive enough to hold your attention but without much stress or effort (spare for a few fish or dishes, but that depends on your level).
+ The environments were beautiful, even if you had to be within 3 feet of them to see it even on a good PC. The flower fields were very nice.
+ I love any game I can pet a small animal (the ferrets were my favorite).
+ As far as it looked, it seemed like you would be housed as close to your friends as possible, though not always (Always on a private server, me and my friends would tend to be in the same row).
+ I really liked that the housing was all in the same world and easy to visit, the multiplayer was seamless in this way.
+ The fashion was very pretty, even if I have concerns on it now. A lot of the pieces had sets, and many looked good with several other options from different sets (which can be rare in a gacha game). My favorite being the embroidered flamingo jacket and the matching pants.
+ The instruments were fun to play with, I was learning how to play Für Elise on as many instruments I could, like da bag pipes. I even began transcribing music myself, though it was a trip.
+ Friend emotes!! Being able to hold hands with your friend and drag them around got me the most excited among everything else.

CONS:
- Though I didn't DESPISE them, the controls were optimized and unchanged from mobile, spare for a handful of hotkeys. not the worst for sure, but they could be annoying sometimes.
- I find the lack of world chat isolating, though I understand wanting to keep players' chats among friends (I have the unpopular opinion of this in my group). It makes me sad I'm unable to help other players who might be struggling head-on (maybe they don't know where a fish/bug is), but also unable to communicate that I don't want to be friends even though they walked up to me with a cute emote and spent a wishing star (which is ridiculous). We should at least have the choice, even if you'd have to moderate your chats, God forbid.
- Cats and dogs would CONSTANTLY bug; Cats would not be interactable; they'd walk through walls and get stuck in the floor, in furniture, in ceilings while crying they were starving. If you choose to play, you can fix this by going to the login screen and back, same with other bugs with similar nature (like notifications that wouldn't fuck off despite being checked).
- They're very stingy with their special currency, which tracks for a gacha, but its especially stingier than other gacha titles from what I've heard (10 wishing stars code for 10mil players? Be serious). The pink crystals (exclusively paid currency) exchange rate is ridiculous, and I'm not unfamiliar to unfair microtransactions (I used to play Roblox FFS).
- The dialogue and writing in general can be vague, which can be attributed by the use of AI. This is one I have less of an issue with in the grand scheme of things, and works as a TEMPORARY solution, since translation can be difficult and slow. I don't think I read a line of dialogue from any of the quests after my first day, but I sure did struggle to understand what I was supposed to do at times.
- I didn't like the daily limiting on the quests, though I understand why they did. It would have been nice to have something else focus on consistently other than grinding cash so I could purchase more clothing the next day. Most of the dailies could be finished just by logging in, since I had a lot of the stuff already.
- The usage of generative AI images. I will not scorn them for plagiarism in this specific post, since it sounds and looks like they were scraped from their own data like screenshots, though I have my doubts still. The undisclosed usage in the first place made me lose complete trust in the company to the point I don't think I want to intentionally pick up a product of theirs ever again, even if they were to remove it and apologize. My main concern here is the amount of resources it continues to take. And yes, I am aware that many other much more day to day things (such as meat production and clothing) take up a horrid amount of water, which should be discussed as well, but I am talking about the pointless luxury of making an image spaghetti from art that already exists. Currently, there are data centers running and being built in the US in places with residential housing and once beautiful countryside. People right now living in Texas do not have clean running water because of these data centers drilling for it to cool their systems, and are having to purify it several times over. Next to that, which may be more relevant to people who fill their Steam library, they are buying up computer parts like RAM and driving up their prices into the thousands for one pack. While not a flaw with the game itself, it speaks more on the devs and the owners of Taptap that have spoken highly about how AI will quicken and even improve game development to their shareholders, none of which I would want to ever be associated with.

I myself am an artist who has given up my admittedly already weak hope of ever entering the game industry under a larger company. Humanity and love put in is the charm that makes me love gaming so much, to see the medium get relentlessly run through AI makes it feel like my soul is being spat on. I understand it will be here all the same whether I like it or not, and one day there may not be a way to avoid it in a meaningful way, but I am still entitled to my stance and refusal to comply until I can't anymore.
For anyone else who would still prefer to fight for lifeless convenience because they don't have an ounce of empathy or thought in their mind, I will give you a few words:
You can look up your own ass, because I won't be up there. Spend your time doing something better than defending corporate greed that will never give back to you. Unless you're actively benefitting financially from AI generation despite it's expense from others, then fuck you.

I'm writing this review as someone who has reached level 19 in both beta tests and has been looking forward to this game for many months.

There's no generative AI use disclosure as of writing this review, even though not disclosing it is against Steam's guidelines. AI has been used for some of the in-game assets (definitely at least some of the puzzles).

[b]Monetisation[/b] is very mobile-game - many currencies, limited time passes and gacha. Gacha is obviously very expensive, especially if you want the non-default colours. There's also no pity to get new items, so you can not get some items you want ever, no matter how much you spend, if you're unlucky. And there's no way to get gacha pulls as f2p except for events, like the launch one- only cash money.

[b]PC port[/b] still feels and plays like a bad mobile emulator - since the 1st public test. They really haven't worked on it much at all, I don't know why they delayed the Steam release as it clearly wasn't to work more on the PC controls.
✦ Horrible pop-in, assets load in roughly 5m in front of you. It's not as noticeable on a small phone screen, but even though pop-in doesn't bother me in most games, it was super distracting here, especially in areas with a lot of foliage.
✦ No way to remap key-bindings.
✦ No controller support. If anything, having a controller that's just connected can cause issues.
✦ Can't hide the jump button, it's permanently on the screen same place as the mobile version.
✦ Can't hide the cursor and have the camera follow the mouse, you have to click-drag to move the camera.
✦ In Advanced Build mode half of your screen is used to rotate the camera, other half to move around.
✦ Mouse isn't locked to the in-game window and will drift to other windows/screens when moving the camera.

You have to use your mouse and click to:
✦ Rotate the camera (as said above, you have to click-drag like tap-swiping on mobile)
✦ Rotate furniture.
✦ Talk to NPCs and interact with many things.
✦ Progress dialogue.
✦ Dismount (you can Jump to get out but the "proper" way to get out of a mount is to click the "Dismount X" button).
✦ Activate special tool functions (aiming with fishing rod/bubble wand, watering multiple plants with watering can).
✦ Access most menus, including settings and to exit the game.

I noticed they finally changed the Store page description, at least - it used to claim there's no daily checklists, even though your progress is literally time-gated by Daily Quests. And if you miss any, there's no way to catch up, you're just behind in level/exp forever.

This game contains Generative AI (used to generate the puzzles), which they didn't disclose until the accusations of it surfaced. They've since then confirmed it in the official Discord server, and their message seems to come across that they won't be stopping anytime soon, only that they will be more transparent about it.

With a game that supposedly fosters a creative player base, it feels like such utter disrespect to have an artistic asset for the game to be outsourced to Generative AI. The environmental, economic, and humanitarian issues of it, to name a few. The argument that "It's alright, it's such a minor thing." makes even less sense. If it is such a minor thing, then why couldn't they afford to pay a real artist to render the screenshots for them? Again, it is hypocritical and disrespectful when your audience is supposed to be creatives. 

I've been playing on the mobile port for over a week now and was excited for the PC port, and I could genuinely see that this game had a lot of potential to grow into becoming the next big cozy game, what with having a lot of things cozy players have been asking from other games into one. The building, the open world co-op, dress-up, and other things, with much more room to become a better game. 

However, this game will not have my support anymore moving forward unless they've completely removed Generative AI out of the equation.

- BUGGED TOOLS: [b]tools will be VERY bugged if you have a controller connected while you play, even if youre not using it, so make sure to disconnect it!! everything im about to mention does NOT happen unless you have a controller connected.[/b] the watering can makes you water the maximum amount of plants without holding down the button, and planting crop seeds and flower seeds also makes you plant the maximum amount with no way of letting you only plant one. the bug net is also bugged, so when you equip it youre forced into making a ranged bubble which you can only release in one direction, and after that itll work normally with close range uses, except it then refuses to let you create ranged bubbles until you unequip and reequip it, which then puts you in the same situation you were in when you first equipped it. the tool affected by this the most is the fishing rod. once you take it out, your character is COMPLETELY STUCK. you wont be able to walk or jump or even fish!! even when you unequip your fishing rod, you will still stay stuck until you unequip it AND leave and rejoin the game. if you rejoin while still holding the fishing rod then youll still be stuck. you can still move with your vehicle, but if you enter an area where vehicles arent allowed, then theres no way out besides rejoining. apparently multiple people have had this same issues, and it astounds me that it STILL hasnt been fixed, but again, [b]if anything ive said sounds similar to a bug youre experiencing, then make sure there isnt a controller connected! all of these issues dont seem to happen unless theres a controller connected to your computer.[/b]

overall the game feels somewhat unfinished in a way? it IS incredibly fun but the issues are too prevalent to ignore as of now. im more than happy to change my review if these problems are fixed.

[hr][/hr][b]tldr;[/b]  online service / mtx / mobile port / casual / uses AI / low spec pc / time gated content / social / building-decorating-collecting gameplay[hr][/hr]
I played a lot (since the 9th january)prior to steam release using taptap laucher, so take my steam game hours with a pinch of salt.
i really enjoyed aesthetic of the game (outfits, buildings etc) but we're talking about a mobile port of an online service game with micro transactions making it really difficult to recommend.

to sum up: you have a plot of land in a 12-house city. In the center is the city itself with stores and the main npcs, around the houses are other biomes with animals, other npcs and activities. The gameplay loop revolves around doing daily missions and activities like farming, cooking, fishing, building, bug catching, bird watching, cat/dog training plus social activities with other players. A lot of those activities convert into gold that you can spend into clothing, decor, the actual buildings etc there are also creative activities like writing books and, my favorite because it makes me remember maple story 2 days: painting. you can paint pictures to hang around your house and design your own clothes, which makes heartopia stand out from other regular life sims
all activities cost energy, and you can recover energy by sleeping, going to a spring or eating (tying into the cooking system).
the game has 4 main currencies: gold, purple stars, pink hearts and blue hearts.
"cooler" items are locked behind premium currencies and gacha. purple stars are mainly obtained by completing collection milestones and quests. blue hearts can be obtained in events. pink hearts... swipe it, swiper.
still talking about currencies; skills need a special currency to upgrade, if you choose to not upgrade it'll keep saving your exp, but it seems to have a cap for how much. 

controls are weird, you have to press down the mouse to move around the camera.
building mode its reminiscent of the sims building, but it lacks [b]C[/b]harisma [b]U[/b]niqueness [b]N[/b]erve and [b]T[/b]alent, [i]if you know what i mean[/i]. it's clunky: you have to use the left side of the screen to move the camera and the other side to rotate. plus the "make-everything-above-[i]*here*[/i]-invisible" mode it's weird to use, but maybe i'm just dumb. on the bright side, the game has a lot of options to customize surfaces. [i]unfortunately[/i] some styles of windows, door etc are locked behind account level. it has co-op building, which i find very neat, but it doesn't seem like you can live together.
i also find neat how you can edit you character ANYTIME using a mirror, and the game has darker skin tones available for customization.

since it is a XD game, it has awful ping (i'm from brazil). i get around 250ms on the "global server" and it can get to a whopping 2000ms sometimes which is not a problem since the game is very casual. except when you're cooking, which is a time-sensitive mini-game, where you have to click the food to avoid burning and losing all materials, making the lagging an issue.

the main reason why i think you would not want to play its the usage of [i]theft machine 2.0[/i]™ aka """AI""", cause according to devs:
[quote=mod wendy on discord]AI is used in the puzzle gameplay to reinterpret and redraw in-game snapshot images.[/quote]

by what they're describing this could be the bird watching mini-game, one of the only "original" skills of the game, which is sad... i really struggle to believe this is the only use of it since i've been getting ads with ai voice over. they also mention using ai for translating in game messages, which... is something that been done for so long before without ai... so........... ???

anyway, i had fun building a house, waving hi to people i'll never talk again, petting cute animals and exploring the colorful and cozy world and i think you would too, especially with friends. it feels to me like heartopia is a great low spec option to palia. but right now i don't feel like the game has enough to make me comeback to it because a lot of the main gameplay loot revolves around grinding to get gold, and spend gold in clothes and decor. also because, like someone else already said, it feels weird to use ai in a game where the core gameplay is creativity and human interactivity.

I genuinely enjoy Heartopia and I can see the charm, creativity, and care put into the game. The world is cozy, relaxing, and full of personality, which is why I was really excited to finally play it on PC.

However, I was honestly disappointed to find that the Steam version feels almost identical to the mobile/TapTap version rather than a true PC release. I expected a PC-optimized UI, smoother camera controls, and movement that feels natural with a mouse and keyboard. Instead, the camera movement requires holding down the mouse button and dragging just to move, which feels unintuitive on PC.

When it comes to building, it’s actually easier than on mobile because placement is more accurate and precise. That part feels good. The main issue isn’t the building itself, but the camera movement while building.

Currently, the left side of the screen controls forward and backward movement, while the right side controls left and right movement—similar to Blender’s rotation-style camera. The left side feels more like character-based movement, while the right side acts as the camera’s point of rotation. While this system is interesting, constantly switching between the left and right sides of the screen becomes frustrating over time.

It would feel much more natural if camera panning (the Blender-style movement) were assigned to the middle mouse button, while right-click or WASD handled character-style movement.

There are also some PC quality-of-life issues. Certain tools have enhanced functions that activate on long press—such as flooring tools that can cover multiple areas—but these only work through the mobile UI and not with keyboard or mouse inputs, instead of relying on UI interaction.

I initially assumed the TapTap PC version was only a temporary solution so players could access the game on PC while a proper PC version was still in development, which is why I believed the Steam release was delayed. That’s why this was particularly disappointing.

At its current state, I wouldn’t recommend Heartopia specifically for PC players. That said, if the PC version receives proper optimization, improved controls, and better keyboard and mouse support in the future, I would gladly change my stance and recommend it for PC use. The game has strong potential—it just needs a PC experience that matches it.

I wanted to love this game. The art is cute, the concept is cozy, and decorating is supposed to be the fun, relaxing core loop. Unfortunately, the furniture system completely undermines that promise.

I’ve been playing for 3–4 weeks and have never once seen pink furniture appear in the furniture shop. Not a set. Not a piece. Nothing. I’ve refreshed consistently, engaged with the system as intended, and still ended up with endless repeats of non-pink furniture.

Before anyone says “it’s just RNG,” here’s the problem:
If a core aesthetic option can never appear for some players, with no transparency, no pity system, and no confirmation of spawn rules, that’s not fun randomness — that’s bad design.

I contacted support multiple times, and the response was essentially: “We hope it refreshes.”
No explanation. No confirmation of whether pink furniture is gated by level, time, region, or events. Just vague reassurance and a shrug.

This matters because the game heavily markets cute, pastel, and pink aesthetics. If your enjoyment depends on those visuals and the game quietly locks you out through opaque RNG, you’re going to feel frustrated, not cozy.

A cozy game should not:

Make players feel punished for showing up consistently

Hide critical spawn rules

Rely on “maybe you’ll get lucky someday” as a design philosophy

If you’re okay decorating with whatever the game randomly throws at you, you might enjoy this.
If you care about aesthetics, color themes, or intentional design choices — be warned.

I’m not angry because I didn’t “win.”
I’m angry because the system isn’t respectful of players’ time, expectations, or trust.

Proceed with caution.

We don't normally write reviews on games without having properly played them, but I felt inclined to point out something [b]ABSOLUTELY ATROCIOUS about this game's marketing[/b] since I don't believe anyone else has.

For the last month and a half or so, I've seen midroll ads for Heartopia on YouTube. These Heartopia ads were quite scummy. [b]But not your average or run-of-the-mill YouTube ad scummy, oh no.[/b] They used a tactic I don't believe I've seen before.

You know how many YouTubers run midroll ads in the middle of their videos, but obviously they have no control over what plays during those? But at the same time, many YouTubers also get sponsorships from companies and intentionally place manual ads in the middle of their video to promote the product - these are integrated ads, which they always disclose with a 'this video was sponsored by _' or 'thanks to _ for sponsoring this video'.

[b]Well! Heartopia's marketing team came up with a 'CLEVER' idea. Why not  make their mid-roll ads start with 'This video is sponsored by Heartopia!'?[/b] And that is precisely what they decided to do with some of their ads.

What this essentially means is that if such a Heartopia ad runs in the middle of a YouTuber's video, that YouTuber will now be incorrectly labeled as 'in support' of Heartopia by the ad, which outright lies in saying that they are sponsoring that YouTuber. [b]This is not only Heartopia straight-up LYING, but also in doing so, attempting to MANIPULATE that YouTuber's audience's trust in them to convince that audience to download Heartopia.[/b] "Well, my favorite YouTuber got sponsored by them, it just said so, so I should try the game out!"

We've been around the block, we've seen many scummy games with scummy business practices, but this is a new one for us. And I just had to say something about it, because it's damned horrible. (Yes, I downloaded and ran the game a bit just so I could write this review, and I do not regret doing so.)

[b]I can't say anything for this game's gameplay, if you enjoy it then cool I guess, but I would NEVER support a company whose marketing team stoops to new lows such as these.[/b]

let's get the elephant in the room addressed first: this game uses generative ai. almost every argument ive heard from the pro gen-ai stance can boil down to: "oh boohoo, ai is going to be in everything anyways why are you being such a crybaby?". the biggest issues are—that is, if you can ignore the other deleterious impacts of gen-ai—trustworthiness, a lack of transparency, and a lack of support for the creative community.

for those who do not know, when heartopia was originally released on steam it did not list an ai generated content disclosure. this openly broke steam's terms of service, and it wasn't until their userbase complained about this that they made an announcement in a game discord server and edited the steam page to reflect the ai usage. in this announcement, they made it clear their stance on generative ai is firm. for a game that heavily encourages its userbase to create (quests won't go away until you record a song, write a book, do a puzzle), it shows massive disrespect in its actions to not only continue using gen-ai, but claiming they will notify their audience if they use it on paid items in the future. in this, XD blatantly reveals they do not care about their userbase, nor their artists that should be paid for their work on the game's visual and creative aspects.

besides the gen-ai discourse, this game is very unpolished and a clear money grab. first of all, it's a f2p gacha game. this type of model is already exploitative, but heartopia takes it a step further. there is no pity system, no free pulls (besides one event when you join that gives you gacha currency for free), and the odds are literally stacked against you without full transparency. if you want to play a casino and gamble your money away for no gain but cosmetics (that may or may not be generated with an ai prompt in the future), be my guest. but heartopia's gacha system by far the most predatory gacha model i have ever seen hit the mainstream.

let's talk about the controls. this game was designed to be used on mobile devices. the developers did not take that many steps further to port it to PC. there are no plans to add controller support and the PC steam app is merely just a poorly-made mobile emulator. you cannot excuse this when they have had plenty of beta tester feedback reflecting these concerns for several months now, and over a year overseas. the developers know the community wants better controls and controller support, but they simply refuse to do it because it's easier to make as much money as they can now. this is inexcusable laziness in my eyes. several other mobile games (gacha games included) spent time to work on proper PC and console ports, adjusting the game to fit the new controls and system requirements. heartopia did not, and i am not sure they will for quite some time.

if you still want to play this "cute, cozy game" after learning about and/or experiencing all of these glaring defects, i genuinely question your sanity. your time and money deserve better. you deserve better.

It's sad because even though I'm kind of obsessed with the game, it's also awful and I could not in good conscience recommend it. Here's why:

1) Being forced to login with Facebook, Apple or Google. That's the first thing you'll see when opening the game, and it was almost enough to make me uninstall right then and there.

2) The genders are hella wrong, at least in French. 99% of the time people are talking to me like I'm a man. 1% of the time they're using really weird gender neutral phrasing that just don't work.

3) Using generative AI without disclosing it is not ok.

4) This is not a PC game, not even a PC port, it's just full on a mobile game that happens to be playable with a mouse. I even got a tutorial telling me to ZOOM ON THE SCREEN WITH MY FINGERS. COME ON MAN.

5) Just overall, the controls and the features of the game are absolutely terrible:
- There's constantly a jump button on screen, and it's really big too. We don't need this, this is not a phone screen. You can remove the jump button we've got this covered, thank you very much...
- Decorating is a nightmare, both to build rooms and to place items.
- Why do we need to click on EVERYTHING?
- Why would you choose ''F'' to interact with things? I feel like it's usually ''E'' in other games. I mean, we don't get to use this much anyway since we click on everything so whatever...
- You can scavenge for berries with an axe in your hands. However, you can't do it with a fishing pole, THAT needs to be put away first (with an extra like 3-4 clicks).
- The mouse isn't locked to the game screen, and it goes to my other screen when moving the camera. Happens all the time.

And I say all of this while also enjoying the game a lot, trust me. Imagine how GREAT it could be if it wasn't, well, that...! This game was NOT ready for release, and needs a LOT of work, but still has great potential depending on how it's handled in the future.

The core of the game is full of potential. However, the PC version urgently needs a full UI/UX rework. If the UI is properly redesigned.
Below are clear, focused suggestions that I believe should be addressed immediately.

[b]UI Scale and Layout:[/b]
The UI is far too large for PC. Most menus take up half the screen or more and feel like an oversized mobile interface. Store menus are especially hard to use because items are too big and require constant scrolling. The UI needs to be more compact and optimized for PC monitors.
The user interface should use small, compact text, and stay in a position that doesn’t block the player’s view.

[b]Controls:[/b] (most crucial because this experience is basically everywhere)
Controls need more polish on PC. Keyboard shortcuts behave inconsistently.

Such as: (there are much more than the stuff i listed down below)
[list]
[*]The Tab key opening the watch but not closing it. 

[*]The B key opens bag but when i click B again, it doesn't close the bag.

[*]The X key summons a vehicle but when pressing X again it doesn't unsummon it.

[*]Swapping between items should be much simpler and fully keyboard-based, such as using number keys or dedicated shortcuts, instead of pressing T and then clicking a tool with the mouse.

[*]Chatting in a vehicle only works if you click on the chat icon using the mouse.

[*]Watering/seeding multiple plants requires a mouse hold, why can't players just do it by holding the F Key?

[/list]

[b]Camera:[/b]
The game also needs better camera controls, including the ability to zoom in and out with the mouse wheel and additional camera shortcuts to make movement and viewing feel easy and natural. Full key rebinding should be supported to meet basic PC expectations.
A big issue is when rotating the camera, holding the right click on the players body won't let me rotate.

[b]UI Customization:[/b]
Many UI elements feel unnecessary and add visual clutter. Players should have the option to hide or disable unused buttons to keep the interface clean and easier to navigate. It shouldn't look like a mobile emulator.

[b]Game Issues:[/b]
Some players have issues interacting with pets, the interaction bubbles on the pet does not appear at all. 
Pet Behavior in general is broken.

With a full PC focused UI and controls rework, Heartopia would be on a strong and successful path. The core game is great. the interface and controls are the main things holding it back.
Make the game smooth and responsive.

The game itself is decent; it’s fun and pretty chill. However, for PC players, the UX/UI is poor, which can really ruin the experience when you’re just trying to relax. I don't mind the use of AI in the game as I said, the core game is good and worth a try but I can't fully recommend it to PC players with the current control scheme.

+ Pretty cute art style... actually it's too cute but also some fashion is pretty cool.
+ Casual and very enjoyable way to pass the time if you want to chill out over the weekend.
+ A great choice if you are looking for something to play with a partner. (if you are in relationship with someone)
+ Decent building system but don't expect this will be massive complex system here.
+ For a life-sim that focuses more on lifestyle and community than hardcore gameplay, there is a pretty good variety of things to do.

- Interactions are clunky, you are forced to use the mouse for almost everything, including talking to NPCs and using items.
- There is no key binding system and no option to rotate the camera using the keyboard.
- On my 3 monitors setup, the cursor keeps "bleeding" onto the other screens every time I try to rotate the point of view.

This is a "1-hour review," so you could call it biased, but based on the controls, it’s clear the developers focused on mobile and tablet users rather than optimizing the experience for PC.

Overall, it’s worth your time if you want a casual community game or something to play with your partner (or maybe your friends but I feel like this game is going to be fun with strangers imo). If you’re playing alone, I highly suggest joining an FB group or Discord, as the community I has found have the pretty active and friendly while some of them just turn this into an ERP and Tinder-like experience somehow...

**Final note this is 17 Jan 2026 review Dev may update for better control in the future so if you are reading this review after I posted for a while please check the patch note or give check it yourself.**

I tried the game first through TapTap and now on Steam, and I’m also someone who played a lot of Palia, so here are some of my thoughts.

Pros:

⋆ The game is very cute. The art style is simple, cozy, and charming.

⋆ Super chill gameplay. Farming isn’t tryhard at all and feels relaxing, same with the food system.

⋆ Nice social vibes: shared emotes, seeing other players’ houses in the open world, and playing with friends feels wholesome.

⋆ If you like light farming + social games, Heartopia can get a bit addictive after a while. The social menu is also really well done.

Cons (and why I don’t really recommend it):

⋆ The gacha system. I expected some greedy monetization since it’s a Chinese mobile game, but it’s still very predatory. A big turn-off for me.

⋆ PC port controls are really bad. The port clearly didn't have any work on this, and the camera/movement feels awful. This seriously needs fixing.

⋆ The game feels very empty unless you already have friends to play with or you’re extremely social. Chats seem limited, and after hours of playing I only saw a few nearby houses. For a “social” game, it feels lonely.

⋆ Leveling and tutorial quests are tedious and kind of boring. The problem is that you have to do them to unlock basic content.

⋆ Very few professions/things to do so far.

⋆ Compared to Palia, you could explore more freely early on, had more professions, saw more players in the world, and NPCs felt way more alive. In Heartopia, NPCs feel pretty bland.

Personally, the main reason I don’t recommend this game is the gacha system. I’ve fallen into these systems before, and if you’re someone who spends real money on games, this is exactly the kind of monetization you should avoid or be careful with especially when most new content and updates are cosmetic.
The game just launched, so maybe it’ll improve over time (especially the controls), but for now I’d say it’s not for everyone and should be tested.

I'd like to change my review because I didn't play long enough to be informed on some things, but I played the app version more and found some more positive things (as well as negatives). I will still keep my review negative for now because the game still has bugs and the PC version is not fixed and because of the scummy microtransactions.

The game looked interesting when I found the steam page. Slightly concerned when I found out it was going to be an app too but that's not always bad. I was more concerned when I discovered it was free because how are they going to make their money? Yep, gacha and paid currency. The game had so much potential but they focused on making it a cash grab instead of developing a good game. The individual purchasable items are way too expensive. I'm sad I'll never have a full game with all furniture content unlocked. If they had focused on making it for PC and made it a one-time purchase with potentially some dlc/content packs I'm sure it would be a big hit. People need to stop giving positive ratings due to all the current issues and we shouldn't encourage the predatory microtransaction formula.

CONS:
QUALITY:
-PC version has horrible buggy controls, doesn't look any better than app version
-A lot of bugs right now even on app version
-Both versions have horrible render distance
-General graphics quality could be better
-UI for some things needs work

-HOBBIES: I've changed my opinion on some things after playing more. But cooking is still boring. You just have to wait a minute for it to cook and sometimes tap on it so it doesn't burn. A proper minigame would give it more life. Needs auto-crafting as well. Birdwatching is difficult to make progress and the event isn't fun and is hard to reach goals. Fishing event is way better and fishing in general is the most developed hobby. Bug catching is okay but a bit underwhelming.

-BUILDING: Some features are good but some are buggy or inconvenient. Needs work.

-TRAVEL: Can't run fast and vehicles are slow. I think they did make them a bit faster than release though. Fast-travel is too expensive and needs a few more bus stops. Also you should be able to fast travel to your house. I assume they want you to pay for the game membership so you can fast-travel without using coins.

-MULTIPLAYER: No public server chat, can't directly message friends so you can only see messages of friends who are directly in front of you. Can't trade items.

-FURNITURE UNLOCK: You can craft some furniture but you can only buy two rotating furniture items per day until you unlock higher ranks of the guild thing. It takes a while to unlock any more and even so it's still not much. Hard to unlock crafting recipes too.

-CONTENT: Still needs more content, couldn't find a way to unlock more hairstyles or change it to colors like pink or purple. I'm sure they'll add more features like more hobbies but some things are lacking.

-AI: AI content is used which is lazy and disappointing to see, though it hasn't tainted my experience too much.

-MICROTRANSACTIONS: See first part of review.

PROS:
-CUTE/FASHION: Very cute, has cute outfits you can get without using gacha.

-HOUSE DECORATING: Decorating system is pretty good and customizable, aside from some bugs and missing features. Co-op housing.

-HOUSING PLOTS: Housing plots are in the server and you can see other player's houses without adding them as friends or loading into the plot.

-MULTIPLAYER: Good amount of things to do together, easy to make friends. Chat auto translates so you can chat with foreign players.

-FREE: It's free (At least base content)

There are a lot of good features and hopefully they fix a lot of things but unfortunately it will never live up to its full potential due to the microtransactions.
Game has good potential to continue growing, will change the review to positive if they make the PC experience better

i'll start off by saying that i agree with the common consensus regarding the controls and camera being horrid, and that controller support would have been greatly appreciated. despite the game's bugs and flaws, however, i was still genuinely enjoying myself. hanging out with people in the game felt like hanging out with your friends in real life, and there was no shortage of things to do even with the deliberately slow pacing (which i also enjoyed). the game is very pretty and i really enjoy the creativity it allows its players to have.

that being said, despite how much i personally enjoyed the game, i'm still going to be joining the people dropping it because of the use of generative AI. i will admit that i knew about it before they disclaimed it, as the Extremely ugly puzzles in an otherwise beautiful game were a dead giveaway, but i kept playing for a little while longer in the hopes that the generative AI elements would eventually be removed. however, if what other people have expressed is true, then it seems that XD is intent on keeping these elements in their game.

i genuinely don't understand why they felt the need to generate the images for their puzzles when it seems that all other imagery in the game was made by humans. as far as i can tell, the textures for the clothes and furniture seem genuine, so why be lazy in this aspect? i'm sure that players would have waited as long as was needed for more puzzles to be drawn by hand, especially since this is a slow paced game in the first place. hell, maybe they could have given fans the opportunity to make the puzzles themselves, therefore adding more depth to the creative freedom they already had.

there have been numerous articles and studies detailing the negative impact gen-AI has on the environment, as well as the exploitative practices that are used to train it. how on earth am i supposed to enjoy a "cozy game" that centers so much around nature in the face of such hypocrisy? how am i supposed to thoroughly enjoy creating books, art, and music in the game when i am afraid of the possibility of XD scraping my submissions for the sake of training gen-AI? as an artist myself, this has definitely soured my opinion of the game and made it hard to enjoy.

this, along with the bugs, glitches, and poor job they did of porting a mobile game to PC, make me think that overall, though the game looks nice and Was fun, it was lazily made. if this game wins any awards, i can see them being rescinded like how they were for Expedition 33 when it had come out that they had also used gen-AI in their game. though i would consider returning upon the removal of such elements, at this time i cannot ethically recommend this game to anyone.

for a game that has promoted a PC release for some time I had hoped that there would have been more work put into ensuring it is functional on that platform. It is not. The graphics are hardly different to mobile despite the quality of the cutscenes being rendered significantly better which is a strange choice. In fact, the whole game is hardly different to mobile. It feels like the whole game is emulating a mobile app. 

Which sucks, because I'm really trying to ignore these disappointing factors that keep getting in the way of my day one experience. There is a lot to do, the online servers are very fun, its just impossible to enjoy navigating them. If you plan to build a nice house on PC, save yourself the trouble and use a mobile instead. You will spend 80% of the time playing a guessing game for when the cursor decides to rotate or move the camera. Despite controls being part of the settings menu, there are none that you can set, and nothing you can do to alter this horrible UI at the moment. 

This game feels stuck beta, I'm surprised a steam rollout was ever considered, since they clearly chose not to branch out from a simple mobile game. I will continue to play this game in hope that that changes are made sooner than later. Their playerbase will suffer immensely if it is ignored.

The concept of the game itself is quite good and it was pretty refreshing to have a new cozy game that you could just be comfortable playing while hanging out with friends, it's just disheartening to know that there is the use of generative AI for a minigame that could've easily been redrawn by an artist or just slap on snapshots from the build itself, no embellishments needed. That would've been preferrable than leaning onto AI just for freaking puzzles. It's a shameful thing to do in a game that is supposed to be all about unleashing your imagination.

On top of it all, while having a simultaneous PC release, the controls are very clunky and unnatural, it almost seems like the platform was just added as an afterthought without proper optimization. The lack of controller support is also a major turn-off. Do better, replace any AI generated media (both in-game and in any promotional media used) and reconfigure the PC controls. Unfortunately, I will no longer be playing the game or endorsing it due to the issues stated above.

3/5 Stars

game_name: 日本事故物件監視協会2 -Japan Stigmatized Property-
review_text:
I actually consider this one to be a lot worse than the first game. The first game had a decent balance between subtle anomalies that you really had to pay attention to find, and creepy (albeit more obvious) stuff. In this one, I guess they were trying to be more scary, so they traded in most of the subtle stuff for extremely obvious stuff like faces and jumpscares.

The problem is that now literally like 95% of the anomalies are super obvious things, so there is no longer any challenge to the game. I wouldn't even classify it as a "spot the anomaly" game anymore, as it's moreso a "sit there and get bombarded with attempted jumpscares" game. And I wouldn't even say that the jumpscares are that scary, because they overdid them to the point where you're expecting them.

I think the best way to catch people with jumpscares is to make them more rare. That way, they'll be really focused and looking for anomalies, so they'll be more surprised when you throw in the odd jumpscare every now and then. But when 95% of the anomalies are attempted jumpscares, the player tends to be already bracing themself for it when it happens.

I'd say that the first game is probably worth the price, but I wouldn't recommend this one unless it's on a steep discount.

game_name: 日本事故物件監視協会2 -Japan Stigmatized Property-
review_text: The first one was really fun and a mix of "spot the difference" and "eerie unsettling atmosphere". There was a lot of boring moments though waiting for something to happen and it's clear the devs took that to heart in this one. The jump scares and "changes" are all super obvious. We found ourselves memorizing the layout of each room only for the anomalies to be people standing around and then jumping at the camera 99% of the time. We got 100% anomalies in 3/4 properties on our first try. The screenshots are the norm. I'd get the first one and maybe pass on this one unless on sale. Also AI Generated jumpscares? Pass. AI ruining everything.

As a stalker fan I was excited to try this game, but I can't recommend it until the developers address ongoing security warnings.

Since launch, Bitdefender and other antivirus software flag the game executable (pioner-win64-shipping.exe) for attempting phishing by connecting to a suspicious endpoint. The devs acknowledged this months ago, calling it a "false positive" and saying they'd fix it….you surrre about that?

The advice given by some in the community is concerning just whitelisting it when even the devs haven't explained why it keeps happening is crazy.

I'm not saying the game is malware. I'm saying the developers have had plenty of time to resolve what they claim is a simple false positive, and they haven't. That raises questions.

If you choose to play keep this in mind, you don't get hacked or exploited right away. Don’t trust people just saying it's fine they will not help you when you're screwed. And will just say it's your fault. Some of these collection types are stored for months or years with your data until you think it didn't come from them before attacking.

I’ll keep an eye out for updates and will update once it's fixed to focus on the game itself.

This game is a masterclass in how to squander potential at every conceivable level. From the moment it launches, it signals its lack of respect for the player’s time, intelligence, and money.
The gameplay is painfully repetitive and mechanically shallow. Core systems feel unfinished, unbalanced, or outright broken, as if they were stitched together hours before release. Controls are unresponsive, animations are stiff, and combat devolves into mindless button-mashing with no tactical depth or sense of progression. Any challenge comes not from clever design, but from cheap difficulty spikes and poorly tuned enemies.
The technical state is indefensible. Bugs are constant and immersion-breaking: frequent crashes, clipping issues, AI that fails to function, and quests that simply do not complete. Performance is abysmal, with severe frame drops and loading times that belong to a different decade. It feels less like a finished product and more like a rushed beta that was pushed out the door prematurely.
The narrative, if it can be called that, is incoherent and lazily written. Characters are one-dimensional, dialogue is wooden, and plot points are introduced and abandoned with no payoff. There is no emotional engagement, no world-building worth caring about, and no justification for the hours the game demands from the player.
Visually, the game is equally disappointing. Environments are bland and repetitive, asset reuse is obvious, and the art direction lacks any cohesive identity. The audio design fares no better: forgettable music, poor sound mixing, and voice acting that ranges from disinterested to outright embarrassing.
Ultimately, this game feels like a cynical cash grab—an exercise in mediocrity wrapped in marketing promises it fails to deliver on. There is no redeeming feature substantial enough to justify playing it, let alone purchasing it. It is not just disappointing; it is a waste of time.

Played it briefly and couldnt get into it. Cutscenes/dialogue that are clunky, combat doesnt feel good. Its a beautiful game, but also seems super linear. Maybe it gets better if you give it more time, but i just didnt enjoy it.

I really love the overall concept of the game and the diversity of its landscapes. The weapons feel satisfying to use, and the attachments genuinely add depth and utility. The story is there and easy to follow, which is great — though that’s not where my main concerns lie.

Issue 1: Performance and FPS Stability
For context, I’m running a GTX 4070 Ti, AMD Ryzen 9 5950X, and 64GB of DDR4 3600MHz RAM — so performance shouldn’t be an issue. However, FPS stability is very inconsistent. Frame drops happen frequently enough to be noticeable. Some optimization would be great before the developers focus on adding more content.

Issue 2: Reload Animation Bugs
Possibly related to performance, but I’ve had to tap reload seven to nine times before the game registers the action. Sometimes the animation completes, yet the magazine doesn’t actually reload. This becomes especially frustrating during large fights (15+ enemies) or in PVP encounters where timing is everything.

Issue 3: Falling Physics
Falling feels off — almost like your character has their own personal gravity well. You hit terminal velocity instantly, and even small steps off a ledge can send you flying forward at high speed. I once crouch-walked off a platform and was launched about 20 feet ahead within a second.

Issue 4: Objective Marker Visibility
The objective markers tend to blend too much into the environment. During my first world event with the Raven Cultists sacrificing Brigade members in pyres, the objective was to “put out the fires” — but there was no indication of how or where. Being the only player there, with enemies constantly respawning, it took me almost the entire event to notice the marker hidden behind all the on-screen chaos.

Final Thoughts
The game has a ton of potential, and I genuinely enjoy the ideas and atmosphere. But improving the gameplay consistency, performance, and clarity of objectives would make the experience much smoother and more enjoyable overall. I will continue to play this game, but for now I cannot recommend until some improvements are made to smooth out the moment to moment gameplay. 

If anyone has any tips on improving the performance, I left the comments open for suggestions.
