I want to share my experience with KIKI Games, a team that acts as both developer and publisher.

From what I’ve seen, they tend to release games very quickly and then abandon them shortly after launch. Many of their titles contain bugs, lack polish, and receive little to no post-launch support. Updates are rare or nonexistent, even when issues are clearly present.

This is disappointing, because some of these games could have real potential if they were properly supported and improved over time. Unfortunately, that doesn’t seem to happen.

In my opinion, this feels like a cash-grab and move on approach. If you’re on a tight budget, I’d strongly recommend thinking twice before spending your money on their games.

I'll be honest with you, Kiki Games' Team emailed me and provided me a key in exchange for a review. I obliged because I love simulator games like Barista Simulator, Supermarket Simulator, etc. I didn't know that the devs for this completely abandoned the game (according to most reviews and users on Reddit). Also, I found out that the most of the capsule images are AI generated content (the images of clothing in the menus). Obviously, the Steam cover art is AI generated content as well. It's totally my fault and ignorant of me to accept the game without fully researching this as I don't support use of AI in videogames or in a lot of different fields, especially art. I played for about 30-45 minutes to at least fulfill my promise in reviewing and I will say that so far, the game feels very lazily made. It's clunky and I would not recommend it.

The developers promised to fix the lag issues multiple times, but went ahead and released the full version of the game knowing that the lag issues still existed. 
Most bugs can be solved with a simple reload, but the lag issue is a forced game mechanic. You must open new contracts to get new clothing. You must display the clothing or your customers will be unhappy. You cannot cancel these contracts. After you open around 12 contracts and display all the clothing from those contracts, you start noticing the lag spikes (even on high-end computers). At 20 contracts you can forget playing the game because anytime that you face your product your screen will drop to 5 FPS. I have never managed to make it through all 31 contracts. 
Until you see some sort of optimization update for this game, stay far, far away.

Clothing Store Simulator is a barebones, buggy, and quick-and-dirty Unity simulation. These types of "games" are all built from the same recycled assets, stuffed with AI art, and padded with grind to hide how little actual game is underneath.

Snowflakes clip through the store, customers get stuck in between clothing racks, every transaction nets you 8 XP, draw distance suffers when your store is expanded so racks look empty when they're not, and once you get staff the game runs on autopilot and plays itself. 

Devs are simultaneously working on at least 2 other simulator projects at the same time and have released another one this year. They all look exactly the same, they just have a different store/theme.

My advice to the dev team would be, instead of working on 4-5 copy/paste low effort simulator games at the same time, focus on one and actually flesh it out.

Unfortunately I cant really recommend it because despite being full release the game feels and looks incredibly unfinished. The street the store sits on is largely empty and lacking any detail, the art in the game is clearly AI, and every item within the online store all have their original file names like Mannequin_1 and so on. It still feels like its in its early testing stages and lacks any real polish to make it feel like a full game. 

If they cleaned it up and worked with real artists for their in-game art I'd definitely change my opinion, but until then its a no from me. Which is a shame because I was looking forward to this game for a long while. 

TLDR:
Pros: Easy to get into, relaxing gameplay loop
Cons:  Lacks polish, uses AI, feels unfinished

Title: A Politically Charged Mess with No Substance

I entered Trump Simulator 2025 expecting some satire, but I got instead a slovenly, one-sided mess both as a political commentary and as a game. Rather than offering clever humor, it goes very far into over-the-top, unfunny stereotypes—trolling Trump supporters in ways that feel contrived and outdated. The game includes cringe-worthy poorly written jokes about the right blaming everything on transgender people and making claims that "woke killed QAnon," which sound more like half-hearted Twitter efforts than thoughtful satire.

It also requires unnecessary jabs at Trump and Elon Musk's beef, forcing in a reference to Elon's cars and flamethrowers for no real reason. Even putting the political foolishness aside, the game itself is just not fun. With only about 10 minutes of content, even at $2, it feels like a waste of time. The gameplay is nonexistent, and rather than being a sharp or witty take on modern politics, it just feels like a lazy attempt at dunking on one side without any real depth.

If you’re looking for a political satire game that actually has some effort behind it, this isn’t it. Avoid.

1/10

While the game is satire and has a number of things to do, none of it is funny or creative enough to keep anyone entertained beyond 10 minutes. Most of the gags are tongue in cheek cliches that have little beyond your liberal aunt's Facebook posts. Only consider buying it if you are after an easy 100% achievement game.

Title: A Politically Charged Mess with No Substance

I entered Trump Simulator 2025 expecting some satire, but I got instead a slovenly, one-sided mess both as a political commentary and as a game. Rather than offering clever humor, it goes very far into over-the-top, unfunny stereotypes—trolling Trump supporters in ways that feel contrived and outdated. The game includes cringe-worthy poorly written jokes about the right blaming everything on transgender people and making claims that "woke killed QAnon," which sound more like half-hearted Twitter efforts than thoughtful satire.

It also requires unnecessary jabs at Trump and Elon Musk's beef, forcing in a reference to Elon's cars and flamethrowers for no real reason. Even putting the political foolishness aside, the game itself is just not fun. With only about 10 minutes of content, even at $2, it feels like a waste of time. The gameplay is nonexistent, and rather than being a sharp or witty take on modern politics, it just feels like a lazy attempt at dunking on one side without any real depth.

If you’re looking for a political satire game that actually has some effort behind it, this isn’t it. Avoid.

1/10

While the game is satire and has a number of things to do, none of it is funny or creative enough to keep anyone entertained beyond 10 minutes. Most of the gags are tongue in cheek cliches that have little beyond your liberal aunt's Facebook posts. Only consider buying it if you are after an easy 100% achievement game.

This is a tough one ... I've rarely seen a game that has such good foundation, but misses the mark this hard.

What's good: there is a LOT of different products, and it's fun to work through the process of creating them. I really like the variety, and there's quite a bit to do with each one. Also, this game is very fun with a friend (or more). 

Alright, what holds it back? The depth that each product has is also it's greatest weakness. In particular, the most complex products are the ones that have the least automation. Having a bunch of in depth items to create doesn't work if you don't have a way to scale it up. There's also no storage system, so if you want to collect resources, you have to toss them on the ground or physically put it on a shelf. Why can't we just get a chest to put things in?

If the storage and automation issues were fixed, this would be a REALLY good game. Until then, I'd mainly recommend this game if you have like, 4 or more friends to play with so that each person can specialize in different product types.

All I can describe this game as is chaotic. Very unbalanced mechanics due to its leveling system in relation to customer intake. More is not necessarily better. What it needs is lower levels and lower customer count or a different way to level up which would also ease off on the pc hardware strain this game creates as its just not realistic to have so many ppl scrambling for the same things and everything is sold out each day which you have to keep restocking nonstop even with stock on hand. A proper inventory system would also benefit as well as product sales each day as there is no way of knowing unless you physically inspect your stock shelves to know what you have available. I would think this is the first and foremost system that would be implemented in such games.
You simply cant keep up even very early on in game where I have found I can just stand and load white bakery loaves all day nonstop because they keep buying everything out of stock and boxes of products are gone before your eyes even before half the day is gone and thats less level 15.  Controls are also a bit clunky and we dont need to be permantly told with text in your face how to stock a shelf. Im sure after 5mins of gameplay one would know how to stock a shelf in these type of games as they are all the same. And money in this game is totally irrelevent as its very easy to make due to the fact of each customer buying multiple same products and they come in nonstop in multiple numbers which leads back to my intial statement. Not a balanced game yet the pricing of products are good. It needs work but the mountainous influx of customers and their buying habits needs a major overhaul.

AI gooner slop. Got this game on sale and had to request a refund. There was no way for me to save my progress and when I logged back in after putting in 118 min. all the work was erased and there was no save file. The gameplay is extremely bare bones when compared to other retail simulation titles. There is no story, no interaction in the world, just open shop, fill shop, close shop for the day, and repeat. Every single item is locked behind incremental upgrade walls. What does that mean? It means you have to sell X amount of the item you start with just to get the second. For example: Sell 100 pillows to unlock the ability to buy the license for the next pillow, then you still have to buy the actual pillow you just unlocked. The game feels entirely like it was coded by AI. Characters necks breaking and heads being in weird positions was expected it happened so much. The only saving grace I found was the RIDICULOUS outfits the customers wear. They actually made me laugh a bit and will make you say " wtf are you wearing" . I guess also being able to upload pictures and put them on pillows to sell is a pretty cool plus. I also had the Big Boob DLC and it is not worth having. It would take you literal DAYS in real life to unlock your first NSFW picture. Literal days of your life for mediocre AI slop art. This game is marketed as fully released but it really seems like a beta, or early access game.   Don't waste your money on this title.

Coming from a fan who genuinely enjoyed all three previous FMV games by these developers, this is easily the weakest entry in the series.

On paper, this game looks bigger. More scenes, more ideas, more ambition. In practice, the story is messy, poorly paced, and ultimately disappointing. The heavy reliance on time hopping sounds interesting but is handled in a way that breaks immersion. Characters react far too casually to major absences and sudden reappearances, accepting drastic changes with little to no questioning. This removes tension and makes the world feel artificial rather than lived in.

Because the narrative constantly jumps around the same timeline, there is almost no room for meaningful character development. Unlike earlier games where relationships and backstories were allowed to unfold naturally, the protagonist here never spends enough sustained time with anyone for those connections to feel earned. As a result, emotional moments fall flat.

The villains are another letdown. Their motivations are generic and underdeveloped, and while the game attempts a major reveal, it lacks impact due to the absence of proper buildup. What should have been a strong narrative payoff instead feels rushed and hollow.

There are also noticeable inconsistencies in the protagonist’s moral framework, which further weakens the writing. Certain actions are treated as acceptable while others are condemned, without the story doing the work to justify that contrast.

Overall, this is a disappointing conclusion to a series that previously delivered tighter storytelling and stronger character focus. Bigger does not mean better, and despite the increased scope, this entry lacks the narrative weight and emotional payoff that made its predecessors memorable.

"Clothing Store Simulator: Prologue"  is not a game.  It's an advertisement for a product called "Clothing Store Simulator".  Clothing Store Simulator is barely more than an asset flip.  It seems to have been copy + pasted from a very basic Unity first person job simulator template (we can see this used in a number of asset flips such as the "Trailer Shop Simulator" game.  In this case a little effort went into "borrowing" some clothing/mannequin assets to make a basic shopkeeping simulator, but the quality is extremely low and any value the product has is questionable..

But this isn't completely relevant because this is not Clothing Store Simulator, it's just an advertisment for it, so it's not correct to try to review that completely distinct product.  We must instead review this for what it is... an advertisement.  If you enjoy advertisements, you can consume them for free on the web or on Youtube without needing to add them to your game library, download them, and then launch the advertising.  It's impossible in a general sense to recommend people should do that over playing real, full games on Steam, and that applies here with "Clothing Store Simulator: Prologue" too.

The objective of this advertisement, unmistakeably, is to get gamers to pay up $13 USD to buy the full "Clothing Store Simulator" game.  I don't recommend you do that.

This is only free in the same way that adverts on Youtube and banner ads in your browser are.  This is a game demo that's been dumped on Steam to advertise another Steam store page in the hopes of raking in some cash.  Just because advertisements are free, doesn't mean they're worth your time.

It's important to understand there's a formal process to create a demo/page for a game which hasn't been followed here:

https://partner.steamgames.com/doc/store/application/demos

While it might seem like an inocuous mistake, publishing demos and advertisements as if they are full games is misleading and in some ways harmful.  This advertisement is taking up marketing space and visibility that would be better made available to a developer that's done the right thing and created a full game.  Instead, that visibility is given over to a demo/advert that's been published incorrectly and is polluting the marketplace.

It's unclear if this mistake was made through incompetence or a deliberate intent to mislead gamers.  Often developers may choose to incorrectly publish demo/advertisements as separate games as a cheap way to bypass paying Valve for marketing presence on the Steam front page, which may seem clever to them, but it's harmful behaviour and it's not good for gamers.

Advertisements and incorrectly published demos don't add +1 to your game collector count, they aren't full games.  It's impossible for me to recommend this incorrectly published advertising spam to gamers, you deserve better than adverts.

This game is trying too hard to be Stanley Parable while misunderstanding it on a fundamental level. Moral Dilemma throws random "quirky" gags every second without any real theme or cohesion beyond being "random." The "choices matter" tag barely matters in the first section of the game, as even staying still in one room will just run the painfully millennial dialogue until it runs out of lines with no real result or change in progression. Anytime the "alternate" path is taken is just railroads you back to the main path with essentially no room for personal exploration besides the aforementioned gags in the hallways that don't have any immediate impact on the playthrough. It seems designed with a very specific demographic in mind that enjoys this form of humor. However, even with its lack of price tag, I cannot in good faith recommend this game to anyone when Stanley Parable provides a far more enjoyable and interactive experience.

While this game is (semi) funny, as it parodies the Stanley Parable decently, this game does have a few flaws already.

Use of AI:
- It doesn't make sense why the creator needed to use an AI-Generated logo & steam store page artwork, when a logo for the game already exists within the pause menu. Why at that point generate a logo when you have one?

DLC is required for (most) of the other experiences.
- Some of the bonus content you unlock after a first quick playthrough requires the paid DLC. Basically this game is the demo, and you need to cough up money to experience the rest. If this was labeled as a demo, and the rest of the content (the game) was paid, I could understand, but this feels like most of the content is locked behind a paywall. Even collectables for an ending is locked beneath the DLC paywall for some reason?

(Some) lack of choice.
- Since this is inspired by the Stanley Parable, this game allows you to make choices, but most of said choices lead to the same outcome. Being rude to the interviewer or being nice leads to the same outcome , getting most things wrong and correct leads to the same dialogue eventually, etc. The Stanley Parable had many branching paths depending on your actions, while this feels linear.

Overall this is a fairly okay Stanley Parable inspired game, but I don't feel like coughing up money to experience the "DLC" when most of the content locked behind it could have just been in the base game, or the whole game itself could have been sold for under $5 without any other paywalls.

A shoddy imitation of the Stanley Parable, Monty Python, and Portal all at once, with none of the appeal of any. Extreme railroading and forcing the player to say the next line in the game's jokes. Most of those jokes were repeated many times and I'm surprised that in less than 3 hours I became this sick of it all. I would be angry so much of the rest of the game and so many achievements are gated by dlc, but I can't make myself care. If I can't bring myself to play through a second time for even 1 other ending, how could I make myself endure the dlc, much less pay for that suffering?
It feels as if someone old, lower middle class, and British wrote down his best shower thoughts, and chuckled as he put them into a "game." He struggled so hard to make the code work (and it doesn't, the failures are just played off as a joke... the same one, over and over...), he didn't stop to wonder if it was fun. It was not.

I mean, It's a good game, it has an interesting and unique sense of humor that honestly I quite enjoyed!

That is where my compliments come to an end, however.
As much as I wanted to give this game a positive rating, I feel that it has many shortcomings that really shoot itself in the foot, here!
Here's just a general list with no real order, which might be a little nuanced and VERY opinionated, so take it with a grain of salt-- okay, maybe the whole shaker-- anyways...

1. AI-generated thumbnails.
 Honestly this one kind of goes without saying, but the usage of AI really does make me dismiss most of the GOOD qualities of this game, simply with just the fact that the developer(s?) dared to even use it for something so trivial as a thumbnail/promotional art. I mean, really?? It's incredibly misleading and mocks the very essence of art-- which a videogame is, of course-- and also makes it seem like an UNTRUSTWORTHY game overall, I mean, what else in this game may be made with AI? The store-page SAYS It's just promo material, which I admit I believe, but it already brings me to such distrust with the creator of the game, It really is NOT a good thing for the dev-player relationship.
Also AI is bad for the environment and steals artists' works without neither consent, justification, notification, nor any compensation!

2. Odd/out-of-place area decoration.
This one is much more opinionated, and comes with extra nuance, to be entirely fair. See, I don't think I'd have much issue with the randomly-placed models without any correlation to their respective environments, if it was at the very least funny or complimented the bizarreness, but it generally does not. The questionable mix of fancy seats mixed with the dullness of office desks doesn't quite align with me, really. Honestly it kind of feels like the devs just made too many rooms with too much space and didn't know how to give it proper decoration so they just cluttered it with random free models they found. Of course, I'm not one to say this is true, but it is the feeling the furniture (and weaponized golfcart in that one room??) gives me.

3. Sound design, generally.
I'm not even exaggerating a little bit when I say I was confounded at the sight (hearing?) of how QUIET the dialog/voice-lines were. I mean really, I had to have my volume up to 60 (normally around (35-42) to even understand the receptionist clearly, whom was very confusing, as I TRIED to hear her better by getting closer to where I THOUGHT her disembodied-voice-of-god was coming from, (you know, the reception desk) but to no avail, as her enigmatic Jiminy-cricket-self was actually just a voice in my head this whole time??
I mean, to be fair, I don't have any headphones, but you'd think I wouldn't need to turn my volume up to the max to be able to hear the characters speaking half-decently.

4. The paywall.
Listen, I COMPLETELY understand wanting to put a DLC on your otherwise completely-free game. Hell, I ENCOURAGE indie devs and the like to do so, but the way it was shoe-horned into the free content really makes me want to peel off my skin like a banana and pour cool water on my exposed muscle-mass in such a profound way I cannot even ARTICULATE.
What I mean is that you typically go into a game (knowing there's a DLC) to see what it has to offer and then maybe after your first run you think "wow! this game is fun! I want more of it; hell, I'd pay off the entire dev-team's collective rent and electricity bills for some extra content!".
That is not what I thought when I finished the game. Honestly? I feel like I only got a little bit of content from the game, with the rest being locked away in Pandora's AI-advertised box (yes, I'm circling back to the AI thing) and being left completely uncertain if I should even consider looking up what half of the game it unlocks, or if I should just play The Stanley Parable or Superliminal, Undertale, even! Or any other sort of meta game that actually has a good bit of content going for it in the first place!

5. Lackluster gameplay and replayability.
Okay this is what I was MOST hyped for-- I when into this game thinking "Stanley Parable, fifty-thousand and a half endings, free will, engaging badge-hunting" and I got a jigsaw missing atleast twelve pieces and none of them are edges. I got a crossword puzzle with only ACROSS answers. I got a wordle with spaces in it.
The badge-hunting was okay. I got all the simpler ones and went back through with a steam guide because none of the badge descriptions made any sense or intrigued me that much.
Badge-hunting aside, the game also gives a sort of "subliminal promise" of something like The Stanley Parable, (I know I'm referencing it a lot here, but that's what I thought this game was atleast making some sort of conscious effort to mimic) in that I thought there'd be separate outcomes, endings, plotlines, stories, literally anything other than some lame excuse of "the illusion of free will" that this game clearly wants to be; it might be some sort of subversion of expectation trickery, sure, like maybe that's the intended effect, but this game really does NOT hit the mark for me in that regard.

Anyways, that's all I have to say about this game. Maybe it's just not for me, I don't know. I would consider myself someone who could/would play ANY game if intrigued, but this game is not one of those many that I find myself to enjoy.

TL;DR: This game was kind of a waste of time. I mean, if you have a spare hour or three that you care to pass by walking down hallways and waiting for cues before you hit a big old paywall, the base game might be a satisfying experience. Heck, it's free! But if it's any indication of what's behind the paywall, it probably isn't worth going for that DLC.

Moral Dilemma's store page makes it out to be something of a discount Stanley Parable, but it's more like a computer's halfhearted attempt to reproduce Stanley Parable without really capturing what people enjoyed about Stanley Parable. The humor in this game is hit-or-miss, with jokes usually either hedged inside spurious attempts at social commentary or reliant on totally random nonsense. The pacing is also LEADEN for the amount of content actually present, so most of your time will be spent waiting for the narrators to finish speaking so you can do something, or slowly getting to the end of a hallway only to find that there's nothing for you to do there. On that note: this game seems like it's begging for more pathways. Its biggest departure from its Stanley Parable aspirations are that it's honestly a pretty linear game. The only door for you is the door into the next room. When one door opens, another closes, funneling you down the way that the narrator wants you to go. Maybe I ought to play it again and try the other misleading dialogue options, but my faith that the game has many alternate pathways does not outweigh my suspicion that continuing to plug away will not be worth it.

The game's mix of resources and art also feels somewhat incongruent. It's not an ugly game, but it falls victim to a certain sterile flatness whenever it isn't cramming polygonal assets into a room. the level design even reminds me of Garten of Banban at times. But if the Garten of Banban guys did a Stanley Parable, I think I could be entertained by how committed they would be to making it garbage (and, hey, they probably wouldn't use ai posters to advertise their DLC, because they evidently know how to use MS Paint). This game is too shallow and unpolished to be the normal sort of fun, but also too superficially competent to be that sort of fun. It's just kind of boring as games go.

So yeah, I can't outright recommend it. But to be fair, it's a free game. You get something for nothing. Perhaps that makes anything worth trying.

Overall, it's more like a showcase/demo of a developer's skills, rather than an actual game. I was expecting a series of moral dilemmas during the interview to determine the candidate's judgmental capability, so it was a let-down that I can only find some of them after finishing the main storyline, not inside the storyline. It tries to be funny with all those signs and sticky notes and all that, but they get stale pretty sooner since they get repeated too much and are quite out of place. Having to buy a DLC to actually enjoy the full game seems like a false advertisement.
But hey, at least it's free-to-play. If you like to spend some time for some goofy jokes, be my guest. Not my cup of tea though.

Ok ok, I have a good bit to say, and I'm not very good at writing reviews so bear with me here.
And yes, I'm aware this game is FREE so I can't shit on it too much since it didn't cost me anything to waste my time.
My experience playing this game was not great to say the very least.

I'm aware that the description of this game notes that ONLY the capsule/logo art is AI-generated, but the way this lackluster game plays makes me feel like this entire game was AI-generated.

---
The Allusion of Choices

Whenever I saw this game, I expected a miniature version of The Stanley Parable, being able to make different choices, see different endings and whatnot.
This game technically gives you choices, but none of them actually matter, since no matter what you do, it'll always put you right back on the main road that leads to the SINGULAR ending of this game. (unless you bought the dlc smh)

For example, at one point in the game, the narrator prompted me to enter this time machine where I immediately thought of the Boss' Office from TSP where if you time it right you can quickly enter and exit the office where the doors instead close in front of you, locking you out of the office, and turning around reveals another door that leads to a new ending. I ended up trying the same thing on the time machine's door and whatdoyaknow it worked! The narrator gives me a bit of dialogue, gives me an achievement, sends me what seems to be a path to a different ending, and surprise surprise that's all there was to it, because right after that, they just send me where the time machine was initially going to send me.

Ironic since two of the tags for this game are "Choices Matter" & "Multiple Endings"

---

Misleading Advertised Gameplay

For starters, the trailer.
(I don't own the DLC but-)
Literally over half of the trailer is gameplay from the DLC.
Some stuff mentioned in the "About This Game" section is only the DLC as well.
bruh.

Moving on, this game is called "Moral Dilemma: The Interview."
The TWO moral dilemmas it gives you in this game don't affect anything except what consequence you see.

The interview aspect of this game doesn't even make any sense. Like at first, sure it's linear... (mostly). But as the game goes on it just feels like the dev (team?) just put the entire script into ChatGPT with it's seemingly random turns and directions the story takes.

---

What Parts of the Game did the Dev(s?) Actually Make???

Upon a lil bit of research, literally (probably all) most of this game's models/assets are from the Synty Store. Most of which are used from the "POLYGON - Office Pack".

This game also uses an assortment of just random assets scattered throughout the office(?) like a toddler just started clicking on whatever model they saw and dragged it in into the game with no rhyme or reason. The way how there is just random models everywhere leads me back to believing this game actually was AI-generated.

---

Anyway that's enough yapping from me, since I know this is far from a professional review.

I might buy the DLC for two reasons.
1. As a donation to the dev(s?) to actually put more effort into their games.
2. Because I'm an OCD freak and I hate having a game that's not 100%'ed. (Especially a free one)

Meh. It's too short, too shallow, too slow. Choices don't matter past the next immediate line of dialogue. There are no branching paths to take. There are a few good gags here and there but most of the content is "LOL SO R4ND0M!!11!" kind.

Half the achievements / secrets are unobtainable without looking at guides. The game basically gives you impossible tasks and you either know the answer in advance or you'll fail it.

The DLC part is essentially the same basegame content repeated again, but in worse ways.

The game is nice. But I can't accept the use of AI in any way shape or form within the context of creative production.
Even tho AI was only use for small things that are outside the game, I cannot support its normalization in our medium.

It's a fun little game to play if you have an hour or so to walkthrough the game, but I'm not crazy how much of the content seems to be locked behind a paywall.. Yes, it was a free experience, but it would have felt better to pay a couple dollars and not have something feel like it's locked behind a paywall. 

Your actions and decisions really don't have any consequences at all, which was a bit disheartening to experience. There's no novel concepts to chew on or cute little moments that make you go, "Hah!" like you experience in the Stanley Parable, Superliminal, or the Beginner's Guide. 

It's not a bad experience, by any means. But it is definitely one that can be skipped.

I'm honestly not sure I played the same game as the other reviewers. For instance, one review mentions "Branching dialogue", but that's just... not in the game? At least not in any meaningful capacity. Yes, there's probably content I missed given my short playtime, but some of what these reviewers are describing sounds like a fever dream. There's very few "moral dilemmas" in a game whose title is "Moral Dilemma". Similarly, there's very little "interview" in a game whose subtitle is "The Interview". I find the reviews that fixate on these things very strange. I'm not saying those reviews are astroturfed but I am saying it wouldn't surprise me. Perhaps it's an inside joke from the game I'm missing, ala SuperHot?

Unlike the Stanley Parable, this game does not naturally lead the player to uncovering new routes. Similarly, you are rarely given options to "shortcut" the narrator's monologue. Consider the break room scene in the Stanley Parable. In Stanley's case, you can just walk out and continue the story without pause. By contrast, most scenes in this game are entirely uninteractable. You just sit there and listen. Yes, I know Stanley Parable has that, but it's really painful in this game.

Credit to the devs: The humor in this game works well! I loved the post-it notes and signage for example. It really gave it a unique feel and I wish they leaned into that feeling throughout the rest of the game and marketing. An AI-generated anime girl for banner art (and out of place in-game model) is not the best fit for this game. It feels slapdash and tacked on. Yes, that's probably the intent, but unless there's a higher purpose intentionally bad is still bad.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Task Force Admiral is a game I want to succeed. I played the first demo extensively, loved it, and backed the Kickstarter at the basic tier that gave you a copy. I think eventually this game will join the ranks of Sea Power and Regiments as the new wave of great Microprose strategy titles. However, like Sea Power when it first launched, the game has a long way to go and not enough meat on the bone. And there's an extra poison pill that keeps me from really recommending this game while it is in Early Access.

The Good: The gameplay loop is, in my opinion, absolutely perfect and the basics should not be changed in any way. The transit, scout, plan, and launch strike flow of each mission is deceptively simple yet engaging. This is helped by the fantastic visuals. It never gets old to watch the dive bombers go on an attack run. Fighter controls work well, with options to manually control your CAP or let the AI handle it. Scramble is very useful. Ship to ship combat isn't as fleshed out and the formation controls could use some more work, but for a "carrier" game they are fine for now.

The Bad: As this moment, the game has two battles, Midway and Coral Sea. There are two versions of each, a historical version that matches the real life engagement and a hard version that throws some curveballs. There is also a battle generator, but its really only a randomized version of Midway and/or Coral Sea depending on your set up. Each battle takes a few hours. I'd say there's 20 hours content tops, though that is mainly based off my experience with demo and I haven't gone through the Midway scenario yet. There is also no tutorial and the manual is the same as from when the demo first launched in spring 2025. This altogether makes it very hard to justify the $40 price tag. I get it's early access, but there is hardly enough meat on the bone for the game to cost that much money at this time.

"So Bob," you may be asking, "if you don't think there's enough content to justify the price, why did you buy the game?"

Excellent question, which takes me to the really unfortunate part of this review.

The Ugly: Back during the Kickstarter, the devs stated the following:
"in a nutshell here are the expected features on release, in a year or so:

Fully playable U.S. Navy side for air and surface operations
30 + historical & hypothetical scenarios  (December 1941 to February 1943)
A random scenario generator
A fully-fledged scenario builder
Working savegames & replay module
South Pacific (Solomons, Western New Guinea) & Midway fully modeled
The extra gameplay options cleared by the Kickstarter campaign & a clear roadmap of future developments" (Pulled from the kickstarter page which can be found here: https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/microprose/task-force-admiral-limited-first-team-physical-edition)

The also stated the following: 
"After 6 years we're ready to have you try out our baby, even though there's still a lot to go. It's not about the money, actually. There are many ways to raise funds in this day and age, and a Kickstarter campaign is just one way among many others. We could very well launch Task Force Admiral in early access, or make people pay for a beta. But this is not what this is about."

Both of these statements combined give the impression that the game would be a full release in a year's time. There was no mention that the game would release in Early Access anywhere in the Kickstarter. After enjoying the demo and seeing that it was in such a good state, I decided to back the game and get myself a copy. I explicitly selected a tier that would not give me beta access, as I wanted to wait for the finished product.

Fast forward to the week after the campaign had ended and the devs had already collected everyone's money. It was only then that the devs began discussing they would launch into Early Access. Since I had already been charged, I could only watch as the devs added a single mission to the game over the entire year's wait for the EA release. Sure, they added a bunch of background systems like land bases in the meantime, but the rate of progress meant there is no way they wouldn't have known they would be doing an EA release prior to the Kickstarter. For some reason, this was not disclosed during the campaign.

I'm going to choose to be charitable and assume this was an unintentional mistake by the devs. However, the failure to disclose this would be an EA release is a huge issue nonetheless. If I had known this game would be launching into Early Access at all, I would not have backed the Kickstarter. As such, I can not recommend this game until full release.

I wish the devs all the luck, but I wish they will be more forthcoming with game information in the future.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
It's fine. I'm sure it will be great in a few years but definitely needs more time in the oven. There are like 4 scenarios available + a pretty barebones battle generator. Were it not for games like KSP2 that had big ambitions and flopped, I'd say trust the process, but I don't think that's guaranteed these days. 

This game really needs some dynamic campaign for replayability but that's not going to be released for quite a while. At this point if you've played the demo you've seen everything this game has to offer. If it were $20 I wouldn't be as harsh, but I can't recommend that someone spends ~$40 on this right now. Really sucks, I've been looking forward to this game for a while.

review_text: I am always interested  in a good Naval simulations game and have been waiting for this one for  several months. I downloaded the Demo and then I purchased the game when it was released.  The game is very very bare bones and really no different than the demo! Only two missions and no tutorial  meaning either you spend a lot of time just playing around trying to figure it out on your own, or going back and forth to U Tube to watch a tutorial which is a real pain. I received a refund and will just let this game cook for awhile and try again and hope that the upgrades make it a much better game.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I would give this game a middle score if I could, but since thats not available, I'm going to go with not recommended for now. While the gameplay is excellent, it is missing extremely simple quality of life features like canceling air sorties, even before they have left your carrier. There are some things I've seen that where I cant tell if its a bug or an unexplained mechanic, such as my planes circling my undamaged carriers until they have to ditch because they refuse to land. Like I said, the gameplay is good, but there is not even close to enough of it for the current price. Game should be $20 in its current state.

Side note: The aircraft control system seems to be basically unusable. Feels like you're skating your aircraft across greased ice.

review_text: Needs a lot of work, I consider this very early access no tutorial, plane flight seems clumsy and starting me off in the middle of the night during Coral Sea without being able to launch planes for another 7 hours is stupid.  Even with speeding up time it's a long wait.  Let this cook for another year or two maybe they can actually make a game.  I like this kind of game but as it is now no campaign and I barely consider this is a working game.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
As a long time Naval Warfare fan, the Graphics, the game feel, the action, its all perfect.

Honestly, I would give the game a Thumbs up, but i know when people look at reviews, you come to look at the negatives, not the positives.

The game has got alot of great positives and super strong bones for a very strong future but i will focus on the negatives here for now.

Negatives:
- No tutorial: I honestly dont understand many of the systems, and their is no clear cut explanation of many of them. Again, i had to stumble around the menus and do 1 mission about 6 times before i was able to finally sink a carrier while still having both of mine.

- No Explanations: Many missions are greyed out, i would assume its because they arent ready yet for players, but their is no explanation, not even a simple "Coming Soon" Deployment on them. Just greyed out. So i am still not sure yet if i need to play the game more to unlock them, or if they are not available.

- Gameplay mechanics are still confusing: Just a quick example, I had about 30 Dive bombers, and 12 Torpedo bombers ready on a carrier, I set up the strike, hit greenlight, and it tells me i dont have any strike aircraft selected for this mission...??? 

Additionally, on many occassions, my carriers just absolutely refuse to launch aircraft from the Decks, I set up a strike, hit greenlight, and watch them shuffle around forever around the deck, my carrier goes "TF-17 going into the wind for air mission" The planes shuffle around and do not take off then the chat log says "TF-17 air mission complete turning back to path". On many multiple occasions, this has crippled my response, from launching fighters to defend my carriers from an incoming attack, to enemy carriers slipping away because mine just wont launch their aircraft.

- AI do not suffer from Fog of War: On many mission starts, very quickly, the enemy aircraft launch their scout planes and almost always find me immediatly. At this point, i scramble some fighters, take out the fighters, and completely change my TF heading. But no matter what i do, the enemy aircraft always find me. Juggling this, with my carriers failing to launch fighters or anything depending on their mood, the start of every mission is honestly just a straight fight for survival.

At the end of the day, its a great game currently, i didnt list any positives, but you arent looking at the negative reviews for positives are you? I would still say purchase it, support the Devs, we dont have enough developers in the strategy sphere who give the pacific war attention, and it truly is great, as well as the future eventually having a dynamic campaign, I think things will absolutely get better.

Otherwise, if you arent willing to drop 40 dollars right now, I would give it a few months to cook. Just some QOL improvements, focused around Carriers following orders, and the enemy AI not ignoring Fog of War as well as more content.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
As much as I want to believe in Task Force Admiral – Vol.1: American Carrier Battles, I cannot in good conscience recommend buying it on Steam in its current Early Access state. This is an extremely complex naval battle simulator that throws the player straight into the deep end with no in-game tutorial whatsoever, demanding mastery of dense systems before you can even begin to enjoy it. While there is a PDF manual, it is explicitly written for an older demo build and does not reliably reflect the current version of the game, leaving new players to piece things together through trial, error, and frustration rather than learning through play.

Content is also severely limited: the unit roster is confined to a small slice of early-war assets, and only four of the roughly thirty advertised scenarios are playable, one of which is simply a duplicate with higher difficulty. I say this as a longtime MicroProse fan who genuinely appreciates the evident care for historical accuracy and the ambition on display. The foundation is promising, even exciting, but in my view this project simply was not ready yet, not even for Early Access. Right now, purchasing it feels less like buying a playable game and more like paying for a hopeful prototype.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I am a sucker for aircraft carrier games.  There is nothing I can do with my clothes on that matches the suspense of searches, the fragmentary reports, the impossible decisions about when to launch, and waiting to see how it all plays out.  (Usually it results in me getting sunk but I don't mind.)  Playing the US is especially interesting because the Japanese nearly always fragment their task forces and you can seldom be sure if you are sending your strike in the right place.  I had this game in my Watchlist for now what now seems forever.  
It doesn't carry the hype.
As an operational level game, it's no better than Carrier Battles: Guadalcanal and that game has a much better player interface.  I don't know that it's as good as Carriers at War, a game that is nearly 20 years old.  As a matter of fact, it's not as good as Great Naval Battles 2,, which is even older.  
The time compression feature sucks, and it doesn't automatically bring you to normal time when something happens, which the other three games do.   Launching strikes is needlessly complicated compared to the others and adds nothing to play enjoyment.  And the older games gave you convenient range circles for your planes.  The developers seem to think that forcing you to screw around drawing rulers on the map is a substitute for a better game experience.
The only thing the game offers is the graphics, which are really just icing on the cake. The core of a carrier game is the decisions about searching, launching, and CAP.  The graphics are just brightly colored lights and distracting noises.  I realize that lots of gamers these days demand that stuff.  Good for you but I personally don't need it.
I have to say, the game has a clear POV. No jumping around from Pearl Harbor to a fighter cockpit.
The rules are really terrible.  I have learned to expect little, but I was still disappointed.  The 45 pages of rules are mostly the kind of historical background that should be segregated into the Designers Notes, or pictures that are not really informative.  There are about six pages of "how to do stuff" in it all.  That being said, the game is intuitive if you have played other carrier games, and if you watch the YouTube tutorial you can be playing pretty quickly.
There is a lot of ink being spilled around here about whether the game is incomplete or not and whether the $35 is worth it.  I think that most of those will be cured by fixes and expanded scenarios. The basic problem is that no matter what they do, it still isn't any kind of advance over games that have been around for a long time.  
I will admit that maybe I am being too hard; if you have a game that puts you in the position of the task force commander with tough decisions to make, there may not be a lot you can do to add to the inherent interest of the situation.  But at the end of the day, I think that this game doesn't do it as well as some that were written for much earlier platforms.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
tl;dr: give it time to cook

did the coral sea scenario (at the moment there are only 4 scenarios, 2 of which are hypothetical variations rather than wholly new) and learning the game is fairly intuitive without a tutorial, if you're familiar with naval games this is very reminiscent of them (destroyer: u boat hunter, dangerous waters, gary grigsby's war in the pacific, cmano)

unfortunately, game released in a state of early access which is inherently troubled but this early access is far from impressive - it's a neat tech demo that demonstrates the game has the capability to grow, but i would not buy it in its current state. models look like they're from fleet command (1991) and the gameplay has much to be desired in its current state. its just really, really lacking and looks nothing like the launch trailer at the moment. 

despite this, i love the way the game immerses you into the role of a task force commander through good briefings, voice acting, and intimate command and control of aircraft direction and fleet dynamics. it has the capability to be really really good - but right now? not worth it unless you want to support the devs moving forwards

game is made by really cool devs who are motivated to make a good game, and i have faith they'll iron it and make it what they're looking for

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: I want to like this game as it scratches that carrier itch. There are not a lot of carrier management games but this games needs to sit in the oven a little longer, especially for its price. 40$ for no tutorial and 4 missions? A tutorial needs to be the top priority. Sure, the game looks good and plays well, but there is too little here in its current state for the asking price. Once the game develops a bit more, I'll try it again

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: At the current state I would not recommend the game - the content is limited (4 missions + quick battle) and the game mechanics feel barebones. For me the core gameplay feels boring after a short while - you send out recon to try to scout the position of enemy fleet, to then send out your aircraft to strike them, then you have fighters to either escort them or intercept enemy aircraft. But most of this is stuff is highly automated - you will be sitting there doing nothing, just watching  (which can be annoying with the speed up time not working). UI is not the greatest and so is camera switching. I would expect some level of micromanagement, more direct authority over the fleet and aircraft and overall a bit more engaging gameplay, plus a progression mode where you ships carry over from battle to battle - but that is my subjective opinion. Objectively there is little content and its more of a very early acess and looking at how slow the developement is going might not make this game a good pickup this year.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Had been keeping an eye on this game ever since it was announced, I picked it up the second it was dropped but I didn't realise that there were only 2 scenarios to play with. 

The gameplay loop is incredible and will offer something unique when it comes to rts where there's no micromanaging and you have to live with the consequences of your decisions. The slow build up followed by intense burst of chaotic action is a lot of fun. 

Lack of tutorial is an issue for sure but I had no problems getting around it after a couple of attempts and messing around... The UI is innovative and the game looks beautiful, I reckon it might need some optimisation. In its current state its barebones but the framework is solid and this game will be incredible when its completed. 

That said.. Its WAY too expensive at its current price point and to add onto that they mention a price increase when its completed? I cannot recommend this "demo" (thats basically what this is in its current state) for this price. This price is way too much for an early access game. 

My suggestion would be to wait a few years until the completed game goes on sale and then pick it up.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
It has the bones of a great game, visuals are beautiful and action is tense.  Needs some command and control improvements.

The bait and switch with Early access was not appreciated, will switch to a positive review once game is feature complete and released.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: So I played this game when it was out for demo and absolutely loved it however I was waiting all day for the release and once it came out I purchased it however its basically the demo, the visuals of the game are amazing and it runs pretty well as far as I can tell but its pretty bare bones especially for the price, I dont see much in the way of differences I can do within the scenarios either they all play out fairly similar. Once a handful of other scenarios come out my stance will change but I have full faith in the project and hope it doesn't turn out like ksp2 but as of right now there doesn't seem to be much point if you have previously played through the demo.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Waited years for this game, and it finally came out.... with only 4 scenarios, are you serious? 4 scenarios, that's it.

Edit after almost two hours: 
It's really only 2 scenarios, each one with al "alternative/variable" scenario, the UI is atrocious and counterintuitive (why can't I just click in a unit to see it instead of having to open the fleet menu and press enter in the specific unit?), units have lots of issues responding to command, scramble, as an example, is broken, it won't work, time compression takes forever, and there's no tutorial, manual or anything to guide you through the game.

This game is nowhere near to be in a good state to be released, even in ea, and certainly not at a $40 price tag.

On the positive note, the gameplay is actually good and the air battles are an spectacle to watch, but again, only 2 battles and nothing else, not much replayability.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I really want to love this game, but it is almost unplayable without playing at 1-10x speed, which means if you want to play... its gonna take forever. And that's even if the game doesn't up and crash on you. without a crash reporter might i add. I have this weird split second delay on all my commands and clicks as well as my courser getting stuck to the left side of my screen, could be that i'm running on a 2560 x 1440 display resolution, which the game appears to not agree with. I played the demo for hours (probably clocked 75+) so thats not to say it isnt a good game. however in its current state, it is severely lacking. THE ABSOLUTE BIGGEST THING is that for 35.99 soon to be 39.99 you only get TWICE the content of the demo which is straight up robbery. I've been trying to play that EXTRA content and it just keeps crashing and running INCRDIBLY slow; and i have a monster of a PC so that is pretty sad. I understand that it is an "Early Access Game" and it's not finished yet but i'm asking myself if its even started yet (maybe a little exaggeration on my part, but you get the idea)

Now for the good... the graphics and details look amazing, especially the bomb blasts (going off the demo play because the full game is broken). I wish more people could get thrown at the developement because it ABSOLUTELY has potential, but for almost 40$ it's not even close yet.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I really really really want this game to succeed. I have waited for a year for it like many friends of mine, and there is none like it on the modern market -- that is why this review is hard to write.

In this state, for $40, I cannot recommend it. It is extremely limited in playability and feels more like a demo than an early access game. There are only a handful of scenarios, but little to no guidance on how to actually play the game, with no tutorial to explain the very clearly important mechanics, such as scouting, how to set up a CAP, and the importance of proper deck scheduling. These things might come naturally for those that have played games like War on the Sea or Sea Power, but this is not inherent to someone who may be new to this scene and just has a love for World War II.

The game is beautiful, atmospheric, and definitely feels like you are an Admiral of a carrier Task Force (Task Force Admiral? Roll credits!) circa 1940s Pacific theatre. If sitting in a plotting room waiting for a scout or CAP to report in is your jam, then yeah, there are definitely no other games like this one. But I wish there was more of a way to actually see what is going on other than going into "3D View" mode and scrolling around trying to make sense of what is happening based on the birds-eye view. Maybe once a follow-cam or direct control for aircraft makes its way into the live version, or once a tutorial comes out, it will be a bit more accessible.

However, in my honest opinion, this is a game that needs to cook for a bit longer. I will NOT be refunding it at this time, because I believe in the game wholeheartedly, but again I cannot recommend it for anyone else who does not feel the way I do.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
MAJOR DISAPPOINTMENT

Not worth even close to a full price of $40. You get only 2 scenario location choices (Coral Sea from demo, and Midway) + the battle editor compared to the demo adding one more carrier possible to each side (4 for IJN and 3 for USN). No tutorial. NOT worth the full price or even close. Bag of chips with 98% air in it when you go to select missions to play. No exaggeration. Missions are a separate issue from having no dynamic campaign with consequences. So you are basically getting one more location (Midway) in comparison to the demo, and a small addition of 1 carrier for each side in the battle generator for the asking price. WOW......

This is charging people an overblown price for the demo and adding ONE other area for a scenario location and adding one carrier total to each side for battle generator. There is no dynamic campaign. No, the release discount does NOT change this dissatisfaction, and blaming consumers having legitimate points for their cost of marketing using a demo is throwing water on a grease fire. The video shows all kinds of sounds that are NOT in the game as well. You don't hear any of that at all. That is dubbed in to give a preview of a final product that is NOT what you will be getting when making a purchase. No disclaimer about that either in the video, leaving customers to expect that when making a purchase. False advertisement. 

Positives: Music is decent and the intro briefings and opening title videos fit the theme well. The music changing based on what is happening in the game is a good swing like what was in the old wing commander game back in 1990. Unfortunately it was banging away the intense music just because my patrol was returning and it didn't fit the situation breaking immersion. 

Not worth more than $20 at best for hardcore pacific fans. Robbery charging $40 and knocking off 10% for people expecting much more to do after waiting for so long to try and make that ok? It isn't. As an added frustration, all of your bomber pilots are are excessively stupid. Don't believe me? Go and try that yourself. I had my dive bombers fly right over the target carrier on the way to the designated point I had launched them to, because they had changed course and moved toward TF17 while the flight time was done. 

The whole 2 carrier strike flew RIGHT OVER the enemy carrier without attacking. It is like they are trying to get people to destroy their computer monitors while playing. If you can get the ships near the target point with dive bombers is your only chance. This should not be happening. Pilots use no judgment to attack what is obviously the target right in front of them and fly right over it because it "Isn't near the target point" so to speak. BAD.

When there were 2 different ships to attack, both naval strikes all decided to go for the same carrier, and left the other one untouched so it could launch the strike PACKING its deck while the one they attacked had an empty deck. *FACEPALM* That is a lot of play time to get to that point even with time acceleration to have these kinds of AI issues. Tailguns are nerfed where they are like bb guns. It is ridiculously bad. BAD!!

I feel obligated to warn other players about all of this because it is so blatantly skimpy compared to the demo released quite some time ago, and the bomber pilot AI is excessively bad. Their marketing cost using a demo is 100% their responsibility and NOT the consumers!!

Fortunately the situation with extreme lack of content even for EA can be detected right when you open the game up, so as long as you have less than 2 hours and 14 days total since purchase, you can still get your money back. Steam has that to prevent exactly this kind of situation. But be warned: Playing a game will take longer than 2 hours, even using time acceleration and you will be stuck with it. You will see right away why they locked away the demo, because an extreme amount of players would just refund and play that. Not much of a difference to be charging such a high price for a title in this state of play.

Should have waited to release this, even for EA. It clearly wasn't ready, and the game will suffer long term from them rushing the release by disappointing the players this hard. No doubt about it. There is no second chance for a first impression. That is the situation the game is in at this point. *Double Facepalm*

The devs had an extreme attitude of blameshifting and leaning on their choice of marketing with a demo to divert attention away from the state of the release title at its price of $40 after intro 10% off. This happened in discussion on steam when players brought up these legitimate issues. Really put off a bunch of us right out of the gate when we were reasonably disappointed. There was a term used back in WW2... 

"Loose lips sink ships."

Dont get me wrong, it feels and plays nice, but something is missing, and it just comes off generic. If it's your first Tactical game, I think it's fine, but overall just not my type of game.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
AI needs some serious work. 

1. US Fighters are outperformed by IJN fighters. This is fine, it's historical- but the AI doesn't coordinate with itself to shake enemy fighters off or perform any kind of maneuvers that lean into the F4's strengths. For example, my fights overshoot or miss targets but have windows where any pilot would have pulled the trigger and hit them with a strafe as they passed by- which when you have a boat load of 50s mounted in your wing is nothing to scoff at. So what ends up happening is 9/10x your CAP and escort fights will be picked off trying to get perfect shots they can't get while Zero's can get that solution, and go down with maybe 1 kill between the lot of them.

2. I just launched a strike package and watched 2 dozen bombers just fly right pass the enemy formation. Not sure why, I had that contact as the target, they had clear LOS, were under fire from the AA and enemy CAP. 

Games got good bones, but between the lack of tutorial, overall content (only 2 missions scenarios at time of review), and AI issues can't recommend it at $40 ($35 launch discount). Can't wait to see it bake more and will definitely pick it back up in a year once it's gotten more along.
