game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: Crap

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Task Force Admiral is a game I want to succeed. I played the first demo extensively, loved it, and backed the Kickstarter at the basic tier that gave you a copy. I think eventually this game will join the ranks of Sea Power and Regiments as the new wave of great Microprose strategy titles. However, like Sea Power when it first launched, the game has a long way to go and not enough meat on the bone. And there's an extra poison pill that keeps me from really recommending this game while it is in Early Access.

The Good: The gameplay loop is, in my opinion, absolutely perfect and the basics should not be changed in any way. The transit, scout, plan, and launch strike flow of each mission is deceptively simple yet engaging. This is helped by the fantastic visuals. It never gets old to watch the dive bombers go on an attack run. Fighter controls work well, with options to manually control your CAP or let the AI handle it. Scramble is very useful. Ship to ship combat isn't as fleshed out and the formation controls could use some more work, but for a "carrier" game they are fine for now.

The Bad: As this moment, the game has two battles, Midway and Coral Sea. There are two versions of each, a historical version that matches the real life engagement and a hard version that throws some curveballs. There is also a battle generator, but its really only a randomized version of Midway and/or Coral Sea depending on your set up. Each battle takes a few hours. I'd say there's 20 hours content tops, though that is mainly based off my experience with demo and I haven't gone through the Midway scenario yet. There is also no tutorial and the manual is the same as from when the demo first launched in spring 2025. This altogether makes it very hard to justify the $40 price tag. I get it's early access, but there is hardly enough meat on the bone for the game to cost that much money at this time.

"So Bob," you may be asking, "if you don't think there's enough content to justify the price, why did you buy the game?"

Excellent question, which takes me to the really unfortunate part of this review.

The Ugly: Back during the Kickstarter, the devs stated the following:
"in a nutshell here are the expected features on release, in a year or so:

Fully playable U.S. Navy side for air and surface operations
30 + historical & hypothetical scenarios  (December 1941 to February 1943)
A random scenario generator
A fully-fledged scenario builder
Working savegames & replay module
South Pacific (Solomons, Western New Guinea) & Midway fully modeled
The extra gameplay options cleared by the Kickstarter campaign & a clear roadmap of future developments" (Pulled from the kickstarter page which can be found here: https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/microprose/task-force-admiral-limited-first-team-physical-edition)

The also stated the following: 
"After 6 years we're ready to have you try out our baby, even though there's still a lot to go. It's not about the money, actually. There are many ways to raise funds in this day and age, and a Kickstarter campaign is just one way among many others. We could very well launch Task Force Admiral in early access, or make people pay for a beta. But this is not what this is about."

Both of these statements combined give the impression that the game would be a full release in a year's time. There was no mention that the game would release in Early Access anywhere in the Kickstarter. After enjoying the demo and seeing that it was in such a good state, I decided to back the game and get myself a copy. I explicitly selected a tier that would not give me beta access, as I wanted to wait for the finished product.

Fast forward to the week after the campaign had ended and the devs had already collected everyone's money. It was only then that the devs began discussing they would launch into Early Access. Since I had already been charged, I could only watch as the devs added a single mission to the game over the entire year's wait for the EA release. Sure, they added a bunch of background systems like land bases in the meantime, but the rate of progress meant there is no way they wouldn't have known they would be doing an EA release prior to the Kickstarter. For some reason, this was not disclosed during the campaign.

I'm going to choose to be charitable and assume this was an unintentional mistake by the devs. However, the failure to disclose this would be an EA release is a huge issue nonetheless. If I had known this game would be launching into Early Access at all, I would not have backed the Kickstarter. As such, I can not recommend this game until full release.

I wish the devs all the luck, but I wish they will be more forthcoming with game information in the future.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
It's fine. I'm sure it will be great in a few years but definitely needs more time in the oven. There are like 4 scenarios available + a pretty barebones battle generator. Were it not for games like KSP2 that had big ambitions and flopped, I'd say trust the process, but I don't think that's guaranteed these days. 

This game really needs some dynamic campaign for replayability but that's not going to be released for quite a while. At this point if you've played the demo you've seen everything this game has to offer. If it were $20 I wouldn't be as harsh, but I can't recommend that someone spends ~$40 on this right now. Really sucks, I've been looking forward to this game for a while.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Dont buy it yet, if you do know that it's needing more. No tutorial, some of the key binds do not work. This thing needs more time in the oven.
That being said, this game has HUGE potential.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: I am always interested  in a good Naval simulations game and have been waiting for this one for  several months. I downloaded the Demo and then I purchased the game when it was released.  The game is very very bare bones and really no different than the demo! Only two missions and no tutorial  meaning either you spend a lot of time just playing around trying to figure it out on your own, or going back and forth to U Tube to watch a tutorial which is a real pain. I received a refund and will just let this game cook for awhile and try again and hope that the upgrades make it a much better game.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I would give this game a middle score if I could, but since thats not available, I'm going to go with not recommended for now. While the gameplay is excellent, it is missing extremely simple quality of life features like canceling air sorties, even before they have left your carrier. There are some things I've seen that where I cant tell if its a bug or an unexplained mechanic, such as my planes circling my undamaged carriers until they have to ditch because they refuse to land. Like I said, the gameplay is good, but there is not even close to enough of it for the current price. Game should be $20 in its current state.

Side note: The aircraft control system seems to be basically unusable. Feels like you're skating your aircraft across greased ice.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: Needs a lot of work, I consider this very early access no tutorial, plane flight seems clumsy and starting me off in the middle of the night during Coral Sea without being able to launch planes for another 7 hours is stupid.  Even with speeding up time it's a long wait.  Let this cook for another year or two maybe they can actually make a game.  I like this kind of game but as it is now no campaign and I barely consider this is a working game.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
As a long time Naval Warfare fan, the Graphics, the game feel, the action, its all perfect.

Honestly, I would give the game a Thumbs up, but i know when people look at reviews, you come to look at the negatives, not the positives.

The game has got alot of great positives and super strong bones for a very strong future but i will focus on the negatives here for now.

Negatives:
- No tutorial: I honestly dont understand many of the systems, and their is no clear cut explanation of many of them. Again, i had to stumble around the menus and do 1 mission about 6 times before i was able to finally sink a carrier while still having both of mine.

- No Explanations: Many missions are greyed out, i would assume its because they arent ready yet for players, but their is no explanation, not even a simple "Coming Soon" Deployment on them. Just greyed out. So i am still not sure yet if i need to play the game more to unlock them, or if they are not available.

- Gameplay mechanics are still confusing: Just a quick example, I had about 30 Dive bombers, and 12 Torpedo bombers ready on a carrier, I set up the strike, hit greenlight, and it tells me i dont have any strike aircraft selected for this mission...??? 

Additionally, on many occassions, my carriers just absolutely refuse to launch aircraft from the Decks, I set up a strike, hit greenlight, and watch them shuffle around forever around the deck, my carrier goes "TF-17 going into the wind for air mission" The planes shuffle around and do not take off then the chat log says "TF-17 air mission complete turning back to path". On many multiple occasions, this has crippled my response, from launching fighters to defend my carriers from an incoming attack, to enemy carriers slipping away because mine just wont launch their aircraft.

- AI do not suffer from Fog of War: On many mission starts, very quickly, the enemy aircraft launch their scout planes and almost always find me immediatly. At this point, i scramble some fighters, take out the fighters, and completely change my TF heading. But no matter what i do, the enemy aircraft always find me. Juggling this, with my carriers failing to launch fighters or anything depending on their mood, the start of every mission is honestly just a straight fight for survival.

At the end of the day, its a great game currently, i didnt list any positives, but you arent looking at the negative reviews for positives are you? I would still say purchase it, support the Devs, we dont have enough developers in the strategy sphere who give the pacific war attention, and it truly is great, as well as the future eventually having a dynamic campaign, I think things will absolutely get better.

Otherwise, if you arent willing to drop 40 dollars right now, I would give it a few months to cook. Just some QOL improvements, focused around Carriers following orders, and the enemy AI not ignoring Fog of War as well as more content.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: the content is pretty slim right now but the foundation built here is incredible.  If you even have a slight interest in ww2 carrier operations this is worth keeping an eye on.   However at the current price and state of the game it's a little hard to recommend.  If all things go well for the devs though this shouldn't take long to flip this review to a recommend.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: Not ready for game time yet, certainly not at the price. The game doesnt come with much more than was on the demo. Will consider purchasing again when it's out of early access.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
As much as I want to believe in Task Force Admiral – Vol.1: American Carrier Battles, I cannot in good conscience recommend buying it on Steam in its current Early Access state. This is an extremely complex naval battle simulator that throws the player straight into the deep end with no in-game tutorial whatsoever, demanding mastery of dense systems before you can even begin to enjoy it. While there is a PDF manual, it is explicitly written for an older demo build and does not reliably reflect the current version of the game, leaving new players to piece things together through trial, error, and frustration rather than learning through play.

Content is also severely limited: the unit roster is confined to a small slice of early-war assets, and only four of the roughly thirty advertised scenarios are playable, one of which is simply a duplicate with higher difficulty. I say this as a longtime MicroProse fan who genuinely appreciates the evident care for historical accuracy and the ambition on display. The foundation is promising, even exciting, but in my view this project simply was not ready yet, not even for Early Access. Right now, purchasing it feels less like buying a playable game and more like paying for a hopeful prototype.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I am a sucker for aircraft carrier games.  There is nothing I can do with my clothes on that matches the suspense of searches, the fragmentary reports, the impossible decisions about when to launch, and waiting to see how it all plays out.  (Usually it results in me getting sunk but I don't mind.)  Playing the US is especially interesting because the Japanese nearly always fragment their task forces and you can seldom be sure if you are sending your strike in the right place.  I had this game in my Watchlist for now what now seems forever.  
It doesn't carry the hype.
As an operational level game, it's no better than Carrier Battles: Guadalcanal and that game has a much better player interface.  I don't know that it's as good as Carriers at War, a game that is nearly 20 years old.  As a matter of fact, it's not as good as Great Naval Battles 2,, which is even older.  
The time compression feature sucks, and it doesn't automatically bring you to normal time when something happens, which the other three games do.   Launching strikes is needlessly complicated compared to the others and adds nothing to play enjoyment.  And the older games gave you convenient range circles for your planes.  The developers seem to think that forcing you to screw around drawing rulers on the map is a substitute for a better game experience.
The only thing the game offers is the graphics, which are really just icing on the cake. The core of a carrier game is the decisions about searching, launching, and CAP.  The graphics are just brightly colored lights and distracting noises.  I realize that lots of gamers these days demand that stuff.  Good for you but I personally don't need it.
I have to say, the game has a clear POV. No jumping around from Pearl Harbor to a fighter cockpit.
The rules are really terrible.  I have learned to expect little, but I was still disappointed.  The 45 pages of rules are mostly the kind of historical background that should be segregated into the Designers Notes, or pictures that are not really informative.  There are about six pages of "how to do stuff" in it all.  That being said, the game is intuitive if you have played other carrier games, and if you watch the YouTube tutorial you can be playing pretty quickly.
There is a lot of ink being spilled around here about whether the game is incomplete or not and whether the $35 is worth it.  I think that most of those will be cured by fixes and expanded scenarios. The basic problem is that no matter what they do, it still isn't any kind of advance over games that have been around for a long time.  
I will admit that maybe I am being too hard; if you have a game that puts you in the position of the task force commander with tough decisions to make, there may not be a lot you can do to add to the inherent interest of the situation.  But at the end of the day, I think that this game doesn't do it as well as some that were written for much earlier platforms.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Not in a good state right now.

RTX 5080 with a Ryzen 9800X3D and the game uses 90-100% of my GPU with drops to 20FPS every few minutes and then back up to 300FPS. Seems like a strange issue with the engine. 

No tutorial and lack of content.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: The game needs a tutorial more then almost anything. The interface is more difficult then it needs to be, looking thru drop down menus and switching screens when it can be as simple as point and click. needs a lot of work on the interface. very confusing when it can be a lot simpler. All the hype this game received, I expected something much better. I recommend not spending the cash at this time.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: I attempted to play it for all of about 10 minutes, but they don't even have the tutorial set up for you to learn a game. I do understand that its in early access but you'd think teaching people how to play would be the starting point. How are they supposed to get honest feedback on their game if people don't even know how they intended it to be played.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: It is a matter of individual taste of course but I was under the impression that you had more of a control over battles and events. Too little for my taste, you are an observer most of the time with little to do other than watch.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
tl;dr: give it time to cook

did the coral sea scenario (at the moment there are only 4 scenarios, 2 of which are hypothetical variations rather than wholly new) and learning the game is fairly intuitive without a tutorial, if you're familiar with naval games this is very reminiscent of them (destroyer: u boat hunter, dangerous waters, gary grigsby's war in the pacific, cmano)

unfortunately, game released in a state of early access which is inherently troubled but this early access is far from impressive - it's a neat tech demo that demonstrates the game has the capability to grow, but i would not buy it in its current state. models look like they're from fleet command (1991) and the gameplay has much to be desired in its current state. its just really, really lacking and looks nothing like the launch trailer at the moment. 

despite this, i love the way the game immerses you into the role of a task force commander through good briefings, voice acting, and intimate command and control of aircraft direction and fleet dynamics. it has the capability to be really really good - but right now? not worth it unless you want to support the devs moving forwards

game is made by really cool devs who are motivated to make a good game, and i have faith they'll iron it and make it what they're looking for

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: I want to like this game as it scratches that carrier itch. There are not a lot of carrier management games but this games needs to sit in the oven a little longer, especially for its price. 40$ for no tutorial and 4 missions? A tutorial needs to be the top priority. Sure, the game looks good and plays well, but there is too little here in its current state for the asking price. Once the game develops a bit more, I'll try it again

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: At the current state I would not recommend the game - the content is limited (4 missions + quick battle) and the game mechanics feel barebones. For me the core gameplay feels boring after a short while - you send out recon to try to scout the position of enemy fleet, to then send out your aircraft to strike them, then you have fighters to either escort them or intercept enemy aircraft. But most of this is stuff is highly automated - you will be sitting there doing nothing, just watching  (which can be annoying with the speed up time not working). UI is not the greatest and so is camera switching. I would expect some level of micromanagement, more direct authority over the fleet and aircraft and overall a bit more engaging gameplay, plus a progression mode where you ships carry over from battle to battle - but that is my subjective opinion. Objectively there is little content and its more of a very early acess and looking at how slow the developement is going might not make this game a good pickup this year.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Had been keeping an eye on this game ever since it was announced, I picked it up the second it was dropped but I didn't realise that there were only 2 scenarios to play with. 

The gameplay loop is incredible and will offer something unique when it comes to rts where there's no micromanaging and you have to live with the consequences of your decisions. The slow build up followed by intense burst of chaotic action is a lot of fun. 

Lack of tutorial is an issue for sure but I had no problems getting around it after a couple of attempts and messing around... The UI is innovative and the game looks beautiful, I reckon it might need some optimisation. In its current state its barebones but the framework is solid and this game will be incredible when its completed. 

That said.. Its WAY too expensive at its current price point and to add onto that they mention a price increase when its completed? I cannot recommend this "demo" (thats basically what this is in its current state) for this price. This price is way too much for an early access game. 

My suggestion would be to wait a few years until the completed game goes on sale and then pick it up.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
It has the bones of a great game, visuals are beautiful and action is tense.  Needs some command and control improvements.

The bait and switch with Early access was not appreciated, will switch to a positive review once game is feature complete and released.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text: So I played this game when it was out for demo and absolutely loved it however I was waiting all day for the release and once it came out I purchased it however its basically the demo, the visuals of the game are amazing and it runs pretty well as far as I can tell but its pretty bare bones especially for the price, I dont see much in the way of differences I can do within the scenarios either they all play out fairly similar. Once a handful of other scenarios come out my stance will change but I have full faith in the project and hope it doesn't turn out like ksp2 but as of right now there doesn't seem to be much point if you have previously played through the demo.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
Waited years for this game, and it finally came out.... with only 4 scenarios, are you serious? 4 scenarios, that's it.

Edit after almost two hours: 
It's really only 2 scenarios, each one with al "alternative/variable" scenario, the UI is atrocious and counterintuitive (why can't I just click in a unit to see it instead of having to open the fleet menu and press enter in the specific unit?), units have lots of issues responding to command, scramble, as an example, is broken, it won't work, time compression takes forever, and there's no tutorial, manual or anything to guide you through the game.

This game is nowhere near to be in a good state to be released, even in ea, and certainly not at a $40 price tag.

On the positive note, the gameplay is actually good and the air battles are an spectacle to watch, but again, only 2 battles and nothing else, not much replayability.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I really want to love this game, but it is almost unplayable without playing at 1-10x speed, which means if you want to play... its gonna take forever. And that's even if the game doesn't up and crash on you. without a crash reporter might i add. I have this weird split second delay on all my commands and clicks as well as my courser getting stuck to the left side of my screen, could be that i'm running on a 2560 x 1440 display resolution, which the game appears to not agree with. I played the demo for hours (probably clocked 75+) so thats not to say it isnt a good game. however in its current state, it is severely lacking. THE ABSOLUTE BIGGEST THING is that for 35.99 soon to be 39.99 you only get TWICE the content of the demo which is straight up robbery. I've been trying to play that EXTRA content and it just keeps crashing and running INCRDIBLY slow; and i have a monster of a PC so that is pretty sad. I understand that it is an "Early Access Game" and it's not finished yet but i'm asking myself if its even started yet (maybe a little exaggeration on my part, but you get the idea)

Now for the good... the graphics and details look amazing, especially the bomb blasts (going off the demo play because the full game is broken). I wish more people could get thrown at the developement because it ABSOLUTELY has potential, but for almost 40$ it's not even close yet.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
I really really really want this game to succeed. I have waited for a year for it like many friends of mine, and there is none like it on the modern market -- that is why this review is hard to write.

In this state, for $40, I cannot recommend it. It is extremely limited in playability and feels more like a demo than an early access game. There are only a handful of scenarios, but little to no guidance on how to actually play the game, with no tutorial to explain the very clearly important mechanics, such as scouting, how to set up a CAP, and the importance of proper deck scheduling. These things might come naturally for those that have played games like War on the Sea or Sea Power, but this is not inherent to someone who may be new to this scene and just has a love for World War II.

The game is beautiful, atmospheric, and definitely feels like you are an Admiral of a carrier Task Force (Task Force Admiral? Roll credits!) circa 1940s Pacific theatre. If sitting in a plotting room waiting for a scout or CAP to report in is your jam, then yeah, there are definitely no other games like this one. But I wish there was more of a way to actually see what is going on other than going into "3D View" mode and scrolling around trying to make sense of what is happening based on the birds-eye view. Maybe once a follow-cam or direct control for aircraft makes its way into the live version, or once a tutorial comes out, it will be a bit more accessible.

However, in my honest opinion, this is a game that needs to cook for a bit longer. I will NOT be refunding it at this time, because I believe in the game wholeheartedly, but again I cannot recommend it for anyone else who does not feel the way I do.

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
MAJOR DISAPPOINTMENT

Not worth even close to a full price of $40. You get only 2 scenario location choices (Coral Sea from demo, and Midway) + the battle editor compared to the demo adding one more carrier possible to each side (4 for IJN and 3 for USN). No tutorial. NOT worth the full price or even close. Bag of chips with 98% air in it when you go to select missions to play. No exaggeration. Missions are a separate issue from having no dynamic campaign with consequences. So you are basically getting one more location (Midway) in comparison to the demo, and a small addition of 1 carrier for each side in the battle generator for the asking price. WOW......

This is charging people an overblown price for the demo and adding ONE other area for a scenario location and adding one carrier total to each side for battle generator. There is no dynamic campaign. No, the release discount does NOT change this dissatisfaction, and blaming consumers having legitimate points for their cost of marketing using a demo is throwing water on a grease fire. The video shows all kinds of sounds that are NOT in the game as well. You don't hear any of that at all. That is dubbed in to give a preview of a final product that is NOT what you will be getting when making a purchase. No disclaimer about that either in the video, leaving customers to expect that when making a purchase. False advertisement. 

Positives: Music is decent and the intro briefings and opening title videos fit the theme well. The music changing based on what is happening in the game is a good swing like what was in the old wing commander game back in 1990. Unfortunately it was banging away the intense music just because my patrol was returning and it didn't fit the situation breaking immersion. 

Not worth more than $20 at best for hardcore pacific fans. Robbery charging $40 and knocking off 10% for people expecting much more to do after waiting for so long to try and make that ok? It isn't. As an added frustration, all of your bomber pilots are are excessively stupid. Don't believe me? Go and try that yourself. I had my dive bombers fly right over the target carrier on the way to the designated point I had launched them to, because they had changed course and moved toward TF17 while the flight time was done. 

The whole 2 carrier strike flew RIGHT OVER the enemy carrier without attacking. It is like they are trying to get people to destroy their computer monitors while playing. If you can get the ships near the target point with dive bombers is your only chance. This should not be happening. Pilots use no judgment to attack what is obviously the target right in front of them and fly right over it because it "Isn't near the target point" so to speak. BAD.

When there were 2 different ships to attack, both naval strikes all decided to go for the same carrier, and left the other one untouched so it could launch the strike PACKING its deck while the one they attacked had an empty deck. *FACEPALM* That is a lot of play time to get to that point even with time acceleration to have these kinds of AI issues. Tailguns are nerfed where they are like bb guns. It is ridiculously bad. BAD!!

I feel obligated to warn other players about all of this because it is so blatantly skimpy compared to the demo released quite some time ago, and the bomber pilot AI is excessively bad. Their marketing cost using a demo is 100% their responsibility and NOT the consumers!!

Fortunately the situation with extreme lack of content even for EA can be detected right when you open the game up, so as long as you have less than 2 hours and 14 days total since purchase, you can still get your money back. Steam has that to prevent exactly this kind of situation. But be warned: Playing a game will take longer than 2 hours, even using time acceleration and you will be stuck with it. You will see right away why they locked away the demo, because an extreme amount of players would just refund and play that. Not much of a difference to be charging such a high price for a title in this state of play.

Should have waited to release this, even for EA. It clearly wasn't ready, and the game will suffer long term from them rushing the release by disappointing the players this hard. No doubt about it. There is no second chance for a first impression. That is the situation the game is in at this point. *Double Facepalm*

The devs had an extreme attitude of blameshifting and leaning on their choice of marketing with a demo to divert attention away from the state of the release title at its price of $40 after intro 10% off. This happened in discussion on steam when players brought up these legitimate issues. Really put off a bunch of us right out of the gate when we were reasonably disappointed. There was a term used back in WW2... 

"Loose lips sink ships."

game_name: Task Force Admiral - Vol.1: American Carrier Battles
review_text:
AI needs some serious work. 

1. US Fighters are outperformed by IJN fighters. This is fine, it's historical- but the AI doesn't coordinate with itself to shake enemy fighters off or perform any kind of maneuvers that lean into the F4's strengths. For example, my fights overshoot or miss targets but have windows where any pilot would have pulled the trigger and hit them with a strafe as they passed by- which when you have a boat load of 50s mounted in your wing is nothing to scoff at. So what ends up happening is 9/10x your CAP and escort fights will be picked off trying to get perfect shots they can't get while Zero's can get that solution, and go down with maybe 1 kill between the lot of them.

2. I just launched a strike package and watched 2 dozen bombers just fly right pass the enemy formation. Not sure why, I had that contact as the target, they had clear LOS, were under fire from the AA and enemy CAP. 

Games got good bones, but between the lack of tutorial, overall content (only 2 missions scenarios at time of review), and AI issues can't recommend it at $40 ($35 launch discount). Can't wait to see it bake more and will definitely pick it back up in a year once it's gotten more along.

The game would benefit significantly from the ability to hire additional workers or staff. As it currently stands, managing all tasks as a solo player quickly becomes overwhelming. Between admitting patients, performing diagnostics, treating them, cleaning beds, disposing of zombies (which appears to require immediate attention), and checking patients out, the workload becomes unmanageable.

This imbalance detracts from the overall enjoyment of the game. After a certain point, it stops being fun and starts to feel like a stressful juggling act. Because of this, I would find it difficult to recommend the game in its current state, especially for solo players. The idea of upgrading the hospital further and attracting even more patients only adds to the strain, making it feel nearly impossible to keep up.

While I understand the game supports multiplayer, there needs to be better balance for those playing alone. I suggest revisiting the game modes and adjusting mechanics such as event timing, patient load, or task frequency based on the number of active players. These changes could greatly improve the experience and accessibility for both solo and group players.

CURE lacks the intensity that makes cooperative gaming fun.  Even after the "Difficulty Level Hotfix" on January 2nd, patients don't come into the hospital fast enough to keep doctors (players) busy.  This leads to players competing to apply care.

Zombie combat is basic.  It's also a little wonky - sometimes zombies can melee attack you when you are well outside melee range.

Patient arrival rates should scale better with the number of players in-game, and the difficulty for healing patients should scale with the number of nights (not hospital size).

Although I expect the developers will continue to fine tune these issues, I recommend that you pass on this game for now.

Let me preface this with the fact that I really do enjoy this game. CURE has very strong bones and a great foundation to expand upon. The gameplay loop is somewhat simple once you wrap your head around it, but it feels all the more rewarding once you have. Most of my experience with this game has been with 1 other person in co-op, so my experience is tailored toward that. We're on day like 80-something. However, the reasoning behind this negative review is because light needs to be shed on some of the minor and critical issues that this game possesses.

Firstly, let me say that none of my complaints are about the zombies, like so many gripe about. They are an intended feature and easy enough to dispose of. They present a challenge that can be overcome. Be smart and quarantine potentially problematic patients from the ones who don't possess any of the cautionary symptoms. (Though it can be frustrating when patients turn RIGHT AFTER you've finished administering your treatments to them)

The hospital upgrade system is well implemented... for the most part. But, as you advance in levels and unlock more diagnostic tools, better medicines, etc., you will come across a dilemma in this juncture. There are patients with symptoms that require diagnostic tools such as 'Urine Test', 'Blood Test', and 'CT Scan'. Why is this an issue? Those diagnostic tools do not exist in the game right now. What is more brow-raising about this particular problem is the way Devs responded to the queries relating to it, as many players were naturally confused about how they were supposed to diagnose patients when they didn't possess the means.

The Devs' response? To paraphrase: "Figure it out."

Sir, this is a hospital at the end of the day. Telling your players (who are supposed to be Doctors) to more or less treat their establishment like some madman's experimental laboratory is absolutely ludicrous. Yeah, sure! Just pump them full of random medications until something sticks! Or, you're free to turn them away and refuse them care! What? Are you kidding me? That's money. Money we NEED. Guessing and checking with the myriad of patients who come with these diagnostic requirements is not only ridiculous, it's not even economically-friendly if you don't get lucky with your first few medical guesses. Some patients who have nausea will reject anti-nausea medications? Huh? At first, this problem is trivial. Moderate at worst. But eventually, these types of diagnostic-requiring patients become the MAJORITY of who you're admitting into your hospital.

And your answer is "Figure it out."? You took the trouble to include the diagnostic requirements in your game but skipped out on implementing the necessary tools to meet said requirements? That's not fun. That's not quirky. It's not haha-funny-mystery-oOoOOO! It's LAZY, and you know it. It would have been easier (and sensical) to NOT throw those into the game UNTIL you had the necessary diagnostic tools included as well. If you HAD, then maybe that 'medical tools pegboard' that you already have in the game would have some legitimate use. And don't throw that "It's a zombie apocalypse scenario! You might not have the equipment you need!" garbage excuse at me when I'm dropping 10 Gs on a god damn X-Ray machine. If we have the means to do that, then I'm sure I can manage to test someone's blood or urine. Alas, I digress.

Another issue with this game, which I imagine becomes more glaring with the less people you have in co-op (or god forbid you're playing solo) is how functionally useless and inefficient all of that extra hospital space by leveling up your hospital becomes. There's just nothing to do with all of this open space. You can only have 1 reception desk (which is a VERY strange move in my opinion, and the problems this creates are exacerbated with every additional person you have in your lobby) so there's no real point to diversify your hospital and have series of beds in a multitude of different locations. Since ONLY the first person in line can be interacted with, whoever you're playing with has to wait until you're done examining the patient you're interacting with, sending them to a chair/bed/X-ray machine/whatever, it unnecessarily crimps the process. It is inefficient and badly implemented. It's a waste of potential.

If you can work on the hospital leveling systems, give us the diagnostic tools that we require, and find a way for us to actually optimize all of the empty space we eventually find ourselves surrounded with, then I will go positive.

Not Recommended

The first 20 nights were great, figuring out the hospital setup, treating patients, and slowly improving the operation. But after that? The gameplay takes a nosedive.

Once the zombies show up, it stops feeling like a hospital game and becomes an endless cycle of killing and bagging zombies. You only see 2–3 real patients a night, so there’s zero incentive to upgrade rooms or build a better hospital. The core gameplay loop shifts from management to repetitive cleanup duty.

A few specific issues:
	•	You can’t upgrade reception or check-out desks.
	•	You can’t contain or cure zombies, just eliminate them over and over.
	•	Only two mini-games for treatment, and they get stale fast.
	•	Zombies are boring, easy, and the biggest time sink.

This game could be awesome if it continued to ramp up patient volume and challenge, focusing more on hospital management instead of zombie janitorial work. But in its current state, it stops being fun right when it should get interesting

I would love to give this game a good review. Don't get me wrong it is a good game but after night 20 it turns into a zombie survival. You'll get 15 people come in and 10 or sometimes all 15 will turn into zombies. You can give them hundreds of dollars in meds just to have them turn and you make nothing. Some will turn without showing any signs. It make progression come to a crawl, almost none at all. When you get into higher levels and people require more time to diagnose its just not possible to progress.

This game is pre-early access. It has potential but as you will see there's no real reason to keep playing. There are unlocks but they dont change anything about the game. The hospital expanding over and over again just adds more space NOT to use. 

Treatment should be based on a percentage of confidence by which additional diagnostics increase confidence in route of treatment. You should be able to guess and get it wrong or right depending on how much of a rush you are in. 

What I would like to see is a rating system from the customers that takes into account their total experience times spent waiting comfort etc. With this there could be a litany of additional objects to simply improve customer satisfaction. The ability to add internal walls would be good as well. Additionally a leveling system would be cool by which you could specialize your team. 

There are minor pathfinding issues but its nothing that cant be played around with a couple bumps to the NPCs. 

Great concept this shit just isn't ready yet. Nearly all reviews share the same sentiment. This is a framework for a game not a game. Keep an eye on it.

I mean its a cool idea to have some chaotic dilemma trying to help these people and theres a break out but I also think zombies is a bit on the nose. Maybe more types of conflicts other than zombies. Ive seen videos of people playing as a guy but Im not sure if that is only in multiplayer. I dont get to pick my character gender in single player. Its whatever but It would be nice to have a choice. Also the items to buy i feel get a bit pricey because of the shipping but I can understand this adds to making the game more challenging. But if youre gonna add bills soon you might need to make sure it all balances out./

Other than all the flaws there is still some good that can come out of this game I just would wait till it gets all the kinks out. I did have fun messing around with the patients making them fly up in the air. I would like a little more creative freedom for making more chaos but its whatever yall feel like adding.

The zombie and combat part of the game seem to be a mere placeholder for something much bigger, but I wouldn't suggest this game has any real combat in it in its current state.

I think the concept has a lot of potential, but the devs have a lot of work ahead to make this game shine. I'll revisit my review when that time comes.

review_text:
It only took my six hours give or take for eating dinner to finish all the quests in the game. game_name: Laundering Simulator - Clean Cash and Laundry
review_text:
I bought this game just for the idea of having a new game to play. Do i regret that decision? Yes I do. The biggest problem I have with this game is the fact that there's not a lot of content but then again, where does it go after the content that is in the game? Like what else do you add to this game?

Once I found out that I can build a money printing machine was when I knew it was over. I upgraded the machine all the way, automated it and then waited for it to print money. Easy $1000 multiple times a day. Once that happens there's really no point of running and operating a laundry mat because with that money alone, it allows you to buy all the skills needed to buy all the upgrades then what?

There's no challenge in this game. The only HAHA moment of this game was when I could buy grannies ball gag. 

Can I recommend this game? NO. Just another waste of time and money.

game_name: Laundering Simulator - Clean Cash and Laundry
review_text: You'd think with them obviously using A.I for voices (and other things) there would be a lot more dialogue lines, but its the same crappy lines repeated until you hate them. Also for some reason the carpet cleaning jobs just stop coming in at some point.

game_name: Laundering Simulator - Clean Cash and Laundry
review_text:
I think this game is a great idea and with a few fixes could be amazing. As it stands right now it's not worth purchasing. The story is fun and interesting, but at the end you never get away from the mafia or get in deeper with them. On your computer it looks like there's courses you could take to improve your relationship with them, but they currently don't work. 

End game is terrible. I only get 3 dry cleaning orders on a good day and never any carpets to clean. And while there's a ticket machine for the self-service laundry, the line gets too long and customers get mad. They leave and give bad reviews. There should be a worker that you can hire to clean and a ironing press. Did I mention the ironing mini game is kinda glitchy? It'll say you're completed an item only for you to find out after you exit the activity that it didn't count. There's shelves and pallets that have no purpose, when there could be a ticket machine for the downstairs laundry space. 

For making counterfeit clothes, it's worthless. Because every time the police raid you, they can tell on sight the items are illegal. It's impossible to move and hide more than a couple items. So why do it at all? I do wish there were more legit items to sell or that police couldn't tell on sight that something is counterfeit. So I end up not using half the machines because it's not worth it. Supposedly you can upgrade the machines and get bonuses, but I haven't seen how to do this.

game_name: Laundering Simulator - Clean Cash and Laundry
review_text:
Where to start with this...

As it stands, this game is just sub-par. I'm not saying it has no appeal, but there are other games out there that are more enjoyable and better designed for similar prices with similar gameplay loops. The game is balanced oddly since you can likely buy all the upgrades and keep your taxes paid and mob protection payments up without ever doing anything criminal.

Then there are the criminal things you can do: IDs, Passports, and Licenses are all RNG-based, you might get an order each day for each one of those, but you also might not and they pay a bit when fully upgraded, but not much compared to even legit earnings. Knock-off shirts and flip-flops do have a better return on investment than buying the real ones, they both cost less and sell for more (if you've found the extra logos at least), but they're not worth the risk because they're never going to be your big money makers. The only thing that's worth it is counterfeiting money because that for some reason sells for face-value and is the only thing other than self-serve laundry that can be automated. There's no point in doing anything else, most of which you can only do one at a time at best and it feels kind of bad. It's not like the minigames for making any of them are fun.

I mentioned earlier that the knock-off clothes aren't worth the risk. But the risk is minimal. If you pay attention to the phone sitting on the counter, you will usually get about two minutes of warning. There's no reason to have any machine other the cash counterfeiter up, so hiding that is easy enough. And then you might worry that you need to dump all the illegal clothes. But no. You just need to set them on the sidewalk outside. It's even faster if you keep a couple of buckets around in which three can be set at a time. The cop will not notice all the knock-offs scattered just outside the giant windows.

The AI-generated artwork warning in the game description undersells how much it's used. At minimum, not only is the art for decorations seemingly exclusively AI-generated, but the character icons, the wall decals, the graphics on NPC clothes that you wash, basically all artwork, basically everything that isn't a 3D asset is a generated image. To the point that it's frankly absurd. And it mostly looks terrible.

I play a lot of sim games. I could name drop a couple that I have well over 100 hours just recorded online, and then I've got some that don't record play hours that might have hundreds more. I do like this genre. It's my comfort genre. I could not see putting 100 hours into this. The crime aspect feels completely divorced from the front business, they never really intersect. There's no money laundering. The crime aspect feels like it doesn't need to be there because it doesn't actually add any gameplay that you need to involve yourself in to make a good profit. And most of the art is unpleasant.

It's not all terrible. I think it's got, like, the foundations of something, maybe. But it really needs a redesign in how the systems interact, it needs additional mechanics that keep it interesting longer or even indefinitely, it needs better artwork for the 2D assets, it needs rebalanced, and overall it needs almost a complete overhaul. There are good things about it, but they don't outweigh how much just feels unfun. And if this were the only game of its type out there, maybe I'd recommend it. But there are better choices that have more features, are more fun, can be played for hundreds of hours, and don't cost much if any more.

Overall rating: 4/10. It's a holiday themed game with a couple of decent jumpscares, good graphics, and smooth gameplay. Outside of that it was incredibly short for the price, most of the jumpscares felt very cheap, and it is incredibly "hand holdy", even for a walking simulator game. The story flow just didnt "flow". Very easy, 30 minutes or less of gameplay. I would recommend this at like $1-$2, but I could never recommend this game for the $5 pricetag. All that aside I do see potential for the developers in future games if they make it more difficult, cohesive, and definitely longer if they are going to charge so much.

Absolutely not with this game. This is a game that technically has all the elements of a horror game, but has no clue what to do with them. 

Like the story is there technically. It's not really complex or interesting, it's just enough to explain that you're on a train and there's a crazed killer on board. 

The horror elements are egregiously bad. This game cannot help itself. It compulsively executes jumpscares around nearly every single corner. It becomes incredibly predictable and just never really creates any tension. The best and easiest way to create tension is to pace out the jumpscares. Let the player feel a false sense of security, a distraction, anything. But this game has a random ragdoll character just pop out with a loud noise. Every little thing you inspect has to have some weird little gross thing or lame jumpscare even if it makes no sense as to why it's there.  

This is also an incredibly short game. It'll run you at most about an hour. It's an hour you'll never get back. For the full price of $5 USD, you can definitely, absolutely find better horror experiences out there.

At first, I genuinely enjoyed this game and found it fun, relaxing, and entertaining. The concept is interesting and the gameplay seemed promising in the beginning. However, the longer I played, the more the enjoyment completely disappeared and turned into frustration.

One major issue is how extremely fast the laundromat gets dirty. It happens so quickly that you’re constantly forced to clean instead of actually playing and enjoying the core mechanics of the game. On top of that, customers become unhappy almost immediately. You barely have enough time to give clean laundry back to a customer before receiving a thumbs-down rating, which feels unfair and poorly balanced.

The most frustrating part of the entire experience is how absurdly fast the washing machines and dryers break down. They seem to break constantly, sometimes after only a very short period of use. This turns the gameplay into a repetitive cycle of repairing machines instead of managing a laundromat in a fun or satisfying way.

Because of these issues, the game no longer feels relaxing or enjoyable at all. Instead, it becomes stressful and irritating. In its current state, the game feels unbalanced and poorly tuned. For me, it completely ruined the experience, and for that reason, I eventually uninstalled the game from my PC.

[h2]Streamer Life Simulator 2 — Early Access Review[/h2]
Streamer Life Simulator 2 attempts to simulate the rise of an online content creator, combining life management, streaming mechanics, monetisation, and audience interaction.
While many recognisable systems from real-world streaming are present, their behaviour often breaks immersion. Progression is extremely fast, rewards feel disconnected from effort, and realism is frequently sacrificed for exaggerated feedback loops.
There is something strangely compelling here, but it rarely feels authentic.

[h2]DEVELOPERS CLAIM vs THE TRUTH[/h2]
[table]
[tr][th]🏗 DEVELOPERS CLAIM[/th][th]🎯 THE TRUTH — DOES IT MATCH THE CLAIM?[/th][/tr]
[tr]
[td]
Streamer Life Simulator 2 is described as a realistic simulation of a streamer’s life, focusing on growth, audience interaction, monetisation, and the challenges of becoming successful online.
[/td]
[td]
Only partially. The systems exist, but they behave unrealistically. Viewer growth and donations escalate far too quickly, effort has limited impact on success, and progression feels closer to an arcade fantasy than a simulation.
[/td]
[/tr]
[/table]

[h2]⭐ SIM UK REALISM SCORES[/h2]
🟩🟩🟩⬜⬜⬜⬜⬜⬜⬜ [3/10] Gameplay Systems
🟩🟩🟩🟩🟩⬜⬜⬜⬜⬜ [5/10] Realism
🟩🟩🟩🟩🟩⬜⬜⬜⬜⬜ [5/10] AI & World Logic
🟩🟩🟩🟩🟩🟩⬜⬜⬜⬜ [6/10] Performance
🟩🟩🟩🟩🟩🟩⬜⬜⬜⬜ [6/10] Overall

[h2]⭐ TOP 3 FEATURES[/h2]
⭐ Clear progression systems that encourage continued play
⭐ Equipment and environment upgrades that feel meaningful
⭐ Addictive core loop despite structural flaws

[h2]💀 WORST FEATURE[/h2]
The lack of believable realism in progression, audience behaviour, and monetisation.

[h2]🧠 RECOMMENDATIONS TO THE DEVELOPER[/h2]
🧠 Slow progression and tie growth to consistency and quality
🧠 Reduce donation frequency to restore credibility
🧠 Improve UI logic to reduce friction
🧠 Decide whether this is a realism sim or an arcade experience

[h2]🧾 FINAL THOUGHTS[/h2]
Streamer Life Simulator 2 contains the framework of a simulator but behaves more like a parody. While entertaining in bursts, it repeatedly undermines its own premise through exaggerated systems and inconsistent logic.

[h2]🟨 SIM UK VERDICT[/h2]
Entertaining, but fundamentally inauthentic.
Worth trying for curiosity or casual play, but it does not deliver a believable streaming simulation.

I really wanted to avoid giving this game a negative review, but it's hard to recommend in it's current state.

-  A lot of things just have no information whatsoever. So you don't understand what the point of many things are. This is not an issue with the game being complicated, but rather an issue of information transparency. 

- It's very laggy for a 2D game, and heavy on system resources. I get around 20% CPU usage, and 2gb ram used with ALL the performance tweaks suggested by the developer and having the game minimized. So the "idle" function of the game is no better than having a bit miner running in the background. Dev says he is trying to fix this but said it's hard cause of the game engine he's using. I didn't get what he meant until I looked at the game files, it's a Gamemaker MZ game, which is basically an incredibly bloated and heavy game engine that runs in packaged browser. His hands actually are quite tied here.

- First game is just better. More coherent and cohesive UI and graphics. Systems made more sense and were more enjoyable. The gatcha was better (characters actually had rarity, and had a point to them outside of just how they look). Most importantly the combat was way more fun to watch in Auto (first game was real time top down action). The army system was actually quite good. The skill system was nice, etc.

- This game just takes the first game, has a slightly better translation, better tutorial, makes it more polished/modern looking, adds a story, and adds a bunch of effects but at the expense of the above issues. Which is really unfortunate. Depending on what things you value here, this game can be considered a sidegrade at most.  

- The character art is way better in this one. So if the waifu collecting/dating sim mechanics were what you valued most you might like this game more.

I personally don't mind this, the bigger issue is the lack of an English community and mods. First game ahd the same issue. Dev talks a lot about the changes being due to moving to newer technologies, I think he should have just stuck to the old stuff in that case. The first game was really good, just needed some polish and extra content.

TLDR: if you go into this game expecting the first one you are going to be disappointed. 

The first Idle Caliber was a hidden gem—rough around the edges, but ultimately rewarding once you took the time to understand its mechanics. While it wasn't immediately clear what was going on when I first played, once I familiarized myself with the system, everything clicked, and the hours melted away. 
Unfortunately, this isn't the case with Idle Caliber Zero.

One of my biggest issues is the user interface. It's a more modern, soulless design with plenty of sharp edges that feels like it was made exclusively to break free of any RPG-maker assets. On top of that, it frequently fails to function properly, especially in the main menu, which undermines the user experience. (To elaborate, i think certain menu options are bound to left and right click and i found myself constantly trying to close the different widgets before being able to navigate away)

The combat system has lost all of its originality. What was previously a chaotic auto-battler with no upper limit is now the same generic, soulless, turn-based RPG combat system featured in every dime-a-dozen RPG under the sun. 

Another problem is the unnecessary tabletop RPG mechanics—such as dice rolls, saving throws, and success/failure checks—that complicate gameplay rather than enhance it. There's a way to implement this, I just don't think it has any synergy with what you have on the table already. 

The inclusion of dating sim elements also feels forced and underdeveloped. The character "chats" appear to be AI generated using a server that was cobbled together. The kissing option is extremely unsettling, and the option to "sleep with" certain characters is disappointing—essentially just a single, sfw CG image paired with some brief dialogue. Despite some passable character designs, I simply don't feel any connection to these characters.

Then there's the day-one DLC, which is self explanatory. It consists of character wallpapers for the main menu. These are literally just paywalled wallpapers ripped from Wallpaper Engine. While the art is fine, it adds no real value to the game, and paying for it feels like a cash grab—which is overall just plain disappointing.

One of the features of the first idle caliber game that was praised by reviewers was the gacha system. Although it hasn't been removed, it has been completely reworked and it is no longer satisfying to roll for new characters. For each roll you get to select between 3 characters or the ability to re-roll which doesn't feel rewarding at all.

In fact, much of the game gives off the impression that it’s AI-generated—especially the scenic backgrounds, which can feel almost uncanny, as if the AI wasn't quite sure how fast a windmill should be spinning.

All in all, Idle Caliber Zero is a step backward from its predecessor. The game's design choices, frustrating mechanics, and lackluster content leave a lot to be desired.

I requested a refund for Photo Studio Simulator. Not because I don't like the game or because it's bad. No, unfortunately it's one of the many simulator games that cause heavy motion-sickness for me after just about 10 minutes. It's a shame, because there are so many interesting simulator games out there, but all are made in the same kind of way and with the same engine, and that is causing massive motion-sickness for people who have those problems with some games. Unfortunately most developers don't really pay much attention to that problem, which gets much more often with more modern games and especially those games made in that engine and from the first person perspective.

So if you are someone who knows you have motion sickness trouble with some games, don't buy this one. Otherwise, you can risk a look at it.

This is a no, it's short (it's got rogue-lite elements). Multiplayer just increases chances of getting caught rather than actually helping. This feels like a first attempt but it's the same guys who did Thief Simulator and certain systems carried over, so it was quick to pick up. But those games were small. This one is small, you've got 3 "neighborhoods". But it's like maybe 15-20 houses, a couple of small businesses and a "heist" that is also the size of a normal map.

Other than leveling up the game world, there really isn't much replay here. The locations are the same, the loot tables are extremely random. You will find the same cheap crap in the expensive homes and behind the hardest locks in the game. It's annoying to spend the time in game to unlock the "hardest" locks and then still get a coffee maker and a cheap toaster.

The game plays really smoothly. Didn't hit any bad glitches, just if you "fill" the box for collection with stuff and send it in, it will leave the stuff from above the rim of the box floating in the air to be dropped into the next box. And it will stay suspended there until you pick it up and drop it again.

And I've looked at the "post launch" updates. They are nice, but again it's not enough space. Expanding the loot tables is fine, if it makes sense for where it is and if you actually have the places to put them. Needs a lot more houses/businesses and main "locations" and if the board says, "Heists" then the game should have more than one.

Pick it up if you wanna support a small dev, but they aren't showing any real improvement over their previous titles. So it may be worth skipping honestly.

[h1]Disclaimer[/h1]
I only tried it for about an hour before deciding to refund.

[h1]PSA[/h1]
Do not buy this thinking this is an upgrade to the flawed but enjoyable Thief Simulator 2. It seems meant to be played with multiple people and some thieving mechanics were removed. Below are the biggest changes I've noticed. Please take into consideration that this is not a review - it's more of a warning to people who may hope for the Thief Simulator 2 experience.

[h1]1. Time limits[/h1]
You are constantly timed, each time you go out to steal you start at the same time and you seem to have a time limit before you have to go back. There's no free-roaming like in Thief Simulator at all. You also have a quota to meet every 3 days, so you are kinda forced to rush and steal at all times.

[h1]2. Carry capacity[/h1]
Seems even more limited than Thief Simulator, but you can drop all loot at your trailer. This turns the game into a run-back-and-forth simulator where you go drop off loot and then back to houses for more loot. But you cannot actually sell your loot without ending the night and proceeding to next day.

[h1]3. Schedule system[/h1]
Completely removed, NPCs are moving about randomly and may have only some moments where they go out for maybe an hour of ingame time (which is not a long time at all). You cannot highlight NPCs either, or rather - the highlight only happens if you look at them and lasts very short time. This leads to situations where you just have to rush in and hope you won't be seen and run away if someone comes into the room you are looting. Gone is the strategic planning of Thief Simulator 2. It seems this mechanic was designed with multiple people in mind - one observing the NPCs while the other one steals.

[h1]4. Combat?[/h1]
I guess you are supposed to fight NPCs if you get seen now? I don't know, I didn't have enough time to engage with this mechanic before I decided to refund

Schedule I, Grand Theft Auto, or Payday 1 or 2 are probably going to be heavier hitters if what you're looking for is a "Crime Simulator". After playing Thief Simulator and disliking the tedium and cryptid braindead AI in that one, we're back with another project that tastes like an part-baked pie.

I'm so exhausted of "concepts" and "experiences" and "XYZ Simulators". Just because a game is possibly fun to play with friends doesn't mean it's a good game, especially if the game gets frustrating even [i]when you're playing with friends[/i].

You can read the other reviews for an in-depth examination into the quirky AI, repetitive NPC behavior, static locations and props (Save for stuff like codes, notes and mechanic-relevant items), static object physics making it easier to get stuck than to actually maneuver around, and having the Stamina of an underdeveloped child with severe asthma.

The common theme with these "Simulator"-type games is that they're pumped out almost a dime-a-dozen. You can clearly see [i]some[/i] effort has been put into this title to make it better than the previous iterations, but it's still just as patience-testing and grindy as how the original Thief Simulator felt to me.

As I usually say with these types of games; Give it a few years to develop, maybe it won't feel like such a chore to wrangle around with.

[h3]What I Liked[/h3]
[list]
    [*] Voice acting is decent, doesn't overstay its welcome. Even the AI barks - While certainly coming out of their mouths like robots - Isn't bad audio quality.
    [*] The character animations aren't as floaty or jank as previous iterations of Simulator games made by the same people.
    [*] The Skill and Ability system is interesting, with Skill books and Skill boxes. Although, the fact that it's a two-part system that both requires XP gates to gain "Skill Points" in order to make use of the Skill books reeks of artificial progression. Hopefully it'll be tweaked in the future.
    [*] Co-op support, giving the game a higher chance of staying relevant in coming years.
    [*] Varied locations offer a reason to revisit to maximize loot potential.
    [*] Expansive toolsets with various methods to approach "marks".
    [*] One doesn't require a Stethoscope to loot Safes, it just makes them easier. (Why isn't this mechanic used with other tools??) .
    [*] Jumping out of the Safehouse map fence has the Handler play a funny line before the Player gets sniped from a distance, respawning back in-bounds, which I thought was pretty funny and cleaver.
[/list]

[h3]How It's Bad[/h3]
[list]
    [*] Rudimentary NPC AI lends to both predictability in some aspects of their behavior and unpredictability in others. For example, NPCs will sometimes exit on to the street going "Who is there?" when you leave, since they're programmed to "Home in" to triggers (Like if you make too much noise, or are seen). The issue is that these triggers are extraordinarily quick to proc in the beginning due to the Player Character having.. Zero Skills.

Police response is almost immediate, offering VERY little time to compensate. Police [i]also[/i] don't run out of Stamina - [i]YOU[/i] do however - So getting away is a back-and-forth of letting my one-lunged elderly wannabe-thief regain their sweet oxygen while Police or angry Civilians keep hot on my tail.

Police Tazers are instant-kills. Regardless of if Co-op players can revive you with a Medkit, one-hit-kills regardless of Health level don't feel good, fair or balanced. Just because you can also buy a "Vest" to survive one or two doesn't make it better, it's artificial difficulty.

Police flip-flop between knowing [i]exactly[/i] where you are at all times (Likely due to overlap from the beginner Player Skill being zero, and funky behavior by the engine in regards to NPC sightlines and level geometry), or straight-up running past you.

Civilians who notice the Player will call the Police every time without fail, regardless of whether you're actively punching their lights out or smashing their face with a crowbar. There actually is no animation for Civilians calling the police (As far as I've observed), it just happens. There's zero reason to try and stop them from contacting the Police once you're seen.

Civilians follow set paths and activities depending on their AI, however the way they go about this is extraordinarily jarring. NPCs will sometimes be crouching in the middle of rooms, standing in one place occasionally turning around only to stare into nothingness, or immediately change what they're doing to begin moving to another location. There is very little telegraphing from them - If at all - Which makes circumnavigating them a frustrating chore.
    [*] Unless the Players are fulfilling Challenges, money (Credits) are impossible to gain until after one has earned enough past the "Quota", which increases with every subsequent submission. In addition, extra cash achieved through going over quota isn't distributed to the Players until the current amassed amount of stolen goods are "locked in" and submitted, leaving Players with the decision to either turn-in early to get cash to continue heisting, or enter missions without extra tools or supplies.
    [*] There's no way to heal the Player for free when at the Safehouse. You must buy and use a Medkit - Which - Are also used to revive downed Coop players.
    [*] Cameras sometimes don't show their "Red FoV" cone in certain angles, or the "edge" of the cone can become so transparent that it's difficult to see until caught. Also, Cameras catch the player [b]immediately[/b]. 
    [*] You cannot open/close doors with a "Two-handed"/"Heavy" item in your hand, requiring the Player to drop the item to interact. In a rush this easily translates to dropping the item from standing height, alerting every NPC in the house whilst also potentially blocking the door you're trying to exit from.
    [*] Figuring out how tall is "too tall" when it comes to vaulting over fences or other objects is tedious, and constantly runs the risk of over-exerting the Player whilst they try to determine if they can proceed. This is typically why other games have visual indicators for the Player in those situations.
    [*] A static physics engine in regards to level geometry makes objects as small as gnomes, standing circular grills, benches and other props impossible to move, easily creating obstacles that can freeze the Player in their tracks if one attempts to push, maneuver, or otherwise jump around them.
[/list]

[h3]Final Thoughts[/h3]

All in all, it's better than Thief Simulator in several aspects (Though I never played the second one), but it still falls flat heavily when it comes to NPC Intelligence, which unfortunately is a large problem right now due to the nature of the game.

Right now I'd give it a solid [b]6/10[/b]. Above average and fun with friends, but frustrating and tasking to take seriously in regards to actual forward progression.

Let me be succinct:

Open a door (not break, open) and an NPC is remotely nearby - detected. Most things are...you know...behind doors.

Use sleep gas while undetected - they instead were alerted and called the cops. They had no way of knowing where I was, yet ran right to me.

Crashed three times during the first hour of playtime. Refunded the game after the third crash.

Get Thief Sim instead.

The initial money given is very little, and if you make a mistake and die on the first day, it takes a long time to get back up. Also, the sound mechanics in the game are incredibly bad; even when NPCs are far away, the sound seems to be coming from close by. Listening for sounds is impossible unless you're looking in that direction because the sound's location is never accurate.

The carrying capacity is very low considering the items you need to carry, and even if you knock out an NPC with sleeping gas and carry the items one by one, you still don't have enough time. Earning spendable money is already difficult, and the sleeping gas you buy for $1000 is only enough to carry 6-7 basic items at most. The climbing and running mechanics are terrible, making it impossible to escape the police at the start, and the random movements of NPCs make it difficult to go unnoticed.

The problem isn't that the game is difficult, or that you have to start over if you can't pay the bail once; the problem is that the skill books you need to enjoy the game are also hard to find, and the difficulty is poorly designed to prevent you from having fun.

Considering what I've said, I wasn't expecting anything perfect from an indie game, but it shouldn't be that hard to make isolated elements on a limited map at least enjoyable to play. If it's improved, it could be playable, but for now, it's just a cheap, multiplier-playable copy of Thief Simulator, and I don't think it even comes close to 20% of the quality. I wish I'd bought 150 grams of döner kebab with the money I spent on it.

There's a slew of issues I have with this game, but namely:

I wanted to like Crime Simulator, but a couple design choices completely kill the fantasy.

First, once you’re wanted, cops basically kill you on sight. There’s little sense of escalation, pursuit, or meaningful counterplay. It turns situations that should feel tense and tactical into instant-fail chaos, and it makes experimentation feel pointless.

Second, the world feels oddly broken at a basic immersion level: everyone is awake in the middle of the night and always home. That makes stealth and planning feel artificial. Nights don’t offer a different rhythm or strategy just the same crowded, always-on NPC presence that undermines the whole “choose your approach” vibe.

Add some clunky feel and general jank on top, and the loop gets repetitive far too quickly.

There’s a cool idea here, but right now it feels more like a rough prototype than a satisfying sim. I can’t recommend it in its current state.
