I bought this game along with Crime Simulator Playgrounds because I'm a huge fan of Thief Simulator and Thief Simulator 2. Played through both games multiple times, and got all achievements for them both.

Playgrounds was a broken, buggy mess that I ended up uninstalling. This one I had higher hopes for because it's got "very positive" feedback, so I assumed the issues were fixed.

While there's no big bugs I've found here, There are several big problems I have with this game:

> The inventory system makes no sense whatsoever. You are (initially) limited to carrying just 5 items. That includes tools, weapons and loot. Regardless of size or weight. Apparently a credit card weights the same as a damn car battery, according to the devs! This means that if you have a lockpick and a crowbar, you can only loot 3 items, regardless of size. throw in a more advanced lock pick, and it's only 2 loot items before having to stash them in your truck.

In the Thief simulator games, you were limited to what you can carry based on weight. So you could ransack a whole house, then just drive off back to your hideout. And even if you ran out of space in your backpack, you could just park your car outside the house, drop a few things off in the car in less than a minute and carry on. Can't do that here, since when you arrive at a street, your have a massive trailer on the back of a lorry that you can't move, meaning if you rob a house on the other side of the map and run out of inventory slots, you have to run a long way to get back to your trailer to drop off the loot.

Seriously, who thought this was a good system? or did the devs just get lazy and decide everything weighed the same?

> XP and skill progression. In the Thief Simulator games, you got xp and skill points by stealing items, selling them on blackbay and completing challenges. You then chose which skills you wanted to advance in. Here, you have to do the same to get the xp and skill points, but instead of choosing what skills you advance in, you have to find leaflets to spend the skill points you get. So you can't just choose what skills you acquire, you take whatever leaflets you get based on the leaflets you find. Oh, and these leaflets take up an inventory slot, exacerbating the issue I bought up earlier.

>  Tenants routines are randomised, and are pretty much always around and awake, and you only rob houses at night. So the mechanic where you picked the right time to loot the houses quickly and escape is gone. Seriously, what kind of criminal would burgle a house when its occupants are present and awake, especially if they're armed?

The only improvement this game makes to thief simulator 1 and 2 is that you can actually fight tenants if they catch you, and either knock them out or kill them, but the game engine and core mechanics of the game aren't well suited for combat.

Disappointed would be an understatement.

To be Fair to this game, it has a very high potential, However there are some major issues that stands between this game being a top tier game or remain in its current state as a lackluster project. Let me list some of the things that leaves a lot to be desired about this game:

1- Poorly explained leveling system; one of the many things that you will come to understand playing is that in order for to become a more capable crook is skill leaflets. which are easy to attain but hard to understand why you have and can't use it at all; unless of course you have the so called 'ability point' necessary to use it, which the game never explains how to obtain this mysteries Ability points. and until you do get it the leaflet and your wishes to carry a piano are dust in the wind

2- Ratios; in order to understands this point let me explain the core mechanic of this game. You break into places steal an amount of items put these items in your truck and then leave each item has a certain value but the loot in each map is set in stone and you can make multiple runs in one round. once you go back to your hideout or while in the mission you buy items that help you with your job such as lockpick bobby pin etc. you have a quota each 3 or 4 days you have to meet it by stealing a certain amount of money worth from everything you steal. 

However, here is where this game really blunders (economically). if the quota is 1000 and you deliver exactly that amount you will not get 1000 to spend, you will only receive 25% of that if you are playing on normal difficulty which is 250. mind you that each time you go back on a mission in the same map there will be less loot because you already cleaned it, having that said know that a glass knife which is an item you can buy to break into windows costs 250 so all of your work will go into buying little items in which some are essential for some properties. or worse yet you have to decide wither you will buy an item or another map and go into that map with no items because maps cost absurd amount. YOU CAN BUY FURNISHER! but you will never have enough money to actually do it 

3- Gameplay fluidity; sooner than you realize this game will make you feel like you accomplished nothing. there is no sense of victory once you make a good run, just sheer disappointment. residents are wandering around making it hard for you to even take a few steps inside once you break in, but that's not the worse part, the worse part is their behavior once they hear you its either immediate calling of police or fight you or even worse both. fighting them is not even worth it will only knock them out for 30 seconds then they will wake up and call the police, even if they called the police before you ko them they will call them a second time when they wake up. the police themselves respond irritatingly quickly leaving little chance to escape, if you run in front of them you will be one shot every single time, if you stop and don't move, they`ll club you to death. this game`s unforgiving nature is massively punishing by account of the lack of mobility that you have, forget about running away from cops once they are chasing because your guy cant run or climb even though his life depends on it. not to mention that on top of how slow and unathletic your guy is, you have a stamina bar which I personally think is hurting this game a lot.  
Furthermore, variety of items you can steal is underwhelming  and their worth is near not worth stealing at all 

4-Unhelpful additions; Jobs, quests and linked items are all great but they offer so little in the grand scheme of this game and could use a little more creativity and quite frankly it needs to be refreshed, I don't want to do a job then have one less job. even though they are useless because whatever reward you get will go straight to your quota not your pocket making them ultimately a waste of time. 


5-No thrill factor: the maps are the exact same nothing really changes expect a few cameras and lasers here and there. same residents same behavior same loot and it gets repetitive really fast. you will find yourself breaking doors with a crowbar rather than lockpick just to get a little mischievous




In conclusion this game is as good as any high tier games like lethal company. it certainly has elements that helps it be fun but it needs to but refined from top to bottom. I sincerely see a great future for this game and hope it will get an overhaul the core gameplay to make a greater success. but in its current state i can't faithfully say that I liked it.

I only give this a bad review because I'm more for single player gaming than multiplayer online gaming. Already played and still play the original thief simulator and thief simulator 2 on all difficulty levels and find them more exciting and fun than this.

Levelling up and gaining XP and other stuff is nearly impossible with having a debt and time frame that leaves no room for comfort. And that's just on the casual difficulty setting! 

Only other problem I've encountered is the fact it's taken more time loading than actually running on the lowest settings. Even though I meet the minimum specs to run this, it's still slow and lagging most of the time, however I think it's because of the online connectivity aspect.

Not bad for those who like online co-op playing with a challenge.

Not really suited for single playing, regardless of the option.

An unexpected dissapointment.

First off people should understand that it appears that this game was designed as a sandbox rather than  a career like previous games

The pro's
-More realism, cannot see tenants through walls
-No more indie jumping on fence pixels
-Combat is now an option rather than only hiding
-No more tossing things across fences, add to the difficulty

Con's
-Items and tools now have durability for no reason
-No more inventory
-Removal of any spot to hide such as under beds or closets
-You can only be active during the night
-Skill unlock is almost RNG based
-No locker or ANYWHERE to store stolen goods
-Poor AI, you can almost walk in front of people and they don't care
-AI pattern is random and unrealistic. People will usually stay in a room for 60 seconds before moving which is not enough time to steal or explore
-Getting spotted by police is a 50% death sentence
-Game is designed to be restarted rather than a career

I would probably recommend people skip this one

Zaccaria Pinball used to be great? What the hell happened?
Well Magic Pixel had the bright idea to swap out their art on most of their original tables to AI Art. Why? Who the hell knows. They already had perfectly good art. It also brings into question some other choices they made.
For example, they censored the art on Farfalla (probably to ensure it can release on Switch) this is all well and good, but then you see the art on Wood's Queen EM+ and you realize they either really didn't need to do that, or whoever made the decision to use the AI art put just as little thought in when using that as well. 
And it just sucks cos the physics in this are great, the recreations of classic Zaccaria machines are great. Then you get to their originals like EM+ and are just assaulted with a barrage of AI slop. For a brand like Zaccaria which has absolutely beautiful artwork, it's hurts to see. I wish there was a way to take back the money I gave them a while ago for this, cos yes, the machines that they added AI art did not launch with AI art. And it seems they're going all in on their new tables here on out, ;with Winter Sports both having AI generated art and an AI generated layout (or so I hear) Seriously Magic Pixel, get your act together.

game_name: Car Dealer Simulator: Prologue - Early Days
review_text:
WARNING: THIS IS PURE TRASH.

I’ve never seen a game so fundamentally broken. It’s clear the developers are either incompetent or just don't care. Car Dealer Simulator is an absolute disaster that shouldn't be allowed on the Steam store.

Major Issues:

Game-Breaking Save Bug: You buy a car, save the game, and when you reload, your car is gone. However, the game still tracks the quest as if you have it. You’re literally stuck with no way to progress.

Terrible Optimization: For a game that looks this bad, it has no business wasting 10GB of data. It's a complete waste of bandwidth.

Shoddy Mechanics: Everything feels clunky, unfinished, and unpolished. It’s a buggy mess from start to finish.

I’ve thrown 10GB of my internet data directly into the trash can by Installing this. Do yourself a favor: DO NOT INSTALL THIS. Save your time, and your sanity. This is not a simulator; it’s a scam.

Rating: -100/10

Let's make a serious review for this. On one hand, this is a nice hsr fangame with cute visuals, great chibi character designs, and great animations. On the other, the difficulty ramps up way too fast. The Herta Space Station section that the game opens with feels really easy compared to the stuff you face later on, whether it's the swam on the Astral Express or the stuff in Belobog (and I don't know what happens after this because I haven't beaten the second Belobog level). You could say that "You're not 83rd in the Genius Society, this isn't for you" and while you'd be right, just because it's made for  number 83 of the Genius Society, doesn't mean it should feel this difficult. Like to put into perspective the difficulty, the first Belobog level has you fight an Ice Out of Space (miniboss) that's hard to kill and then Gepard who has two phases. Like it's just kicking you while you're down. The Silvermane Guards are built incredibly strong in general, it often feels like the game is just trying to screw you over with how some characters start off really great but get absolutely demolished later on (Stelle is a perfect example of this, she seems like an amazing tank but is actually just dogshit in the later parts), and that's just a few of the problems with the game's difficulty. What truly ads insult to injury though is just how there's no guides on youtube to help players figure things out.

As a former HSR player, I can see the appeal, something to play on the side if you crave more HSR content but there is no new event going on.

Too bad this game seems to be abandoned. Despite having some interesting visuals and strategy, I can't get over some things that bug me:
- the chibi models seem very off in the combat cutecenes
- no fullscreen option
- in windowed mode, the very bottom text is cut off and I can barely lift up the windows to check what it is written there (the game forces a certain positioning of the window)
- some tutorial parts are either not explained right away or not clear enough
- why use in-game animations for ultimates if they are so laggy in-game?
- can't seem to be able to choose your protagonist
- it's clunky to navigate through the menus in combat
- narrative feels disjointed

You could say that "You're not 83rd in the Genius Society, this isn't for you" and while you'd be right, just because it's made for  number 83 of the Genius Society, doesn't mean it should feel this difficult. Like to put into perspective the difficulty, the first Belobog level has you fight an Ice Out of Space (miniboss) that's hard to kill and then Gepard who has two phases. Like it's just kicking you while you're down. The Silvermane Guards are built incredibly strong in general, it often feels like the game is just trying to screw you over with how some characters start off really great but get absolutely demolished later on (Stelle is a perfect example of this, she seems like an amazing tank but is actually just dogshit in the later parts), and that's just a few of the problems with the game's difficulty. What truly ads insult to injury though is just how there's no guides on youtube to help players figure things out.

As a former HSR player, I can see the appeal, something to play on the side if you crave more HSR content but there is no new event going on.

A Beautiful World Where Time Stands Still — Warning for Series Veterans

I wanted to love this remake, but as a veteran of the series, this is a major disappointment. It feels like a hollowed-out "Easy Mode" version of the franchise that prioritizes "frictionless" convenience over meaningful gameplay and atmosphere.

The Main Issue: Zero Town Agency The biggest problem is how static and "dead" the world feels. Compared to the classic entries in this series, the town has no heartbeat; everything is on rails:

The Rival System is Gone: Unlike the original standards of this series, marriage candidates no longer have independent romantic lives or events. They will never marry or progress on their own. It makes the NPCs feel like cardboard cutouts waiting for your permission to exist.

The "Center of the Universe" Syndrome: The town doesn't function without you. If you don't participate in a festival, it essentially doesn't happen. You can't even "just watch" the Pet Show; you are forced into a mini-game because the game doesn't know how to exist without the player at the center of the frame.

Regression in Game Design It is embarrassing that games released 20 years ago on the GBA and DS (like Friends of Mineral Town) had more complex NPC AI and social consequences. Those older systems handled rival marriages, animal deaths, and unpredictable weather perfectly.

Seeing a modern game simplify mechanics—like the wind being a predictable one-directional "daily buff" or the Bazaar Day weather being hard-coded to always be sunny—is a massive step back. In older titles, rainy days were a constant factor you had to adapt to. While I haven't played the original Grand Bazaar, the fact that the weather is now sanitized to ensure the player never faces a setback gives the impression that Marvelous has taken the easy and lazy route yet again instead of adding toggles, difficulty settings, or better AI.

Price: While the updated graphics are nice and the voice-acting is okay, neither justifies the insanely high price considering all the depth that has been removed or simplified.

Final Verdict: If you are new to the series and want a zero-stress, beautiful sandbox, you'll enjoy this. But if you’re looking for a community simulator that respects your intelligence, this remake has lost its soul.

imagine starting a cozy game that literally looks perfect, it asks you to choose a pronoun and a gender. okay, you choose a FEMALE body type. FEMALE. and then you see the customization options. EVERYTHING. and i mean EVERYTHING is androgynous. hello??? i am a WOMAN, i didn’t pick nonbinary, i didn’t pick BEING A FEMBOY, i picked FEMALE. that is INSANE. it’s becoming a trend where devs avoid making feminine designs because they want to be “safe” or “inclusive" the FUCK,

i don’t have a problem with gender-neutral options existing!! they’re great for the people who want them!! the problem is when they become the ONLY option. inclusivity isn’t removing one identity to include another, it’s ADDING CHOICES. this game is disrespectful, LAZY, DISMISSIVE, and CARELESS. the only feminine clothing available; and keep in mind i have EVERYTHING unlocked; are DRESSES. DRESSES. are we in the 60s?? REALLY?? the game is insane. how is it almost 2026 and we can’t have a FEMALE character with CURVES AND CHEST?? the model looks flat and androgynous. dresses are literally the only “feminine” option. it makes BIOLOGICAL WOMEN players look like a joke.

i love this game, and that’s what makes it so frustrating. it could be amazing. however, the devs clearly don’t care about anything but their own “safe” designs. how can a game supposedly about inclusion completely ignore the most BASIC group of people? as a BISEXUAL WOMAN, part of the community, i am furious. this isn’t inclusivity it’s erasure!!!!

Not a bad idea, but the progression is really weird. Most of the boxes are too heavy to move, and if you start at level 1 and just play, you'll eventually end up with a mountain of huge containers, desperately searching the pile for the lighter crates you can potentially pull out and deliver. Furthermore, those tiny light crates can be worth up to an order of magnitude more than the big heavier ones, making the heavy containers not just immovable, but not even worth moving. 
To be fair, the game does introduce a crutch - a popup asking you to clear all the boxes when too many has been spawned in - but I think it's a lazy and very unsatisfying solution. 
The later levels that demand a specific type of cargo are made worse by the fact that boxes are not labeled, at all, so you have to check them one by one. There is a skill that lights up the current objective-related boxes as a later game option, but giving the crates labels by weight and cargo type would have been a nice in-between option.
tl;dr Just make the weight and cost balance make sense, and visually label the different crates by weight and cargo, and you have a simple solid game. Until then, not a particularly enjoyable experience, but I'll update my review if this ever gets implemented.
Game's bad. Why does the game spawn infinite boxes? Why are they all too heavy to move? A lot of weird design decisions here

There also seemed to be levels that we could progress through, but we couldn't figure out how to do them in multiplayer, we always got randomly generated levels no matter how we selected it. I think the game concept is actually not bad, but either there's a lot of bugs, or there's a lot of explaining to be done. Actually regardless, there's a lot of explaining that needs to be done.

I genuinely tried to get into Maid of Salvation, but the game ended up feeling too repetitive and not particularly interesting. Artistically, I never vibed with the mix of anime and chibi aesthetics. The character and enemy designs feel inconsistent — sometimes you're fighting a giant-headed cartoonish skeleton, other times a semi-realistic one. The gap in style makes the art feel uneven, and not in a way that adds charm to me.

Enemy variety is also very limited. Many enemies are just recycled palette swaps with slightly higher stats or a different elemental attack. It genuinely feels like you’re fighting the same six or so enemies in each zone, and even the boss roster gets reused. Combat itself is fine — inoffensive and functional — but it doesn’t really expand or evolve in a way that kept me engaged.

If you’ve played the demo, understand that is the game. It represents the experience accurately, but the full game never meaningfully builds on those systems. Hours go by without any real innovation, and the repetition becomes draining.

There are also random difficulty spikes, presumably to discourage players from entering areas too early. But since the enemies are just the same ones with higher numbers, the difficulty feels artificial rather than interesting.

Ultimately, I grew bored and exhausted with the game. It doesn’t do anything outright terrible, but it never develops into something compelling, and the repetition wore me down long before the credits.

Trailer Park Tycoon: Raccoon Ranch is… definitely a strange little game. You play as a raccoon running a store in a chaotic trailer park full of quirky neighbours, with a mix of farming, shop management, and adult humour.

The gameplay mechanics are solid enough, farming resources, crafting drinks, and stocking your store is satisfying, and the interface is easy to navigate. That said, the humour and themes are very niche. Personally, I didn’t enjoy all of it, but it’s clear the game knows its audience.

I can see this being entertaining for players who enjoy offbeat, bizarre tycoon games and are curious about oddball simulation experiences. For me, it was a fun one-off curiosity, but not something I’d play long-term.

Overall: Interesting mechanics, very weird tone, definitely a game you need to approach with the right mindset.

Recommendation: If you enjoy strange, quirky tycoon games and aren’t easily offended by adult humour, it might be worth a try. Otherwise, maybe watch some gameplay first.

🎥 Here’s a video I recorded of the first hour: https://youtu.be/p5_TXDNs7fU

Definitely not a game for everyone.

Definitely not a game for everyone.

First, the good:
* The story is pretty well written, the characters evoke a wide spectrum of emotional response, and it unfolds in a mostly satisfying way. I found the connection between neighboring time periods generally satisfying, with most being narratively grouped into 2-3 time periods to tell an arc, and bridging neighboring arcs with each other through bloodlines and the occasional artifact.
* The actual game mechanics are relatively straightforward, such that even when things aren't explained well, they're intuitive enough to figure out quickly
* The difference in setting feels more than cosmetic, especially with the dialogue
* It's pretty ambitious in the setting and scope, especially for this genre of game. It works most of the time, and works well.

The Bad:
* The UI sucks. Rather than unveiling more of it as plot points and time periods show up, most of the game is visible (but replaced with "Locked" or "???" or similar) from the get-go. And on the inverse side, you will eventually entirely complete some aspects of the game, but still need to scroll past them every time you want to check something new. This is especially bad because as you progress, the periods get further and further away from each other, making things very impractical to scroll through.
* I found a moderate amount of the handwritten notes to be difficult to read
* The balancing for some of the eras is very poor, both in terms of time scaling (for the Perk tree and area completion rate) as well as the endgame eras where you need to move stone across almost the entire timeline. 
* There's some minor bugs, mostly UI-related. 

I did want to also speak towards the pacing of the game, which was a significant enough issue for me that I think it warrants its own section. I played about an hour on normal speed (1x) before switching to 4x, and currently I'm at 97.66% completion of the game. I'm guessing that the remaining 2.34% will take more than 24 hours of real time, compared to the 15 hours of total game time that I have now. I enjoyed this game well enough at 4x speed, but at 1x, I have to imagine it'd be a slog. Unlike other incremental games, there's relatively little optimization being requested of you, as well as relatively few upgrades to choose between, with a high amount of time (30+ minutes, even at 4x speed!) to wait for the next series of upgrades. While these alone aren't necessarily deal breakers, the thought of this story being told at 1/4 speed of what already felt like a slow pace to me is rough. That the Perk tree is apparently required for 100% Victory is going to be a boring slog of waiting with no benefit, because I've completed everything else in the game. This is the most significant example, but the problem is more pervasive than this instance.

An ambitious game that tells the story about how more than ten generations of residents impacted an area and how the decisions of one person can have profound effects on both the future and the past.

Unfortunately, the same sense of interconnectedness that permeates the storyline didn't get factored into the gameplay.  The last ten percent of progress takes several times longer than the first 90%, most of that happening even after the final scraps of lore has been squeezed out of the story.  The perks system is cumbersome and the rewards are never things that might are applicable to your current progress in the game.  The entire endeavor seems motivated towards taking a strong 15-hour game with a good payoff and stretching it into a plodding 40-hour game with nothing but Steam achievements to show for it.

Of course the game is early access, so hopefully they will eventually fix the balance of the game.  I look forward to checking back in six months to see if they eventually got the game to be as good as it deserves to be.

I usually don't write reviews for games on steam, but I felt compelled to put one down for this because I'm so divided on this.  Going to divide this into Mechanics and Story (Since the story is such an integral part of the game). Also going to obfuscate some of the concepts here to avoid spoilers, even though there are plenty of them in other reviews.  FWIW, I binged this over 3-4 days and completed almost everything.

Mechanics

If you've played idle games before it's not unlike a most of them.  Good news here is that there are no MTX, just plain old gameplay.  And It's really Idle in name only, because there is pretty consistently something to do apart from at the endgame, and even then, there are built-in free time accelerators and time skips, so if you want you can still jump past any really long idle bits.  Assign characters to locations to get resources, with the ultimate goal of advancing storylines using xp and sometimes, other specific resources.  As you progress, new "areas" unlock and resources collected in one area can spread to other areas.  Extra characters can be assigned to different "research" options for various permanent buffs.  It's interesting how not all characters are equally skilled at each kind of task, so you're often swapping them around so that you can advance their storylines, and outside of research, each character is only useable in the area where they are found.  It's definitely an interesting premise.  Big downside comes near the last 25% of the game.  Due to the resource "spread" between areas, they can justify resources that are required in one particular area for progression that aren't directly accessible there, but the transfer rate between said region can get to infinitesimally small that it can become a real annoyance in moving storylines forward at the very end.  Very uncharacteristically tedious for what you'll have experienced so far, and it's a very odd design choice for me.  That being said, enjoyable for the most part, but if you don't push through it like I did, You might have a better balance because you can spend that idle offline time pushing research forward.

Story

It starts off as an interesting kind of "murder mystery" plot with a seemingly obvious culprit, and then spreads out into a web of character stories and events that seem to indicate some grander, far more complex series of events that make the plot what it is.  A super interesting premise with what starts to looks like a hint of supernatural elements that really get you intrigued.  As you dig into the multiple areas and the events with the characters there, you see tidbits of linking stories and get more explanations as to what you're dealing with, which is what kept me going at the game for such a long time without being able to put it down.  You keep digging deeper and deeper, revealing either more flavor about the world or just dropping more hints about what actually happened, character motivations, etc.  A really good hook if I’ve ever seen one, with some wrid crazy WTF moments along the way that tease a larger payoff once you really find out what’s going on.  This keeps up largely until the last 15-10% of the story, at which point the bulk of the final reveal happens, which imo, falls flat on its face.  To explain why I’d have to go into spoilers, but for the sake of this review, there are many shenanigans that happen that show that people who are supposed to be real smart and morally advanced end up making quite possibly the most dumb and baffling decisions just to lead to a very weird payoff that would defy the game’s own logic if not for one vague side-mention implying that everything that was explained so far might have been wrong.  The whole concept to me of a story where you get told “remember everything we said before, all of it was a lie/wrong/unreliable” can be an interesting premise, but only if it’s done right, and I don’t feel like it’s done right here.  It’s something to have a grand reveal that makes events click and tie together in a wonderful knot, it’s another to have a story just tell you to ignore everything that you thought was true and just “accept this is actually how everything works”.  It cuts off my investment in the story, because as a writer, you’ve shown me that you have no investment in it either.  The rules were changed at the last minute so that you could insert a payoff.  You didn’t do a setup and then a payoff, you nuked the setup from orbit and then handed me a built a payoff on the ruins of what was left.  And this is how this game felt.  The very last minute pivot at the end of the game, from the annoyingly slow resource gain, the incredible amount of time it suddenly takes to complete locations, and the extremely disjointed story ending make me not recommend this game to someone who would really be invested in the story.  While not as colossal of a failure as that, I can’t help to compare this with why I wouldn’t recommend someone who’s never seen it to watch Game of Thrones, no matter how good the first several seasons are.  They’ll inevitably end up wanted to see it to the end and get disappointed.

A game unfortunately held back by the fact it's a game.  Having tried the very surface of it, I thought the writing was pretty good, but the UI remained terrible to navigate through and I hear it gets even worse as the game goes on and more eras are unlocked.

i really want to play this game but the text is waaaaaaay too small. you'd need a huge screen.

I have to give this one a negative, because Steam won't let us give a neutral review.  

tldr: It IS a novel take on storytelling, and you'll probably have a good time if you're into the sort of human suffering sort of story this brings.  It is engaging to see how the various plots intertwine, and it is interesting to see how things proceed, particularly in the future eras.  But it isn't flawless and both the gameplay and story drag at times.


On the positive side, Asbury Pines is a novel take on exploring a story that slowly reveals itself over time.  I can't recall another blend of an idle game where the story itself is the hook providing the motivation.  And while the story does attempt to portray how one time period affects another and even includes some light time travel, it falls short of what could be done with this approach.

The flip side of relying on the story is that the actual gameplay is incredibly basic.  There's no meaty, crunch gameplay loop, nor does the gameplay expand beyond what you experience in the first ten minutes.  You have people, you can assign them to places and wait for resources.  Or you can click a button, wait, and get their story.  That's it.  The game does try to spice this up by having some resources carry over from one era to the next, but it just doesn't matter in all but two cases and can almost be entirely ignored.  You'll spend so much time waiting for other things, that the resource generation is never really an issue.

So the game has to lean entirely on the story side of things to carry it, and the story is... okay enough, I guess?  There are parts of it that are good, particularly late game when you start getting some time travel that explains previous events.  But most of the story seems irrelevant or just flavor.  That would be okay, except it is so drip fed and start-and-stop that it somewhat becomes a jumbled mess, particularly if multiple eras all have story events firing at once.  Several times I'd be trying to read some bit of story, and suddenly unlock another era, half a dozen more characters, an animal, and 8 different work locations, all of which get a notification on screen.  Or I'd be unlocking story events from two or even three different eras, which may or may not have any interaction with each other.

Most of the characters just aren't important and don't really move the story forward.  They do flesh out the world a bit, but particularly the past time periods.  it will be a while before there is any payoff for exploring them.

Then at the end, the full extent of the story is revealed in just a few instances and you see how time travel has nicely closed the loop and put a bow on things.  Except THE MOST IMPORTANT story event, which is left as a wishy-washy unclosed time loop.  If a certain event happens, then one time stream is followed, which results in that certain event not happening, which means that the other stream is followed, which means that events cause the first event to happen...  It's an unclosed paradox, and it's the most pivotal event in the entire story.  All the other events close nicely, except for the one the entire story is about.

Because of the way that the story is revealed slowly, the majority of the character stories in the first half of the game are people looking to various gods for help, their lives inevitably falling apart, and then them complaining about it.  Future, present, past, doesn't matter, they're all wrapped up in religion and the philosophy thereof.  There's so much of it that it starts to overshadow what ultimately turns into the actually important storyline.  Eventually the game gets away from this about the time the snails start coming around, but it takes a while, and it overstays its welcome.  Seriously, outside of the first 5 characters or so, you follow that pattern for probably the next 50 characters before there's any real deviation from this pattern.

This is not a child's game.  There is some *dark* stuff in there.  Murder, depravity, society collapse, alcoholism, exploitation, tribalism, etc, but nothing a mature individual can't handle.  Just understand that there are very few happy people in this game, and almost every character in this game is going to have a terrible life.  There is payoff for this in the end, but you're going to be reading the lives of roughly 80 or so people who are just miserable on your way to that point.

Character stories are richly detailed.  Artifact, locations, etc are not.  They're just blurbs that don't really seem to have any significance.  Again, it's fleshing out the world, but not in meaningful ways.

The UI is oddly separated.  You'll spend a lot of time switching back and forth between scavenging locations, work locations, and nature locations.  They're separated... for reasons?  Same with the three tech trees.  I guess it helps distinguish which sets various characters are good at, but it means you're flipping between different sections of the game as though they were different game systems, but they're really just artificially separated to make the game seem deeper even if it's an extremely shallow system.

One notable weird thing is how sexuality is presented.  Every relationships in this game is heterosexual, but all homosexual interaction is reduced to raw sexualily (and conversely, all sexual encounters are strictly homosexual), in a way seemingly designed to frustrate everyone, regardless of your position.  If you're against homosexuality, all sexuality in this game is homosexual (except for one off-hand comment by one character).  If you are on board with homosexuality, homosexual relationships are reduced to sexual encounters, nothing deeper or lasting or more than a sexual encounter.  It's like they wanted to include it to check a checkbox, but did so in a way that's just going to annoy everybody.

These leaves the game feeling like a fresh take on storytelling, but falling just short in almost every category.  Gameplay is weak.  UI is scattered.  Worldbuilding is shallow and fragmented.  Characters lack depth, and many feel formulaic or have little impact.  

And yet I went ahead and played the game through.  It IS a novel take on storytelling, and you'll probably have a good time if you're into the sort of human suffering sort of story this brings.  It is engaging to see how the various plots intertwine, and it is interesting to see how things proceed, particularly in the future eras.  But it isn't flawless and both the gameplay and story drag at times.  Interesting to play through once, but falls short of what could be done with this approach to idle story.

What a downer.

For the most part I enjoyed Asbury Pines. The little stories it told about ages and the 'aha' moments when the connections came together in my head. But then I hit the last act and it really made the entire trip feel completely worthless.

Obviously massive spoilers follow, but I need to get this off my chest.
[spoiler]So ultimately this game boils down to a time travel plot. A super advanced society in the future determines that it can only come to exist if a particular person in the modern day dies tragically. This leads to them sending an assassin back in time with the purpose of [i]murdering an innocent child[/i], because it will traumatise her family in just the right way that they'll prioritise writing down their history and preserving it in a way that ultimately leads to perfect civilisation.

So it's the plot of Terminator, but we're expected to be rooting for Skynet.

This also means that all the events in between - multiple global catastrophes that threaten to wipe out humanity, one of which [b]the time travellers actively cause[/b] - are allowed to continue unabated, killing billions upon billions of innocents, all so the Perfectly Enlightened future assholes get to have everything they want. (They also try to spread their Enlightened opinions to cavemen and get said cavemen killed as a result.)

What's especially weird is it's acknowledged this is screwed up. There's a future cult that wants people to stop screwing with the timeline and focus on the present rather than dwelling on the past. But not only is this cult ignored, but the plot actively says they're WRONG.

(The time travel logic doesn't even make sense here - if certain events HAVE to happen to cause the present, then by definition they've already happened because the present exists and thus they shouldn't have to interfere in the timeline at all!)

The worst part is I waited the whole game for a subversion. The assassination attempts fail so ultimately it's Everly Praetis who has to go back in time and kill Everly Praetis. I was wondering if she'd reject the idea and rewrite the timeline, or find a way to cheat herself into the future, or SOMETHING. But no, she just kills herself and then ceases to exist as a result. That's the ending.[/spoiler]

I hate this. I feel like [spoiler]'centuries of toil and agony and unimaginable trauma is okay because in the future everyone will be happy'[/spoiler] is such a miserable moral to end your story on that I had to write this review. I did a lot of idling to get through this game, and the outcome I got at the end of it was just a slap in the face.

Full transparency - I refunded because it's just not my cup of tea. I see a lot of others dig this game, which more power to them, but I really had a tough time getting into it, and refunded quick because I wasn't sure I would be allowed to with early access. I'm going to list my impressions off the bat with my personal pros and cons:

Pros:
- Dig the vibe as a Twin Peaks fan
- The concept is what drew me in as a puzzle mystery fan
- Narratively, the idea of a story spanning millennia is very appealing and unique. As a writer myself IRL I dig the writing challenge this presents and the feelings you get from recognizing something bigger than yourself. (I got reminded of a book called All Tomorrows that deals with this is a sci-fi way)
- The little bits of writing you get as rewards are the most interesting part. Some are better than others but I still think this is a highlight.

With other idlers I've played, the building of resources is primarily circular: You get more to be able to get more. Adding the element of mystery to this one makes that mystery especially important. To make up for the lull, every bit of narrative has to be juicy and compelling. That being said:

Cons:
- Gameplay does not gel with the story at all. I love a great story, especially a great mystery story, but I have to  want to keep playing to find out what happened. In my opinion, the idle genre does not serve the intrigue, thrill, and constant stakes that are necessary for an engaging mystery. This game doesn't know if it wants to be a passive idler or an active investigation, and i think both parts suffer a bit as a result. 

- In that vein, some writing you get as a reward is not even really story related, and purely just vibes based to enrich the setting. The writing itself is good, and I can appreciate this in a book where I'm experiencing what a character is going through, but when it's supposed to be me doing this work and waiting forever, it's a tad disappointing for my next reward to be about ~ sunlight through the trees ~ and not the juicy stuff I'm after. Doesn't give me a whole lot of motivation to continue idling.

- Experiencing the characters and then immediately being able to use them as pawns in my own game kind of removes any personal care I had for who they are as people. I'm trapped between them acting as they are written, as individuals, and then a hard left turn to basically the Sims. The mayor can scavenge on the sidewalk? Would he actually do that as a character? Probably not but I guess I have to let him since this is the beginning and we're moving so slow actually making this town.

(I think the developers noticed this because there are only specific characters that can unlock certain artifacts, which I think is a step in the right direction, but ultimately for me the problem runs much deeper. This narrative dissonance that you create as the player makes it tough to get me to care about the characters, and therefore tough to want to continue.)

- I won't comment much on the progress collecting things and workings things since that wouldn't be fair with how long I played, but I will say, being able to worship stone pillars and unlock astronomy in 2025 also felt really weird. I get wanting to start at the very beginning like Civilization tech trees, but that was one more minor thing that took me out of it. Maybe starting from the middle and being able to work back and forward would make more sense

- Resource pop-ups and notifications could be much clearer to the user.

Finally, Price: Given everything I've laid out, in my opinion this game is not worth almost 10 dollars at early access. I would have given it more of a shot if it we only $5-$7 dollars.

I appreciate all the hard work that went into this game. I hope my review is helpful in some way to the developers and to people interested in this product.  Have a good day.

This game is absolutely baffling, a story and plot that's exceptionally well written and presented but behind a gate of time that sadly bricks it. 


The idea is simple, you have various characters, animals, even plants that you assign work and scavenging to to learn more about the central mystery and plot.  This plot spans decades, from the somewhat distance past, present, and dystopian future. You learn about a murder mystery with unexplainable circumstances. A schizophrenic drunkard that knows significantly more about the world than what you would expect. A bumbling but loveable oaf of a cop with a glowing family history that he can't come close to himself. A corrupt and sleezy mayor that ends up turning the town into a police state with a dramatic end. I want to know more, I love the writing and the characters here. You got me eagerly waiting to unlock more about the story from the perspective of a pine tree of all things!

That's the problem though, it's waiting. I'm waiting to unlock story segments, characters to reveal more about themselves and the central story. It goes until the point where my interest has all but faded and I start to contemplate just what I'm doing with my life. Even concepts like research are either from the perspective of rebuilding society after the dramatic event in the future or it's completely unlinked to the town itself and I really don't understand that. Regarding I'm waiting even longer to unlock perks that don't benefit the overall mystery or plot, just means "less" waiting for everything else. A huge time sink with no reward. There's a speed adjustment setting, and an offline progress option but that doesn't fix the problem. In fact it makes it more glaring, this entire concept doesn't work as an idle game.

Normally an idle game core hook is having you start with effectively nothing and building yourself up. This game tries to do that with this set of mysteries but it doesn't work that way. it's hard to stay engaged and recall what's going on. Great story, fantastic writing, the abject worst way to present it. 


I hate not recommending it, but for me this premise doesn't work with the gameplay.

Edit: I lost my save file after Early Access Patch 3. I don't know how. I advise anyone playing this to ensure they have manual saves and backups. This game might not autosave, or there may have been a bug in the latest update. It's not a big deal for me; I already beat the game and was 3h away from finishing the last researches I needed to 100% it. What a shame, though.
My experiences below were on Early Access Patch 2:

I don't think this was worth it. A game like this is all about its writing. I saw the writing degrade over time. It was better at the start, and just got worse and worse without getting better. By worse I mean more one-note, more templated or boilerplate, and just plain lame. Everyone has the same authorial voice. They all talk the same, no matter their age or place in time. Archetype characters fill their role in the same way as everyone else of that archetype. Like, the villains are all cartoonish; the religious characters are all either manipulative or naive; scenes play out like a template from a screenplay textbook. When you've seen one, you've seen them most.

Maybe it's some kind of meta commentary about how we're all the same? But I think in truth it's just poor writing.

It's also a murder mystery. But unfortunately, my initial guess for what happened with the murder when I had no information, was precisely what happened when I had all the information. Have you seen a time travel story before? You'll probably guess it too.

So the whole game I'm waiting for an exciting twist that just never comes.
- I guessed what the mystery was right away
- The game never gave me cause to doubt that initial guess
- The writing got worse as the game went on
- The gameplay doesn't interplay or jive with the story

Also, the game totally drops the murder mystery aspect for ~70% of its runtime. You're meant to follow the lives of other characters instead, but it doesn't come back around until the end.

The game's UI and presentation start off impressively good, but it became tedious and annoying after a while.

My biggest issue is:
- When a new story is unlocked, you should get a notification on the menu that it comes from, and perhaps on the timeline too. The notifications on the Stories menu are extremely small. Why couldn't the whole line be highlighted a new color? Or the menu be auto-zoomed to a new entry?

Other issues I had were:
- Scavenging, Working, and Exploring should really all be on one screen. I did a lot of clicking & hotkeying back and forth while getting confused about where I was, because I had to go between screens with their own stored position. Other idle/incremental games are a lot worse about getting lost in menus, but still, it happened to me throughout most of my playtime.
- When researching science, religion, and governing, there should be a sort to find whoever has the highest score.
- Resources flowing across time sounds like a great idea, and the UI for it is pretty good. But It doesn't really matter. You're only bottlenecked on one resource at a time, and most eras can produce what they need. You don't have to do anything clever.
- There's a disconnect in the game with the workstations and their outputs. Get this:
The butchery produces food. The steel plant produces steel. The gas station produces oil. All good!
The newspaper produces food? The police station produces steel? The brewery produces computers? Why?
This disconnect is there for the whole game. Half of your workstations make sense, and the other half have no connection between what they are actually, what they are for the story's purposes, and what their gameplay effect is.
- Several systems are ultimately timelocked. That is, they're locked by time investment and nothing else. The animals have the guts to admit it - I liked that. But I didn't like it with plants or artifacts. Only occasionally do you have to weigh whether you want to put a particular worker in one slot or another. Most of the time, there's no real choice. You've put people where the have the biggest numbers and you're just waiting.

So, ultimately, I don't think it was worth it. Mechanically and visually, it has shortcomings that become obvious after a day or so. Storywise, it was nothing special.

At around 30% completion I've lost motivation to play this game. The story is so-so; parts are intriguing, but then there is much irrelevant filler, such as the stag beetle or ladybird stories - it's all well written, but kind of meh.

The UI is a mess, and involves a lot of needless scrolling.

More than this though, there is no real strategy to playing this game. Despite initial appearances, progression is fairly linear as the story passes through gated events. It just feels like a step-by-step chore. I probably would prefer it as a VN, rather than it pretending to be an incremental.

I may make changes to this review if I can make myself play further, and if the game surprises me.

This game seems too casual for me, even for an idler. The game play loop is simple: use people to gather resources so you can spend them on story progression which unlocks more people and resources. If you don't care to read the story you're unlocking, then there's nothing to push you to keep playing except maybe collecting and completionism for the sake of it. There is some semblance of complexity with nested resource chains, but the UI does not work hard enough for this aspect to be engaging.

The progression systems are nowhere near as numerous or complex as the screenshots make them appear to be. Much of the UI is duplicate information. For example, unlocked stories can be read in the both the people menu and the stories menu. The quest menu is a visual overview of all of the stories you've unlocked and how they relate to each other, but adds nothing new, especially if you're able to follow the story on your own. Passive unlocks can be seen in the perks menu as well as each of the research trees where those perks are unlocked.  So basically it looks like there's more to the menus than there really is. People don't gradually get better at tasks that you assign them to, so the only meta progression is the research trees, which VERY quickly hits a wall. It goes from like 5 minutes for the first unlock to 120 minutes for the second unlock. There's just not much to do except assign people to tasks, wait for resources to accrue, unlock stories, and then read those if you care about that.

The game play loop of managing people and tasks did not feel rewarding as someone who enjoys min-maxing. People have stats that supposedly improve their ability to perform specific tasks. For example, one person can have +5 scavenging while another person has +6 scavenging. What that means is not explained anywhere I could see. Since +6 is 20% greater than +5, does that mean that person does the task 20% faster? Who knows. It didn't seem that way from what I could tell, possibly due to some hidden diminishing-returns formula. In fact, a person could have a stat for a specific task even if the game does not allow you to assign them to that task at all, such as the mayor who has a "work" stat, but cannot be assigned to work. A person could be assignable to only one variant of a task but not another variant, such as the eye-patch girl who can work at the market but not work at the gas station, with no reason given. Restrictions seem haphazard and consequently confusing. It seems like the game just wants you to pick who ever has the higher stat number without any further thought or comparison between alternatives, go AFK, and then come back to unlock some stories to read.

Where I thought the game would start to get interesting is when multiple eras was introduced. Resources from the past contribute to the future. This was not adequately explained, and was something I had to figure out on my own after after my initial confusion. At first it seemed interesting, but then it just led to more confusion. Some resources like money have a 300% multiplier, meaning money earned in the past is worth 3x more in the future, but then other resources like EXP have a 20% multiplier, meaning 80% of what's earned in the past is unusable in the future. Every resource has a different multiplier and I couldn't sense any pattern or logic to it. I guess it boils to game balance and you just have to trust that the devs know what they are doing. Why they couldn't just give everything the same multiplier and rebalance the value of resources within the same era they are earned and spent is not clear to me. Lastly, the production information is not as informative as I would like. There's no resource per time unit, so you can't tell when you're going to run out of something and preemptively fix it by adjusting production chains.

All in all, I don't think it's a bad game, just not for me. I can only recommend it if you like being fed video-game-quality story-writing piecemeal. If my complaints resonate with you, then I can recommend "Idle Awakening: Mages Path" instead of this game. It's also early access, but it's half the cost and at least twice as enjoyable. It also has story snippets that you unlock through progression, along with more complex, interwoven, and impactful progression systems that encourage you to consult a spreadsheet or an experienced player if you want to be optimal. I haven't written a review for it yet because I'm waiting for the official release, but I very much enjoyed it in its current state.

First, the good:
* The story is pretty well written, the characters evoke a wide spectrum of emotional response, and it unfolds in a mostly satisfying way. I found the connection between neighboring time periods generally satisfying, with most being narratively grouped into 2-3 time periods to tell an arc, and bridging neighboring arcs with each other through bloodlines and the occasional artifact.
* The actual game mechanics are relatively straightforward, such that even when things aren't explained well, they're intuitive enough to figure out quickly
* The difference in setting feels more than cosmetic, especially with the dialogue
* It's pretty ambitious in the setting and scope, especially for this genre of game. It works most of the time, and works well.

The Bad:
* The UI sucks. Rather than unveiling more of it as plot points and time periods show up, most of the game is visible (but replaced with "Locked" or "???" or similar) from the get-go. And on the inverse side, you will eventually entirely complete some aspects of the game, but still need to scroll past them every time you want to check something new. This is especially bad because as you progress, the periods get further and further away from each other, making things very impractical to scroll through.
* I found a moderate amount of the handwritten notes to be difficult to read
* The balancing for some of the eras is very poor, both in terms of time scaling (for the Perk tree and area completion rate) as well as the endgame eras where you need to move stone across almost the entire timeline. 
* There's some minor bugs, mostly UI-related. 

I did want to also speak towards the pacing of the game, which was a significant enough issue for me that I think it warrants its own section. I played about an hour on normal speed (1x) before switching to 4x, and currently I'm at 97.66% completion of the game. I'm guessing that the remaining 2.34% will take more than 24 hours of real time, compared to the 15 hours of total game time that I have now. I enjoyed this game well enough at 4x speed, but at 1x, I have to imagine it'd be a slog. Unlike other incremental games, there's relatively little optimization being requested of you, as well as relatively few upgrades to choose between, with a high amount of time (30+ minutes, even at 4x speed!) to wait for the next series of upgrades. While these alone aren't necessarily deal breakers, the thought of this story being told at 1/4 speed of what already felt like a slow pace to me is rough. That the Perk tree is apparently required for 100% Victory is going to be a boring slog of waiting with no benefit, because I've completed everything else in the game. This is the most significant example, but the problem is more pervasive than this instance.

I like the idea of the game: basically an incremental visual novel that unveils its plot as you progress its various systems.

The problem is, uh...  There's just a lot of narrative nonsense in the game.  The individual tidbits of plot make sense, and some of the stories, but the overarching world is nonsensical, the tech trees are absurd, etc.  The Devs confessed to using AI for "brainstorming ideas" and frankly, that would make a lot of sense of things.  Like, for example, how "legion of scientific mystics" is the pre-req for "neotranscendentalism" and "conservative judaism" or how a floating city of marauders crossed the "shallow seas" of a flooded US from Philidelphia and DC to someplace at the base of the Rockies or uh, the whole thing with the snail society.

An ambitious game that tells the story about how more than ten generations of residents impacted an area and how the decisions of one person can have profound effects on both the future and the past.

Unfortunately, the same sense of interconnectedness that permeates the storyline didn't get factored into the gameplay.  The last ten percent of progress takes several times longer than the first 90%, most of that happening even after the final scraps of lore has been squeezed out of the story.  The perks system is cumbersome and the rewards are never things that might are applicable to your current progress in the game.  The entire endeavor seems motivated towards taking a strong 15-hour game with a good payoff and stretching it into a plodding 40-hour game with nothing but Steam achievements to show for it.

Of course the game is early access, so hopefully they will eventually fix the balance of the game.  I look forward to checking back in six months to see if they eventually got the game to be as good as it deserves to be.

I usually don't write reviews for games on steam, but I felt compelled to put one down for this because I'm so divided on this.  Going to divide this into Mechanics and Story (Since the story is such an integral part of the game). Also going to obfuscate some of the concepts here to avoid spoilers, even though there are plenty of them in other reviews.  FWIW, I binged this over 3-4 days and completed almost everything.

Mechanics

If you've played idle games before it's not unlike a most of them.  Good news here is that there are no MTX, just plain old gameplay.  And It's really Idle in name only, because there is pretty consistently something to do apart from at the endgame, and even then, there are built-in free time accelerators and time skips, so if you want you can still jump past any really long idle bits.  Assign characters to locations to get resources, with the ultimate goal of advancing storylines using xp and sometimes, other specific resources.  As you progress, new "areas" unlock and resources collected in one area can spread to other areas.  Extra characters can be assigned to different "research" options for various permanent buffs.  It's interesting how not all characters are equally skilled at each kind of task, so you're often swapping them around so that you can advance their storylines, and outside of research, each character is only useable in the area where they are found.  It's definitely an interesting premise.  Big downside comes near the last 25% of the game.  Due to the resource "spread" between areas, they can justify resources that are required in one particular area for progression that aren't directly accessible there, but the transfer rate between said region can get to infinitesimally small that it can become a real annoyance in moving storylines forward at the very end.  Very uncharacteristically tedious for what you'll have experienced so far, and it's a very odd design choice for me.  That being said, enjoyable for the most part, but if you don't push through it like I did, You might have a better balance because you can spend that idle offline time pushing research forward.

Story

It starts off as an interesting kind of "murder mystery" plot with a seemingly obvious culprit, and then spreads out into a web of character stories and events that seem to indicate some grander, far more complex series of events that make the plot what it is.  A super interesting premise with what starts to looks like a hint of supernatural elements that really get you intrigued.  As you dig into the multiple areas and the events with the characters there, you see tidbits of linking stories and get more explanations as to what you're dealing with, which is what kept me going at the game for such a long time without being able to put it down.  You keep digging deeper and deeper, revealing either more flavor about the world or just dropping more hints about what actually happened, character motivations, etc.  A really good hook if I’ve ever seen one, with some wrid crazy WTF moments along the way that tease a larger payoff once you really find out what’s going on.  This keeps up largely until the last 15-10% of the story, at which point the bulk of the final reveal happens, which imo, falls flat on its face.  To explain why I’d have to go into spoilers, but for the sake of this review, there are many shenanigans that happen that show that people who are supposed to be real smart and morally advanced end up making quite possibly the most dumb and baffling decisions just to lead to a very weird payoff that would defy the game’s own logic if not for one vague side-mention implying that everything that was explained so far might have been wrong.  The whole concept to me of a story where you get told “remember everything we said before, all of it was a lie/wrong/unreliable” can be an interesting premise, but only if it’s done right, and I don’t feel like it’s done right here.  It’s something to have a grand reveal that makes events click and tie together in a wonderful knot, it’s another to have a story just tell you to ignore everything that you thought was true and just “accept this is actually how everything works”.  It cuts off my investment in the story, because as a writer, you’ve shown me that you have no investment in it either.  The rules were changed at the last minute so that you could insert a payoff.  You didn’t do a setup and then a payoff, you nuked the setup from orbit and then handed me a built a payoff on the ruins of what was left.  And this is how this game felt.  The very last minute pivot at the end of the game, from the annoyingly slow resource gain, the incredible amount of time it suddenly takes to complete locations, and the extremely disjointed story ending make me not recommend this game to someone who would really be invested in the story.  While not as colossal of a failure as that, I can’t help to compare this with why I wouldn’t recommend someone who’s never seen it to watch Game of Thrones, no matter how good the first several seasons are.  They’ll inevitably end up wanted to see it to the end and get disappointed.

I am currently about to 100% this game within the next half hour. I have enjoyed the vast majority of it, but there are 2 issues with the game that I want to talk about that, if fixed, would turn this into an absolute recommend. As it is, I leave feeling honestly unsatisfied.

First off, and most important, the ending legitimately doesn't make sense. I won't get into details on this front, but the ending breaks the world building it had just established. It breaks the world building severely enough that it honestly brought this game from a recommend to a not recommend. The ending is not satisfying and offers more questions than it answers, but none of the questions are interesting and satisfying. It just seems as though the story writer(s) weren't sure how to get the outcome they wanted and just wrote in the first thing they thought of.

Secondly, the perk systems need some work. The religion tree being as expansive as it is means that it takes a lot of time and some of its bonuses tend to get useless the further on in the game you get. If you get a bonus to science after the science tree is done, that doesn't help, but you need it for 100% completion. Since the perk effects aren't shown beforehand (which is fine), it's hard to fast track to stuff that would make the grind of it quicker. I personally would probably drop the costs of this since, ideally, you would like to get the bonuses before the game is already over. I would also rebalance the trees to come closer to 1:1:1 perk ration instead of 1:1:2. As it is, I'm currently half an hour away from 100% solely due to the religion tree. I am waiting on 1 perk to finish, I'm running the game on 4x speed because there is nothing else of interest to do, but I'm so close to 100% that I don't want to quit.

Both of these issues can absolutely be fixed. As is, I think they ruin what was previously an absolutely wonderful game.

Yet another case of wishing that Steam had a "Maybe" option.  The drawback of this game is strangely enough, its main draw: The story.  I applaud what they tried to do, and I was engaged at the beginning, but the pacing is completely off.  Especially as you tech up, new events start flying at you and every new beat starts to feel like a item to check off the list.

I feel like this game could have had half the content, a tighter focus on a few main threads, it could say the same things, and it be better off for it.

There's an attempt here at a storytelling game, but the gameplay is so disconnected from the main story and so nonsensical that it detracts from the experience. The sheriff builds a gas station for some reason, where the gas station attendant (who we met before the gas station was built) can be put to work spending some money that the mayor scavenged from the streets to buy meat and oil. The mayor can also worship in order to increase everyone's scavenging skill. None of this is referenced in the story, it's just a bunch of random events and tasks happening in parallel while the story is concerned with a murder. Characters keep working after they die, by the way.

If the idea was to make a story driven game and have idle game mechanics to space out the story plot points, then make the things we do in the idle game actually reflect what's going on in the story. Ideally, also design the idle game mechanics to be more satisfying and less of a menu jumping micromanagement hassle.

Decent enough idler, but I sort of felt railroaded into making certain decisions. Writing was very self-indulgent, but the narrative kept me going.

Speaking of the narrative, I was 92% of the way through the game, then when I loaded it, intending to finish, my save was completely wiped and I was all the way at zero. Oh well. Thumbs-sideways if that hadn't have happened, but it will be a thumbs down for me right now.

[b]TL:DR: Soft Do Not Recommend. It does some interesting things but there’s enough holding it back that it’s not one I’d necessarily recommend unless it’s something you know will match your tastes. [/b]

[i]This review will avoid spoilers up until a marked section below. [/i]

Asbury Pines is … interesting, to the point it’s making me write a review. At it’s core it’s an idle/incremental narrative game, trying to tell a story across a few hundred years. It’s highs are high, some reveals are genuinely great, and especially by genre standards, it’s a very quick experience. Despite this however, it’s sometimes a slog, and a lot of the story feels undercooked - which is killer when that’s really the only thing the game has going for it. 

[h3] GRAPHICS & SOUND [/h3]

Graphics are straightforward (beyond accusations of AI use, but that’s a whole other can of worms), the soundtrack is nice for an idler but will inevitably get muted. Nothing to write home about, it does the job it has to. 

[h3]GAMEPLAY[/h3]

Gameplay consists of unlocking new characters, assigning them to either a) creating resources, b) turning resources into different resources (2 tiers), c) “living”, thereby advancing the story and unlocking new options or characters or d) working on long-term (up to ~20 hours) upgrades. This system essentially creates a few time gates, where you’re waiting to amass enough resources to advance the next story. 

And that’s about it. There’s a few moments where it’s used nicely, but for the most part there aren’t really any interesting choices, as the assigning and the resources are too limited to really create them, and if you do actually idle, you get up to 12 hours of offline progress, which negates any need to balance resources from that point onwards. For better or for worse, the game also isn’t very much of an idle game. I finished the game’s main story in about 15 hours ingame time, reaching 100% after 18, and finishing the final grind achievement after 20. Real-time this was about 4-5 days of on-and-off play. 

This relatively quick pace does help the story not drag on too much, with you regularly unlocking new stories and learning how it all ties together, but it also means that it’s a pretty short experience overall. The UI has some nice QoL (and they’re adding more throughout early access), and while there’s a few things that aren’t perfectly clear, it does what it needs to. 


[h3]STORY - LIMITED SPOILERS[/h3]

If you’ve read the store page, you’ll have learned that this is a game about “unveil[ing] a murder mystery across the small town’s history – from the late stone age to the deep future” and that’s accurate. As you play through the game, you follow individual characters throughout key events in their lives, represented by journal entries, conversations, posters, and so on, in order to understand how it all leads up to - and is affected by - a murder. When the game uses this idea to it’s full strength, in the first third of the game and the finale, it is pretty strong: seeing how choices a character made 30 years ago have an impact now and wondering how things could have been different is great.

The rest of the time, however, it just kind of drags on, touching on a lot of ideas but not really exploring them, leading to a slog where the story kind of meanders around - accompanied by more and more typos and weird storytelling decisions. It feels like there’s a lot of missing depth once you leave the starting story block, and instead of deep, interconnected stories, the game kind of rushes you through a few hundred years of history, all the while repeating a few story beats over and over. Perhaps it’s a narrative choice to try and tell something about the human condition and the nature of society, but it leads to a lot of predictable and unfulfilling storytelling. Notably, one of these recurring elements the game uses to establish the awfulness of some situations are mentions of sexual assault/coersion which I personally do not think do anything positive for the game or it’s narrative (but this is very much a personal dislike I’ll admit). 

Nevertheless, it’s quite interesting to see how the developers manage to pull off some of the storytelling and the interconnected nature of the story, so if you’re fine with the weaknesses I’ve listed here, it may still be worth checking out.

[h3]STORY - SPOILERS[/h3]

[spoiler]Asbury Pines is not really a small town mystery story; it’s three flavours of undercooked post apocalypse story with a prelude, leading to a sci-fi time travel story that at very few points is a mystery. And that’s the game’s big weakness.

The first act, where you’re getting to explore the town and get to know the characters is great and opens a lot of interesting threads. Then, boom, Apocalypse. Fine, seeing how the characters do and don’t cope with that is still interesting. Then, boom, now it’s Waterworld, and also all the characters you knew from the past few hours are gone (albeit in a great climax!). The character that seems to be the new protagonist gets unceremoniously murdered offscreen (which again, could be commentary but just felt unfulfilling), and then, in what was the closest I ever came to quitting my playthrough, the game jumps the shark: The Snail Age. Suddenly it’s a post-apoc fantasy story where people ride giant snakes, greet each other using the phrase “Sn-hail!” and … yeah.

All the while the timelines extends backwards too, where there’s a few interesting if very undercooked developments. These are more grounded in the story of the first part of the game, have direct influences to it, and are generally quite a bit better/more to my taste and expectation. They feel similarly rushed, but since they are working towards a modern-day setting, feel much better. 

At this point we move into the third act, where the game remembers it has teased time travel and does some basic stuff with it. Some of the mysteries which have been driving the story’s developments are explained, new ones appear, and the game turns into a full-on sci-fi time travel story, with both some amazing reveals, and a lot of pretty predictable tropes. Because early-on we had an Acid-fuelled ghostly apparition, that also means that in thise otherwise fairly grounded story (with the only “true” supernatural element being a time travel stone) there’s suddenly a capital-G Ghost now! This raises quite a few implications that are never addressed or discussed, he’s just there, does the thing he needs to do so an earlier storybeat happens, and disappears. The ending is very trope-y too. Once you realise what kind of story this is, it’s not really a surprise or reveal, beyond maybe the exact path we get to the situation. As some people have pointed out in discussions here, it’s also a plothole about how time travel works, and creates unanswered questions about the setting’s assumptions

From the moment the game goes beyond the initial apocalypse, there is a strange feeling of things being rushed, underdeveloped, and at the same time too spread out. I’m not sure if it’s part of the development process, ideas changing during the development, or just the way the game always was intended, but while the interconnected story about the town of Asbury Pines and the Praetis family is great, most things tangential to it aren’t. Much like the game’s timetravel, things are there because they have to be there, not because they are integral to the story being told. I feel like a game which had taken a smaller scope - say the 100 years before and 20 years after the murder, but gone much more indepth (so what’s the cult actually about? How did Cal’s criminal empire work? ) would have led to a more coherent and satisfying result - and also wouldn’t have needed undercooked time travel. But that’s just my opinion and I don’t have any published games to my name, so take it with a mountain of salt big enough to wipe out the snail age. [/spoiler]
