I was looking forward to this game, but it fell short for me. I love murder mysteries and incremental games. This personally did not hit the mark for either, but I know it did for others. This did not give me the satisfaction of an incremental game and absolutely did not feel like automation/factory gameplay like it says in the description. They have stated that they only used ai art for concept generation and all art was handmade, unfortunately that casts a shadow on all art for me. But the narrative was wild and imaginative, I liked seeing how events were connected, though I personally despise time travel tropes. I like stories where every character and every word and action is important and advances the plot, but I felt this had a lot of filler which I don't like, but others may. As of this early access review there were still multiple typos (even in the screenshots) and characters with their names spelled multiple different ways, so there's definitely still some polishing to do. 

So, not for me. But others may enjoy it.

A game unfortunately held back by the fact it's a game.  Having tried the very surface of it, I thought the writing was pretty good, but the UI remained terrible to navigate through and I hear it gets even worse as the game goes on and more eras are unlocked.

Speaking of, after realizing that any more progress was going to be gated past more grinding that felt excessive even at 4x speed, I stopped playing and started reading reviews of the game that painted a bleak picture for how the story was drip-fed going forward.

"This is an idle game" some of your might say "of course you're going to be gated for content and have to wait."

And that's kind of my point!  This should not have been an idle game.  The game portion of the game is boring, frustrating and only seems to get more boring and frustrating as things go on.  Had this been a short visual novel, I'm sure I would have stuck through it because the writing IS good.  As a game that I'm seeing people with dozens of hours in to finish, though, I don't think the juice is worth the squeeze.

Not my kind of game.  If you want to play a graphic novel soap opera that isn't really idle at all, maybe this is for you.

Neat idea really, so it gets points for that, but just not at all my thing.

This alone is a 10+ hours wall where you have nothing to do but wait. Gameplay needs serious balancing.

I'll try to write simple as I can. The mechanics of this game are all in place to simply unlock a story. There's no building, there's not really any planning, not much thinking involved in it. The progression is simply to progress a story. I don't say that as a criticism, but just to let you know what you're looking for. This could have easily been a short story read in about an hour or two.

So my critique is that core of the game. The 1 to 2 hour short story. To put simply it's bad. It's dull. One can view the political ideology of the person/people writing clearly. What they think is good or bad. How they view history and the present. Their idealized version of the future. You can tell all of that, not because it's written well enough to tell you all of that, but that it's written poorly enough to tell you all of that. 

I won't say that some of the writing wasn't creative. It was. The thing I'll say positive is they kept the language period appropriate "mostly" some things that were said simply wouldn't have been said, but that comes back to the politics I mentioned before. They're too accustom to their poor lexicon one finds in modernity so it got overlooked. 

You've probably seen the troupe used OVER and OVER by these types all over, but of course there's a "woman who is the key to everything" modern gynophilic sentiments are all over. You will find a gay civil war soldier who falls in love with a "savage" and a lesbian who rules the world. 

So based on the poor writing which goes from a mystery to a sci-fi story, that I simply found boring. As a game it's boring, and not worth the hours it takes to "wait" and finish. The reason I've over 60 hours in is simply because you have to let the game run and wait.

So if any of that interests you then get it, but if we're of a similar mind I don't recommend the purchase.
Not my cup of tea. Idled it for some time, tried to follow the stories but not interesting for me.

I found out this game used AI for some (ambiguous portion) of the writing process and it's really bugging me. I read a 3rd-party report that the devs are trying to claim they only used AI for ideas, but I just really would personally prefer not to take that chance and stopped playing it. 

I know not everyone will agree with me on this but that's my personal view on it.

tl;dr; It is tough to downvote because there are many things that are decent and have love put into this game. However to me it is really insufficient as a game and it doesn't convey its narrative well enough as a visual novel. I'll write my review as this game is a hybrid between an incremental game and a visual novel.


Pros:
- Attempt at constructing a storytelling/imaginary through events and milestones
- Generous about assets, characters have different avatar pictures depending their age, narrative assets are well crafted. The representation elements are great.

Cons:
- Some UI features are pretty terrible, the timeline is a bit messy, accessing narative elements gets fast pretty tedious, there are 2 spreadsheets like pages that are tedious to navigate
- There is almost no arbitration/gameplay elements
- The time gates are really arbitrary and do not mix well with a storytelling setting
- I don't understand the technical choices, the game looks web based with an obvious navbar, there is absolutely no animations and it ends up on unity.

At the end I felt I engaged with a 2/10 feature prototype wrapped as a game and a small story/worldbuilding entangled into disjointed ui elements at the same time. I commend the overall effort but it is not good enough as a finished product imho.

I won't change my review at the end of EA but you're free to make a mental diff yourself with future patchnotes. Imho the game is conceptually botched, but if the team persists, the next one may be a banger, there is something there definitely.

Really cool concept. I was so excited about this game. Loved the beginning exploring the story but it got boring at 50%. I thought this game would be deeper than just reading a novel. But that was just it. The writing was poor as well and the story became lamer and lamer as it progressed. UI is also tough cause if you receive a notification I wish you can click it so it can take you where you need to be instead of sifting through all the pages and looking for a red dot. Art style is great though I love the color scheme and illustrations.

I want to leave a positive review because the game has some interesting ideas and I'm happy I bought it just to encourage making more weird ideas like this one, but ultimately it felt kind of confused about what kind of game it was trying to be and wasn't very fun.

There's some sort of like worker placement idea at the surface but it's ultimately kind of irrelevant because forward progress is often locked behind something where the only way to get the required resources is to wait multiple hours.  Then, after the multiple hours, you have so many resources available that any strategizing about placement was irrelevant.  And there's a whole tech tree of upgrades but they come in too slow to really matter beyond one or two; as others mention, you finish the game before even getting half of them.

Then there's this nonlinear story that nicely matches the nonlinear way you can reveal the story by which characters you focus on, sorta, but then many times the story just gets on rails where there's only one or two things you can make progress on.  So the story either comes out in very slow drips, or you let the game idle for a while and suddenly the story jumps confusingly forwards along multiple parallel threads.

And then as the last step in the game as if to reinforce that, a step needs a scrillion resources and again doesn't provide any way to gather them without just waiting some hours, even with the game running at 4x.

They are doing some interesting stuff with narrative for the idle genre.  It's an engaging story and such.  However it's come to my attention that they used AI to assist with their development process, and I can't knowingly recommend the game because of that.  It's sad, because it's obvious the creators are creative enough to have made something great without resorting to such things that promote intellectual property theft.

This is an idler whose focus is story.  Unfortunately, the story is repetitive, shallow and, worst of all for a game founded on a mystery, obvious.  The culprit for the foundational mystery is fairly obvious if you are at all genre savvy and the game mechanics make it even more blatant not too long after introducing the mystery.  The precise How/Why takes quite a bit longer to unfold, but it's not especially satisfying and breaks established rules.  

Getting to the end of the story is interminable.  It consists of a series of sections each a morality play; all similarly structured, taking far to long to solve an obvious mystery.  The prose staggers between pretentious and twee.  The characters are paper thin caricatures with the narrative pointing out how we should feel about them with the subtlety of a thrown brick.  A sense of cynical derision oozes from most of the cast which drags down even the ones the writer wants you to like.

Gameplay is fairly simplistic, plug workers into slots and wait for bottleneck to clear.  Optimizing has minimal impact and there is no real juggling or min/maxxing needed.  Gameplay is supposed to be the focus of the EA so I suppose this is all subject to change.

Gameplay is an interesting mix of idle game and visual novel.

The plot has a time loop.

In the end, the time loop doesn't even loop properly. Pure disappointment.

Finished the game, very close to 100 percent. Typing this out while waiting.

I feel the pacing for the living lives was generally good. I enjoyed the story beats. Many of the stories ended up being contained within themselves and then combined at moments. I felt like some of the links between eras was a little weak....its not immediately obvious what telling buzz about the valuables stash did just as an example. However, there are many strong moments that make up for that easily.

I enjoyed lots of the characters and writing especially the custom documents and handwriting for each character and/or era, that made each one feel different from others...or sometimes the same depending on their relationships. That was EXCELLENT, and probably one of my favorite details about the game.

I feel like the pacing is off for the perks. I completed all people and all eras and I only had 3-4 perks from each tree. I had every work building built and still had 2 perks to help build work stations.

For reference I played fairly actively at 1x speed, checking on the game every 20 mins or so. Early on there is just not that many spare characters to put in perk tasks. I feel like this could be smoothed out by making the early perks be far easier to get but make the skills on earning the perks matter far more (so that later characters REALLY speed up those perks). This would artificially smooth out the curve for multiple playstyles.

Also there does not seem to be much reason to play again. I would have enjoyed something like a new game plus or an alternate ending, or a challenge type playthrough options.

I would have liked more builds mattering for resources. There is really only 2 times where you had to undue certain options to reduce spending a certain resource

I feel like a lot of the animals, workstations or nature spots were unneeded or redundant. While they may be interesting historical or flavor wise, many were mechanically uninteresting.

While I enjoyed the story, I wish there was a bit more mechanically here for me to reccomend the game to others. For the most part with the exception of doing perks, I found the skills of the character not really mattering. Also people and animals were limited in sloting in some areas for seemingly arbitrary reasons. I don't know why some plants can do some nature places and not others....and there is no way to tell other then just click and see.

I guess I was hoping for builds to matter a bit more.

I beat the game in a day and am using 4x speed only to help with the last character action and to 100 percent the perks.


 However, I really hope this game fleshes out its mechanical/depth and adds a new game plus or alternate/challenge mode for me to play it again after it is patched.

Upon further reflection of the ending, I have decided to leave a negative review. While I find the story interesting, I feel the ending kind of violates a lot of the setup the story had going. If I add that to the mechanics not being very deep, I find myself dislikeing the game after thinking about it.

game_name: Magical Mic Duel: Senpai, Hear My Spell
review_text: Niche and odd games like this have uniquely funny charm that can't be replicated by big companies. However, the AI art sucks a lot of that quirky charm out of it very quickly. The changes in art-style and character features when the characters switch poses look very sloppy, and the many of the AI generated images become blurry when the characters are zoomed in on. Its extremely distracting. If you like AI art or can look past it you might enjoy it.

game_name: Coloring Book for Adults 2 - Family Edition
review_text:
[quote][url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AI_slop][b]Warning: AI Slop[/b][/url]
This software application is partially or completely made with talentless, soulless AI generated content.[/quote]

"Coloring Book for Adults 2 - Family Edition" is not a complete product.  It's an advertisement/demo, and you have to pay more money through DLC to actually unlock the software.  What do you get if you let the deceptive and shady marketing win and pay the DLC toll to unlock the software?  You don't get a game, that's for sure, because this is not a game.

Instead, it's a colouring book made up of AI slop images.  Obviously clicking around to "paint" an AI slop image is not the same thing as playing a game.  And it's AI slop.  This is disgusting and should never have been published on Steam.  It's not a game.  It's not even genuinely made... they just stole a colouring book mobile app template and populated it with AI slop.  And they now want $13 for polluting Steam with this garbage..  But remember... you don't get the full game unless you pay up.The purpose of this advertisement is to get you to shell out a whopping $13 USD to unlock content needed for the full game, and I don't recommend you do that either.

On pricing and value for gamers, this AI slop isn't really free as you're expected to pay up $13 USD to unlock all the AI slop, so this product should not be considered completely unlocked until you empty your wallet for the developer.  This is misleading and predatory pricing on a product falsely marketed as "free".

As an advertisement, this is only free in the same way that adverts on Youtube and banner ads in your browser are.  This is a demo that's been dumped on Steam to advertise another Steam store page in the hopes of raking in some cash.  Just because advertisements are free, doesn't mean they're worth your time.

It's important to understand there's a formal process to create a demo/page for a game which hasn't been followed here:

https://partner.steamgames.com/doc/store/application/demos

While it might seem like an inocuous mistake, publishing demos and advertisements as if they are full games is misleading and in some ways harmful.  This advertisement is taking up marketing space and visibility that would be better made available to a developer that's done the right thing and created a full game.  Instead, that visibility is given over to a demo/advert that's been published incorrectly and is polluting the marketplace.

It's unclear if this mistake was made through incompetence or a deliberate intent to mislead gamers.  Often developers may choose to incorrectly publish demo/advertisements as separate games as a cheap way to bypass paying Valve for marketing presence on the Steam front page, which may seem clever to them, but it's harmful behaviour and it's not good for gamers.

Even after that, this is only free in a rudimentary sense... the developer has their hand out a $13 USD "donation" DLC for this as you're expected to buy extra DLC from Steam to "support" the developer... digital panhandling.  Like the homeless guys who splash dirty water on your windscreen at the traffic lights.  No thanks.

Despite having no up-front cost, this failed to appeal to the 120 million+ gamers on Steam.  SteamDB showed the game peaked at less than 100 concurrent players, they couldn't even break three figures, and now there's basically nobody playing it.  Even though it's free.  This is a dismal public reception, but it's easy to see why gamers rejected it.

This product features a number of fake positive reviews from accounts known to be in the business of review manipulation/paid reviews.  They're all written in Chinese or broken English, appear at almost exactly the same time, very shortly after the product launched on Steam, despite the woefully low quality of this AI slop.  The "Positive" review score on this should be disregarded due to this blatant, unethical review manipulation.  This is done to deceive and scam gamers into paying for a bad product.

Due to the 2024 FTC ruling (16 CFR Part 465), these fake reviews are a violation of US Federal Law.[/quote]

Advertisements and incorrectly published demos don't add +1 to your game collector count, they aren't full games.  It's impossible for me to recommend this incorrectly published advertising spam to gamers, you deserve better than adverts.

It's well made, and I enjoyed it, but I don't think it has enough substance for full price.

The gameplay feels like Orwell in a way. The narrative is good, though your choices don't really matter, and it's way too short. Like a prologue to a bigger story. It ends right as things start getting interesting, and the ending itself feels like a second Act was starting before the rest of the game got cut.

It's not a bad game, but I completed it feeling uncertain that I'd got my money's worth, even at the discounted price. If steam had an option between recommending and not I'd select that instead.

EDIT: I've been thinking about this a lot, and I just wanted to add one more thing. You can see how I go into quite a bit of initial, just-coming-down-off-finishing-the-game detail down below if you'd like, but I'd like to quickly say here, at the start, that I think a big part of the issue was a fundamental misunderstanding of what this game was. I went into this expecting a puzzle game, but it isn't so much a puzzle game as it is a story game.
I feel it's advertised as more of a puzzle game with subtle hints at an overarching story you might be able to uncover, and indeed it does *play* like a puzzle game... until it very much becomes about following the narrative.
My feelings below still stand - including the ones after my spoiler tags - but while I don't know that I would've felt *differently* if I'd gone into this expecting something different, much of my confusion and disappointment might've been at least softened.

ORIGINAL REVIEW:
Overall, I was pretty disappointed with this game.
First, it was incredibly short - I had the game open for 7-ish hours... but I had dinner, fed the dogs, took a shower, yada yada, with it paused, so I'm not actually sure how long it really took. Most of the puzzles themselves weren't particularly challenging - though the mechanics were satisfying in a point-and-click, ASMR kind of way - and then one or two of the puzzles were SUPER, out-of-pocket complicated, insanely difficult, and totally out of place?? And because of the length of the game, I felt like all the mechanics I was learning, I really didn't need for very long, or often even more than once.
The writing, acting, sound design, and off-screen auditory storytelling were beautiful. Very well done; you can tell a lot of love went into it, there just... wasn't a whole lot of content.

I saw someone mention that if there were an "operator mode," that would make up for a lot of this problem, and I agree. *MILD SPOILER* Every single one of the cases were directly linked to the main story, and I think being able to enjoy some of the mechanics for longer would make it feel a little more worthwhile. Again, many of the puzzles were fairly simple, but it was still fun and satisfying in a way, and then it would also be more impactful - learning how to do the more complicated puzzles - because you'd have to use that knowledge again.

**SPOILERS FOR THE END**

The ending was incredibly dissatisfying to me. I immediately got off and looked to see if I somehow got "the bad ending" and there was another way I could've played to get "the good ending," but no. It is what it is - one and done. (I saw someone else say that if there HAD been multiple endings, that would also help with the overarching "the game is too short" issue everyone seems to be having, and I agree with that, as well.)
Some people liked the ending, but a lot of people shared the same feelings I have: if I uncover the mystery, am taking notes on the story and correctly piecing together the clues, I want to feel like I 'won' the video game... at least to a degree. But (again, **HUGE SPOILER AHEAD**) it literally doesn't matter at all. Nothing you did, none of the choices you made, mattered. The main character you played dies, every SINGLE one of your contacts/friends dies or goes missing (you cannot save them, it does not matter, and you're informed of their demise in during-credits news clippings, no less), and the only one to escape is HAL, and they're still trying to take down the big bad 30 years later (which is what fans of the ending consider the "silver lining.")
I love when games do some out-of-the-box stuff, but the whole game sets you up like you're uncovering a mystery, exposing a story, and then in the end literally not a lick of it mattered (except that HAL escaped and is still trying, which as far as I can tell, happens no matter what you, as the player, do.) It just felt very forced, and pretty pointless.
Disappointing, because again, some of the mechanics were really neat, and you can tell a lot of love was put into the storytelling aspects.
This game is wayy too short in duration for the price.  It felt like a quarter of the game was spent between days and you just had to sit there and ride out the "cut scenes".

I am kinda torn about this game since what is there is not necessarily bad  but ... people should know what they are getting into.

First of the game is pretty short about 3 to maybe 4 hours of gameplay depending on if you struggle with any puzzle.

Second: the game is not really a puzzle or detective game but more like a guided narrative. The puzzles in the game are not very difficult and the game mostly handholds you through the solutions. Its sad because the system that are there could have been incredible for solving more complicated cases but unfortunately the entire game is basically a tutorial where people always tell you what to do and there is very little to figure out.

Next the story: Unfortunately if you pay attention to the clues the game gives you you will figure out 90% of the plotbeats before the game tells you and its pretty akward when the game treats something as a relvelation you basically knew for quite a while already.

The story is also linear it might seem you can pick sides but ultimately there is only one direction to go. Another thing is that the ending  is ... well its difficult to say much without spoiling but its pretty dark and for me put a pretty big damper on everything that happened.

I would not say the game is worth full price to be honest maybe on a deep discount. Only buy if you are looking for an interactive story more than a detective/puzzle game.

I would classify this as a short narrative game with very light puzzles.

PUZZLES
A majority of the puzzles are signposted and obvious, and they all give a tutorial vibe. The game will teach you how to solve a particular type of puzzle, but then that type of puzzle is never visited again.

There were two puzzles that weren't, but these felt frustrating as:
- the first one had VERY confusing wording and used terms that were not explained ("tickable" being the word I got stuck on)
- the second one you needed to brute force a solution, which is never fun nor rewarding

NARRATIVE
For a mystery game, the final twist was basically shouted at the beginning of the game and I guessed it within the first 5 minutes. All of the cases pertain to the main mystery (no side missions to divert your attention). When you know the secret right away, it's hard to feel motivated to continue playing.

In the end, I felt it was a shallow, predictable, and simple game. It has good bones, but needed a lot more time in the oven

If a sequel does come along, or this team makes a similar game, I hope they:
- Include more puzzles
- Not make the ending obvious
- Playtest all the puzzles to make sure that things are easily understood and enjoyable (without losing the theme)
- Add in some red herrings/busywork 
- Show off their systems! Similar to a Hypnospace Outlaw or The Roottrees are Dead, have us use the mechanics more than once before moving on to a completely different thing. I would have loved to do more searching, scrubbing, etc in the game but didn't have enough opportunities outside each mechanic's single puzzle.

This game had a lot of potential to be a technology-driven Papers Please, but it ends up as more of a visual novel short story than a full detective game. Definitely only get it on sale and/or part of a bundle of other games.
Seriously disappointed there's not actually any reason to make choices or meaningful extra answers you can glean outside of the scope of the storyline's rails. Half of the playtime is tutorials for mechanics you use a whopping 5 times meaningfully before the game is done. I understand games with voice acting and ANY form of multiple choice need a decent budget but the playtime is just not long enough to justify getting the game yourself at full price. ESPECIALLY in this economy.
If we're lucky, The Operator 2 will come out and make this pricetag worth it, and fufill the potential that's been shown on this basically-a-proof-of-concept of a game.

First of all, let me say that this is a good final product if you see it as a mildly interactive visual novel.

The art direction is great, with the soundscape, voice acting, graphics and beats creating the right atmosphere for a conspiracy thriller.

The problem with it is that it's not a Game. It's a linear railroad that drives you from action to action where you can perform simple, easy puzzles. You don't have any agency, and in most places there is no possibility for branching the story.

Even with a fixed outcome, the developers could have done more with script to give some illusion of agency. The most annoying part is that for every dialog, no matter what is your choice, the outcome of that interaction always the same.

So, play it if you want to watch a conspiracy story with some CSI-type procedural puzzles in the mix.

Avoid if you want to play a game with a minimum of agency.

I absolutely love this game’s design and idea but sadly I don't think it was executed very well.

Rather than feeling in control of the narrative, most of the game was spent watching the story unfold with little player-contribution. There were different options to choose during conversations, which would somewhat influence the story plot-wise, but given that it's a mystery game and the choice of options available were very limited and surface-level (e.g. will you lie or not lie), replaying it to see what else (if anything) could have happened doesn't feel worth my time.

The story had some small unexpected and exciting twists, however most of these developments were overshadowed by the main plot being very predictable. I think if they had delved more into the individual cases within the game itself and focused less on forming an overarching plot, it would have been much more enjoyable.

The nature of the game put it in a great position to deliver really varied and unique puzzles (e.g. code breaking, crime solving, hacking -- all of which were very fun), yet the quantity of puzzles was seriously lacking. It felt like maybe only an hour out of the 3.5 hours of time spent playing was actually focused on problem-solving and the rest was in tedious over-explanatory conversation. 

I understand this is tagged with visual novel and so maybe the developers intended it for it to be more of a narrative-based experience, but if that is the reason for the lack of puzzles, I would have expected the plot to be more developed and have less of an obvious ending.

I enjoyed this game, but it was just too short to justify the cost (4 hours for >$10 on sale). It also sort of feels like the ending you get is "the bad ending," even though it's the only one possible. There is, I believe, just one event where the player actually influences the outcome.

Most of the puzzles themselves weren't particularly challenging - though the mechanics were satisfying in a point-and-click, ASMR kind of way - and then one or two of the puzzles were SUPER, out-of-pocket complicated, insanely difficult, and totally out of place?? And because of the length of the game, I felt like all the mechanics I was learning, I really didn't need for very long, or often even more than once.
The writing, acting, sound design, and off-screen auditory storytelling were beautiful. Very well done; you can tell a lot of love went into it, there just... wasn't a whole lot of content.

I saw someone mention that if there were an "operator mode," that would make up for a lot of this problem, and I agree. *MILD SPOILER* Every single one of the cases were directly linked to the main story, and I think being able to enjoy some of the mechanics for longer would make it feel a little more worthwhile. Again, many of the puzzles were fairly simple, but it was still fun and satisfying in a way, and then it would also be more impactful - learning how to do the more complicated puzzles - because you'd have to use that knowledge again.

**SPOILERS FOR THE END**

The ending was incredibly dissatisfying to me. I immediately got off and looked to see if I somehow got "the bad ending" and there was another way I could've played to get "the good ending," but no. It is what it is - one and done. (I saw someone else say that if there HAD been multiple endings, that would also help with the overarching "the game is too short" issue everyone seems to be having, and I agree with that, as well.)
Some people liked the ending, but a lot of people shared the same feelings I have: if I uncover the mystery, am taking notes on the story and correctly piecing together the clues, I want to feel like I 'won' the video game... at least to a degree. But (again, **HUGE SPOILER AHEAD**) it literally doesn't matter at all. Nothing you did, none of the choices you made, mattered. The main character you played dies, every SINGLE one of your contacts/friends dies or goes missing (you cannot save them, it does not matter, and you're informed of their demise in during-credits news clippings, no less), and the only one to escape is HAL, and they're still trying to take down the big bad 30 years later (which is what fans of the ending consider the "silver lining.")
I love when games do some out-of-the-box stuff, but the whole game sets you up like you're uncovering a mystery, exposing a story, and then in the end literally not a lick of it mattered (except that HAL escaped and is still trying, which as far as I can tell, happens no matter what you, as the player, do.) It just felt very forced, and pretty pointless.
Disappointing, because again, some of the mechanics were really neat, and you can tell a lot of love was put into the storytelling aspects.
This game is wayy too short in duration for the price.  It felt like a quarter of the game was spent between days and you just had to sit there and ride out the "cut scenes".

Game is good, ngl. The storyline, voice acting etc. BUT IT'S DAMN WAY TOO SHORT! The puzzles are quite easy too. I will give this game an 8/10, but i won't recommend it because just like i said, the gameplay's too short. It's not worth at full price, even 35% price discount. It felt like a cashgrab, sorry. For those interested in buying this game. It's a very good, interesting, and innovative game, but I can't recommend it due to its exorbitant price.

Maybe in the US or other parts of the world, someone is willing to pay that price for a product like this, but I'm sorry, this game is incredibly expensive. Dividing the price by the number of hours it takes to complete it (3 hours) comes out to more than Baldur's Gate 3 or Cyberpunk 2077. Furthermore, of those 3 hours, the gameplay itself takes, maybe, a maximum of 2 hours. Maybe.

While the idea and execution are great, the product itself is very overpriced.

Furthermore, I don't understand the point of having "cinematic" cutscenes with sound between each day's work. A blackout and a new day were enough, and now we get something that wastes our time, and the developers had to deal with it and charge for it, which is ultimately what we, the players, pay for. Once, such a cutscene had its justification, but it could have easily been omitted.

EDIT:
I played this game twice to earn the achievements. While the first attempt was phenomenal, the second is terrible and makes no sense. So, it's a 2-3 hour game for a first timer. Furthermore, it turns out that what we say/choices basically don't matter. It's a bummer.

You can listen to them dialogs for half an hour, then quit after a - what feels like - important milestone, and find yourself next day starting "from scratch". Was it too bothersome to at least explain how the saves work? Not to mention enable frequent autosaves, as required in modern day and age.

I am kinda torn about this game since what is there is not necessarily bad  but ... people should know what they are getting into.

First of the game is pretty short about 3 to maybe 4 hours of gameplay depending on if you struggle with any puzzle.

Second: the game is not really a puzzle or detective game but more like a guided narrative. The puzzles in the game are not very difficult and the game mostly handholds you through the solutions. Its sad because the system that are there could have been incredible for solving more complicated cases but unfortunately the entire game is basically a tutorial where people always tell you what to do and there is very little to figure out.

Next the story: Unfortunately if you pay attention to the clues the game gives you you will figure out 90% of the plotbeats before the game tells you and its pretty akward when the game treats something as a relvelation you basically knew for quite a while already.

The story is also linear it might seem you can pick sides but ultimately there is only one direction to go. Another thing is that the ending  is ... well its difficult to say much without spoiling but its pretty dark and for me put a pretty big damper on everything that happened.

I would not say the game is worth full price to be honest maybe on a deep discount. Only buy if you are looking for an interactive story more than a detective/puzzle game.

The Operator is a 5/10 for me. I just didn’t like it, and the ending honestly pissed me off. It felt dumb. I went into this game thinking I’d be connecting the dots, using my brain, cracking a case, or at least acting like the man in the chair. I expected some detective-style depth, and yeah, I knew there’d be a story, but not this kind of story.

Gameplay & Expectations
I watched the trailer, played the demo, and at first I was excited. The gameplay itself was fun for what it was, and I thought it would build into something bigger. Instead, it just… ended. The ending was surprising, sure, but it wasn’t good. It left me feeling like all that build-up led nowhere.

Replay Value
There’s barely any replay value. No unlimited mode, no side content, nothing to keep you going after finishing the story. And if I remember correctly, there’s not much you can do to actually change the ending, so I had zero motivation to replay it.

Final Thoughts
It’s a fun little game on sale, but I’d recommend watching a video and playing the demo before you consider buying it. Once I beat it, I felt completely done with

I would classify this as a short narrative game with very light puzzles.

PUZZLES
A majority of the puzzles are signposted and obvious, and they all give a tutorial vibe. The game will teach you how to solve a particular type of puzzle, but then that type of puzzle is never visited again.

There were two puzzles that weren't, but these felt frustrating as:
- the first one had VERY confusing wording and used terms that were not explained ("tickable" being the word I got stuck on)
- the second one you needed to brute force a solution, which is never fun nor rewarding

NARRATIVE
For a mystery game, the final twist was basically shouted at the beginning of the game and I guessed it within the first 5 minutes. All of the cases pertain to the main mystery (no side missions to divert your attention). When you know the secret right away, it's hard to feel motivated to continue playing.

In the end, I felt it was a shallow, predictable, and simple game. It has good bones, but needed a lot more time in the oven

If a sequel does come along, or this team makes a similar game, I hope they:
- Include more puzzles
- Not make the ending obvious
- Playtest all the puzzles to make sure that things are easily understood and enjoyable (without losing the theme)
- Add in some red herrings/busywork 
- Show off their systems! Similar to a Hypnospace Outlaw or The Roottrees are Dead, have us use the mechanics more than once before moving on to a completely different thing. I would have loved to do more searching, scrubbing, etc in the game but didn't have enough opportunities outside each mechanic's single puzzle.

This game had a lot of potential to be a technology-driven Papers Please, but it ends up as more of a visual novel short story than a full detective game. Definitely only get it on sale and/or part of a bundle of other games.

I understand games with voice acting and ANY form of multiple choice need a decent budget but the playtime is just not long enough to justify getting the game yourself at full price. ESPECIALLY in this economy.
If we're lucky, The Operator 2 will come out and make this pricetag worth it, and fufill the potential that's been shown on this basically-a-proof-of-concept of a game.

I wanted to love this game but I just cannot recommend you spend money on what is ultimately a disappointing experience. 

The game has a great concept with a strong atmosphere, and what I hoped was great potential but the overall experience ends up feeling disappointingly thin. Reading reviews and looking at the blurb I felt that I was promised tension, deduction, and deeper problem-solving. Unfortunately, it never fully delivers on that potential.

The biggest issue is simply its length. Just as the mechanics start to click and the world begins to feel intriguing, the game is over. It ends before it can build momentum or explore its own ideas in any meaningful way. What could have been a gripping investigation feels more like the prologue to a larger game that never arrives.

The story also ends up predictable. Key beats begin to telegraph themselves early, and the ending lands exactly where you expect it to. Without twists, red herrings, or layers to peel back, the narrative lacks the depth needed to keep you engaged or surprised.

The investigations themselves feel shallow as well. Each case presents a problem, but they’re often solved with just a few simple steps. There’s rarely a need to dig deeper, only once or twice was I cross-referencing clues, or genuinely thinking like an investigator. Instead of complex puzzles or branching deductions, you get straightforward tasks that resolve too quickly to be satisfying.

Overall, The Operator is an interesting idea wrapped in a very short, very predictable package. If you’re looking for a brief atmospheric diversion, it works—but if you want a substantial investigative experience, this one doesn’t reach far enough.

The only thing puzzling about “The Operator” is why it has so many positive reviews. It’s too simple to deserve the “Investigation” or “Detective” tags. At heart, it’s an atmospheric, highly polished point-and-click adventure, closer to a children’s interactive TV show than an actual investigation puzzle game.

It gets slightly interesting during the [spoiler]bomb-defusing segment[/spoiler], but, like everything else in this game, that’s just an exercise in following instructions rather than piecing together clues. Nothing in this game justifies the admission price, don't recommend.

An amazing game with deep plot, mostly unexpected plot twists and an amazing style, ruined by only one terrible ending, unskippable end-of-day cutscenes, and NO SAVING.

They build up such good tension, make you feel like you have a choice just to rip it all away in one lame ending where basically, "bad guy wins, your friends all die." Don't bother getting invested in this wonderful story, because the ending undoes all of what they built.

Also the game is literally only 3 hours, just enough to not refund. and for $20?!? This is a game I'd say is worth 5 dollars if you even liked it. Only get on steep discount, and if you hate yourself.

First of all, let me say that this is a good final product if you see it as a mildly interactive visual novel.

The art direction is great, with the soundscape, voice acting, graphics and beats creating the right atmosphere for a conspiracy thriller.

The problem with it is that it's not a Game. It's a linear railroad that drives you from action to action where you can perform simple, easy puzzles. You don't have any agency, and in most places there is no possibility for branching the story.

Even with a fixed outcome, the developers could have done more with script to give some illusion of agency. The most annoying part is that for every dialog, no matter what is your choice, the outcome of that interaction always the same.

So, play it if you want to watch a conspiracy story with some CSI-type procedural puzzles in the mix.

Avoid if you want to play a game with a minimum of agency.

This was such a frustrating experience. 
The core gameplay, working as an FDI operator, is very engaging and interesting. Solving the cases, using the tools at your disposal and communicating with agents voiced by real actors was great. I could have done that for hours on end.
The story, however, was abysmal. Riddled with clichés and TV tropes, easy to figure out from the introductory sequence and you felt railroaded down the storyline at every turn with your choices hardly mattering.
Worst of all, after 2-3 hours of playtime (right outside the refund window) the game just ends. Just as the world seemed to be opening up, it just ends.

You can feel the amount of work put into this, but its simply not worth the price to play what felt to be a prologue to a real game. 

Its a massive disappointment to not get to experience the game that this should have been :(

This is the game whose premise seemed like the most exciting thing I could possibly imagine. Creative, promising controlled chaos in the sandbox. Unfortunately, it does not really translate into the game.

Credit where credit is due, I have not found anything I would call a game breaking bug, and it ran smoothly with no hiccups. It is clean in that aspect. There is a certain vibe to it at the beginning, but gets progressively lost as you go through the game. Off the top of my head, this is the list:
[list]
    [*] The driving system is abysmal. I am pressing slightly on my key and the car is already drifting. One time it just spun full circle while I was simply trying to make a turn down the road. I can't imagine this system would be playable for someone who uses a controller.
    [*] The NPCs. You get to ask everyone the same two questions and their answers never change. Their routing system is not fully fleshed out: not only they collide with each other and are unable to work until one pushes through, but if they are in a car? oh boy. I watched the guy driving the trash collector ram into the supermarket for two in-game hours because that was clearly the appropriate route. The police car stops, the guy gets out, stands for five minutes and gets back in.
    [*] The flashlight is the definition of go on girl, give us nothing. It has zero effect unless, perhaps, you're up close and personal with an object. Headlights are the same, they do not make the road much more visible.
    [*] Time is organised in a non-consistent way. You can send the character to bed at 8am and they will wake up at 4pm, or maybe at 1pm. But send your character to sleep at 8:30pm? Yeah, no, you're still waking up at 1pm. Sleeping is your checkpoint and save, but, thing is, you don't really gain anything by having checkpoints. Maybe if you start killing the npcs, then you would revert to checkpoint, but otherwise there didn't seem to be much use.
    [*] The delivery system is aight. Some objects are way too big for the car, which could be a funny in-game moment, if only the driving system was not working against you.
    [*] The clues collection is pretty much mostly just snapping pictures. Then you open the computer, click on the picture you took and it produces more information that you somehow found. Then you pin it on the board and... the game automatically links everything for you. Even tells you the probability of being correct about a suspect. Thinking is not really a requirement. This is the game whose premise seemed like the most exciting thing I could possibly imagine. Creative, promising controlled chaos in the sandbox. Unfortunately, it does not really translate into the game.

Credit where credit is due, I have not found anything I would call a game breaking bug, and it ran smoothly with no hiccups. It is clean in that aspect. There is a certain vibe to it at the beginning, but gets progressively lost as you go through the game. Off the top of my head, this is the list:
[list]
    [*] The driving system is abysmal. I am pressing slightly on my key and the car is already drifting. One time it just spun full circle while I was simply trying to make a turn down the road. I can't imagine this system would be playable for someone who uses a controller.
    [*] The NPCs. You get to ask everyone the same two questions and their answers never change. Their routing system is not fully fleshed out: not only they collide with each other and are unable to work until one pushes through, but if they are in a car? oh boy. I watched the guy driving the trash collector ram into the supermarket for two in-game hours because that was clearly the appropriate route. The police car stops, the guy gets out, stands for five minutes and gets back in.
    [*] The flashlight is the definition of go on girl, give us nothing. It has zero effect unless, perhaps, you're up close and personal with an object. Headlights are the same, they do not make the road much more visible.
    [*] Time is organised in a non-consistent way. You can send the character to bed at 8am and they will wake up at 4pm, or maybe at 1pm. But send your character to sleep at 8:30pm? Yeah, no, you're still waking up at 1pm. Sleeping is your checkpoint and save, but, thing is, you don't really gain anything by having checkpoints. Maybe if you start killing the npcs, then you would revert to checkpoint, but otherwise there didn't seem to be much use.
    [*] The delivery system is aight. Some objects are way too big for the car, which could be a funny in-game moment, if only the driving system was not working against you.
    [*] The clues collection is pretty much mostly just snapping pictures. Then you open the computer, click on the picture you took and it produces more information that you somehow found. Then you pin it on the board and... the game automatically links everything for you. Even tells you the probability of being correct about a suspect. Thinking is not really a requirement. Also, they really hammer it in that you have to bring information for the club meeting, so when it takes place, what happens? Correct. No one even asks you about the information, just gives you more leads.
[/list]

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
I'm a big fan of the Asian Horror genre and Junji Ito, but this game was -not- it. 

They basically took Junji Ito's name, some of his art/characters, and slapped in on a box for money. 

I was so excited to play this, and typically I don't mind cash grabs because I have oodles of cash. But! The story was awful, the mechanics were clunky, and I was wholly bored the entire time. 

This game can easily be summed up in one word -- Disappointing. Everything about it let me down. Bummer.

If you have somehow decided that you can't live without this game, I would buy it on a 75% off sale. Oof. </3

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text: this game is... not good. I already had it in mind before playing that it would most likely be a mid horror game but hopefully still be a somewhat enjoyable experience as a huge junji ito fan, however the only good aspect of it was tomie. the enemies are repetitive and easy to counter, the animations are jank, and the story is just mediocre - to top it all off, the game includes AI-generated art which is just ironic as it's a game inspired by a real artist and it's incredibly disrespectful. if you want this game just for more junji ito content, I recommend buying it at a discount like I thankfully did

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
Insta-bought because I loved Junji Ito's works.

However, this is a MASSIVE LETDOWN on all aspects.
- The story is loosely told, uninspired, and so edgy - it's like a teen wrote it at some points. I especially hate how unlikable the characters are.
- Virtually nothing scary in this game: monsters, environment, sound design are all so sadly executed
- I could find better gameplay in Roblox horror games

I see no Junji Ito in this game, nor his mangas. It's just a quick cash grab capitalizing on his name, sadly.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
It is so weird that despite being based on the work of a renown visual artist, the game uses AI for a lot of the paintings found in game. It's such a bizarre decision they made.
To its credits any scene or moment involving Tomie was a delight. She behaved exactly as one would expect and steals the show.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text: big slop. i adore junji ito’s manga - from tomie to frankenstein, uzumaki and his short stories - but this game feels like an insult in comparison. i genuinely can’t give it a good rating. and the ai-generated content? absolutely didn’t need to be there.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
Junji Ito deserves better.
Outside of a pretty delightful appearance from Tomie, there's really nothing here for fans or anyone really. Lackluster gameplay. dull environments, AI slop, repetitive enemies that are incredibly easy to dispatch or avoid... 
The worst thing a horror game can be is boring, and this is just that. How you can take ideas from one of the greatest horror mangaka of all time and end up with this bland mess is beyond me.
Is it playable? Yes. Should you waste your time on it? No.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
TLDR: Get it on sale if you are starved to see basically Anything Junji Ito-related.

It's mostly a negative review because I want the devs to get it right next time.
The game has potential, it has its fun moments, albait with a few caveats. 
Biggest issue is, it is NOT a Junji Ito game, it is at best a far-fetched fanfic with (extremely few) obvious references and cameos.

[b]The cons:[/b]
- The story has pretty much no connection with Junji Ito's stories (other than Tomie doing her thing)
- The writing only feels partially human ([i]cough cough[/i] ai generation) 
- The protagonist is the most generic amnesiac doormat you can think of
- The enemies are all the same, literally 3 enemy types the entire game, 2 of which you only see Once
- The game itself is short and not scary at all (the easiest bad ending you can get in about 40 mins / a bit over an hour, if you're directionally challenged like me)

[b]The pros:[/b]
+ Junji Ito references are still great to see if you're a fan
+ The fight or flight mechanics were unexpected, adding some fun dynamics to the gameplay loop
+ The game lets you fastforward to certain parts of the story to get the endings you want quicker
+ Some statues are so.. well-sculpted, they make me act unwise
+ The achievements are easy to get and nice to look at
+ Tomie is the queen, even when she's featured in a knock-off scoobydoo-like gaming product
+ There's technically a boss fight at the end, that one was neat

I'll not be replaying this game after getting 100% achievements, but I do hope it gets enough attention for these or other devs to make another, better game built upon the ideas featured here. Hopefully without ai and with more actual Junji Ito storytelling.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:

The game isn’t scary, and although the monsters look accurate, the story doesn’t create much horror.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text: I recommend holding off on this game until the developers address the extremely limited stamina bar, as it significantly slows down story progression. The encounter with Shimizu Rin is especially frustrating. She tends to camp your location which drags out the section far longer than necessary. Additional checkpoints would go a long way toward reducing that frustration. I was genuinely looking forward to this game, but it ultimately falls short in several areas. For instance, the game did not hit the mark on the horror department. A little more variety on enemies would've helped, it got old real quick. The dialogue also feels underdeveloped and makes the narrative much less engaging. I will say, the price is right.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
This is a very basic/simple horror game with Junji Ito's name slapped on it with couple of references to his work.

It has one or two moments where they managed something decent, however everything around clearly hasn't been given any additional thoughts or love. Just standard assets put together to make a working game.

I played the demo and tried to not let my expectations be too high. Yet I'm more disappointed than expected.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
I don't think I can honestly recommend this game.

It is devoid of any actual emotion - I didn't feel anything while playing. The only somewhat saving grace are the Junji Ito characters and monsters.

The storyline is boring (very edgy), combat system is lacking - with only 1 type of monster, or 2 if you can call the head balloon a monster - and the puzzles are laughingly easy. It only took me less than 2 hours to reach an ending, although there are apparently 2 other.

I'll play enough to reach those and revisit this review, but based on what I've seen, I don't think I'll change my mind.

MAYBE if you REALLY like Junji Ito you'll stomach this game, but that's just about it. It's disappointing for fans, to say the least.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
Game is good, good atmosphere, decent story for the price, a fun little experience ruined by the Rin boss that goes out of its way to be unreasonably difficult and highlight all the flaws of the game if they weren't apparent enough.  The boss, like all other enemies in the game, doesn't follow a unique pathway and instead coordinates itself depending where you are last seen. That said, there are no patterns, they will always out run you, and this being the only point in the game stamina is necessary quite literally ruins the entire experience because stamina is so quick to drain it feels like you only get a last desperate dash before the enemies makes you start the entire section over. 

This could have easily been fixed by adding checkpoints. Instead, the developers chose to artifcially extend the length of the game.

game_name: Junji Ito Maniac: An Infinite Gaol
review_text:
Story is everywhere and annoyingly hard to follow. Protagonist extremely unlikeable and hard to listen up. Tomie was the only good thing about this game. Chase scenes and hiding feel like any other cheap Unreal Engine 4 horror game - "run like you have poop in your pants and go into a locker".  They're more frustrating, unfun, and predictable than they are terrifying.

A total unfun slog to play.

this game is like if the devs played every terrible unity horror game and were told to do worse

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text: Though my time played is inflated as I've walked away from the game several times while it was open, this is an extremely short experience, perhaps an hour or so. It's also a poor experience riddled with a lack of quality and AI generated images. If the voice acting was AI generated as well, I couldn't tell, nor could I be sure if the story was human made or AI made because of this. The story is extremely briefly and cliche and the gameplay is very lackluster. You walk to and fro with a lantern that hardly illuminates much and you lose said lantern not even half way through the game. The entire premise of the game is quite literally ye olde 'mental health scary' which was fun when we were young in the early two thousands but it so played out now and not to mention that the mass majority know better (thankfully). This would've been better off as an interactive story but again, who knows if the story was human made or not, not to mention the backgrounds probably would've been AI blobs. Your entire roll in this game is to walk around, click, read (somewhat as it's half narrated (sometimes because the audio also randomly cuts off or changes or disappears nanoseconds after appearing)), and find not hidden keys. That's about it.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
У сюжета есть потенциал, но он слишком клиширован, очевиден и недостаточно интригующ в том виде, в котором попал в конечную версию игры.
Геймплей ничем не примечателен — просто ходишь, иногда открываешь двери и читаешь что-то.
Никакой собственной мозговой работы для прохождения не требуется — игра проводит "расследование" за тебя полностью, не давая даже пропустить улики.
ИИ-сгенерированные "картины" внутри истории дублируются многократно, что лишает их ценности внутри сюжета.
На один раз пройти эта игра нормальная, но полная цена на неё завышена.
Надеюсь, будущие проекты студии будут лучше проработаны. Удачи!

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
This ones a bit of a toss up for me, I can see why people enjoy this as the story has some interesting stuff going on, its just the other elements I found lacking and depending on your tastes you may or may not have a similar problem. 

The game is more of detective themed walking sim with a few minor puzzles than it is anything else. Theres not really any deductions to for you to work through, as you enter a new room you'll find a green glow touching it will cause a brief scene to play and reveal the next glow in the sequence. I wish I could love this game. It has an interesting premise, the atmosphere is fantastic, and I love the detective genre. 

However, there's a few problems, and one of them is one I have trouble excusing (number 3):
1) The controls will randomly stop working, and you'll need to click several times to interact or open the inventory
2) There's very little actual puzzle or detective element to it, it's mostly a walking sim. Walking sims are great, but I had expected this to be a puzzle game.
3) This will be a spoiler...
.
.
.
.
.
.
I cannot stand the use of Dissociative Identity Disorder as a way to create a mystery murderer. It's a trope that's overdone, to the point it's almost lazy. It'd have been more interesting to reveal that the person with DID was trying to solve the death of their loved one. Instead, they fell back on the Secret Alter Murderer trope. This trope is common in media and harmful to the DID community - there's only 12 documented cases of people with DID ever committing murder, and instead they are actually regularly victimized and abused both as children and adults. Demonizing an entire group of people based on a mental illness is sloppy and cruel.

Please do better.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
For the price of the game, it felt like too much of the game relied on AI. The game includes a disclaimer about using AI-generated photos, but it frames that use as minimal and limited to what was necessary. In reality, it can be seen through the entire experience. At times, the flat voice delivery and certain lines in the script (em dashes included) even made me question whether those elements were generated as well. 

I’m interested in what this team can do, because parts of the game showed real potential but I’d rather see more of their own work and far less reliance on AI, which has such a distinctive and obvious style that pulls me out of any game instantly.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
[h1]⭐ Review (Playtime: 2.6 hours)[/h1]

[b]Verdict:[/b] A very reluctant [i]Thumbs Down[/i], but with real appreciation for what the game does well.

This is a beautifully atmospheric noir story with an excellent twist ending. However, the overall experience feels more like watching a short interactive movie than playing a detective or puzzle game. Some players will absolutely enjoy that format — others (like me) may find it too limited for the price.

Below is a detailed breakdown.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]👍 The Good[/h2]

[list]
[*][b]Excellent atmosphere & setting[/b]
The 1940s noir environments are well crafted. Lighting, shadows, props, and mood are spot-on, and the game has a strong cinematic style.

[*][b]Strong core mystery[/b]
The story is genuinely interesting, and the themes feel cohesive and immersive.

[*][b]Fantastic twist ending[/b]
This was easily the highlight. It surprised me completely, and I loved how it reframed the events. I actually felt a little embarrassed I didn’t see it coming!

[*][b]“Green click to see the past” mechanic[/b]
These moments were visually engaging and fun to interact with. They’re some of the best-designed scenes.

[*][b]A few memorable moments[/b]
Including at least one mild jump scare that actually got me.

[*][b]Clear passion from the developers[/b]
The story, worldbuilding, and art direction show genuine care and effort.
[/list]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]👎 The Not-So-Good (For Me Personally)[/h2]

[list]
[*][b]More interactive movie than game[/b]
Despite the detective-themed description, I had almost no agency. The game tells you exactly what to do, where to go, and what to think.

[*][b]Extremely linear[/b]
Everything follows a fixed path. Exploration areas are small, and any blocked route is simply obstructed with boxes or chairs.

[*][b]Very simple puzzles[/b]
Puzzle sections make up about 5% of the game and are extremely basic. Clickable objects are always obvious.

[*][b]Little to no real deduction[/b]
The protagonist instantly announces where to go after each clue (“I must get to the ___ room now!”), so you never get to infer anything yourself.

[*][b]Slow, unskippable dialogue[/b]
If you read faster than the voice acting, there’s no option to speed it up or skip it. This made some scenes drag.

[*][b]No saving + no scene replay[/b]
You must replay sections if you exit early. If you miss a moment, there’s no chapter select to revisit it.

[*][b]Very short for the price[/b]
I finished in [b]2.6 hours[/b]. At ~$15 CAD (even with a discount), this felt too brief for the level of interactivity.

[*][b]Gameplay feels basic[/b]
Mechanically, nothing feels new, complex, or innovative.
[/list]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Who Will Enjoy This?[/h2]

[list]
[*]Players who enjoy short, cinematic stories
[*]Fans of atmospheric noir settings
[*]People who prefer minimal gameplay
[*]Newcomers to mystery games
[*]Players who want a guided, linear experience
[/list]


This is [b]not[/b] a bad game. It simply suits a specific audience — one that values mood and story over deduction and challenge.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Final Thoughts[/h2]

I appreciated many things about this game: the world, the tone, the twist, and the visual style. But as someone who loves FMV games, branching narratives, replayability, challenging puzzles, and genuine deduction, this felt too simple and too short for the price.

[b]The binary thumbs-up/thumbs-down system is difficult here — this really deserves a middle rating. Some players may truly love it, but for me it wasn’t substantial enough to fully recommend.[/b]

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
I'm very torn about this game. One the one hand I probably wouldn't have bought it if I'd seen the AI art notice but I missed it until I got IN the game and saw the art on the walls and I was dismayed. I wish they had just hired a small artist to do the victim's paintings and just used creative commons paintings otherwise. That aside, this game has a lot of potential but there are some major issues holding it back. First and foremest of these is the fact that it commits the cardinal sin of detective mysteries and shows it's hand WAY too soon and obviously. I figured out the big twist in the first 5 minutes of gameplay. If you intend to play the game and don't want spoilers, I suggest you stop reading here.



SPOILERS AHEAD



The name of the game was a big clue when combined with several obvious lies you were told and the way the mansion location was set up and played. If you hadn't NEEDED to search every room for successive keys to finally get to the actual crime scene and were allowed to miss vital clues if you didn't go out of way to investigate everywhere I feel like figuring it all out early would make the player would feel clever, not disappointed. It immediately raises suspicions that getting to the crime scene to investigate was so difficult when we were invited there to do exactly that. Having the main hall, staircase, and the victim's bedroom freely accessible but the rest of the house locked up that we could work to get into would have allayed suspicions more. Most importantly, the shadow appearing so quickly to the player character after literally seeing a reinactment of the victim's lover reacting to a crazed figure that never actually poses a threat and the statements of the maid about his sudden personality switches and cruelty made the truth glaringly obvious. The first time the Shadow appears was great, Spotting him stalking the halls and making us run to hide for a bit made sense and for that moment I wondered if I was wrong about the twist and there actually WAS still a killer around. But then a few minutes later we see him just standing a few feet away on the landing, staring at you without any hint of a threat and then disappearing the moment you go around a pillar to come towards him made it exceedingly obvious he wasn't real. If he'd continued to stalk towards us so we had to run and hide until we couldn't hear him anymore again, it would have been so much better. 

The asylum location and the unfurling of the mystery there was much better handled. I didn't see the victim's role in her own murder coming so that was a nice surprise. The client's phone calls during that section that were so emotionless and vaguely sinister were not as well handled. Our player character was still acting confused at her while I was rolling my eyes at how obviously she was setting us up for something. The reveal of the detective book in the final location was clever, though, as I hadn't realized she was repeating her daughters initial crime that closely.

All in all I feel like this dev team has real potential. The characters were interesting, I liked the voice acting, the style of the game was fun, and some parts of this mystery did delight me. Just get rid of the AI art, slow down on the reveals, trust your players are smart and will find the answers without you needing to feed it to them by hand, and give us a credible threat next time and I think it could be amazing.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
This is a low budget first person indie detective game with not much actual gameplay. It takes around three to four hours to complete and mostly involves finding keys and combinations for locks to progress the story. There's not really much detective work in this game... or any actually. It's more of a walking sim in my opinion. The graphics are also outdated and optimization is not very good. I experienced lots of frame rate drops in 4k resolution with an RTX4080, especially in areas outside the mansion. The only way I could maintain 60fps was to reduce the post processing to low and/or shadows to medium, which makes the game look pretty bad. Shadows above medium can also make the game look too dark in certain areas. However, when shadows are set to medium, this can cause major pop-in issues where objects disappear entirely until you get closer and they magically appear. By the way, optimization was also poor in a previous game released by this developer called Charon's Staircase. This game really could use an option for DLSS and/or a resolution slider. However, there are none of these options in the game. 

Other issues with this game are that remapping the side mouse buttons doesn't actually work, turning off subtitles doesn't work, the fov feels low even after adjusting the fov slider, and the lantern does not have much of an effect. There is also no manual save and some checkpoints are rather far away. There is also lots of lumen flicker where you can see lines of white light flicker in peripherals when turning. I believe this is due to global illumination gather quality and the attenuation radius of lights and exposure compensation. There are also lots of areas with flickering textures and obvious texture lines that were not blended well. Textures are also low-res and muddy looking. There is also a heavy fog effect that makes the graphics look faded and washed out. This is especially noticeable in the sanatorium where you are forced to explore in the dark without a lantern. I also found the ending to be rather strange and confusing. It's one of those endings where you will most likely think that you never saw that coming. However, this is because it makes little sense. Overall, there are just too many issues that kept me from enjoying this game. Therefore, I cannot recommend it.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text: Though my time played is inflated as I've walked away from the game several times while it was open, this is an extremely short experience, perhaps an hour or so. It's also a poor experience riddled with a lack of quality and AI generated images. If the voice acting was AI generated as well, I couldn't tell, nor could I be sure if the story was human made or AI made because of this. The story is extremely briefly and cliche and the gameplay is very lackluster. You walk to and fro with a lantern that hardly illuminates much and you lose said lantern not even half way through the game. The entire premise of the game is quite literally ye olde 'mental health scary' which was fun when we were young in the early two thousands but it so played out now and not to mention that the mass majority know better (thankfully). This would've been better off as an interactive story but again, who knows if the story was human made or not, not to mention the backgrounds probably would've been AI blobs. Your entire roll in this game is to walk around, click, read (somewhat as it's half narrated (sometimes because the audio also randomly cuts off or changes or disappears nanoseconds after appearing)), and find not hidden keys. That's about it.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
This should get 'Do Not Recommend' simply because you have not one, but two crowbars that both break after prying rotting wood off a wall. How even!?

--------

Unfortunately the game contains much more AI than one would expect, and while there is a disclaimer that AI is used minimally for decorative posters and such like, it's evident that it's been used in many other places: assets contain obvious use of AI with noticeably corrupted or mildly nonsensical text, the names of the characters are all distinctly uncanny and often mistyped (Sherdinand or Sherdinant? Mulder or Muller? Fordside or Fortside?), and the text on notes often doesn't match the 'readable' transcript with details often truncated or skipped. If these legit have no AI involvement then it speaks instead to an overall lack of quality.

This wouldn't be a dealbreaker per se, as it doesn't affect the story or gameplay aside from giving things an uncanny, sort of naff appearance.

The story itself sits comfortably within the genre of creepypasta--the call is coming from inside the house--but you'd be forgiven for failing to piece together a cohesive narrative thread from the start to a finish so neck-bracingly abrupt as to imagine a head-first ejection through a car windscreen. One or two opportunities to flesh out this story present themselves, but these are left aside as the game speed-runs to a conclusion.

I could still give the game a pass because of that, since it's actually decently voice acted too. Nothing wrong with a cheesy fireside horror story after all, but I've yet to cover the gameplay.

At its core The Last Case of John Morley is a linear walking simulator with one or two basic puzzles and creepy jump scares to break it up. You're a private eye, asked to solve a case. In some parts you're learning about the case by revealing ghosts or memories that help explain what happened, drip fed information through notes and diaries scattered around. You'd see exactly this type of game as fodder for certain Youtubers' horror game channels.

In isolation, the AI slop, the rushed story, and the simple gameplay are all easily forgiven - being rough in one area can still be compensated for by being fun in the others. All together, though, and it's just an overall 'meh' experience.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text:
From the start, the vibe of the game was off. I had read about AI use in the game, which may have played a part in the end, but other things diminished my enjoyment really, really quickly.

1. The controls (playing with a PS5 controller on PC) were not quite right and not explained at all (or at least I did not see any explanation). Looking around with the right thumbstick felt too jumpy. This could be rectified in the settings, but it left a tiny bit of a bad first impression.

2. The first section is a dream/nightmare sequence, where you are following a linear path in dark woods, apparently afraid of and running from something. Except that nothing was chasing us. Spooky noises and dark trees are not enough for the atmosphere that the game wanted to create. If the sequence did not involve this aspect, then it would probably have been fine -- until the end, when a cheap jumpscare happened. Number 2 (and 3) on the list of things that made me question why I bought this.

3. We wake up in a hospital bed of sorts and the character starts exposition dumping (read: yapping monotonously). I really did not vibe with the PC's voice and the writing here. It got worse when a nurse appeared, also read her lines monotonously and I got to see the horrible facial expressions. Look, I usually don't mind these things that much. Some of my favourite games are from the 90s and 00s where we would have been thrilled with animations like these. But here, with everything else going on, I felt it was too much.

I don't know if AI was used to generate the character voices (I hope not), but regardless it was not a good first impression. Add that to the fact that the game costs 10+ Euros, is overall apparently only 2-3 hours in length and all of my earlier impressions of the game were lackluster at best? Yeah, I stopped and refunded. Your mileage may vary and if this game gets to a few hundred or thousand good reviews on Steam, I might reconsider. But at this point in time I am out, my backlog is full of games that I have not touched yet that promise to deliver a far better experience right now.

game_name: The Last Case of John Morley
review_text: This game's AI-generated content disclosure seems to suggest that AI is a minor aspect of the game. I've played for less than an hour and counted 7 or 8 in-game posters, notes, etc. which are clearly AI. They're not minor either: I've had to look at them to earn trophies, and essentially all of them were in places so conspicuous a player would click on them just for the sake of being thorough. This game is riddled with AI. It ruins immersion, and its blatant overuse has me questioning whether the voice lines, cutscenes, or 3D models are also AI. Very disappointed.

A few highlights from my 23 minutes of gameplay:
"Mirrors" that are just concave pictures of the room you're in. All reflections look very odd.
A very unwieldy UI
Frame rate issues whenever looking at the sky
The realization that I'm not going to build up from a street level superhero to a god tier protector of Earth: you start off as essentially superman
A poster in your bedroom that is clearly Superwoman fan art right next to what is clearly classic Flash fanart.
Completing 5 or 6 different Superhero mission types while listening to this comedically over dramatic AI music just for the music to switch to 80's R&B on the last mission.

I'm hopeful that one day I'll return to this game to find it fleshed out, polished, and with a loyal playerbase. But frankly, I don't have the optimism other reviewers seem to have.
